```text
id: 100-inherent-impl-and-self
move: write an *inherent implementation* — `impl Type { ... }` — for a struct, author one *associated function* `fn new() -> Self` (no `self` parameter) and one *method* `fn current(&self) -> u32` (first parameter `&self`) inside it, and call them from `fn main` via the path form `Type::name()` and the dot form `value.method()` respectively, using `Self` inside the impl as an alias for the implementing type; concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } impl Counter { fn new() -> Self { Counter { count: 0 } } fn current(&self) -> u32 { self.count } } fn main() { let c = Counter::new(); println!("count = {}", c.current()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `count = 0` and exits 0 — four composed pieces (impl block, associated function with `Self` return, `&self` method, two call shapes in `main`) all witnessed in one minimal program; the centered contrast probe with the same source modulo `&self` removed from `current`'s signature (body trivialized to `0` to keep `self` out of scope) and call site `c.current()` left unchanged fires `error[E0599]: no method named \`current\` found for struct \`Counter\` in the current scope` with `--> no_self.rs:10:30`, caret under `current`, the inline label `this is an associated function, not a method`, the `= note:` line `to be used as methods, functions must have a \`self\` parameter`, a supplementary `note:` pointing at `fn current() -> u32 { 0 }` at the definition site, and a `help:` block proposing the path form `Counter::current()` as the alternative call shape; the auxiliary E0061 probe with the same `&self` method but the call site switched to `Counter::current()` (no argument) fires `error[E0061]: this function takes 1 argument but 0 arguments were supplied` with `argument #1 of type \`&Counter\` is missing` — empirical witness that a `&self` method *is* reachable via the path form but the receiver becomes a positional argument; the auxiliary working probe `Counter::current(&c)` compiles and prints byte-identically to the dot-form working probe — empirical witness that both call shapes resolve the same method per Reference items/associated-items.md lines 105-112; the auxiliary `Self { count: 0 }` probe compiles and runs identically to the `Counter { count: 0 }` working probe — empirical witness for the `Self` alias on the construction-expression side; the auxiliary reordered probe (method declared before associated function inside the impl block) compiles and runs identically — empirical witness that associated-item order is free per the Reference grammar `AssociatedItem*`
main_concept: an *inherent implementation* is the smallest unit of method authoring in Rust; one centered claim with four coupled pieces under Book ch05-03 lines 4-11 (load-bearing, the method-vs-free-function framing), 51-66 (load-bearing, the `impl` block + `&self`-shorthand-for-`self: &Self` + `Self`-as-alias rules), 283-298 (load-bearing, the *associated function* terminology and the no-`self`-first-parameter rule), 322-331 (load-bearing, `Self` in body and return + the `Type::name()` call form) and Reference items/implementations.md lines 12-16 (load-bearing, the formal `InherentImpl` grammar) and 41-67 (load-bearing, the semantics — implementing type, associated items, multiple impl blocks allowed, kinds of items inherent impls may contain) and items/associated-items.md lines 36-79 (load-bearing, *associated functions* definition + the canonical `Struct::new() -> Struct` example), 105-112 (load-bearing, "associated functions whose first parameter is named `self` are called *methods*"), 153-159 (load-bearing, the `&self`-as-`self: &Self` shorthand table) and error_codes/E0599.md (load-bearing, the corpus warrant for the centered contrast). (1) *Block syntax*: `impl Type { ... }` is an inherent implementation per Reference grammar `InherentImpl → impl GenericParams? Type WhereClause? { InnerAttribute* AssociatedItem* }`. The body is a sequence of *associated items* attached to the *implementing type*. Today exercises only the `fn`-item shape; associated constants and associated types (Reference grammar `AssociatedItem` admits both, plus `MacroInvocationSemi`) are deferred. (2) *Associated function*: a `fn` inside the impl whose parameter list does *not* start with `self` is an associated function (Book ch05-03 line 285 verbatim "All functions defined within an `impl` block are called *associated functions*"). Called from outside via the path form `Type::name()` per Book ch05-03 lines 326-331 and Probe 1. The Book's canonical example is `String::from`; today's is `Counter::new`. (3) *Method*: a `fn` inside the impl whose first parameter is `&self` is a method per Reference items/associated-items.md line 105 verbatim "Associated functions whose first parameter is named `self` are called *methods*". The `&self` is shorthand for `self: &Self` per Reference items/associated-items.md line 154 and Book ch05-03 lines 61-63 verbatim. Called via the dot form `value.method()` (lesson 040, unchanged); also reachable via the path form with the receiver as positional argument #1 per Reference line 111 "as well as the usual function call notation" (Probe 4). Without `&self`, the dot call fails — Probe 2 witnesses E0599 with the diagnostic message itself stating today's rule twice ("this is an associated function, not a method" + "to be used as methods, functions must have a `self` parameter"). (4) *`Self` keyword*: inside the impl, `Self` is an alias for the implementing type per Book ch05-03 lines 62-63 verbatim "Within an `impl` block, the type `Self` is an alias for the type that the `impl` block is for." Today uses `Self` in the return-type slot of `new`; the auxiliary Probe 5 witnesses that `Self { count: 0 }` also works in struct-expression position (Book ch05-03 lines 311-313 use this shape in the `square` example). The audience crosses from "I declare a struct (095) and call methods on standard-library types (040)" to "I author my own methods on my own struct." Today is *shallow operational*: install only inherent impl with two receiver shapes (`&self` for methods, no receiver for associated functions) plus `Self` as a type alias, on a primitive-field struct with primitive return types; explicitly defer `&mut self` (lesson 101 follow-on), `self` by-value receiver, multiple impl blocks for one type (Book ch05-03 lines 333-381 / Listing 5-16), trait impls `impl Trait for Type { ... }` (blocked on trait machinery), associated constants and associated types, generic impls `impl<T> Type<T> { ... }`, lifetime-parameterized impls `impl<'a> Type<'a> { ... }`, the auto-deref method-resolution rule (Book ch05-03 lines 135-180), `Self::name()` self-references inside the impl body, `pub` on impl items (composes lesson 096 with today), the path-form call shape `Counter::current(&c)` for `&self` methods (named in evidence Probe 4 but not centered in the lesson body), and trait methods showing up under the dot. This unlocks reading the rmp target repo's first inherent-impl block `impl BigUInt { pub fn zero() -> Self { BigUInt { limbs: vec![] } } pub fn is_zero(&self) -> bool { return self.limbs.len() == 0; } }` in `biguint/basic.rs` per the rmp-target-audit recommendation §11 chain item 5; `pub`, `vec![]`, `Vec<u64>::len()`, `&mut self` methods, multiple impl blocks, and trait impls all remain blocked downstream and are listed as deferred future moves.
depends_on: 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — declaration `struct Name { field: Type }`, struct expression `Name { field: value }`, field access `instance.field`; today reuses all three inside the impl: `Counter` is the impl target, `Counter { count: 0 }` is the body of `new`, and `self.count` is a field access on the receiver; the only new piece on the field-access side is that the receiver name `self` is the implicit shorthand parameter rather than a `let`-bound name), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — the call-site form `value.method(args)`; today's `c.current()` is exactly that shape, and today fills in the *authoring* side — `&self` in the signature is what makes the dot call resolve; Probe 2's E0599 contrast witnesses what fails when the authoring side omits `&self`), 020-function-with-parameter (load-bearing — `fn name(p: T) { ... }` with one typed parameter; functions inside an impl are the same `fn` shape, with the new rule that the first parameter slot may be `&self`), 021-function-return-value (load-bearing — `fn name(p: T) -> R { ... }` with a return type; today's `fn new() -> Self` and `fn current(&self) -> u32` are the same return-type form, with `Self` as a new spelling for the return type), 043-nested-module-paths (load-bearing — the path call `path::name(args)` with `::` between segments; today's `Counter::new()` is the same shape with a *type* on the left of `::` rather than a module; Book ch05-03 lines 327-330 names this directly: "the `::` syntax is used for both associated functions and namespaces created by modules"), 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — the field type and method return type used today; no new claim about `u32` is introduced), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point; the probe uses one `fn main` block, unchanged), 005-let-binding (cited — `let c = Counter::new();` is the same form lesson 005 installed), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with one `{}` slot consumed by `c.current()`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0599 transcript and Probe 3's E0061 transcript; Probe 2 carries an additional `note:` block with a second `-->` line at the definition site, the same shape lesson 096 installed for E0603), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), 098-enum-with-unit-variants (cited only — cross-reference for the E0599 E-code; lesson 098 installed E0599 in the "no variant ... found" shape, today extends to "no method ... found"; the Reference catalogs E0599 broadly as "method, associated function, or item not found"), 036-multiple-parameters (cited only — Probe 3's E0061 transcript reuses lesson 036's E-code on a path-form method call where `&self` becomes positional argument #1), 069-rustc-warnings (cited only — Probes 2 and 3's `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` trailer is read with lesson 069's category map; the working probe produces no warnings), 096-inline-module-and-pub (cited only — *What To Ignore For Now* names the rmp target's `pub fn zero() -> Self` as composing lesson 096's `pub` with today's associated-function shape), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`&mut self` receiver — the mutation method shape; the natural follow-on to today (lesson 101); composes today's `&self` rule with lesson 006's `mut` and lesson 047's `&mut` reference vocabulary; method body may write through `self.field = value` patterns" moves, future "`self` by-value receiver — the consuming method shape; rare in practice (Book ch05-03 lines 76-79 verbatim); used when a method transforms `self` into something else and prevents further use of the original" moves, future "multiple `impl` blocks for one type — Book Listing 5-16 (lines 333-381) shows two `impl Rectangle { ... }` blocks side by side, each adding more associated items; the rmp target uses this heavily (separate `impl BigUInt` blocks for arithmetic, comparison, conversion, formatting, etc., split across files in `biguint/`); composes today's single-block move trivially" moves, future "trait impls `impl Trait for Type { ... }` — different syntax, different semantics; Reference items/implementations.md lines 18-24 give the formal grammar; blocked on trait machinery (Q07); the rmp target's `impl PartialOrd for BigInt`, `impl Add for BigUInt`, etc. all wait on this" moves, future "associated constants `const N: u32 = ...;` inside an impl block — Reference items/associated-items.md lines 290-329 cover the formal rules; the rmp target uses these for things like `BASE: u64`" moves, future "associated types `type Output = ...;` inside an impl block — Reference items/associated-items.md lines 230-289 cover the formal rules; only allowed inside trait impls per items/implementations.md line 59; downstream of trait machinery" moves, future "generic impls `impl<T> Type<T> { ... }` — Reference items/implementations.md lines 205-316 cover the formal rules; type parameters on the impl block; downstream of generics installation" moves, future "lifetime-parameterized impls `impl<'a> Type<'a> { ... }` — composes today's impl with lifetime parameters; the rmp target uses these for `impl FromStr for BigInt`-shaped trait impls" moves, future "the auto-deref method-resolution rule — Book ch05-03 lines 135-180 'Where's the `->` Operator?' explains that Rust automatically inserts `&`, `&mut`, or `*` at the call site so the receiver matches the method's `self` type; today's `c.current()` uses this rule implicitly, but the rule itself is its own move" moves, future "calling `Self::name()` inside the impl body — the `Self` shorthand for `Type::name()` self-references; useful when an associated function calls another associated function on the same type without naming the type explicitly" moves, future "`pub` on impl items — visibility on associated functions and methods; composes lesson 096's `pub` keyword with today's authoring shape; the rmp target's `pub fn zero() -> Self` and `pub fn is_zero(&self) -> bool` are the canonical shapes; `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` on impl items are the deferred restricted-visibility variants" moves, future "calling `&self` methods via the path form `Counter::current(&c)` — the auxiliary Probe 4 today witnesses that this is a valid call shape per Reference items/associated-items.md line 111 'as well as the usual function call notation'; useful for disambiguating method resolution and for explicit trait dispatch; not centered today" moves, future "trait methods showing up under the dot — many real-world dot calls resolve to trait methods, not inherent ones; the resolution rule is shared between today's inherent methods and trait methods, but the trait-impl authoring side is its own move" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable inherent-impl block — `impl BigUInt { pub fn zero() -> Self { BigUInt { limbs: vec![] } } pub fn is_zero(&self) -> bool { return self.limbs.len() == 0; } }` in `biguint/basic.rs`; composes today's impl + associated function + `&self` method + `Self` keyword with deferred `pub`, `vec![]`, `Vec<u64>::len()`, and the explicit `return` form lesson 025 deferred" moves, future "the rmp target's longer inherent-impl chains — `impl BigUInt { ... }` blocks in each of `add.rs`, `cmp.rs`, `convert.rs`, `div.rs`, `format.rs`, `mul.rs`, `shift.rs`; composes today's single-block move with the multiple-impl-blocks deferral" moves
evidence:
```

```text
id: 101-mut-self-receiver
move: extend lesson 100's inherent impl with the third receiver shape `&mut self` — author one mutating method `fn bump(&mut self) { self.count = self.count + 1; }` alongside the existing `&self` reader and no-receiver associated function, so the impl block now carries all three of {associated function, `&self` method, `&mut self` method}; on the caller side, bind the instance with `let mut c = Counter::new();` (lesson 006's reassignable-binding `mut`, now load-bearing for the dot call), then call `c.bump()` twice and read back via `c.current()`; concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } impl Counter { fn new() -> Self { Counter { count: 0 } } fn current(&self) -> u32 { self.count } fn bump(&mut self) { self.count = self.count + 1; } } fn main() { let mut c = Counter::new(); c.bump(); c.bump(); println!("count = {}", c.current()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `count = 2` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe with the same source modulo `let mut c = Counter::new();` changed to `let c = Counter::new();` (everything else unchanged) fires `error[E0596]: cannot borrow \`c\` as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` with `--> no_mut.rs:18:9`, caret on the binding line `let c = Counter::new();` labeled `not mutable`, two secondary annotations on the call sites `c.bump();` both labeled `cannot borrow as mutable`, and a `help:` block proposing `let mut c = Counter::new();` with `+++` markers — empirical witness that the binding-side `mut` is what licenses the `&mut self` dot call; the secondary contrast probe with the working source modulo `bump`'s receiver `&mut self` replaced with `&self` (body unchanged) fires `error[E0594]: cannot assign to \`self.count\`, which is behind a \`&\` reference` with caret under the assignment expression and a `help:` proposing `&mut self` — empirical witness that the body-side `&mut` is what licenses the `self.field = value` assignment target (and a co-emitted `unused_mut` warning on `let mut c` confirms rustc proves the body is unreachable for write under `&self`); the auxiliary witness probe with `bump`'s receiver written long-form as `self: &mut Self` compiles and runs byte-identically to the shorthand probe — empirical witness for the third row of the Reference shorthand table at items/associated-items.md lines 153-159
main_concept: `&mut self` is the third receiver shape inside an inherent `impl` block, the receiver-shorthand for `self: &mut Self` per Reference items/associated-items.md line 159 (third row of the same shorthand table that gave us `&self` ↔ `self: &Self` in lesson 100). Today's contribution is light because most machinery is reused from lesson 100. (1) *Authoring*: a `fn` inside the impl whose first parameter is `&mut self` is a method that may *write* to fields of the receiver (Book ch05-03 lines 67-70: "Methods can take ownership of `self`, borrow `self` immutably ... or borrow `self` mutably"; lines 73-75: "If we wanted to change the instance that we've called the method on as part of what the method does, we'd use `&mut self` as the first parameter."). (2) *Mutation through the receiver*: `self.field = value;` inside a `&mut self` body is a legal assignment target — lesson 095's field-access expression `self.count` extends to *lvalue* position because `self` is `&mut Self` rather than `&Self`. (3) *Calling*: the dot-call site `c.method()` is unchanged from lesson 040, but the binding must now be `let mut c` (lesson 006). The dot call elaborates to a `&mut c` borrow per the auto-referencing rule (Book ch05-03 lines 147-180, deferred); without `let mut c`, that elaboration fires E0596. The Book's three-way *reading*/*mutating*/*consuming* split (lines 175-180) parallels the three receiver shapes exactly — `&self` (lesson 100, reading), `&mut self` (today, mutating), `self` by value (lesson 102, consuming). The audience crosses from "I can author read-only methods on my own struct" to "I can author methods that mutate the struct's fields." Today is *shallow operational*: install only the third receiver shape and the binding-side `mut` rule, on the same `Counter { count: u32 }` probe lesson 100 used; explicitly defer `self` by-value receiver (lesson 102), the full borrow-check aliasing rule "one `&mut` xor any number of `&`" (E0499/E0502/E0505 are future moves), reborrows inside `&mut self` bodies (`self.vec.push(x)`-shaped patterns that take a fresh `&mut` of a field), methods returning a `&mut` reference into `self` (`iter_mut`-shaped APIs), the auto-deref method-resolution rule (still deferred from lesson 100), the `*self = expr` deref-write-through-pointer form (the rmp target's `Sign::flip` uses this, but today writes through `self.field = value` only), interior mutability (`Cell`/`RefCell`/`Mutex`), and compound assignment (`+=` etc.). This unlocks reading the many `&mut self` methods on the rmp target's `BigUInt` — `pub fn flip(&mut self)` in `bigint.rs`, `pub fn limb_lshift_add_assign(&mut self, lshift_limbs: usize, rhs: &Self)` in `biguint/add.rs`, etc. — though the `*self = ...` form, the additional parameters, and reborrows inside the body all remain blocked downstream and are listed as deferred future moves.
depends_on: 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `impl Type { ... }`, `&self` methods, `Self`, and associated functions; today extends 100 with one new receiver shape, reusing `Counter`, `new`, `current`, and the impl block untouched), 006-mut-binding (load-bearing — `let mut name = value;`; today's caller side requires `let mut c`, and Probe 2 witnesses E0596 when the `mut` is dropped — the consequence of missing `mut` is now extended from "can't reassign with `c = new_value;`" to "can't dot-call a `&mut self` method"), 047-mutable-reference (load-bearing — `&mut T` as the mutable sibling of `&T`; today's `&mut self` is `&mut Self` in the receiver-shorthand position per Reference items/associated-items.md lines 153-159; lesson 047 already named E0596 in *What To Ignore For Now* as a future move, and today centers it), 095-struct-with-named-fields (cited — `instance.field` access; today extends to lvalue position `self.field = value` under `&mut self`), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — the dot-call form `value.method()`, unchanged), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0596 transcript and Probe 3's E0594 transcript), 069-rustc-warnings (cited — Probe 3 emits both an error and a warning; the lesson body does not center the warning, but the appendix reads it with 069's category map), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), 002-fn-main-entry-point, 005-let-binding, 011-println-positional-args, 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — all reused unchanged from lesson 100's probe), 020-function-with-parameter, 021-function-return-value (cited — every `fn` inside today's impl is the same `fn` shape lesson 100 inherited from 020/021), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`self` by-value receiver — the consuming-receiver shape, the third and final receiver form; Book ch05-03 lines 76-79 verbatim 'Having a method that takes ownership of the instance by using just `self` as the first parameter is rare; this technique is usually used when the method transforms `self` into something else and prevents the caller from using the original instance after the transformation'; the natural follow-on (lesson 102), composes today's split with ownership transfer rules" moves, future "the full borrow-check aliasing rule — 'one `&mut` xor any number of `&`'; today surfaces E0596 (one piece of the rule); the full rule covers E0499 (multiple mutable borrows), E0502 (mutable + immutable borrows), E0505 (move while borrowed); each is its own move" moves, future "reborrows inside `&mut self` bodies — `self.vec.push(x)`-shaped patterns where a method body takes a fresh `&mut` of a struct field through `self`; subtle, deferred" moves, future "methods returning a `&mut` reference into `self` — `iter_mut`-shaped APIs and shape `fn get_mut(&mut self, i: usize) -> &mut T`; composes today's `&mut self` with reference-typed return values (lesson 045/047 vocabulary)" moves, future "compound assignment `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `%=` — today's `self.count = self.count + 1` is the long form; the compound-assign sugar collapses both sides; deferred" moves, future "auto-deref method resolution — Book ch05-03 lines 135-180 'Where's the `->` Operator?' explains that Rust automatically inserts `&`, `&mut`, or `*` at the call site so the receiver matches the method's `self` type; today's `c.bump()` uses this rule implicitly (the dot site elaborates to `(&mut c).bump()`), but the rule itself is its own move; deferred since lesson 100" moves, future "`*self = expr` deref-write-through-pointer inside `&mut self` — composes today's `&mut self` with lesson 047's deref-assign form; the rmp target's `Sign::flip` is the canonical use; not centered today" moves, future "method-takes-other-instances-of-same-type as additional parameter — `fn can_hold(&self, other: &Rectangle) -> bool` and the rmp target's `pub fn add(&self, rhs: &Self) -> Self`; composes today's receiver shape with reference-typed parameters (lesson 046/048 vocabulary already installs the parameter side)" moves, future "interior mutability — `Cell<T>`, `RefCell<T>`, `Mutex<T>`; orthogonal mechanism that lets methods mutate state through a `&self` receiver; deferred" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable `&mut self` method — `pub fn flip(&mut self) { *self = self.flipped() }` in `bigint.rs` (the `Sign::flip` method); composes today's `&mut self` with the deferred `*self = expr` form" moves, future "the rmp target's longer `&mut self` method chains — the many `&mut self` methods on `BigUInt` in `biguint/add.rs`, `biguint/shift.rs`, etc.; composes today's receiver shape with multiple parameters and reborrows" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch05-03-method-syntax.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/associated-items.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0596.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0594.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/101-mut-self-receiver.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/101-mut-self-receiver.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 102-self-by-value-receiver
move: extend lessons 100/101's inherent impl with the third and final receiver shape `self` (no `&`, no `mut`) — author one consuming method `fn into_inner(self) -> u32 { self.value }` on a fresh `struct Wrapper { value: u32 }`, call it once via the dot form `let inner = w.into_inner();`, and read back the returned value via `println!`; concretely the working probe `struct Wrapper { value: u32 } impl Wrapper { fn into_inner(self) -> u32 { self.value } } fn main() { let w = Wrapper { value: 42 }; let inner = w.into_inner(); println!("inner = {}", inner); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `inner = 42` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe with the same source plus a single added line `let inner2 = w.into_inner();` after the first call (and the `println!` updated to consume both `inner` and `inner2`) fires `error[E0382]: use of moved value: \`w\`` with `--> use_after_move.rs:14:18`, caret on the second call site, three coupled annotations — the binding line `let w = Wrapper { value: 42 };` labeled `move occurs because \`w\` has type \`Wrapper\`, which does not implement the \`Copy\` trait`, the first call line `let inner = w.into_inner();` labeled `\`w\` moved due to this method call`, and the second call line `let inner2 = w.into_inner();` labeled `value used here after move` — and a separate `note:` block with its own `-->` line pointing at the method-definition site `use_after_move.rs:6:19` reading `\`Wrapper::into_inner\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`w\``, witnessing today's rule verbatim from rustc's mouth; the auxiliary witness probe with the same source modulo `into_inner`'s receiver written long-form as `self: Self` compiles and runs byte-identically to the shorthand probe — empirical witness for the first row of the Reference shorthand table at items/associated-items.md lines 153-159 (`self` ↔ `self: Self`), parallel to lesson 100's `&self` ↔ `self: &Self` witness and lesson 101's `&mut self` ↔ `self: &mut Self` witness
main_concept: bare `self` (no `&`, no `mut`) is the third and final receiver shape inside an inherent `impl` block — the *consuming* receiver — completing the three-way split Book ch05-03 lines 175-180 names ("reading (`&self`), mutating (`&mut self`), or consuming (`self`)"). Today's contribution is light because most machinery is reused from lessons 100 and 101. (1) *Authoring*: a `fn` inside the impl whose first parameter is bare `self` is the receiver-shorthand for `self: Self` per Reference items/associated-items.md line 157 (first row of the shorthand table that gave us `&self` ↔ `self: &Self` in lesson 100 and `&mut self` ↔ `self: &mut Self` in lesson 101); witnessed empirically by today's auxiliary Probe 3. (2) *Consumption at the call site*: the dot site `value.method()` is unchanged from lesson 040, but for a `self`-receiver method on a non-`Copy` type, the call *moves* the value into the method, which means the binding `value` cannot be used again afterward — Probe 2 witnesses this verbatim with E0382. The diagnostic itself names today's rule from rustc's mouth: the `note:` block reads `\`Wrapper::into_inner\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`w\``, mapping the *receiver shape* directly to the *move outcome*. (3) *Use-case framing*: Book ch05-03 lines 76-79 verbatim "Having a method that takes ownership of the instance by using just `self` as the first parameter is rare; this technique is usually used when the method transforms `self` into something else and you want to prevent the caller from using the original instance after the transformation" — the consuming receiver is a *transform* shape, not a typical reader/mutator. Most real-world methods use `&self` or `&mut self`. The audience crosses from "I can author read-only and mutating methods on my own struct" (lessons 100, 101) to "I can author the third method shape that consumes the value." Today is *shallow operational*: install only the third receiver shape and the consume-on-call rule, on a fresh `Wrapper { value: u32 }` probe; explicitly defer move semantics in depth (the full move-vs-borrow framework anchored in Book Ch4), the `Copy` trait and the Copy/non-Copy distinction (visible in the contrast probe's "does not implement the `Copy` trait" annotation but not centered), `Drop` (what happens to a moved-then-dropped value), builder patterns (`value.method1().method2()` chains where each step takes `self` and returns `Self`), `mut self` without `&` (Reference items/associated-items.md lines 165-167 — `fn method(mut self)` makes the consumed `self` a mutable variable inside the body), cloning to avoid the move (`value.clone().method(...)` — trait machinery), the full move-rule grammar in the Reference, the move-out-of-field rules, and `*self = expr` deref-write inside `&mut self` bodies. This unlocks reading `impl Neg for BigInt { fn neg(self) -> Self::Output { ... } }` in the rmp target's `bigint.rs` — the *receiver shape* is today's, though the `impl Trait for Type` shape is its own deferred move (blocked on trait machinery, Q07); also unlocks reading `Vec::into_iter`, `String::into_bytes`, `Box::into_inner`, and other `into_*` standard-library methods that take `self`.
depends_on: 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `impl Type { ... }`, `Self`, `&self` methods, and associated functions; today's `impl Wrapper { ... }` is the same block shape with one new associated item, the `self`-receiver method `into_inner`; today's auxiliary long-form witness `self: Self` parallels lesson 100's `&self` ↔ `self: &Self` witness from the same Reference shorthand table), 101-mut-self-receiver (load-bearing — installs `&mut self` and explicitly frames the three-receiver-shapes trio in *What To Ignore For Now* and *Mental Model Delta*; today completes the trio that 101 could only point at, adding the consume column to the `&self` (read) / `&mut self` (read + write) split that lesson 101 installed; the Book's "reading / mutating / consuming" classification cited in lesson 101's evidence motivates today's third position), 095-struct-with-named-fields (cited — `struct Wrapper { value: u32 }` declaration, `Wrapper { value: 42 }` construction, and `self.value` field access reused unchanged; no new claim about field expressions today — `self.value` sits on the right-hand side of the implicit return like in lesson 095, not on a left-hand assignment target which would be lesson 101's case), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — the dot-call form `value.method()`, unchanged from lesson 040 via lesson 100), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0382 transcript; today's diagnostic carries a separate `note:` block with its own `-->` line pointing at the method-definition site, the same multi-`-->` shape lesson 096 first installed for E0603), 069-rustc-warnings (cited — Probe 2's `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` trailer is read with lesson 069's category map; the working probe produces no warnings), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), 002-fn-main-entry-point, 005-let-binding, 011-println-positional-args, 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — all reused unchanged from lesson 100/101's probe shape), 020-function-with-parameter, 021-function-return-value (cited — every `fn` inside today's impl is the same `fn` shape lesson 100 already inherited from 020/021, now with `self` (no `&`) in the first-parameter slot), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Copy` trait and the Copy/non-Copy distinction — `let a = 5; let b = a; let c = a;` works for `u32` because `u32` is `Copy`, but the same shape on a non-`Copy` struct moves; visible in today's contrast probe's `does not implement the \`Copy\` trait` annotation but not centered; the smallest atomic move on the path to full move semantics" moves, future "move semantics in depth — the rule 'non-`Copy` types are moved when bound to a new name or passed as a non-reference argument'; Book Ch4's main arc; today is the operational surface, the framework is deferred" moves, future "the `Drop` trait — what happens to a moved-then-dropped value; trait machinery, deferred until trait arc opens" moves, future "builder patterns — `value.method1().method2()` chains where each step takes `self` and returns `Self`; common in real-world Rust (`std::fs::OpenOptions::new().read(true).write(true).open(path)` shape); composes today's `self` receiver with method chaining and `Self` return type" moves, future "`mut self` without `&` — `fn method(mut self) { ... }` makes the consumed `self` a mutable variable inside the body, similar to `mut` on regular parameters; Reference items/associated-items.md lines 165-167; subtle and rarely needed but completes the parameter-mutability table" moves, future "the `Clone` trait and `value.clone().method(...)` — trait machinery; lets the caller keep the original by cloning before consuming; deferred until trait arc opens" moves, future "move-out-from-a-field rules — when the body of a `self`-receiver method moves out of `self.field` (today's `self.value` happens to be a `Copy` field, so it is copied not moved); subtle, deferred" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable `impl Trait for Type { fn method(self) -> ... }` — `impl Neg for BigInt { fn neg(self) -> Self::Output { ... } }` in `bigint.rs`; the *receiver shape* is today's but the `impl Trait for Type` shape is blocked on trait machinery (Q07)" moves, future "standard-library `into_*` consuming methods — `Vec::into_iter`, `String::into_bytes`, `Box::into_inner`, `Result::into_ok`; all take `self`; the `into_` naming convention for `self`-consuming conversions; cosmetic but ubiquitous" moves, future "the full borrow/move/copy interaction for method receivers — when `value.method()` moves vs borrows, the auto-referencing rule for `&self`/`&mut self` (Book ch05-03 lines 135-180) interacts with today's no-auto-rule for `self` by value; today's `w.into_inner()` is a clean *no auto-ref* call site (the binding is moved as-is); subtle"
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch05-03-method-syntax.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/associated-items.md

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 103-restricted-visibility
move: extend lesson 096's `pub`-on-an-item-inside-an-inline-module shape with the two restricted-visibility modifiers `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` — both go in the same syntactic position as plain `pub`, directly in front of a function item; concretely the working probe `mod inner { pub(super) fn for_super() -> u32 { 1 } pub(crate) fn for_crate() -> u32 { 2 } } fn main() { println!("super = {}, crate = {}", inner::for_super(), inner::for_crate()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `super = 1, crate = 2` and exits 0 — both modifiers exercised in one inline submodule at the crate root, both calls resolved from `fn main` because `fn main`'s call site is in the crate root which *is* `inner`'s parent module *and* is part of the current crate; the centered E0603 contrast probe with the same source plus a third unmarked item `fn private() -> u32 { 3 }` and a third call argument `inner::private()` fires `error[E0603]: function \`private\` is private` with `--> private_call.rs:11:16` underlining `private` at the call site, plus a `note: the function \`private\` is defined here` block with a second `-->` line `private_call.rs:4:5` underlining the entire `fn private()` line at the definition site, plus the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` trailer — the *same* E0603 lesson 096 installed, now witnessing that both `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` *opt-in* to the access rule alongside plain `pub`; the auxiliary `pub(super)`-out-of-reach probe with a two-level `mod outer { pub mod inner { pub(super) fn for_super() -> u32 { 1 } } }` setup and `fn main() { println!("{}", outer::inner::for_super()); }` fires `error[E0603]: function \`for_super\` is private` with `--> super_out_of_reach.rs:8:34` underlining `for_super` at the call site and a `note:` block with `-->` line `super_out_of_reach.rs:3:9` underlining the entire `pub(super) fn for_super()` line at the definition site — empirical witness that `pub(super)` is genuinely restricted, only reaching the *parent* of the declaring module, not the whole crate; the auxiliary `pub(crate)`-reach-through probe with the same nested setup but `pub(crate)` in place of `pub(super)` compiles silently and prints `2` — empirical witness that `pub(crate)` *does* reach `fn main` from two levels deep because `fn main` is in the same crate, the operational difference between the two modifiers
main_concept: `pub`'s reach is one shape from the Reference's five-row `Visibility` grammar (visibility-and-privacy.md lines 6-15: `Visibility → pub | pub ( crate ) | pub ( self ) | pub ( super ) | pub ( in SimplePath )`); today centers two more rows from the same production, both in the same syntactic position as plain `pub`. (1) *`pub(super)`*: per Reference line 141 verbatim "`pub(super)` makes an item visible to the parent module. This is equivalent to `pub(in super)`." Today's probe places `pub(super) fn for_super()` in `mod inner` at the crate root, so the modifier grants visibility to the crate root, where `fn main` lives — the call resolves. The auxiliary out-of-reach probe (Probe 3) places the same function inside `mod outer { pub mod inner { ... } }` so the modifier grants visibility to `outer` only — the call from `fn main` (outside `outer`) fails with E0603 carrying the same diagnostic shape lesson 096 installed. (2) *`pub(crate)`*: per Reference line 137 verbatim "`pub(crate)` makes an item visible within the current crate." Today's probe witnesses this from the crate root (Probe 1) and the auxiliary reach-through probe (Probe 4) witnesses it from a non-ancestor module two levels deep — both succeed because both call sites are in the same crate. The radius hierarchy: plain `pub` (everywhere, library-consumers included) > `pub(crate)` (anywhere inside the current crate) > `pub(super)` (only the immediate parent module) > no marker (only the declaring module; lesson 096). The diagnostic on a failed access check is the same E0603 lesson 096 installed; only *which* call sites pass changes. The audience crosses from "I have plain `pub` and unmarked-private as my two visibility states" (lesson 096) to "I have a *family* of `pub(...)` modifiers with different reach radii, all in the same syntactic position." Today is *shallow operational*: install only `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` on a function item inside an inline module, with the unmarked-private E0603 contrast as the centered teaching point and the `pub(super)`-out-of-reach E0603 as the operational distinguisher. Explicitly defer `pub(self)` (Reference line 145, equivalent to no `pub` at all, almost never used), `pub(in path)` (Reference line 133, the most general form, takes a SimplePath argument like `pub(in crate::foo::bar)`, rare in practice), cross-crate visibility (the deeper rule for what plain `pub` actually means when another crate links against this one — operationally opposite to today's `pub(crate)` but the binary-and-library-crate framework is its own move), visibility on the `mod` declaration itself (`pub(super) mod foo { ... }` vs today's `mod foo { pub(super) fn ... }`; Reference line 207 "all of its parent items up to the current scope must still be visible as well" makes this its own composition move), restricted visibility on struct fields (`struct S { pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64> }`; composes today's modifier with lesson 095's field declarations; the rmp target uses this on `BigUInt`'s `limbs` field; the natural next move), restricted `pub` on enum variants (composes today with lessons 098/099), `pub(crate) use foo::Bar;` re-exports (composes today with lesson 044's `use`), trait associated items (blocked on trait machinery), edition-specific differences (Reference line 149: "Starting with the 2018 edition, paths for `pub(in path)` must start with `crate`, `self`, or `super`"; this run uses edition 2024 throughout). This unlocks reading the rmp target's pervasive `pub(super) fn` and `pub(crate) const` lines across `biguint/*.rs` and `bigint.rs`, and sets up the natural follow-on of restricted visibility on struct fields (specifically `pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64>` in `biguint/basic.rs`).
depends_on: 096-inline-module-and-pub (load-bearing — installs `mod foo { ... }`, `pub` on a function item, the privacy-by-default rule, the access form `module::item`, and the E0603 diagnostic; today extends 096 by adding `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` to the same `pub` position; Probe 1 reuses 096's `mod foo { ... } fn ... fn main { ... }` skeleton verbatim with the only diff being two `pub(...)` modifiers in place of `pub`; Probe 2 reuses 096's E0603 contrast exactly, now embedded alongside the two new modifiers; lesson 096's *unlocks* list explicitly named "future restricted visibility `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` moves" — today is exactly that pair), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0603 transcript and Probe 3's E0603 transcript; the diagnostic shape is the same one lesson 096 first installed for E0603 with caret at use site and `note:` block with `-->` at definition site), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` as entry point), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — `fn name() -> Type { ... }` definition shape and `name()` call shape, both unchanged), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with two `{}` slots in Probe 1 and three in Probe 2), 043-nested-module-paths (cited — the path call form `module::name(args)`; today's `inner::for_super()`, `inner::for_crate()`, `inner::private()`, and the deeper-nested `outer::inner::for_super()`, `outer::inner::for_crate()` are all this form, unchanged), 005-let-binding (cited only — not used in today's probe, the call results flow directly into `println!`), 069-rustc-warnings (cited only — Probes 2 and 3's `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` trailer read with lesson 069's category map; the working probe Probe 1 emits no warnings — an early iteration included an unmarked `fn private` which fired `dead_code` warn-by-default, so the committed working probe deliberately omits it, deferring the unmarked case to Probe 2), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "restricted visibility on struct fields — `struct S { pub(super) field: T }` or `struct S { pub(crate) field: T }`; composes today's modifier with lesson 095's struct-field declarations; the rmp target's `pub struct BigUInt { pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64> }` is the canonical use; the syntax is identical to today's function-item example with the modifier in the field-decl position" moves, future "restricted visibility on enum variants — `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` modifiers in front of enum variants; composes today with lessons 098/099; less common than the field case but present in real code" moves, future "`pub(self)` — the fifth `Visibility` form, equivalent to no `pub` at all per Reference line 145; almost never used in practice but completes the grammar enumeration" moves, future "`pub(in path)` — the most general form, takes a SimplePath argument; Reference lines 133, 149-151 cover the formal rules; the path must resolve to an *ancestor* module; rare in practice but the only way to express \"visible to a specific named module that isn't the immediate parent\"" moves, future "visibility on the `mod` declaration itself — `pub(super) mod foo { ... }` vs today's `mod foo { pub(super) fn ... }`; the privacy chain rule from Reference line 207 (\"all of its parent items up to the current scope must still be visible as well\") makes this composition non-trivial; the rmp target's `pub mod bigint;` and `pub mod biguint;` in `lib.rs` are the canonical uses, but with restricted variants like `pub(crate) mod helpers;` also possible" moves, future "cross-crate visibility — what plain `pub` actually *means* when another crate links against this one; today's `pub(crate)` is the operational opposite (visible only inside this crate); the framework requires the binary-and-library-crate split as its own move; the rmp target's `lib.rs`/`main.rs` setup is the natural next concrete instance" moves, future "`pub(crate) use foo::Bar;` and `pub(super) use foo::Bar;` re-exports — composes today's modifier with lesson 044's `use`; the rmp target's `biguint/mod.rs` uses `pub use basic::BigUInt;` — the restricted variants would narrow that re-export's reach" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` lines in real source — `pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64>` and `pub(crate) const LIMB_SIZE_BITS: u64 = 64` and the many `pub(super) fn` / `pub(crate) fn` items across `biguint/*.rs` and `bigint.rs`; composes today's modifiers with deferred struct-field visibility, restricted const items, and the multi-file module structure from lesson 097" moves, future "the privacy-chain rule for nested modules under restricted visibility — Reference visibility-and-privacy.md lines 206-207's 'all of its parent items up to the current scope must still be visible as well'; today's auxiliary Probe 4 needed `pub mod inner` to make the path resolve, witnessing this rule operationally; the full unpacking is its own move" moves, future "edition-specific path-resolution differences — Reference line 149 'Starting with the 2018 edition, paths for `pub(in path)` must start with `crate`, `self`, or `super`. The 2015 edition may also use paths starting with `::` or modules from the crate root.'; this run uses edition 2024 throughout but the older convention shows up in legacy code" moves, future "trait-associated-item visibility — Reference line 31 names trait-associated items as one of two exceptions to privacy-by-default ('Associated items in a `pub` Trait are public by default'); applying `pub(super)` or `pub(crate)` to trait-associated items has its own subtleties; blocked on trait machinery (Q07)" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/visibility-and-privacy.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0603.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch07-02-defining-modules-to-control-scope-and-privacy.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch07-03-paths-for-referring-to-an-item-in-the-module-tree.md

  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/103-restricted-visibility.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 104-super-and-crate-paths
move: write *relative* path prefixes — `super::name` (walks up one level to the parent module) and `crate::name` (jumps to the crate root) — at the leftmost segment of a path-call expression, contrasting with the absolute `module::name(args)` form lessons 043/044 installed; concretely the working probe `fn at_root() -> u32 { 1 } mod outer { pub fn at_outer() -> u32 { 2 } pub mod inner { pub fn use_super() -> u32 { super::at_outer() } pub fn use_crate() -> u32 { crate::at_root() } } } fn main() { println!("super = {}, crate = {}", outer::inner::use_super(), outer::inner::use_crate()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `super = 2, crate = 1` and exits 0 — both prefixes exercised inside the inner-most module so `super::` reaches `outer` (a non-trivial parent rather than the crate root) and `crate::` reaches the crate root, the two prefixes routing to *different* items by construction; the centered E0433 contrast probe `fn main() { super::missing(); }` (a single `super::` from `fn main` at the crate root, where there is no parent module) fires `error[E0433]: too many leading \`super\` keywords` with `--> too_many_supers.rs:2:5` underlining the keyword `super` (5 chars), the verbatim phrase "there are too many leading `super` keywords" stating the rule today installs, plus the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0433` trailer; the operational distinguisher Probe 3 — same nested setup as the working probe but `crate::at_outer` substituted for `super::at_outer` (asking the crate root for an item that lives one level down in `outer`) — fires `error[E0425]: cannot find function \`at_outer\` in the crate root` with the diagnostic literally naming the search location ("in the crate root"), empirical witness that the two prefixes route to genuinely different destinations; the multi-level Probe 4 `mod outer { pub fn try_super_super() -> u32 { super::super::at_root() } }` from one level deep fires the same E0433 with caret at column 16 underlining the *second* `super`, witnessing each `super::` walks up exactly one level
main_concept: Rust paths come in two styles — *absolute* (the form lessons 043/044 installed, starting with a crate name and walking down) and *relative* (anchored by a keyword in the leftmost segment, starting from where the calling code lives), per Book ch07-03 lines 8-14 verbatim "An *absolute path* is the full path starting from a crate root; for code from an external crate, the absolute path begins with the crate name, and for code from the current crate, it starts with the literal `crate`. A *relative path* starts from the current module and uses `self`, `super`, or an identifier in the current module." Today centers two of the four relative-anchor keywords from the Reference's `SimplePathSegment`/`PathIdentSegment` grammar (paths.md lines 32-33, 64-65: `IDENTIFIER | super | self | crate | $crate` / `IDENTIFIER | super | self | Self | crate | $crate`). (1) *`super::name`*: per Reference paths.md line 387 verbatim "`super` in a path resolves to the parent module" and line 391 "It may only be used in leading segments of the path, possibly after an initial `self` segment." Each `super::` walks up exactly one module level — Book ch07-03 lines 296-297 names the filesystem analogy verbatim "This is like starting a filesystem path with the `..` syntax that means to go to the parent directory." Reference paths.md line 407 verbatim "`super` may be repeated several times after the first `super` or `self` to refer to ancestor modules" — multi-level form named in *What To Ignore For Now*, exhaustion case witnessed by Probe 4 firing E0433. (2) *`crate::name`*: per Reference paths.md line 431 verbatim "`crate` resolves the path relative to the current crate" and line 435 "`crate` can only be used as the first segment, without a preceding `::`." `crate::` jumps to *the crate root* (the file `rustc` was given) regardless of how deep the call site is. The error_codes/E0433.md page itself reads "To use a module from your current crate, add the `crate::` prefix to the path" — the corpus's official error-code page directly instructs the reader to use today's prefix as a fix. The audience crosses from "paths I write start at a crate name and walk down" (lessons 043/044) to "there is a second style — relative paths anchored by `super::`/`crate::`/`self::` — that starts from where the *calling* code lives, not at an external crate name." Today is *shallow operational*: install only the leftmost-segment use of `super::` (one level) and `crate::` (any depth) on a function-call expression; centered teaching point is the E0433 "too many leading `super` keywords" contrast which shows the walk-up-one-level rule by exhaustion. The two prefixes are sibling keywords in the same `PathIdentSegment` production but encode genuinely different routing; Probe 3 witnesses that swapping one for the other in the same call site reaches a different item. Explicitly defer `self::name` (Reference paths.md line 284, equivalent to bare `name` in most positions, named in *What To Ignore For Now*), capital-S `Self` (lesson 100's type-level qualifier, named only), multi-level `super::super::...` chains (Reference paths.md line 407, named only), `super::`/`crate::` inside `use` declarations (composes today with lesson 044, the natural follow-on for lesson 105), edition-specific differences (Reference paths.md lines 244-250 covers Rust 2015's leading `::` for the crate root; this run uses edition 2024), `$crate` (macro-only qualifier, Reference paths.md lines 449-468, blocked on macros), cross-crate paths (blocked on the binary-and-library-crate move). This unlocks reading the rmp target's pervasive `super::` and `crate::` lines across `biguint/*.rs` and `bigint.rs` (e.g. `use super::biguint::BigUInt;` in `bigint.rs`) — the natural follow-on composition is `use super::path::Item;` and `use crate::module::Item;` (composes today with lesson 044's `use`).
depends_on: 096-inline-module-and-pub (load-bearing — installs `mod foo { ... }` and the rule that items inside the braces live in a new namespace called by the module name; today's probe nests `mod inner` inside `mod outer` to make `super::` observable; without lesson 096, the audience would not know how to declare a module), 043-nested-module-paths (load-bearing — installs the call form `module::name(args)`; today's `super::at_outer()`, `crate::at_root()`, and `outer::inner::use_super()` are all this shape with the prefixes in the *leftmost* segment, the rest unchanged; lesson 043's *unlocks* explicitly named "the `crate::` / `self::` / `super::` path roots" as a future move — today is exactly the first two), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0433 transcript and Probe 4's E0433 transcript with caret at the second `super`; the diagnostic structure is unchanged from lesson 003), 097-file-based-module (cited only — `super::` and `crate::` work unchanged across file boundaries; today's working probe is single-file for compactness, the rmp target uses both prefixes across many files), 044-use-declaration (cited only — natural follow-on composition `use super::Item;` and `use crate::module::Item;` is named in *Prerequisites* and *What To Ignore For Now* as deferred; not used in any committed probe today), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (cited only — capital-S `Self` is named in *What To Ignore For Now* as a different keyword in a different namespace), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` as entry point), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — `fn name() -> Type { ... }` definition shape and `name()` call shape, both unchanged), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with two `{}` slots in Probe 1), 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — return type for the four functions in Probe 1), 069-rustc-warnings (cited only — Probes 2-4's `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` trailer read with lesson 069's category map), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`use super::Item;` and `use crate::module::Item;` — composes today's prefixes with lesson 044's `use` declaration; the natural follow-on (lesson 105 candidate); the rmp target's `use super::biguint::BigUInt;` in `bigint.rs` is the canonical pattern" moves, future "`self::name` — the third relative qualifier from the Reference grammar; equivalent to bare `name` in most positions; useful for disambiguation; named in today's *What To Ignore For Now*" moves, future "multi-level `super::super::...` chains — Reference paths.md line 407 'may be repeated several times'; today only exercises one level, Probe 4 witnesses the exhaustion case via E0433" moves, future "`pub(in path)` with relative path roots — Reference visibility-and-privacy.md line 149 'paths for `pub(in path)` must start with `crate`, `self`, or `super`'; composes today's prefixes with lesson 103's restricted visibility" moves, future "edition-specific path-resolution differences — Rust 2015's leading `::` meaning the crate root vs Rust 2018+'s `crate::` form; Reference paths.md lines 244-250" moves, future "`$crate` — macro-only path qualifier; Reference paths.md lines 449-468; blocked on macros" moves, future "cross-crate absolute paths — what `std::cmp::min` actually looks up when another crate is named at the leftmost segment; today's `crate::` is the operational opposite (crate-root anchor for *this* crate); blocked on the binary-and-library-crate move" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable `super::` and `crate::` lines in real source — `use super::biguint::BigUInt;` in `bigint.rs`, `use super::basic::BigUInt;` in `biguint/cmp.rs`/`biguint/add.rs`/etc.; composes today's prefixes with deferred `use` composition" moves, future "the canonical-paths section of Reference paths.md lines 471-549 — formal definition of canonical path; today's relative paths are aliases for the canonical absolute path; framework move" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/paths.md
```
