```text
id: 105-pub-use-re-export
move: re-export an item with `pub use Path::Item;` — extend lesson 044's plain `use Path::Item;` (which brings a name into the current scope) by adding `pub` in front, so the same line *both* binds `Item` locally inside the current module *and* re-exports it under the current module's path for outside callers to reach via `current_module::Item`; concretely the working probe `mod inner { pub mod hidden { pub fn value() -> u32 { 42 } } pub use self::hidden::value; } fn main() { println!("via re-export: {}", inner::value()); println!("via original:  {}", inner::hidden::value()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `via re-export: 42` and `via original:  42` and exits 0 — both the new path `inner::value` and the original path `inner::hidden::value` reach the same function, witnessing that `pub use` *adds* a public path without removing the first; the centered E0603 contrast probe with the same source modulo `pub use self::hidden::value;` changed to plain `use self::hidden::value;` (a single deleted `pub` keyword) fires `error[E0603]: function import \`value\` is private` with `--> no_pub.rs:11:42` underlining `value` at the call site, a chain of `note:` blocks (first pointing at the `use` line at `no_pub.rs:7:9` labeled "the function import `value` is defined here..." with the entire `use self::hidden::value;` underlined, second chasing the redirect to `no_pub.rs:3:9` labeled "...and refers to the function `value` which is defined here" with the entire `pub fn value() -> u32 {` line underlined and the trailing remark "you could import this directly"), two `help:` suggestion blocks (one proposing `inner::hidden::value()`, the other proposing `hidden::value()`), and the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0603` trailer — same E0603 lesson 096 installed, with the slightly specialized "private function *import*" phrasing because the gating item is now a `use` declaration; the auxiliary witness probe with plain `use self::hidden::value;` plus a `pub fn local_caller() -> u32 { value() }` defined *inside* `inner` (calling `value()` bare from `inner`'s body) compiles silently and prints `42` from `fn main { println!("{}", inner::local_caller()); }` — empirical witness that the bare `use` does bind `value` in `inner`'s local scope (lesson 044's behaviour), the `pub` adds the *re-export* half on top of that local binding
main_concept: `pub use Path::Item;` is the visibility composition from the Reference's `Visibility?` slot on a `use` declaration item — use-declarations.md lines 19, 68-78 verbatim "A use declaration is also sometimes called an *import*, or, if it is public, a *re-export*. [...] Like items, `use` declarations are private to the containing module, by default. Also like items, a `use` declaration can be public, if qualified by the `pub` keyword. Such a `use` declaration serves to *re-export* a name. A public `use` declaration can therefore *redirect* some public name to a different target definition: even a definition with a private canonical path, inside a different module." Today centers exactly that composition: lesson 044's `use Path::Item;` (the `use` declaration item) plus lesson 096's `pub` (the visibility marker on items) compose into `pub use Path::Item;`. (1) *Two effects from one line*: a `pub use` declaration creates a local binding for the imported name (lesson 044's effect) *and* makes the binding public to outside callers (a re-export). The original path keeps working — `pub use` adds a second public path, it does not remove the first. (2) *The Reference's example* (use-declarations.md lines 80-97) is exactly today's probe shape: `mod quux { pub use self::foo::{bar, baz}; pub mod foo { pub fn bar() {} pub fn baz() {} } } fn main() { quux::bar(); quux::baz(); }`. The `self::` prefix on the `use`-path is the Reference's own choice; the lesson uses the same shape. (3) *Book ch07-04 lines 214-258 verbatim* "When we bring a name into scope with the `use` keyword, the name is private to the scope into which we imported it. To enable code outside that scope to refer to that name as if it had been defined in that scope, we can combine `pub` and `use`. This technique is called *re-exporting* because we're bringing an item into scope but also making that item available for others to bring into their scope." The Book's Listing 7-17 framing — "Before this change, external code would have to call [...] `restaurant::front_of_house::hosting::add_to_waitlist()` [...]. Now [...] external code can use the path `restaurant::hosting::add_to_waitlist()` instead" — directly motivates the rmp pattern `pub use basic::BigUInt;` in `biguint/mod.rs` letting callers say `bignum::biguint::BigUInt` instead of `bignum::biguint::basic::BigUInt`. (4) *The contrast diagnostic* fires the same E0603 lesson 096 installed, with the slightly specialized phrasing "private function *import*" because the gating item is now a `use` declaration rather than the underlying function. The chain of `note:` blocks (first pointing at the `use` line, second at the `pub fn` definition) operationally surfaces the Reference's "redirect [...] to a different target definition" framing — the diagnostic itself walks the redirect. The audience crosses from "I have `use` (bring a name into local scope) and `pub` (expose an item from a module) as two separate visibility actions" to "I can compose them — `pub use` does both at once, and the re-export pattern is the standard way Rust libraries expose a clean public surface that differs from their internal directory structure." Today is *shallow operational*: install only `pub use Path::Item;` on a single function-item from a deeper module; explicitly defer `pub(crate) use ...` / `pub(super) use ...` (composes today with lesson 103, future move), `pub use Path::Item as Alias;` (composes today with use-declarations.md's `as` form, future move), glob re-exports `pub use module::*;`, re-export of an entire module (`pub use module;`), re-exporting trait items (blocked on traits), cycle detection in re-export chains (Reference use-declarations.md line 78 names this as a compile-time error, out of scope), and the path-resolution rule that requires `self::` in front of the inner module name under `rustc` (edition-dependent and orthogonal to today's `pub` move; the appendix records the observation but the lesson body does not center it). This unlocks reading the rmp target's `pub use basic::BigUInt;` in `biguint/mod.rs` — exactly today's shape — and sets up the natural composition follow-ons with lesson 103 (restricted-visibility re-exports) and the `as` form (rename re-exports).
depends_on: 044-use-declaration (load-bearing — installs `use Path::Item;`: the `use` keyword as a top-level item, the path-syntax argument, the trailing `;`, and the effect of bringing the final segment into the current scope; today's `pub use self::hidden::value;` is exactly this shape with `pub` added in front; the auxiliary Probe 3 witnesses that the bare `use` half still binds `value` in `inner`'s local scope, exactly lesson 044's behaviour), 096-inline-module-and-pub (load-bearing — installs `mod foo { ... }`, `pub` on a function item, the privacy-by-default rule for items inside a module, the access form `module::item`, and the E0603 diagnostic; today applies the *same* `pub` keyword to a *different* item kind (a `use` declaration); the Reference's "Like items, `use` declarations are private to the containing module, by default. Also like items, a `use` declaration can be public" makes the analogy explicit; Probe 2's E0603 transcript carries the same E-code lesson 096 first installed, with only the specialized "private function *import*" phrasing differing because the gating item is now a `use`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0603 transcript with caret at use site and *two* `note:` blocks each carrying its own `-->`; the diagnostic structure is unchanged from lesson 003 with one extension visible — the multi-`note:` chain that operationally surfaces the redirect), 043-nested-module-paths (cited — both call sites `inner::value()` and `inner::hidden::value()` in Probe 1 are this shape, unchanged), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` as entry point), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — `fn name() -> Type { ... }` definition shape and `name()` call shape, both unchanged), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with one `{}` slot in each of Probe 1's two lines), 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — return type for `value`), 069-rustc-warnings (cited only — Probe 2's `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` trailer read with lesson 069's category map; the working probe Probe 1 emits no warnings), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), 103-restricted-visibility (cited only — natural composition for `pub(crate) use ...` and `pub(super) use ...` named in *What To Ignore For Now* as deferred; not used in any committed probe today), 104-super-and-crate-paths (cited only — named in the rmp framing about callers writing `bignum::biguint::BigUInt`; not load-bearing for today's centered `pub use` move), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`pub(crate) use Path::Item;` and `pub(super) use Path::Item;` — restricted-visibility re-exports; composes today's `pub use` with lesson 103's `pub(crate)` and `pub(super)`; the rmp target uses these patterns to expose items only inside the crate (the natural follow-on)" moves, future "`pub use Path::Item as Alias;` — re-export under a *different* name; composes today with the `as` aliasing form from Reference use-declarations.md lines 163-179; useful for normalizing re-exported names across versions of a library API" moves, future "glob re-exports `pub use module::*;` — re-export *every* public item from a path; composes today with the wildcard form (Reference use-declarations.md lines 259-339); the prelude pattern uses this, but it is generally discouraged in library APIs" moves, future "re-export of an entire module — `pub use module;` (no final segment); the rmp target's `lib.rs` uses `pub mod biguint;` which is a sibling pattern but not literally the same; the `pub use module;` re-export shape is its own move" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable `pub use basic::BigUInt;` in real source — `biguint/mod.rs` line 10 verbatim; today installs exactly this shape, but the file-based composition with sibling `mod basic;` declarations is its own follow-on" moves, future "re-exporting trait items — `pub use module::Trait;` brings the trait into the current module's namespace and exposes it for outside callers; blocked on trait machinery (Q07)" moves, future "cycle-and-ambiguity detection in re-export chains — Reference use-declarations.md line 78 'If a sequence of such redirections form a cycle or cannot be resolved unambiguously, they represent a compile-time error.'; the formal rule and its diagnostic family; out of scope today" moves, future "the path-resolution rule for the `use`-path itself — why `pub use self::hidden::value;` works under `rustc demo.rs` but `pub use hidden::value;` (no `self::`) fires E0432; edition-dependent; the appendix's *Note — `self::`-prefix observation* records the observation but the framework is its own move" moves, future "Cargo's library-build path-resolution behaviour — the rmp target's `pub use basic::BigUInt;` works without the `self::` prefix because Cargo's library compilation differs from `rustc`'s single-file compilation; blocked on the binary-and-library-crate move" moves, future "re-exporting from external crates — `pub use external_crate::Item;` exposes a dependency's item under your crate's namespace, the canonical 're-export the runtime' pattern (e.g. tokio re-exporting bytes); blocked on cross-crate paths" moves, future "the multi-`note:` diagnostic chain — Probe 2's `note:` blocks chase the redirect from the call site through the `use` line to the `pub fn` definition, surfacing the Reference's 'redirect [...] to a different target definition' framing; the formal rule for what diagnostics may chain through use-redirects is its own move" moves, future "documentation-comment effects on re-exports — `pub use` items appear in `cargo doc` output under both the original path and the re-export path; the Book's Chapter 14 'Exporting a Convenient Public API' section covers this; composes today with deferred `///` doc-comment machinery" moves, future "the `pub use` ↔ `mod` interaction — `mod foo;` declares the module is *part of the crate*, `pub use foo::Item;` re-exports an item from inside; the two are different actions that often appear adjacent in `mod.rs` files (the rmp `biguint/mod.rs` uses both); the formal interaction is its own move" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/use-declarations.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch07-04-bringing-paths-into-scope-with-the-use-keyword.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0603.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/105-pub-use-re-export.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/105-pub-use-re-export.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 106-subdirectory-module
move: declare a submodule using the file-based `mod foo;` form, but place the body in a *subdirectory* `foo/` containing a file named exactly `mod.rs` rather than in a sibling file `foo.rs`; concretely with `main.rs` containing `mod foo; fn main() { foo::hi(); }` and `foo/mod.rs` containing `pub fn hi() { println!("hi from foo/mod.rs"); }` (no `foo.rs` adjacent to `main.rs`), the command `rustc main.rs` from the directory holding `main.rs` and `foo/` compiles silently and `./main` prints `hi from foo/mod.rs` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe with the same source plus a sibling `foo.rs` next to `main.rs` (so both candidate file paths are populated at once) fires `error[E0761]: file for module \`foo\` found at both "foo.rs" and "foo/mod.rs"` with `--> main.rs:1:1` underlining the whole `mod foo;` declaration, a `help:` block reading `delete or rename one of them to remove the ambiguity`, and the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0761` trailer — the E0761 headline message itself names the two admitted candidate paths verbatim, witnessing the rule that `mod foo;` resolves to *either* `foo.rs` *or* `foo/mod.rs` (mutually exclusive); the neither-file-present case is lesson 097's E0583 with `help:` block "create file `foo.rs` or `foo/mod.rs`" — that *or* is the same rule, witnessed retroactively by 097's already-captured probe and not re-witnessed today
main_concept: the file-based form of the `Module` grammar rule (Reference items/modules.md lines 9-15) admits *two* file paths for the same `mod NAME;` declaration. Lesson 097 installed the first — `NAME.rs` adjacent to the declaring source. Today installs the second — `NAME/` directory adjacent to the declaring source, containing a file named exactly `mod.rs`. Reference items/modules.md lines 82-88 verbatim: "Module filenames may also be the name of the module as a directory with the contents in a file named `mod.rs` within that directory. ... It is not allowed to have both `util.rs` and `util/mod.rs`." Book ch07-05 lines 87-105 verbatim: "Rust also supports an older style of file path. For a module named `front_of_house` declared in the crate root, the compiler will look for the module's code in: *src/front_of_house.rs* (what we covered) — *src/front_of_house/mod.rs* (older style, still supported path). [...] If you use both styles for the same module, you'll get a compiler error." (1) *Two admitted file paths*: `mod foo;` resolves to `foo.rs` (lesson 097, idiomatic) *or* `foo/mod.rs` (today, older style still admitted). Either is a valid body for the same module; the choice is the author's. (2) *Mutual exclusivity*: rustc rejects both forms populated simultaneously with E0761. The diagnostic message names the two admitted candidate paths verbatim, making the rule statement directly readable from the error itself. (3) *Carry-through from 097*: the privacy-by-default rule, `pub`'s effect on items, the `foo::item` access form, and the lesson-097 mental model "file-based `mod` is the same module, just a different body location" all carry through unchanged. The body of `foo/mod.rs` is the same kind of content that would have gone in `foo.rs`. (4) *Pedagogical motivation*: the subdirectory form is the standard organizational pattern for spreading one conceptual module across many files. `foo/mod.rs` holds the parent body, and `foo/` becomes a folder for sibling files (`foo/add.rs`, `foo/cmp.rs`, etc.) that the parent declares with further `mod NAME;` lines (the same rule applied recursively). The audience crosses from "I author a module across two adjacent files with `mod foo;` + `foo.rs`" (lesson 097) to "I author the same module across a subdirectory with `mod foo;` + `foo/mod.rs`. Same `mod` keyword, same module, same privacy rule — only the body's *file path* changes, and rustc admits two of them." Today is *shallow operational*: install only the `foo/mod.rs` resolution case for the top-level adjacency, with the E0761 ambiguity contrast as the centered teaching point. Explicitly defer multi-level subdirectory module trees (`foo/bar/mod.rs` requires a `mod bar;` inside `foo/mod.rs`; the same rule applied recursively), the 2018+ alternative form (`mod foo;` resolves to `foo.rs` while submodules live under `foo/` *without* `mod.rs` on the parent — modern Cargo convention, named in the Reference's "Note prior to rustc 1.30" passage), the `#[path = "..."]` attribute (already deferred in 097), edition-specific path-resolution differences (already deferred in 097), and Cargo's `src/lib.rs` and `src/main.rs` crate-root files (which are special and not subject to the `mod.rs` rule). New E-code in the run's collection: E0761 ("Multiple candidate files were found for an out-of-line module"), captured verbatim in Probe 2. This unlocks reading the rmp target's `pub mod biguint;` line in `lib.rs` (loading `biguint/mod.rs`) and the `mod add; mod basic; mod cmp; ...` chain inside `biguint/mod.rs` (each loading a sibling `biguint/<name>.rs`), composing today's rule with deferred `pub mod` and the recursion into multi-level subdirectory trees.
depends_on: 097-file-based-module (load-bearing — installs `mod foo;` (semicolon, no body) as the file-based form of the `Module` grammar rule, the Reference's two-shape grammar at items/modules.md lines 9-15, and the lesson-096 carry-through (privacy rule, `pub`, `foo::item` access form). Today extends 097 by *only* naming the second admitted file path. The body of `foo/mod.rs` in Probe 1 is `pub fn hi` unchanged from lesson 097's `foo.rs`. Lesson 097's E0583 contrast already witnessed the *or* in the `help:` block — the same rule today centers, observed retroactively from 097's evidence appendix), 096-inline-module-and-pub (cited only — the `pub fn hi` body comes from 096/097 unchanged; no new claim about `pub` or the privacy rule today), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — the host `fn main` block in `main.rs`), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — the definition shape `fn name() { ... }` and the call shape `name();` are both unchanged from 097), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with one `{}` slot in the body of `hi`), 043-nested-module-paths (cited — the call form `module::name(args)`; today's `foo::hi()` is that shape, unchanged from 097), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0761 transcript; the four-part structure is identical to lesson 097's E0583 (declaring-line location, caret under the whole `mod foo;`, `help:` block, standard trailer), with the new shape that the headline message itself names the two candidate file paths verbatim — the diagnostic statement is the rule statement), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc main.rs` then `./main`, silent on success), 069-rustc-warnings (cited only — Probe 2's `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` trailer is read with lesson 069's category map), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell, the ability to make a *subdirectory* and place a file inside it)
unlocks: future "multi-level subdirectory module trees — `foo/bar/mod.rs` requires a `mod bar;` line inside `foo/mod.rs`, then `foo/bar/mod.rs` follows the same two-candidate rule; the same rule applied recursively; the rmp target uses this pattern across `biguint/` plus its sibling files `add.rs`, `basic.rs`, `cmp.rs`, etc. (one level only — but the recursion is admitted by the rule)" moves, future "the 2018+ `foo.rs` plus sibling `foo/` form — `mod foo;` resolves to `foo.rs` while submodules live under `foo/` (no `mod.rs` required for the parent); the modern Cargo convention; Reference items/modules.md lines 86-88 'Note: Prior to `rustc` 1.30, using `mod.rs` files was the way ... It is encouraged to use the new naming convention'; Book ch07-05 walks the form; the rmp target uses today's older `foo/mod.rs` style instead" moves, future "the `pub mod foo;` declaration with the subdirectory form — composes lesson 096's deferred `pub mod` with today's `foo/mod.rs` resolution; the rmp target's `pub mod biguint;` is exactly this shape" moves, future "submodules-of-a-subdirectory-module — inside `foo/mod.rs`, a `mod bar;` line resolves to `foo/bar.rs` (the parent path component is the directory, per Reference items/modules.md lines 70-81); the rmp target uses this in `biguint/mod.rs` to declare `mod add; mod basic; mod cmp; ...`" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable subdirectory `pub mod` line — `pub mod biguint;` in `lib.rs` loading `biguint/mod.rs`; composes today's subdirectory form with deferred `pub mod` and the multi-file organization pattern" moves, future "Cargo package layout vs `rustc` file rules — Cargo's `src/main.rs`, `src/lib.rs`, `src/foo.rs`, `src/foo/mod.rs` conventions wrap today's `rustc` rule; the conventions are Cargo's, the file-resolution rule itself is `rustc`'s; same deferral as lesson 097" moves, future "the `mod` ↔ `pub use` interaction inside `mod.rs` — `mod foo;` declares foo is part of the module, `pub use foo::Item;` re-exports an item from inside; both often appear adjacent in `mod.rs` files (the rmp `biguint/mod.rs` uses both); composes today with lesson 105" moves, future "edition-specific path-resolution differences — Rust 2015 used the `mod.rs`-only convention; Rust 2018+ admits both forms; this run uses edition 2024" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/modules.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch07-05-separating-modules-into-different-files.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0761.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0583.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/106-subdirectory-module/main.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/106-subdirectory-module/foo/mod.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/106-subdirectory-module.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 107-vec-basics
move: install the smallest set of `Vec<T>` operations needed to read rmp's basic-method trio — three coupled pieces: (1) build a vector with the `vec![]` macro, both the empty form `vec![]` (the macro's `() => { ... }` arm per the std `macro.vec` page lines 7-11) and the prefilled form `vec![v1, v2, ...]` (the `($($x:expr),+ $(,)?) => { ... }` arm); (2) ask the element count with `.len()` returning `usize` (std `vec/struct.Vec` lines 2114-2126 verbatim "pub const fn len(&self) -> usize / Returns the number of elements in the vector, also referred to as its 'length'."); (3) read an element with `v[i]` where `i: usize` (std `vec/struct.Vec` lines 84-92 verbatim "The `Vec` type allows access to values by index, because it implements the `Index` trait. ... `let v = vec![0, 2, 4, 6]; println!("{}", v[1]);`"). Concretely the working probe `fn main() { let empty: Vec<u64> = vec![]; let three: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; println!("empty.len() = {}", empty.len()); println!("three.len() = {}", three.len()); println!("three[0] = {}", three[0]); println!("three[2] = {}", three[2]); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exits 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `empty.len() = 0` / `three.len() = 3` / `three[0] = 10` / `three[2] = 30` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe `fn main() { let three: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let bad = three[5]; println!("bad = {}", bad); }` compiles silently (exit 0) and at runtime panics with stderr `thread 'main' (...) panicked at broken.rs:3:20: / index out of bounds: the len is 3 but the index is 5 / note: run with \`RUST_BACKTRACE=1\` environment variable to display a backtrace` and exit status `101` — the same `index out of bounds: the len is N but the index is M` shape lesson 078 installed for arrays, with the centered pedagogical contrast that lesson 077's auxiliary `nums[10]` on a `[i32; 5]` *failed at compile time* via the deny-by-default `unconditional_panic` lint because the array's length lives in the type, while `Vec<u64>`'s length is *not* part of the type, so rustc cannot constant-evaluate the bounds check and the same shape rejects only at runtime; the auxiliary E0277 carry-through probe (`let i: i32 = 1; let v = three[i];`) fires `error[E0277]: the type \`[u64]\` cannot be indexed by \`i32\`` with the inline gloss `slice indices are of type \`usize\` or ranges of \`usize\`` and the trait-talk trailer `SliceIndex<[u64]>`/`Index<i32>` — same E0277 family lesson 077 installed, with `[u64]` replacing `[{integer}]` in the headline because the operand's element type is concrete.
main_concept: `Vec<T>` is a heap-backed growable list of elements of one type; today installs *only* the three operations needed to read rmp's `biguint/basic.rs` smallest method trio (`zero`, `is_zero`, `num_bits`). (1) *Construction with the `vec![]` macro*: the std `macro.vec` page lines 7-11 verbatim "`macro_rules! vec { () => { ... }; ($elem:expr; $n:expr) => { ... }; ($($x:expr),+ $(,)?) => { ... }; }`" — three admitted arms. Today centers the empty arm `vec![]` and the prefilled arm `vec![v1, v2, ...]`; the repeating arm `vec![elem; n]` is named in the deferral list. The `!` is lesson 071's macro mark; the `[...]` is the bracket-flavored macro form (the std page leads with "`vec!` allows `Vec`s to be defined with the same syntax as array expressions" — array expressions use `[...]`). The `Vec<u64>` annotation pins the element type for the empty form because rustc cannot infer `T` from `vec![]`; the prefilled form usually infers `T` from the element types (Book ch08-01 lines 32-44 "the integer type is `i32` because that's the default integer type"). Today's probe annotates explicitly to keep the syntax visible. (2) *Length with `.len()`*: the std signature `pub const fn len(&self) -> usize` and the gloss "Returns the number of elements in the vector, also referred to as its 'length'" — same lesson-040 method-call shape, same `usize` return as the array `.len()` from lesson 076. The method name carries through unchanged from arrays; today's only new claim is "the same name works on `Vec<T>` and returns the element count." (3) *Indexing with `v[i]`*: the std page leads with "The `Vec` type allows access to values by index, because it implements the `Index` trait" and the example `let v = vec![0, 2, 4, 6]; println!("{}", v[1]);` — exactly today's shape. Indices count from `0`. The index expression must be of type `usize` per lesson 077's E0277 rule; the auxiliary probe witnesses the carry-through. *Bounds-check timing differs from arrays*: arrays carry length in the type `[T; N]`, so a constant out-of-bounds index fires the deny-by-default `unconditional_panic` lint at compile time (lesson 077 auxiliary, lesson 078 contrast); `Vec<T>` does *not* carry length in its type, so even a literal index past the end compiles silently and the bounds check moves to runtime — the same `index out of bounds: the len is N but the index is M` panic message lesson 078 installed for arrays, just at runtime instead of compile time. This is the centered pedagogical surprise: same shape, different timing. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the three pieces needed for the rmp trio. Explicitly defer `Vec::new()` (lesson-041 qualified-form constructor; equivalent to `vec![]` for the empty case), `v.push(x)` (most common mutating method, needs `let mut`), `Vec::with_capacity(n)` (capacity vs length is its own concept), all iteration forms (`for x in &v`, `v.iter()`, `v.iter_mut()`, `.enumerate()`, `.zip()`, `.map`/`.filter`/`.collect`, lesson 079 installed only the array iteration), slicing `&v[a..b]` and `v.as_slice()` (range indexing is a separate move from single-element indexing), other mutating methods (`pop`, `resize`, `reserve`, `set_len` (`unsafe`), `clear`, `truncate`, `fill`), `v.get(i)` returning `Option<&T>` (the non-panicking sibling — already deferred for arrays in lesson 078), full type-parameter inference machinery, equality on `Vec` (`v1 == v2`), `Vec<T>` as a parameter or return type, heap allocation and capacity growth strategy details, and the `vec![elem; n]` repeating form. The audience crosses from "I know `Vec<T>` is a prelude name (lesson 093) but I have not built one or read an element" to "I can build a `Vec<T>` empty or prefilled with the `vec![]` macro, ask its element count with `.len()`, and read element `i` with `v[i]` where `i: usize` — three pieces composing prior lessons' shapes (071's macro mark, 040's method-call shape, 077's brackets-and-`usize` indexing) onto a different type." This unlocks reading the rmp target's `biguint/basic.rs` smallest-method trio: `pub fn zero() -> Self { BigUInt { limbs: vec![] } }` uses the empty `vec![]` form; `pub fn is_zero(&self) -> bool { return self.limbs.len() == 0; }` uses `.len()` plus equality; `pub(crate) fn num_bits(&self) -> u64 { ... self.limbs.len() ... self.limbs[n - 1] ... }` uses both `.len()` and `v[i]` — every operation in the trio is exactly today's vocabulary.
depends_on: 093-standard-library-prelude (load-bearing — installs `Vec<T>` as a prelude member written bare with no `use` line; today's two `Vec<u64>` annotations rely on that membership, no new claim about the prelude is made), 077-array-indexing-and-usize (load-bearing — installs `a[i]` with `i: usize`, the E0277 diagnostic on a non-`usize` named index, and the zero-based rule; today reuses *exactly* that shape on a `Vec<T>` operand: same brackets, same `usize` requirement, same E0277 family with `[u64]` replacing `[{integer}]` in the headline; the auxiliary probe with `let i: i32 = 1; let v = three[i];` witnesses the carry-through; today does *not* re-install the `usize` rule, only reuses it), 071-macro-invocation-syntax (load-bearing — installs the syntactic distinction `name(...)` vs `name!(...)` and the `!` as the macro mark; lesson 071's *What To Ignore* listed "the bracketing alternatives `name![...]` and `name!{...}`" as deferred future moves — today uses the bracket-flavored form `vec![...]` and the operational behaviour is witnessed by the working probe compiling silently and producing the expected output), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs `value.method()` dot-after-receiver shape; today applies it to `.len()` on a `Vec<T>` receiver, no new syntactic rule), 019-type-annotation-i32 (load-bearing — installs `let name: TYPE = value;` with `i32` as the worked TYPE; today plugs `Vec<u64>` into the same slot; the angle-bracket type-parameter syntax `Vec<u64>` is not centered today since lesson 093 already used `Vec<i32>` and `Result<i32, String>` annotations unchallenged), 076-array-literal-and-type (cited — `.len()` was first installed on arrays; today uses the same method name on a different type with the same `usize` return), 078-array-out-of-bounds-panic (cited — installs the runtime panic shape `index out of bounds: the len is N but the index is M`; today reuses that shape on a `Vec<T>` operand and centers the contrast that the same shape fires at *runtime* on `Vec<T>` because length is not in the type, vs. *compile time* on arrays via the `unconditional_panic` lint), 053-result-expect-and-panic (cited — installs the panic trailer shape `thread 'main' ... panicked at file:line:col:`, `note: ... RUST_BACKTRACE=1 ...`, exit `101`, output on stderr; today's contrast probe transcript reuses that trailer unchanged), 080-integer-type-family (cited — the lesson's "would default to `Vec<i32>`" parenthetical names lesson 080's default-`i32` rule for integer literals; also names `u64` as one of the twelve integer types installed there, used as the probe's element type), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to the auxiliary E0277 transcript and the panic transcript; same map as lesson 077 used), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point), 005-let-binding (cited — `let name = value;` for the four bindings in the working probe), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with `{}` slot; four invocations in the working probe), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell, stdout-vs-stderr split with `echo $?` for the contrast probe — already used since lesson 053)
unlocks: future "`Vec::new()` — the associated-function constructor (lesson-041 qualified-form shape, `Vec::<T>::new()` or `Vec::new()` with type inference); equivalent to `vec![]` for the empty case but uses different syntax; the rmp target uses `vec![]` exclusively in the smallest-method trio so today centers the macro form" moves, future "`v.push(x)` — append an element to the end of a `Vec<T>`; needs `let mut` on the binding (lesson 006); Vec's most common mutating method; the rmp target's `BigUInt::add` and many other arithmetic methods use this shape" moves, future "`Vec::with_capacity(n)` — preallocate a `Vec<T>` with capacity for `n` elements but length 0; introduces the capacity-vs-length distinction (std `vec/struct.Vec` lines 135-145); useful for performance but not load-bearing for the rmp basic trio" moves, future "iteration over a `Vec<T>` — `for x in &v` (immutable borrow), `for x in &mut v` (mutable borrow), `v.iter()` and `v.iter_mut()` and `v.into_iter()` as iterator-producing methods, `.enumerate()` and `.zip()` for paired iteration; lesson 079 installed `for x in array` and the Vec analogue is its own move" moves, future "iterator adapters `.map`/`.filter`/`.collect`/`.fold`/`.sum` — composable transformations on iterators; depends on iterator forms above and the `Iterator` trait" moves, future "slice indexing `&v[a..b]` and `v.as_slice()` — range indexing is a separate move from single-element indexing; the index slot also accepts ranges of `usize` per lesson 077's contrast probe gloss" moves, future "`v.get(i)` returning `Option<&T>` — the non-panicking sibling of `v[i]`: `Some(&v)` when `i < v.len()`, `None` otherwise; needs `Option` and `&T` first; lesson 078 already named the array version of this deferral" moves, future "other mutating methods on `Vec<T>` — `pop`, `resize`, `reserve`, `set_len` (`unsafe`), `clear`, `truncate`, `fill`; each composes today with `let mut` and lesson 040's method-call shape" moves, future "type-parameter inference for `vec![v1, v2, ...]` — without an annotation, rustc usually infers `T` from the elements; today's probe annotates `Vec<u64>` explicitly to keep the syntax visible" moves, future "equality on `Vec<T>` — `v1 == v2` compares element-wise (depends on `T: PartialEq`); not exercised today but used by rmp's `BigUInt::is_zero` shape `self.limbs.len() == 0` (which is integer equality, not Vec equality)" moves, future "`Vec<T>` as a parameter or return type — composes today with function signatures (lessons 020/021) and references (lesson 045); the rmp target's methods take `&self` receivers reading `self.limbs: Vec<u64>`" moves, future "heap allocation and capacity growth strategy — `Vec` is a heap-allocated growable buffer; the std `vec/struct.Vec` page lines 135-220 cover the (pointer, capacity, length) layout; today reads `Vec` operationally without naming the heap" moves, future "the `vec![elem; n]` repeating form — the macro's third arm (`($elem:expr; $n:expr) => { ... }`) builds a vector with `n` copies of `elem`; useful but not load-bearing for the rmp basic trio" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable `Vec<u64>` operations — `BigUInt::zero` returning `BigUInt { limbs: vec![] }`, `BigUInt::is_zero` checking `self.limbs.len() == 0`, `BigUInt::num_bits` reading `self.limbs[n - 1]` — composes today's three pieces with deferred `&self` receivers and field access on a struct" moves, future "the runtime-vs-compile-time bounds-check distinction as a centered concept — today named the distinction operationally (`Vec<T>` does not carry length in its type, so the same shape rejects only at runtime); the formal rule that array indexing with constant indexes is constant-evaluable and Vec indexing is not is a deeper move" moves, future "`Box<T>` heap-allocation type — the prelude type that introduces explicit heap allocation; named in lesson 093's deferral list and a natural sibling of `Vec`'s implicit heap allocation" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/macro.vec.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/vec/struct.Vec.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch08-01-vectors.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/107-vec-basics.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/107-vec-basics.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 108-leading-zeros
move: call the inherent method `n.leading_zeros()` on a `u64` value to get the count of leading zero bits in its 64-bit binary representation as a `u32`; concretely the working probe `fn main() { println!("0u64 leading zeros = {}", 0u64.leading_zeros()); println!("1u64 leading zeros = {}", 1u64.leading_zeros()); println!("0x100000000u64 leading zeros = {}", 0x100000000u64.leading_zeros()); println!("u64::MAX leading zeros = {}", u64::MAX.leading_zeros()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exits 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `0u64 leading zeros = 64` / `1u64 leading zeros = 63` / `0x100000000u64 leading zeros = 31` / `u64::MAX leading zeros = 0` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe `fn main() { let x = 1.0f64.leading_zeros(); println!("x = {}", x); }` fires `error[E0599]: no method named \`leading_zeros\` found for type \`f64\` in the current scope` with caret on the method name, inline label `method not found in \`f64\``, and the standard `--explain E0599` trailer — same dot-call shape, same method name, only the receiver type changed
main_concept: integer types in Rust carry *inherent methods* — functions called via the lesson-040 dot-shape `value.method()`. The std `primitive.u64.md` page lines 113-115 give the signature verbatim `pub const fn leading_zeros(self) -> u32` with the gloss "Returns the number of leading zeros in the binary representation of `self`." Three coupled rules. (1) *Receiver shape `self` (no `&`)*: lesson 102's consuming-receiver shape; for non-`Copy` types the receiver gets moved, but `u64` is `Copy` (lesson 080's family is the canonical Copy set; lesson 102's *What To Ignore* names this), so the call does not consume the value — surface effect matches a `&self` method. (2) *Return type `u32` (not `u64`)*: the std library uses `u32` as the count type for every integer width's bit-counting methods because the count is small (at most the type's BITS); witnessed by Probe 3's E0308 when a `u64` annotation is forced on the receiver of `let n: u64 = 1u64.leading_zeros();`. (3) *Method present on every integer width but not on floats or `bool`*: cross-checked against `primitive.u8.md` lines 113-130, `primitive.u32.md` lines 113-130, `primitive.i64.md` lines 82-94 (each shows `leading_zeros(self) -> u32` with same shape and an integer-edge-cases example block); negative coverage by `grep -n "leading_zeros" primitive.f32.md primitive.f64.md primitive.bool.md` returning no matches; operationally witnessed by Probe 2 (the centered contrast: `1.0f64.leading_zeros()` -> E0599 verbatim) and Probe 4 (`true.leading_zeros()` -> E0599 with inline `method not found in \`bool\``). The std doc edge cases `0u64.leading_zeros() == 64` and `u64::MAX.leading_zeros() == 0` are spelled out verbatim in the source page; the probe also covers two interior cases (`1u64` -> `63`; `0x100000000u64` -> `31`) to witness "value with bit `k` as highest set bit returns `64 - 1 - k`." Today is shallow operational: install the single method `leading_zeros` on `u64` only. Explicitly defer the bit-counting siblings (`trailing_zeros`, `count_ones`, `count_zeros`, `leading_ones`, `trailing_ones` — same signature shape, same `u32` return), the wrapping/checked/overflowing/saturating arithmetic method families (lesson 083 named these as deferred), the bit operators `<<` `>>` `&` `|` `^` `!` (lesson 013 named the shift operators as deferred; today's probe avoids `<<` by using lesson 081's hex literal `0x100000000u64` for `2^32`), the `BITS`/`MAX`/`MIN` associated constants on integer types as a centered `Type::CONST` move (today uses `u64::MAX` as an opaque constant), the `std::mem::size_of::<T>()` generic associated function used in rmp's `LIMB_SIZE_BITS`, and the `unbounded_shl`/`unbounded_shr` family used elsewhere in rmp's `biguint/shift.rs`. The audience crosses from "I know `u64` is one of the twelve integer types from lesson 080 and I can use the dot-call shape on values whose methods I have seen demonstrated" to "Integer types carry inherent methods discoverable on the per-primitive `primitive.uN.md` doc page; `n.leading_zeros()` is the first one I have used; the dot shape from lesson 040 reaches it; the receiver-`self`-but-`Copy` mechanism from lesson 102 keeps the receiver usable; the return type is uniformly `u32`; calling the same method on a non-integer type fires E0599 verbatim." This unlocks reading `self.limbs[n - 1].leading_zeros() as u64` in rmp's `BigUInt::num_bits` method (rmp target audit line 68): the `as u64` cast is lesson 034's shape, converting the `u32` return to `u64` for the surrounding arithmetic.
depends_on: 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs `value.method(args)` dot-call shape; today applies it to `.leading_zeros()` on a `u64` receiver, no new syntactic rule), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs `u64` as one of the twelve typed integer names plus the rule that the family carries to every width; today uses `u64` as the worked receiver type and names that the same method exists on every integer width per the per-primitive doc cross-check; lesson 080 also named `u64::MAX` as deferred, today uses it as an opaque constant), 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — `u32` as the return type of today's method; auxiliary Probe 3 witnesses the typing rule via E0308), 081-integer-literal-forms (cited — installs typed-decimal `0u64`/`1u64` and typed-hex `0x100000000u64` literal forms used in the working probe), 102-self-by-value-receiver (cited — installs `self` (no `&`) as the consuming receiver shape; today's method has signature `(self)` but `u64` is `Copy` so the consume rule is invisible at the call site, as lesson 102's *What To Ignore* anticipated), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to E0599 in the contrast probe), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (cited — first lesson to surface E0599; today's contrast hits the same E-code via a different cause, "method does not exist on this type at all" rather than "associated function called as method"), 034-as-cast-i32-to-f64 (cited — the `as u64` cast in the closing parenthetical referencing rmp's `num_bits`), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point), 005-let-binding (cited — `let` in the contrast probe), 011-println-positional-args (cited — four `println!` invocations with `{}` slots in the working probe), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell, `echo $?` for exit-status checks)
unlocks: future "the bit-counting sibling methods on integers — `trailing_zeros`, `count_ones`, `count_zeros`, `leading_ones`, `trailing_ones` — same signature shape `(self) -> u32`, same per-width availability; one batch lesson or one-per-method moves" moves, future "the `wrapping_*` arithmetic method family on integer types — `wrapping_add`, `wrapping_sub`, `wrapping_mul`, `wrapping_neg`, `wrapping_shl`, `wrapping_shr`; the rmp target uses these heavily and lesson 083 named them as deferred" moves, future "the `overflowing_*` arithmetic method family — `overflowing_add` returns `(value, bool)`; rmp's `BigUInt::add` uses `overflowing_add` for limb-wise carry propagation" moves, future "the `checked_*` arithmetic method family — returns `Option<T>` on overflow; complements `wrapping_*`" moves, future "the `saturating_*` arithmetic method family — returns saturated value on overflow" moves, future "the bit operators `<<` `>>` `&` `|` `^` `!` on integer values — operator-level (Reference operator-expr.md), separate from method-call shape; lesson 013 named the shift operators as deferred" moves, future "the `BITS` associated constant on integer types — `u64::BITS == 64`, the formal way to recover the type's width without the literal `64`; introduces the `Type::CONST` shape" moves, future "the `MAX` and `MIN` associated constants on integer types — `u64::MAX`, `u64::MIN`, etc.; today uses `u64::MAX` as an opaque constant, the `Type::CONST` shape is its own move" moves, future "`std::mem::size_of::<T>()` — the generic associated function used in rmp's `LIMB_SIZE_BITS` constant; introduces turbofish `::<T>` and the `mem` module" moves, future "`unbounded_shl` and `unbounded_shr` — the unbounded-shift methods used in rmp's `biguint/shift.rs`; future move under bit operators" moves, future "the `Copy` trait as a centered concept — today's `u64` `Copy`-ness is named operationally; the trait machinery is deferred since lesson 040" moves, future "rmp's `BigUInt::num_bits` method — `pub(crate) fn num_bits(&self) -> u64 { ... self.limbs.len() ... self.limbs[n - 1].leading_zeros() as u64 ... }` — composes today (`leading_zeros`), lesson 107 (`Vec` `.len()` and `v[i]`), lesson 034 (`as` cast), and deferred `&self` field-access machinery" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/primitive.u64.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/primitive.u8.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/primitive.u32.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/primitive.i64.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/u64/constant.MAX.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0599.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/108-leading-zeros.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/108-leading-zeros.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 109-module-scope-const
move: declare a `const NAME: TYPE = value;` at *module scope* (outside any `fn`, in the crate root or inside a `mod` block) so it becomes a *module-level item*, then optionally gate it with one of the `Visibility` shapes lessons 096/103 installed (`pub`, `pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`) in the same syntactic position those modifiers occupy in front of a function item; concretely the working probe `const MAX_BYTES: u32 = 1024; mod inner { const LOCAL: u64 = 100; pub(crate) const SHARED: u64 = 64; pub fn read_local() -> u64 { LOCAL } } fn main() { println!("MAX_BYTES = {}", MAX_BYTES); println!("inner::SHARED = {}", inner::SHARED); println!("inner::LOCAL via fn = {}", inner::read_local()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exits 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `MAX_BYTES = 1024` / `inner::SHARED = 64` / `inner::LOCAL via fn = 100` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe drops `pub(crate)` from `SHARED` (single-keyword diff) and tries to read `inner::SHARED` from `main` — fires `error[E0603]: constant \`SHARED\` is private` with caret at the use site under `SHARED`, inline label `private constant`, and a `note:` block at the definition site `--> broken.rs:3:5` underlining the entire `const SHARED: u64 = 64;` line, closing with the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0603` trailer; the `pub(super)`-reach-exhaustion auxiliary probe `mod outer { pub mod inner { pub(super) const PARENT_ONLY: u32 = 42; } pub fn read() -> u32 { inner::PARENT_ONLY } } fn main() { println!("via outer::read = {}", outer::read()); println!("direct = {}", outer::inner::PARENT_ONLY); }` fires the same E0603 on the `outer::inner::PARENT_ONLY` line from `main` (column 43, 11 chars caret) — same exhaustion pattern lesson 103 installed for fn items, now witnessed on a const; the auxiliary observation probe `fn main() { pub const X: u32 = 5; println!("X = {}", X); }` compiles silently exit 0 and prints `X = 5`, demonstrating that the `pub` modifier is accepted but has no effect at function scope (a function body is a `Block`, not a `VisItem`-bearing position)
main_concept: a `const NAME: TYPE = value;` declaration at module scope is a *module-level item* per the Reference's `Item` grammar (`items.md` lines 10-29: `Item → OuterAttribute* ( VisItem | MacroItem )` and `VisItem → Visibility? ( Module | Function | TypeAlias | Struct | Enumeration | Union | ConstantItem | StaticItem | ... )`), with the `ConstantItem` alternative slotting next to `Function`/`Struct`/`Module` in the same enumeration — load-bearing for the lesson's framing that "`pub`-on-`const` is the same rule as `pub`-on-`fn`". Two coupled framings under one centered move. (1) *Position*: lesson 075's `const NAME: TYPE = value;` shape works *outside* every function, at the crate root or inside a `mod` block, where it becomes a *module-level item*. The Reference's `items/constant-items.md` line 23 verbatim "The constant declaration defines the constant value in the value namespace of the module or block where it is located" — load-bearing for the module-or-block disjunction; lesson 075 covered the block case (function bodies are blocks), today covers the module case. The Book Ch3-1 lines 122-123 verbatim "Constants can be declared in any scope, including the global scope" was already cited by lesson 075 as the fifth difference from `let`, but lesson 075's *centered* teaching content was the five-fact difference list, not the visibility consequences of the global-scope position. Today centers exactly that follow-on. (2) *Visibility*: once a const sits at module scope, the `Visibility?` slot from the `VisItem` rule applies. The visibility shapes lesson 096 (`pub`) and lesson 103 (`pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`) installed all sit in the same syntactic position they did on functions, exactly because both `Function` and `ConstantItem` are alternatives inside the same `VisItem` production. Default is private to the declaring module per `visibility-and-privacy.md` line 31 (already installed). The diagnostic on a failed access is the same E0603 lesson 096 first installed, with the inline label `private constant` substituting `private function` — same E-code, item-kind-specialized phrasing. The `pub(super)` reach-exhaustion is the same pattern lesson 103 installed for fn items: the modifier reaches *only* the parent of the declaring module. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the module-scope position and the three already-installed visibility shapes (`pub`, `pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`) on a `const` item. Explicitly defer `static` items (sibling form, different memory semantics — Reference `items/constant-items.md` line 19 "Constants are essentially inlined wherever they are used"); associated constants (`impl Type { const N: ... }` and `trait T { const N: ...; }` are different items per Reference `items/constant-items.md` line 91, blocked on traits and on the impl-with-constants composition); generic const items / const generics (same keyword, different role; named in lesson 075's deferral); right-hand-side function calls (lesson 075 saw E0015; unchanged today, today's RHS stays at literals); `pub(self)` and `pub(in path)` (already deferred in lesson 103); underscore-named consts `const _: () = assert!(...);` (Reference `items/constant-items.md` lines 87-101, future move for top-level compile-time assertions); `std::mem::size_of::<T>()` (rmp's `LIMB_SIZE_BITS` right-hand side; generic associated function, its own future move); cross-crate `pub` (already deferred in lesson 103); the silent acceptance of `pub` on a function-scope const (auxiliary observation in evidence Probe 5 — `pub` is accepted but has no effect because a function body is a `Block`, not a `VisItem`-bearing position; not centered today). This unlocks reading rmp's `pub(crate) const LIMB_SIZE_BITS: u64 = 8 * (std::mem::size_of::<u64>() as u64);` line in `biguint/basic.rs` — the left-hand side `pub(crate) const NAME: TYPE = value;` is exactly today's shape; the right-hand side `8 * (std::mem::size_of::<u64>() as u64)` involves `std::mem::size_of::<u64>()`, named-but-deferred today. The rmp-target-audit at line 395-396 explicitly named "const items at module scope with pub(crate) visibility — lesson 075 covered const at function scope only" as a direct unblock — today is exactly that move.
depends_on: 075-const-declaration (load-bearing — installs `const NAME: TYPE = value;` syntax, the SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE convention, the required `: TYPE` annotation, and the constant-expression rule for the right-hand side; lesson 075's working probe placed `THREE_HOURS_IN_SECONDS: u32 = 60 * 60 * 3;` at global scope but the centered teaching was the five-fact difference list against `let`, not visibility on the result; today extends 075 with the visibility consequences of the global-scope position), 096-inline-module-and-pub (load-bearing — installs `mod foo { ... }`, `pub` on a function item, items private by default, the `module::item` access form, and the E0603 diagnostic with caret at use site and `note:` block at definition; today applies the same `pub`-position rule to a `ConstantItem` rather than a `Function`; Probe 2's E0603 transcript matches 096's transcript shape exactly, only the inline label changes from `private function` to `private constant`), 103-restricted-visibility (load-bearing — installs `pub(crate)` and `pub(super)` as two more shapes from the `Visibility` grammar, both in the same syntactic position as plain `pub`, both diagnosed by the same E0603 when access fails; today reuses both shapes verbatim on a const item, including the `pub(super)` modifier on Probe 4), 104-super-and-crate-paths (cited — named for completeness as the lesson that installed `super::` and `crate::` path tokens; today's probes do *not* exercise `super::name` token syntax, only the `pub(super)` *modifier* from 103 and absolute paths like `outer::inner::PARENT_ONLY`), 040-method-call-syntax (cited only — not used in today's probes; the constant is read by direct path access `inner::SHARED`, not by method call), 043-nested-module-paths (cited — `module::name(args)` path call form; today's `inner::SHARED` and `inner::read_local()` are this shape with `inner` as a user-declared module), 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — `u32` and `u64` in the `: TYPE` slot of the const declarations), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` body runs when the executable launches; the host of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — not used directly in today's probes; cite is for the `let`-vs-`const` contrast already drawn in lesson 075), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — `fn name() -> T { ... }` definition shape; the helper `fn read_local` in the working probe is unchanged from 008's shape), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with `{}` placeholders, three calls in the working probe), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to E0603 in Probes 2, 3, 4, 6), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp's `pub(crate) const LIMB_SIZE_BITS: u64 = 8 * (std::mem::size_of::<u64>() as u64);` line in `biguint/basic.rs` — composes today's `pub(crate) const` shape with the deferred `std::mem::size_of::<T>()` generic associated function and the lesson-034 `as` cast" moves, future "`std::mem::size_of::<T>()` — the generic associated function used in rmp's `LIMB_SIZE_BITS` right-hand side; introduces turbofish `::<T>` and the `mem` module; the natural follow-on for fully reading the `LIMB_SIZE_BITS` line" moves, future "`static` items — `static NAME: TYPE = value;` shares today's shape but has different memory semantics (one fixed memory location vs the const's 'essentially inlined wherever used' rule per Reference items/constant-items.md line 19); the natural sibling-level follow-on" moves, future "associated constants — `impl Type { const N: T = value; }` and `trait T { const N: T; }` per Reference items/constant-items.md line 91; different item kind, different rules; blocked on traits and on the composition with lesson 100's impl block" moves, future "underscore-named consts — `const _: () = assert!(...);` per Reference items/constant-items.md lines 87-101; pattern for top-level compile-time assertions; future move" moves, future "`pub use` on a const item — composes today's visibility on a const with lesson 105's `pub use` shape; would let a deeply-nested const be re-exported under a shorter path" moves, future "the `non_upper_case_globals` lint when a module-scope const violates the SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE convention — already deferred in lesson 075; today's probes all use SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE so the lint does not fire" moves, future "`pub(self)` and `pub(in path)` on a const — already deferred in lesson 103 for fn items; same deferral on const items" moves, future "constant-expression rules at module scope — Reference items/constant-items.md line 31 names the `'static` lifetime requirement on references in the initializer; today's const values are primitive integers with no references, so the rule does not bite" moves, future "the silent acceptance of `pub` on a function-scope const — Probe 5 today witnessed this; the formal rule (function bodies are not `VisItem`-bearing positions) is its own pedagogical observation" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/constant-items.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/visibility-and-privacy.md

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 110-capstone-rmp-biguint-trio
move: capstone for the rmp arc (lessons 095-109) — read end-to-end the rmp slice `bignum::biguint::BigUInt` plus the foundational trio `zero` / `is_zero` / `num_bits` (and the constant `LIMB_SIZE_BITS`) defined in `/Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/basic.rs`. **No new mechanic.** Three coupled axes covered. (1) *Module-path chain* `bignum::biguint::BigUInt`: `Cargo.toml:2` `name = "bignum"` → `src/lib.rs:2 pub mod biguint;` (lesson 097's file-mod rule + lesson 106's subdirectory disambiguation, since rmp uses `biguint/mod.rs` not `biguint.rs`) → `src/biguint/mod.rs:2 mod basic;` (no `pub`; lesson 097, 096 default-private) → `src/biguint/basic.rs:1-4 pub struct BigUInt { pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64> }` → `src/biguint/mod.rs:10 pub use basic::BigUInt;` (lesson 105's re-export, load-bearing: without it, the path is `bignum::biguint::basic::BigUInt` which is unreachable from outside `biguint` because `mod basic;` is private). (2) *Type declaration token-by-token*: `pub` (096) on the struct, `struct` (095), name `BigUInt`, `pub(super)` (103) on the field — composed into Reference `items/structs.md:22` grammar `StructField → OuterAttribute* Visibility? IDENTIFIER : Type`, lesson 103 having explicitly deferred this composition in *What To Ignore*; field name `limbs`; type `Vec<u64>` (107 + 080). (3) *Three methods, line by line*. `LIMB_SIZE_BITS = 8 * (std::mem::size_of::<u64>() as u64)` is `pub(crate) const` at module scope (109); right-hand side uses `std::mem::size_of::<u64>()` (deferred, value 8 grounded by `std/mem/fn.size_of.md:30` table row `u64 | 8`) and `as u64` cast (034); evaluates to 64 at compile time. `pub fn zero() -> Self { BigUInt { limbs: vec![] } }` is associated function (100) with empty `vec![]` (107) and struct literal (095). `pub fn is_zero(&self) -> bool { return self.limbs.len() == 0; }` is `&self` method (100) with field access (095), `.len()` (107), `==` (013), explicit `return` (operational equivalent to lesson 025's implicit form). `pub(crate) fn num_bits(&self) -> u64 { match self.limbs.len() { 0 => 0, n => (n as u64 - 1) * LIMB_SIZE_BITS + (LIMB_SIZE_BITS - (self.limbs[n - 1].leading_zeros() as u64)) } }` is the algorithmic method: `pub(crate)` (103), `&self` (100), `match` on `usize` with arms `0` and `n` (031 + 058 binding-pattern slot), lower-limbs term `(n as u64 - 1) * LIMB_SIZE_BITS` counts the bits in the n-1 fully-used limbs, MSL term `LIMB_SIZE_BITS - (... .leading_zeros() as u64)` counts bits actually used in the most-significant limb (107 indexing + 108 leading_zeros + 034 cast). Five empirical probes plus rmp's own `num_bits_correct` test plus an honest-observation probe (`From<u64>` non-canonicalization, witnessing that the no-trailing-zero invariant is constructor-side discipline, not enforced inside `is_zero`). Working probe — self-contained `.rs` file — compiled with `rustc 110-capstone-rmp-biguint-trio.rs -o probe` exits 0 silent and `./probe` prints exactly `LIMB_SIZE_BITS = 64` / `zero.limbs.len() = 0` / `zero.is_zero() = true` / `zero.num_bits() = 0` / `one.is_zero() = false` / `one.num_bits() = 1` / `v256.num_bits() = 9` / `max1.num_bits() = 64` / `two_64.num_bits() = 65` / `max2.num_bits() = 128` / `bad.is_zero() = false  (length-only check)` / `bad.num_bits() = 0` and exits 0; the cross-crate driver via path dependency `bignum = { path = "/Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp" }` confirms `use bignum::biguint::BigUInt;` resolves and `BigUInt::zero().is_zero()` returns `true` from outside the crate; three centered contrast probes each fire the expected E-code from outside — `z.num_bits()` fires `error[E0624]: method \`num_bits\` is private` with `note:` block pointing at `basic.rs:45 pub(crate) fn num_bits(&self) -> u64 {`, `use bignum::biguint::basic::BigUInt;` fires `error[E0603]: module \`basic\` is private` with `note:` block pointing at `biguint/mod.rs:2 mod basic;`, and `z.limbs.len()` fires `error[E0616]: field \`limbs\` of struct \`BigUInt\` is private` — three different E-codes for three different visibility positions (item method, item module, struct field), each composed of installed lessons; `cargo test --lib num_bits` against the rmp project itself prints `test biguint::basic::tests::num_bits_correct ... ok / 1 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 16 filtered out` confirming the algorithm walked today is correct on rmp's own canonical inputs (`BigUInt::from(0b0u64).num_bits() == 0`, `BigUInt::from(0b1u64).num_bits() == 1`, `BigUInt::from(0b1u64 << 32).num_bits() == 33`, and `(1u64 << shift).num_bits() == shift + 1` for `shift in 0..100`)
main_concept: this is *Capstone Mode* — no new Rust mechanic, instead a witness that the rmp arc (lessons 095-109, fifteen moves) composes into a real-world Rust slice readable end-to-end. The audience crosses from "I have installed every named ingredient the trio uses, but I have not read a real-world slice end-to-end" to "I can read `bignum::biguint::BigUInt` plus the trio `zero` / `is_zero` / `num_bits` plus `LIMB_SIZE_BITS` in rmp's `biguint/basic.rs` — every token, every line, every visibility modifier, every arithmetic step — using only the 109 installed concepts plus `std::mem::size_of::<u64>() == 8` looked up in the std doc table; I can predict each method's behavior on hand-built inputs, confirm the predictions empirically, and reason about which invariants the trio depends on (canonical-form: empty `Vec` means zero, no trailing-zero limbs, little-endian limb order) and which the project enforces vs leaves as constructor-side discipline (Probe 7's `BigUInt::from(0u64).is_zero() == false` honestly surfaces that `From<u64>` does not normalize and `is_zero` is a length-only check, not value-equality)." Three understanding axes covered explicitly: (1) *Module layout*: how `lib.rs` reaches `BigUInt::num_bits` via the path `bignum::biguint::BigUInt` — every link in the chain (`pub mod biguint;`, `mod basic;`, `pub use basic::BigUInt;`) is composed of installed lessons; the contrast probe `bignum::biguint::basic::BigUInt` fires E0603 from outside `biguint`, witness that the re-export is load-bearing. (2) *Type declaration with full visibility coverage*: every visibility modifier in the slice is explained — plain `pub` on the struct and on `zero`/`is_zero` (096); `pub(super)` on the `limbs` field (composed into lesson 103's modifier-on-a-field slot, grounded by Reference `items/structs.md:22` grammar `StructField → ... Visibility? IDENTIFIER : Type`; Probe 5 fires E0616 from outside, witnessing field-level reach exhaustion); `pub(crate)` on `num_bits` and `LIMB_SIZE_BITS` (103 + 109; Probe 3 fires E0624 from outside, witnessing crate-boundary reach exhaustion). (3) *Algorithmic walkthrough with empirical confirmation*: `num_bits` walked step by step — the match arm `0 => 0` for the canonical-zero case, the non-zero arm computing `(n - 1) * 64 + (64 - leading_zeros(MSL))` where the lower-limbs term counts bits in the n-1 fully-used limbs and the MSL term counts bits actually used in the most-significant limb — predicted on six hand-built inputs (`zero` → 0, `vec![1]` → 1, `vec![0x100]` → 9, `vec![u64::MAX]` → 64, `vec![0, 1]` → 65, `vec![u64::MAX, u64::MAX]` → 128) and confirmed by Probe 1 transcript; the non-canonical input `BigUInt { limbs: vec![0] }` returns `num_bits() == 0` because `leading_zeros(0) == 64` makes the MSL term zero, witness that the algorithm is well-defined on non-canonical inputs even though `is_zero` returns `false` on them. The arc closes here. Two follow-on arcs unlocked: the *trait arc* (so the rest of rmp's machinery — `From`, `TryFrom`, `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `PartialOrd`, `Display`, etc. — becomes readable) and *deeper rmp algorithmic methods* (the carry-propagation in `add.rs`, the schoolbook multiplication in `mul.rs`, the long division in `div.rs`, the bit shifts in `shift.rs`).

today applies the same `pub`-position rule to `pub struct BigUInt`, `pub fn zero`, `pub fn is_zero`, and to the `pub mod biguint;` line in `lib.rs`; Probe 4's E0603 transcript carries the same shape verbatim), 097-file-based-module (load-bearing — installs `mod foo;` resolves to a sibling file `foo.rs`; today applies the rule to read `mod basic;` in `biguint/mod.rs` resolving to `biguint/basic.rs`), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `impl Type { ... }` block, associated functions `fn name() -> T { ... }` with no receiver, `&self` methods, and `Self` as a return type alias for the impl block's type; today reuses all four pieces: the `impl BigUInt { ... }` block, the `fn zero() -> Self` associated function, the `fn is_zero(&self) -> bool` and `fn num_bits(&self) -> u64` `&self` methods, and `Self` as the return type of `zero`), 103-restricted-visibility (load-bearing — installs `pub(super)` and `pub(crate)` as two more shapes from the `Visibility` grammar, both in the same syntactic position as plain `pub`, both diagnosed by E0603/E0624 when access fails; today reuses both modifiers in three positions — `pub(crate) const LIMB_SIZE_BITS` (composed with lesson 109's module-scope-const shape), `pub(crate) fn num_bits` (method-on-impl visibility), and `pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64>` (struct-field visibility — the composition lesson 103's *What To Ignore* explicitly named "Restricted visibility on struct fields — composes today's modifier with lesson 095's field declarations; IDENTIFIER : Type` admitting Visibility in the field position); Probes 3, 4, 5 witness E0624, E0603, and E0616 from outside the crate, respectively), 105-pub-use-re-export (load-bearing — installs `pub use Path::Item;` as a re-export; today reads the line `pub use basic::BigUInt;` in `biguint/mod.rs:10` exactly as 105 installed; Probe 4's contrast `use bignum::biguint::basic::BigUInt;` fires E0603 witnessing what fails *without* the re-export — i.e., that the re-export is load-bearing for the path `bignum::biguint::BigUInt`), 106-subdirectory-module (load-bearing — installs `mod foo;` resolving to `foo/mod.rs` as the sibling-directory alternative to lesson 097's sibling-file `foo.rs`; today reuses all three: `vec![]` in `zero`'s body, `self.limbs.len()` in `is_zero` and `num_bits`, and `self.limbs[n - 1]` in `num_bits`), 108-leading-zeros (load-bearing — installs `n.leading_zeros()` on a `u64` returning `u32`; today reads `self.limbs[n - 1].leading_zeros()` exactly — `u64` receiver because `Vec<u64>` indexing returns `u64`, `u32` return cast to `u64` via `as u64`), 109-module-scope-const (load-bearing — installs `pub(crate) const NAME: TYPE = value;` at module scope; today reads `pub(crate) const LIMB_SIZE_BITS: u64 = 8 * (std::mem::size_of::<u64>() as u64);` exactly — left-hand side is precisely 109's shape; right-hand side composes lesson 034's `as` cast with the deferred `std::mem::size_of::<u64>()` whose value 8 is grounded by `std/mem/fn.size_of.md:30` table row), 098-enum-with-unit-variants (cited only — installed in the rmp arc as a foundational shape for completeness; the trio doesn't declare an enum but `Ordering` and `Result` follow the same shape), 099-enum-tuple-variants (cited only — same reasoning as 098), 101-mut-self-receiver (cited only — `&mut self` not used by the trio; installed in the arc for completeness and used elsewhere in rmp), 102-self-by-value-receiver (cited only — `self` consuming receiver not used by the trio; the receiver shape of `n.leading_zeros()` is `self` but `u64` is `Copy` so the consume rule is invisible at the call site, as lesson 108 already named), 104-super-and-crate-paths (cited only — the trio's source uses neither path token; the rmp source uses `super::` once in `convert.rs:1` `use super::basic::BigUInt;`, outside today's slice; the *meaning* of `pub(super)` (parent-module reach) is set by 104's framing of "what the parent of a module is"), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let z = BigUInt::zero();` etc. in the probes), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — `fn name() -> T { ... }` definition shape; operationally equivalent), 030-match-on-bool (cited — match shape on a small finite value, foundational), 031-match-integer-and-wildcard (cited — today's `match self.limbs.len() { 0 => 0, n => ... }` matches on a `usize` with a literal-pattern arm `0` and an identifier-pattern arm `n`), 034-as-cast-i32-to-f64 (cited — `n as u64`, `... as u64`, and `std::mem::size_of::<u64>() as u64` in the trio), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — `value.method()` dot-call shape used everywhere), 044-use-declaration (cited — `use bignum::biguint::BigUInt;` in the cross-crate driver), 058-match-result-payload-variants (cited — installs the binding-pattern slot in a `match` arm; the trio's arithmetic stays in `u64` after casts), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to E0603, E0616, E0624 in Probes 3, 4, 5), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc probe.rs && ./probe` shape in Probe 1), 032-cargo-new-and-run (cited — `cargo new --vcs none rmp_driver`, `cargo run`, `cargo test`, `cargo build --bin <name>` in Probes 2-7; the path-dependency form is one shape lesson 065 named-but-deferred, and today's auxiliary use doesn't center it — it just composes), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` and `cargo` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell, ability to `cd` into a directory, `echo $?` for exit-status checks, `cargo new` directory layout)
unlocks: future "the *trait arc* — `From<T>`, `TryFrom<T>`, `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `Rem`, `Shl`, `Shr`, `AddAssign`/`SubAssign`/`MulAssign`/`ShlAssign`/`ShrAssign`, `PartialOrd`, `Ord`, `PartialEq`, `Eq`, `Display`, `Debug`, `Clone`, `Copy`, plus the `impl Trait for Type { ... }` shape that defines them — this is the next major arc; it unlocks the rest of rmp's machinery (`bigint.rs`'s `BigInt`/`Sign`, `biguint/cmp.rs`'s `PartialOrd`/`Ord`, `biguint/format.rs`'s `Display`, `biguint/convert.rs`'s `From`/`TryFrom`, etc.) and is also the foundation for reading any modern Rust library" moves, future "deeper rmp algorithmic methods — `BigUInt::add` (carry-propagation via `overflowing_add`), `BigUInt::sub` (borrow-propagation via `overflowing_sub`), `BigUInt::mul` (schoolbook multiplication via `overflowing_mul` and `Bones`-style splitting), `BigUInt::div` (long-division via the helper methods in `div.rs`), `BigUInt::shl_assign`/`shr_assign` (bit shifts via the deferred `unbounded_shl`/`unbounded_shr` family); each composes today's trio with the trait arc plus the arithmetic-method families lesson 108 named (`overflowing_add`, `overflowing_sub`, `overflowing_mul`, `wrapping_add`, etc.)" moves, future "`std::mem::size_of::<T>()` — generic associated function with turbofish syntax `::<T>`; the natural follow-on for fully reading the `LIMB_SIZE_BITS` right-hand side; introduces the `mem` module and the turbofish path-segment-with-type-arguments form" moves, future "derive macros — `#[derive(Clone)]`, `#[derive(Debug)]`, `#[derive(PartialEq)]`, etc. — the `Clone` derive on `BigUInt` is treated as decorate-only-deferred today; the formal rule (derive macros expand to `impl Trait for Type` at compile time) needs trait machinery first" moves, future "the `#[cfg(test)]` and `#[test]` attribute machinery plus `assert_eq!`/`assert_ne!`/`assert!` macros — testing infrastructure used by rmp's `num_bits_correct` test; `cargo test` was used today as a Probe-6 form but the inside-the-test-block syntax is deferred" moves, future "the `Bones` and `BonesU32` structs and `limb_to_bones`/`limb_to_bones_u32` functions in the same `basic.rs` file — internal helpers used by `mul.rs`'s schoolbook-multiplication implementation; each composes today's struct shape with the bit-shift and bit-mask operators lesson 013 deferred" moves, future "auto-deref and method receiver coercions — when `is_zero` writes `self.limbs.len()`, `self` is `&BigUInt`, `self.limbs` reads the field as `&Vec<u64>`, and `.len()` is called on the `&Vec<u64>` receiver via auto-deref; the formal rule is its own pedagogical move" moves, future "the `pub use` re-export composed with restricted visibility — `pub(crate) use foo::Bar;` etc., already deferred in lesson 105's *What To Ignore* and lesson 103's; rmp doesn't use this in the trio but more advanced library layouts do" moves, future "non-trivial `match` patterns on a `usize` — range patterns `0..=64`, `or` patterns `0 | 1`, struct/tuple destructure patterns; today's `0 => ..., n => ...` is the simplest case" moves, future "field-level `pub(crate)` and `pub(in path)` and `pub(self)` — the four other field-visibility shapes lesson 103 named-but-deferred; today demonstrated `pub(super)` only" moves, future "`unsafe fn`, `MaybeUninit`, manual `set_len` — the heavy machinery elsewhere in rmp (`mul.rs:19` uses `Vec::with_capacity` and a later `set_len` pattern); each its own future move" moves
```
