```text
id: 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type
move: declare a *user-defined trait* with one method *signature* — body replaced by a single semicolon — using the Reference grammar `Trait → unsafe? trait IDENTIFIER GenericParams? ( : TypeParamBounds? )? WhereClause? { InnerAttribute* AssociatedItem* }` (`output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md:10-15`); then *implement* that trait for a concrete struct using the *trait-impl* grammar rule `TraitImpl → unsafe? impl GenericParams? !? TypePath for Type WhereClause? { InnerAttribute* AssociatedItem* }` (`output/docs/rust/reference/items/implementations.md:18-24`), distinct from lesson 100's `InherentImpl → impl GenericParams? Type WhereClause? { ... }`; then call the trait method via the dot-call shape `value.method()` (lesson 040, unchanged) — three new top-level items (`trait`, `impl Trait for Type`, and the call resolving to a *trait* method) all in one minimal program; concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } trait Doubled { fn doubled(&self) -> u32; } impl Doubled for Counter { fn doubled(&self) -> u32 { self.count * 2 } } fn main() { let c = Counter { count: 21 }; println!("doubled = {}", c.doubled()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `doubled = 42` and exits 0 — three composed pieces (trait declaration with semicolon-body, trait-impl block with `Trait for Type` header, and a dot call resolving to a trait method) all witnessed in one minimal program; the centered contrast probe with the same source modulo the entire `impl Doubled for Counter { ... }` block deleted (struct, trait declaration, and `fn main` unchanged) fires `error[E0599]: no method named \`doubled\` found for struct \`Counter\` in the current scope` with `--> no_impl.rs:11:32`, caret under `doubled` at the call site, the inline label `method not found in \`Counter\``, the `= help:` line `items from traits can only be used if the trait is implemented and in scope` naming today's rule from rustc's mouth verbatim, and a `note: \`Doubled\` defines an item \`doubled\`, perhaps you need to implement it` block with a second `-->` line `no_impl.rs:5:1` underlining the entire `trait Doubled {` line at the trait declaration site, plus the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0599` trailer; the auxiliary E0599 sharpening probe — same trait declaration and `impl Doubled for Counter { ... }` impl block, plus a *second* struct `Other { n: u32 }` and `fn main` rewritten to call `o.doubled()` on an `Other` value (which does *not* implement `Doubled`) — fires the *same* E0599 with `method not found in \`Other\`` and the same `= help:`/`note:` shape, empirical witness that an `impl Trait for Type` block attaches the trait's methods to *that specific type only*, not globally to all types in scope
main_concept: `impl Trait for Type { ... }` is the *trait-impl* form of the `Implementation` grammar — distinct from lesson 100's `impl Type { ... }` *inherent-impl* form per Reference `items/implementations.md:10-24` `Implementation → InherentImpl | TraitImpl` — and a `trait` declaration is a *separate* item that names the method *signatures* the impl will fill in. The smallest atomic trait machinery is *three composed pieces* — declare a trait with one method signature whose body is replaced by `;` (Reference `items/traits.md:43` verbatim "Trait functions may omit the function body by replacing it with a semicolon. This indicates that the implementation must define the function." + Book ch10-02 lines 51-55 verbatim "After the method signature, instead of providing an implementation within curly brackets, we use a semicolon. Each type implementing this trait must provide its own custom behavior for the body of the method. The compiler will enforce that any type that has the `Summary` trait will have the method `summarize` defined with this signature exactly."), implement it for one type with the `Trait for Type` header (Book ch10-02 lines 106-112 verbatim "after `impl`, we put the trait name we want to implement, then use the `for` keyword, and then specify the name of the type we want to implement the trait for. Within the `impl` block, we put the method signatures that the trait definition has defined. Instead of adding a semicolon after each signature, we use curly brackets and fill in the method body with the specific behavior that we want the methods of the trait to have for the particular type."), and call the trait method via the unchanged dot-call shape from lesson 040. The signature inside the impl must match the signature inside the trait. (1) *Block syntax for the trait*: `trait Doubled { fn doubled(&self) -> u32; }` instantiates the Reference grammar `Trait → unsafe? trait IDENTIFIER GenericParams? ( : TypeParamBounds? )? WhereClause? { InnerAttribute* AssociatedItem* }` with no `unsafe`, no `GenericParams`, no `TypeParamBounds`, no `WhereClause`, no inner attributes, and one `AssociatedItem` (the `fn doubled(&self) -> u32;` signature with `;` in the body slot). The trait declaration on its own attaches no methods to any type — Reference `items/traits.md:19-25` verbatim "A *trait* describes an abstract interface that types can implement. This interface consists of associated items, which come in three varieties: functions, types, constants." Today exercises only the *function* variety; *types* (associated types like `type Output`) and *constants* (associated consts like `const N: i32`) are deferred. (2) *Block syntax for the impl*: `impl Doubled for Counter { ... }` instantiates the Reference grammar `TraitImpl → unsafe? impl GenericParams? !? TypePath for Type WhereClause? { InnerAttribute* AssociatedItem* }` with no `unsafe`, no `GenericParams`, no negative `!`, the `TypePath` `Doubled`, the `Type` `Counter`, no `WhereClause`, no inner attributes, and one `AssociatedItem` (the `fn doubled(&self) -> u32 { self.count * 2 }` body). The header diff from lesson 100's inherent impl is exactly *the trait name and `for` keyword between `impl` and the type*. (3) *Method body* uses only installed mechanisms — `self.count` is field access from lesson 095 on a `&self` receiver from lesson 100; `* 2` is integer multiplication from lesson 009 on a `u32` value from lesson 080. (4) *Dot call*: `c.doubled()` is the shape from lesson 040, unchanged. The same call-site form now resolves to either an inherent method (lesson 100) or a trait method (today). The audience crosses from "an `impl` block looks like `impl Type { ... }` and attaches functions directly to `Type`" (lesson 100) to "there are *two* kinds of `impl` block, one inherent and one trait, distinguished by whether `Trait for` appears between `impl` and the type; trait declarations live in a separate `trait` item that the impl points back at." Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic trait machinery on a single struct with one signature and a primitive return type. Explicitly defer default method bodies (Reference `items/traits.md:43` covers them; today exercises the no-default form), `pub trait` for cross-module visibility (lesson 096's `pub` composes into the trait header, today's single-file probe doesn't need it), multiple methods in one trait body, multiple types implementing the same trait (the *point* of trait machinery — one signature, many types — named as the future unlock without being probed today), trait method signatures with parameters beyond `&self`, trait method receivers other than `&self` (`&mut self`, `self` by value — composes lesson 101/102 with today), bringing the trait into scope across modules (`use module::TheTrait;`, future move), generic traits `trait Seq<T> { ... }` and trait bounds `fn f<T: Doubled>(t: T) { ... }`, `impl Trait` returns and `dyn Trait`/trait objects (all blocked on the generics arc), associated types `type Output = ...;` and associated constants in trait bodies, operator traits from `std::ops` (`Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `AddAssign`, `Display`, `Debug`, `PartialEq`, `Eq`, `PartialOrd`, `Ord`), `#[derive(Clone)]`/`#[derive(Debug)]` etc. derive macros, `Self` inside the trait body, and the orphan rule. This unlocks reading the rmp target's `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` in `src/biguint/cmp.rs:10` — *exactly* today's `impl Trait for Type { ... }` header with an empty body (because `Eq` declares no methods that lack defaults) — and partially-unlocks `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt { fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool { self.limbs == other.limbs } }` in `src/biguint/cmp.rs:4-8` modulo the generic trait parameter `<BigUInt>` (deferred); the eventual capstone target `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` in `src/biguint/add.rs` adds generic trait parameters, associated types, and a non-`&self` receiver on top of today's shape and remains blocked downstream.
depends_on: 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — declaration `struct Name { field: Type }`, struct expression `Name { field: value }`, field access `instance.field`; today reuses all three: `Counter` is the trait-impl target, `Counter { count: 21 }` is the struct literal in `fn main`, and `self.count` is field access on the `&self` receiver inside `doubled`'s body), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs the `impl Type { ... }` block, the `&self` receiver and its `self: &Self` shorthand, the rule that `&self` is what makes the dot call resolve, and `Self` as a type alias inside the impl; today reuses *every* piece except the impl-block *header*, which gains `Doubled for` between `impl` and the type — the diff from `impl Counter { ... }` to `impl Doubled for Counter { ... }`; lesson 100's *What To Ignore For Now* explicitly named "Trait impls `impl Trait for Type { ... }` — different syntax, blocked on trait machinery" as a future move and today is exactly that move's first cycle), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — the call-site shape `value.method(args)`; today's `c.doubled()` is exactly that shape with no new call syntax; the same call-site form that resolved to inherent methods in lesson 100 now resolves to a *trait* method without any source-level distinction), 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — the `fn name(p: T) -> R { ... }` definition shape; today's trait signature `fn doubled(&self) -> u32;` is the same `fn` shape with the body replaced by `;`, and today's impl method body `fn doubled(&self) -> u32 { self.count * 2 }` is the standard full form), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of the probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let c = Counter { count: 21 };` in `fn main`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("doubled = {}", c.doubled())` with one `{}` slot consumed by the trait-method call's return value), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `*` (multiplication) is the operator used in the working probe's method body `self.count * 2`; lesson 009 is the move that installed `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` on integers, and today reuses the `*` operator unchanged on a `u32` value), 012-bool-literals (cited — `bool` appears as the return type of `as_bool` in Check Yourself's `tiny.rs` (`fn as_bool(&self) -> bool`); lesson 012 installed the `bool` type and its two literals `true`/`false`, and today's Check-Yourself probe reuses the type in the return-type slot and the literal `true` in the predicted output `as_bool = true`), 013-comparison-operators (cited — `>` is the comparison operator used in Check Yourself's `tiny.rs` body `self.n > 0`, returning a `bool`; lesson 013 installed `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` on integers, and today's Check-Yourself probe reuses `>` unchanged), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: u32` annotation in `count: u32` and the `-> u32` return-type annotation; the type annotation slot is unchanged from lesson 019), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` as the named member of the integer family used in today's struct field type and method return type), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0599 transcript and Probe 3's E0599 transcript; the diagnostic shape extends lesson 100's E0599 (which fired with the inline label "this is an associated function, not a method") to today's trait-not-implemented shape (which fires with the `= help:` line "items from traits can only be used if the trait is implemented and in scope" plus the `note:` block pointing at the trait declaration); the multi-`-->` shape — caret at use site plus secondary `-->` line at the trait declaration — is the same shape lessons 096 and 100 already installed for E0603 and E0599 respectively), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "default method bodies in trait declarations — `fn method(&self) -> u32 { 0 }` (curly braces, not `;`) inside a trait acts as a default body the impl may override; Reference `items/traits.md:43` covers the rule and the example trait `Example` at lines 49-55 of the Reference shows both forms side-by-side; a small natural follow-on, composes today's no-default form with a default-providing form" moves, future "`pub trait` and `pub fn method` inside trait bodies — visibility on trait declarations and on associated trait items; lesson 096's `pub` composes into the `Visibility?` slot of the trait grammar; today's single-file probe doesn't need cross-module visibility but the rmp target uses `pub` heavily on traits like `From`, `Add`, etc." moves, future "multiple types implementing the same trait — *the* point of trait machinery, since one signature can describe behavior shared by many types; the next cycle should probe two types implementing one trait and demonstrate that `value.method()` dispatches to the right impl based on the value's type" moves, future "trait method signatures with non-`&self` receivers — `&mut self`, `self` by value; lessons 101 and 102 installed these for inherent methods, today's inherent-vs-trait split is orthogonal and the receiver shapes compose into trait methods identically; the rmp target's `impl AddAssign<&BigUInt> for BigUInt { fn add_assign(&mut self, rhs: &BigUInt) { ... } }` is the canonical `&mut self`-in-trait-method shape" moves, future "trait method signatures with parameters beyond `&self` — `fn add(&self, other: &Counter) -> Counter;` is the standard shape; the rmp target's `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool` in `cmp.rs` is the canonical pattern" moves, future "bringing a trait into scope across modules — `use module::TheTrait;` is required when caller and trait live in different modules; the Book ch10-02 lines 116-118 spell out that 'the user must bring the trait into scope as well as the types'; composes today with lessons 044/097/106's module/use machinery" moves, future "generic traits `trait Seq<T> { ... }` — Reference `items/traits.md:73-84` shows the formal grammar with `GenericParams?` after the `IDENTIFIER`; type parameters on a trait declaration; blocked on the generics arc" moves, future "trait bounds `fn f<T: TraitName>(t: T) { ... }` — generic functions whose parameter types are constrained to implement a trait; this is *the* central machinery that gives traits their pay-off (one trait, many types, generic functions abstracting over all of them); blocked on the generics arc" moves, future "associated types `type Output = ...;` inside a trait — Reference `items/traits.md:43` mentions associated types alongside functions and constants; the shape `type Output;` declares an associated type and `type Output = SomeType;` inside the impl resolves it; the rmp target's `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; ... }` is the canonical pattern" moves, future "associated constants in traits — `const N: i32;` declares a required constant, `const N: i32 = 0;` provides a default; less common than associated types but completes the Reference's three-variety list" moves, future "operator traits from `std::ops` — `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `Rem`, `Shl`, `Shr`, `AddAssign`, `SubAssign`, `MulAssign`, `Neg`, `Not`; each is a real trait whose impls compose today's `impl Trait for Type { ... }` shape with deferred generic parameters and associated types; the rmp target's arithmetic methods are organized around these traits" moves, future "comparison operator traits — `PartialEq`, `Eq`, `PartialOrd`, `Ord`; the rmp target's `biguint/cmp.rs` has all four; today's lesson body cites `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` and `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt { ... }` as the immediate readability unlocks" moves, future "format traits — `Display`, `Debug`; the rmp target's `biguint/format.rs` impls these to control how `{}` and `{:?}` format `BigUInt` values; composes today's shape with the format-string DSL" moves, future "conversion traits — `From<T>` and `TryFrom<T>` and the symmetric `Into<T>`/`TryInto<T>`; the rmp target's `biguint/convert.rs` impls `From<u64>`, `From<u32>`, etc. on `BigUInt`; canonical shapes for the trait arc" moves, future "marker traits — `Copy`, `Clone`, `Send`, `Sync`, `Sized`; declared with empty trait bodies and impl bodies, and signal capability rather than provide methods; lesson 102 already named `Copy` in its evidence; today's `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` empty-body shape is the same syntactic skeleton" moves, future "`#[derive(Clone)]`, `#[derive(Debug)]`, `#[derive(PartialEq)]`, `#[derive(Eq)]`, etc. — derive macros that expand at compile time to `impl Trait for Type { ... }` blocks; lesson 095 named these as deferred and today's installation of the trait-impl shape lets the derive expansion finally be readable; the rmp target's `#[derive(Clone)]` on `BigUInt` is one canonical use" moves, future "the orphan rule and trait coherence — the rule that an `impl Trait for Type` is allowed only if either the trait or the type is local to the current crate; Reference `items/implementations.md:178-203` covers the formal rules; named in today's *What To Ignore For Now* and important for understanding why some impls are forbidden" moves, future "the rmp target's first readable trait impl — `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` in `src/biguint/cmp.rs:10`; exactly today's `impl Trait for Type { ... }` header with an empty body; the path through the trait arc starts here" moves, future "the rmp target's partially-readable next trait impl — `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt { fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool { self.limbs == other.limbs } }` in `src/biguint/cmp.rs:4-8`; today's outer shape is readable, the `<BigUInt>` generic trait parameter is the next deferred mechanic" moves, future "the eventual capstone — `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` in `src/biguint/add.rs`; multi-axis composite end-state of the trait arc, composing today's shape with generic trait parameters, associated types, references in both the `for` slot and the method-receiver slot, and a non-`&self` consuming receiver" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch10-02-traits.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md

```text
id: 112-trait-method-extra-parameter
move: extend lesson 111's trait method signature with one ordinary parameter beyond `&self` — the parameter-list shape `(&self, name: T)` reusing lesson 008's `(p1: T1, p2: T2)` grammar inside both the trait declaration's `;`-bodied signature and the matching impl method body — and center the *contract-matching rule* that the impl's signature must reproduce the trait's signature exactly (Reference `output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0053.md` lines 4-5 verbatim "The parameters of any trait method must match between a trait implementation and the trait definition."); concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } trait Scale { fn scaled(&self, factor: u32) -> u32; } impl Scale for Counter { fn scaled(&self, factor: u32) -> u32 { self.count * factor } } fn main() { let c = Counter { count: 7 }; println!("scaled = {}", c.scaled(6)); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints `scaled = 42` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe `mismatch.rs` keeps the impl `fn scaled(&self, factor: u32) -> u32 { self.count * factor }` and changes the trait declaration to `fn scaled(&self, factor: u64) -> u32;` (single character diff `u32` → `u64` in the *trait*, body unchanged so `self.count * factor` still typechecks as `u32 * u32` with no cast — the trait-signature mismatch is the only diagnostic and no `as`-cast prerequisite is required) and fires `error[E0053]: method \`scaled\` has an incompatible type for trait` with `--> mismatch.rs:10:30`, three carets under the impl's `u32` parameter type, the inline label `expected \`u64\`, found \`u32\``, a `note: type in trait` block with secondary `--> mismatch.rs:6:30` line and caret directly under the trait declaration's `u64`, a `= note: expected signature \`fn(&Counter, u64) -> _\` / found signature \`fn(&Counter, u32) -> _\`` summary, a `help: change the parameter type to match the trait` block proposing the exact diff to bring the impl back into conformance, and the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0053` trailer; the symmetric witness probe `mismatch_cast.rs` (footnote-style only, not centered) swaps the diff direction (trait says `factor: u32`, impl says `factor: u64` with `(factor as u32)` inside the body to keep multiplication well-typed — the cast itself uses an `as` expression that is *not* a centered prerequisite, so this probe is appendix-only) and fires the *same* E0053 with the same `note: type in trait` shape — only the `expected`/`found` labels swap — empirical witness that the diagnostic always treats the trait declaration as the contract regardless of which side is edited; the auxiliary probe `no_arg.rs` (call site `c.scaled()` with zero arguments instead of one) fires `error[E0061]: this method takes 1 argument but 0 arguments were supplied` with caret at the call site, the inline label `argument #1 of type \`u32\` is missing`, and a `note: method defined here` block at `--> no_arg.rs:6:8` underlining the trait's `fn scaled` token plus the `(......)` parameter list — the same E0061 shape lesson 008 installed for free functions, now witnessed on a trait method; the Check Yourself (c) ground probe `check_yourself_c.rs` (changes the *trait declaration*'s `addend: u32` to `addend: i32` while leaving the impl unchanged so `self.n + addend` still typechecks as `u32 + u32`) fires a single `error[E0053]: method \`plus\` has an incompatible type for trait` with caret on the impl's `addend: u32` and `note: type in trait` pointing at the trait declaration's new `addend: i32` — empirical witness that the single-E-code answer in Check Yourself (c) is E0053 with no auxiliary type-mismatch diagnostics on the body's `+`
main_concept: a trait method's parameter list extends naturally past `&self` using the same `(p1: T1, p2: T2)` parameter-list grammar lesson 008 installed for free functions — Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md` lines 73-84 show `trait Seq<T> { fn elt_at(&self, n: u32) -> T; ... }` whose `(&self, n: u32) -> T` shape is the exact same parameter pattern today's `Scale` trait uses with a primitive return — and the impl's signature must match the trait's *exactly*; mismatch fires `error[E0053]` whose `note: type in trait` block points at the trait declaration as the source of truth. The audience crosses from "a trait method has `&self` and a return type; the impl writes a body" (lesson 111) to "a trait method's parameter list is `(&self, p1: T1, p2: T2, ...)` — same grammar as lesson 008's free-function parameter list, slotted in after `&self` — and the impl must reproduce that parameter list exactly: parameter *names* may differ between trait and impl, but parameter *types* must match. On mismatch, rustc fires E0053 with the impl flagged at the caret site and the trait flagged in the `note: type in trait` block." Two coupled framings under one centered move. (1) *Parameter-list extension*: the trait body's signature shape gains an extra parameter slot. Reference `items/traits.md:73-80` lists `fn elt_at(&self, n: u32) -> T;` as a method on a trait — corpus warrant that trait methods syntactically admit parameters beyond `&self`. The `name: Type` shape after the comma is the same shape lesson 008 installed in `fn area(width: u32, height: u32) -> u32`; the trait declaration just slots that grammar in after `&self`. The impl method body uses the parameter via the standard binding rule — `factor` is in scope inside the body, just like a free-function parameter. (2) *Contract-matching rule*: the impl signature must match the trait signature exactly. Reference `error_codes/E0053.md` lines 4-5 verbatim "The parameters of any trait method must match between a trait implementation and the trait definition." The diagnostic E0053 is invariant under which side is edited — the centered Probe 2 puts the diff on the trait (so the body still typechecks with no cast), and the symmetric-witness Probe 3 puts the diff on the impl (with a cast inside the body so the body still typechecks); both fire the same E0053 with the same `note: type in trait` block pointing at the trait declaration. The "expected"/"found" labels swap with the diff direction, but the structural framing (trait = contract, impl = candidate) is fixed. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic extension to lesson 111 — one ordinary parameter beyond `&self`, primitive type only. Explicitly defer reference parameters in non-receiver slots (`fn eq(&self, other: &Counter) -> bool` is the rmp `cmp.rs:5` pattern; passing `&Type` as a non-receiver argument is its own mechanic, blocked downstream); multiple extra parameters (the rule extends naturally — each parameter must match — but is not centered today; future move via something like `fn fma(&self, a: u32, b: u32) -> u32`); other receivers in trait methods (`&mut self`, `self` by value — lesson 101/102 compose into trait methods identically); default method bodies (`fn method(&self, p: T) -> T { ... }` curlies-not-`;` inside the trait acts as a default the impl may override; deferred from 111); multiple types implementing one trait (the *point* of trait machinery, still deferred from 111); generic trait parameters `trait Add<Rhs> { ... }` (blocked on the generics arc); associated types `type Output = ...;` (blocked on generics); trait bounds `fn f<T: Scale>(t: T)` (blocked on generics); operator traits from `std::ops` (compose today's shape with the deferred mechanics above); `#[derive(...)]` macros, lifetime parameters, and the orphan rule (all named in 111 and unchanged today). This unlocks two future moves on the trait arc: (a) trait methods with reference parameters in non-receiver slots — the next mechanic toward reading rmp's `cmp.rs:5` `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool` end-to-end and toward the eventual `Add` capstone in `add.rs`; (b) multi-type dispatch (one trait, two structs implementing it) — the canonical demonstration that the *point* of trait machinery is shared interfaces across types, set aside in 111 with no new mechanic.
depends_on: 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing — installs the trait declaration with one method signature whose body is replaced by `;`, the `impl Trait for Type { ... }` block whose method body fills in what the trait declared, the dot-call shape resolving to a trait method, and the high-level rule that the impl signature matches the trait signature; today extends 111 with the smallest atomic move — one ordinary parameter beyond `&self`, primitive type — and centers the contract-matching rule that 111 only stated in passing; the working probe is structurally identical to 111's working probe with the parameter list extended), 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs the `fn name(p1: T1, p2: T2) -> R { ... }` definition shape and the parameter-list grammar `(p1: T1, p2: T2)` with comma-separated `name: Type` slots; today reuses the same parameter-list grammar inside both a trait declaration's signature `fn scaled(&self, factor: u32) -> u32;` and the matching impl method body, with `&self` as a fixed first slot; lesson 008 also installed E0061 for arity mismatches on free function calls, and Probe 4's auxiliary E0061 transcript on a trait method confirms the same arity-checking machinery applies), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs the dot-call shape `value.method(args)` with arguments; today's `c.scaled(6)` is exactly that shape with one argument passed to a trait method; the call site is unchanged from 040, only the resolution target differs — the same dot-call form that resolved to inherent methods in lesson 100 and to no-argument trait methods in lesson 111 now resolves to a trait method with one argument), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — installs `struct Name { field: Type, }`, struct expression `Name { field: value }`, and field access `instance.field`; today reuses all three in the working probe — `Counter` is the trait-impl target, `Counter { count: 7 }` is the struct literal in `fn main`, and `self.count` is field access on the `&self` receiver inside the impl method body), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (cited — installs the `&self` receiver and the rule that `&self` is what makes the dot call resolve, plus the inherent-vs-trait impl distinction that 111 fully developed; today reuses `&self` as the fixed first slot in the trait method's parameter list and in the impl method body), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let c = Counter { count: 7 };` in `fn main`), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `*` (multiplication) is the operator used in the working probe's method body `self.count * factor`; lesson 009 is the move that installed `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` on integers, and today reuses the `*` operator unchanged on a `u32` value), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("scaled = {}", c.scaled(6))` with one `{}` slot consumed by the trait-method call's return value), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: u32` annotations in `count: u32`, `factor: u32`, and the `-> u32` return-type annotation; the type annotation slot is unchanged from lesson 019), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` as the named member of the integer family used in today's struct field type, parameter type, and method return type; `u64` as another named member used in the contrast probe to create the type mismatch that fires E0053), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0053 transcript and Probe 4's E0061 transcript; the multi-`-->` shape — caret at impl-site plus secondary `-->` line at trait declaration in the `note: type in trait` block — is the same shape lessons 096, 100, 109, 111 already installed for E0603, E0599, E0603, E0599 respectively; today extends the diagnostic-pattern catalog with E0053), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "trait methods with reference parameters in non-receiver slots — `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool` is the canonical pattern in rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:4-8`; passing `&Type` as a non-receiver argument is the next mechanic on the path toward fully reading rmp's `PartialEq` impl and the eventual `Add` capstone in `src/biguint/add.rs`" moves, future "multi-type dispatch — one trait, two (or more) structs implementing it; the canonical demonstration that the *point* of trait machinery is shared interfaces across types; set aside in 111 with no new mechanic and remains the natural next centered teaching of why traits exist as a separate feature from inherent impls" moves, future "default method bodies in trait declarations — `fn method(&self, p: T) -> T { ... }` (curlies, not `;`) inside a trait acts as a default body the impl may override; Reference `items/traits.md:43-58` covers the rule and the example trait `Example` shows both forms side-by-side; a small natural follow-on, composes today's no-default form with a default-providing form" moves, future "trait method signatures with multiple extra parameters — `fn fma(&self, a: u32, b: u32) -> u32`; the contract-matching rule extends naturally (each parameter must match) but the multi-parameter shape is its own pedagogical witness" moves, future "trait method receivers other than `&self` — `&mut self` (composes lesson 101 with today), `self` by value (composes lesson 102 with today); the rmp target's `impl AddAssign<&BigUInt> for BigUInt { fn add_assign(&mut self, rhs: &BigUInt) { ... } }` is the canonical `&mut self`-in-trait-method shape, and the eventual `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { ... fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` capstone uses `self` by value" moves, future "generic trait parameters `trait Add<Rhs> { ... }` — Reference `items/traits.md:73-84` shows the formal grammar with `GenericParams?` after the `IDENTIFIER`; type parameters on a trait declaration; blocked on the generics arc; once installed, partially unlocks rmp's `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt { ... }` and the operator-trait family" moves, future "associated types `type Output = ...;` inside a trait or impl — `type Output;` declares an associated type and `type Output = SomeType;` inside the impl resolves it; the rmp target's `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; ... }` is the canonical pattern" moves, future "trait bounds `fn f<T: Scale>(t: T)` — generic functions whose parameter types are constrained to implement a trait; this is the central machinery that gives traits their pay-off (one trait, many types, generic functions abstracting over all of them); blocked on the generics arc" moves, future "operator traits from `std::ops` — `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `Rem`, `Shl`, `Shr`, `AddAssign`, `SubAssign`, `MulAssign`, `Neg`, `Not`; each is a real trait whose impls compose today's `impl Trait for Type { ... fn method(&self, ...) }` shape with deferred generic parameters, associated types, and reference parameters; the rmp target's arithmetic methods are organized around these traits and the eventual capstone `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` is the multi-axis composite end-state of the trait arc" moves, future "the rmp capstone trait impl in `src/biguint/add.rs` — `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }`; multi-axis composite, composes today's shape with generic trait parameters, associated types, references in both the `for` slot and the method-receiver slot, a non-`&self` consuming receiver, and the carry-propagation algorithm; the eventual end-state of the trait arc" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md

```text
id: 113-reference-parameter-in-trait-method
move: extend lesson 112's trait-method parameter list with one *reference* type — `fn combine(&self, other: &Counter) -> u32;` in the trait declaration and the matching `fn combine(&self, other: &Counter) -> u32 { self.count + other.count }` in the impl method body — and install the matching call-site shape `a.combine(&b)` where the argument expression begins with `&`; center the rule that `&value` *borrows* (the caller still owns `value` after the call) by exercising two call sites on the same `&b` and reading `b.count` directly afterwards as empirical witness; concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } trait Combine { fn combine(&self, other: &Counter) -> u32; } impl Combine for Counter { fn combine(&self, other: &Counter) -> u32 { self.count + other.count } } fn main() { let a = Counter { count: 7 }; let b = Counter { count: 35 }; let first = a.combine(&b); let second = a.combine(&b); println!("first  = {}", first); println!("second = {}", second); println!("b.count still = {}", b.count); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `first  = 42 / second = 42 / b.count still = 35` and exits 0 — the second call returning the same value as the first plus the final field read are the empirical witness that `&b` did not consume `b`; the centered contrast probe `no_amp.rs` (same source modulo dropping the `&` at the call site `a.combine(b)` and removing the second call to keep the only diagnostic on the type mismatch) fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with `--> no_amp.rs:18:27`, single caret directly under the bare `b`, the inline label `expected \`&Counter\`, found \`Counter\``, a secondary annotation under `a.combine` reading `arguments to this method are incorrect`, a `note: method defined here` block at `--> no_amp.rs:6:8` pointing at the trait declaration with carets under `fn combine` and dashes under the parameter list, a `help: consider borrowing here` block proposing the exact diff `a.combine(b)` → `a.combine(&b)` with a single `+` marker under the inserted `&`, and the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0308` trailer; the auxiliary E0382 probe `consumed.rs` (changes both trait and impl signatures from `other: &Counter` to `other: Counter` and the call sites from `a.combine(&b)` to `a.combine(b)`) fires `error[E0382]: use of moved value: \`b\`` on the second call site (`--> consumed.rs:19:28`), with `move occurs because \`b\` has type \`Counter\`, which does not implement the \`Copy\` trait` annotating the binding, `value moved here` annotating the first call's argument, `value used here after move` with caret on the second call's argument, and a `note: consider changing this parameter type in method \`combine\` to borrow instead if owning the value isn't necessary` pointing at the trait declaration's `Counter` parameter type with the inline label `this parameter takes ownership of the value` — empirical witness that the `&` in the parameter type and the `&` at the call site are jointly load-bearing for the "still owns" property and that without them rustc itself frames the choice as ownership-passing vs borrowing
main_concept: `&Type` is a *reference type* (Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/types/pointer.md:18` `ReferenceType → & Lifetime? mut? TypeNoBounds`) and it can sit in *any* parameter slot of any function or method — the `&self` receiver of lessons 100/111/112 was the special-cased instance all along; the matching call-site shape is the expression `&value` which the Book defines (`output/docs/rust/book/ch04-02-references-and-borrowing.md:66-68` verbatim "The `&s1` syntax lets us create a reference that *refers* to the value of `s1` but does not own it. Because the reference does not own it, the value it points to will not be dropped when the reference stops being used.") as creating a reference to `value` without owning it; the action of creating a reference is called *borrowing* (Book lines 95-97 verbatim); without `&` at the call site, rustc fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` (`output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md:29-32`) with the inline label `expected `&Counter`, found `Counter`` and a `help: consider borrowing here` block proposing the `&` insertion. The audience crosses from "`&self` is what makes the dot call resolve; trait methods can take ordinary parameters like `factor: u32`" (lesson 112) to "`&Type` is a reference type that can appear in any parameter slot, and the matching call-site argument is `&value`; a `&value` argument *borrows* — the caller still owns `value` after the call, witnessed by passing the same `&b` twice and reading `b.count` afterwards." Two coupled framings under one centered move. (1) *`&Type` as a parameter type*: the trait method's parameter list now has `&Counter` filling the type position. The grammar production `& Lifetime? mut? TypeNoBounds` is instantiated with no `Lifetime`, no `mut`, and `TypeNoBounds = Counter`. The impl method body reads fields through the reference via the same auto-deref-on-field-access machinery lesson 100 already installed for `self.field` on `&self`. (2) *`&value` at the call site*: the argument expression `&b` creates a shared reference to `b`. Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/types/pointer.md:30` verbatim "any number of shared references to a value may exist" — corpus warrant for the working probe creating two separate `&b` references (one per call) without conflict. The "still owns" property (Book lines 66-68) is empirically witnessed by the two-call pattern plus the final `b.count` field read. The contrast probe drops the `&` at the call site only; with the trait/impl signatures unchanged, the bare `b` (type `Counter`) doesn't match the parameter type (`&Counter`) and rustc fires E0308 with the canonical type-mismatch diagnostic. The auxiliary probe drops `&` from *both* the parameter type and the call site, which compiles past type-check but then fires E0382 *use of moved value* on the second call — empirical witness that the `&` and `&` together are what make repeat-passing legal. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic move into the reference-arc — one `&Type` parameter on a trait method, one `&value` call site, the witness that the caller still owns the value. The named-type form `&Counter` is centered because it is the directly readable shape used in rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:5` `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool`. Explicitly defer `&Self` as an alias for `&Counter` inside an impl (the same file `cmp.rs` uses both forms — `other: &BigUInt` on line 5 and `other: &Self` on lines 13 and 19 — naming today's stylistic choice as a future move not centered here); `&mut Type` parameters in non-receiver slots (composes lesson 101's `&mut self` mechanic into the parameter slot, distinct mechanic); the full borrowing rules — borrow checker, shared-vs-mutable exclusivity rule (Reference `pointer.md` mentions but does not formalize today), lifetime parameters like `&'a Counter` and the `'static` lifetime (deferred wholesale); multiple reference parameters in one signature (`fn cmp(&self, a: &Counter, b: &Counter)` — the rule extends naturally but is not centered today); reference-of-reference `& &Counter` (deferred); `&Type` as a return type `fn first(&self) -> &Counter` (distinct mechanic — returning a borrow has its own rules and triggers lifetime questions); trait method receivers other than `&self` (`&mut self` from 101, `self` by value from 102 — compose into trait methods identically and not exercised today); multi-type dispatch, default method bodies, generic trait parameters, associated types, operator traits — all named in 111/112 and unchanged today. This unlocks reading rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:5` `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool` end-to-end modulo the deferred `<BigUInt>` generic trait parameter on the `impl PartialEq<BigUInt>` header; the next mechanic is generic trait parameters, after which the trait arc drives toward associated types, operator traits, and the eventual `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` capstone in `src/biguint/add.rs`.
depends_on: 112-trait-method-extra-parameter (load-bearing — installs the trait method signature `fn name(&self, p: T) -> R;` with one parameter beyond `&self`, the matching impl method body, and the contract-matching rule that the impl signature must reproduce the trait signature exactly; today extends 112 with the parameter type changed from a primitive (`u32`) to a reference (`&Counter`) — the parameter list grammar is unchanged, only the type slot is filled with `&Type` instead of a bare type — and the contract-matching rule remains load-bearing for the contrast probe's `note: method defined here` block pointing at the trait declaration), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `&self` as the borrowing receiver shape, `self.field` as field access through the `&self` reference, and `Self` as the type alias for the impl-target type inside the impl block; today's centered move generalizes the `&Type` shape from the receiver-only case to any parameter slot, and the impl method body's `other.count` reuses the exact same auto-deref-on-field-access machinery 100 installed for `self.count`; the lesson body's framing that `&self` was the special-cased instance and today's `&Counter` is the general form is directly grounded in 100's note that `&self` is shorthand for `self: &Self`; lesson 100's *What To Ignore For Now* deferred "auto-deref on method calls — the rule that lets `c.current()` match `&self` without writing `&c`" — today's contrast probe shows that this deref does *not* extend to non-receiver parameter slots: `a.combine(b)` with `b: Counter` does *not* coerce to `&Counter`, and rustc fires E0308 instead), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — installs `struct Name { field: Type, }`, struct expression `Name { field: value }`, and field access `instance.field`; today's working probe builds two `Counter` values, the impl method body reads `self.count` and `other.count`, and the final `println!` reads `b.count` directly after the calls — the field access on `b` is what witnesses that `b` is still owned and accessible after both `&b` borrows ended), 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs the parameter-list grammar `(p1: T1, p2: T2)` with comma-separated `name: Type` slots; today reuses the same grammar with `&Counter` filling the type position in the second slot, after `&self`; the parameter-list shape is unchanged from 008/112), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs the dot-call shape `value.method(args)`; today's `a.combine(&b)` is exactly that shape with the argument expression now beginning with `&`; the call-site grammar is unchanged, only the argument expression's leading token differs), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let a = Counter { count: 7 };`, `let b = Counter { count: 35 };`, `let first = a.combine(&b);`, `let second = a.combine(&b);` in `fn main`), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `+` (addition) is the operator used in the working probe's method body `self.count + other.count`; lesson 009 is the move that installed `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` on integers, and today reuses the `+` operator unchanged on `u32` values), 011-println-positional-args (cited — three `println!` lines in the working probe each consume one `{}` slot for `first`, `second`, and `b.count`), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: u32` annotations in `count: u32` and the `-> u32` return-type annotation; the type annotation slot is unchanged from lesson 019), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` as the named member of the integer family used in today's struct field type and method return type), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0308 transcript and Probe 5's E0308 transcript; the multi-`-->` shape — caret at call site plus secondary `-->` line at trait declaration in the `note: method defined here` block — is the same shape lessons 096, 100, 109, 111, 112 already installed for E0603, E0599, E0603, E0599, E0053 respectively; today extends the diagnostic-pattern catalog with E0308), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), 102-self-by-value-receiver (cited — installs `self` by value as the consuming-receiver shape, named in today's *Mental Model Delta* "Before" and the auxiliary E0382 probe in the appendix as the contrast-of-contrasts that grounds the deferral — without `&` in the parameter type, the call consumes; with `&`, the call borrows), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "multi-type dispatch — one trait, two (or more) structs implementing it; the canonical demonstration that the *point* of trait machinery is shared interfaces across types; set aside in 111 with no new mechanic and remains the natural next centered teaching of why traits exist as a separate feature from inherent impls" moves, future "trait methods with `&mut Type` parameters in non-receiver slots — composes lesson 101's `&mut self` machinery into a non-receiver parameter slot; the rmp target's `impl AddAssign<&BigUInt> for BigUInt { fn add_assign(&mut self, rhs: &BigUInt) { ... } }` combines `&mut self` and `&Type` non-receiver parameters; today's `&Type` non-receiver shape is one half of this composition" moves, future "generic trait parameters `trait Add<Rhs> { ... }` — Reference `items/traits.md:73-84` shows the formal grammar with `GenericParams?` after the `IDENTIFIER`; type parameters on a trait declaration; the next mechanic toward reading rmp's `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt { ... }` end-to-end and toward the eventual `Add` capstone" moves, future "associated types `type Output = ...;` inside a trait or impl — the rmp target's `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; ... }` is the canonical pattern; declares an associated type in the trait and resolves it in the impl" moves, future "operator traits and the rmp Add capstone — the eventual `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` in `src/biguint/add.rs`; multi-axis composite end-state of the trait arc, composing today's `&Type` non-receiver parameter with deferred generic trait parameters, associated types, references in the `for` slot of the impl header, and a non-`&self` consuming receiver" moves, future "trait method signatures with multiple reference parameters — `fn cmp(&self, a: &Counter, b: &Counter)`; the contract-matching rule extends naturally (each parameter must match) but the multi-reference shape is its own pedagogical witness; the rmp target uses this in cmp.rs's chained comparison logic" moves, future "trait method signatures with `&Type` as a *return* type — `fn first(&self) -> &Counter`; distinct mechanic from today's parameter-position case because returning a borrow triggers lifetime-elision rules (Reference `lifetime-elision.md` covers the simple cases) and the explicit `&'a` lifetime forms; deferred from today's What To Ignore For Now" moves, future "the borrow-checker rule that shared and mutable references cannot coexist on the same value — the central rule that gives borrowing its safety guarantee; deferred wholesale today, name-deferred in What To Ignore For Now" moves, future "lifetime parameters `&'a Counter` and the `'static` lifetime — explicit lifetimes on reference types; deferred wholesale today, name-deferred in What To Ignore For Now" moves, future "the rmp `cmp.rs:5` line `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool` — directly readable end-to-end after today's lesson modulo the deferred `<BigUInt>` generic trait parameter on the `impl PartialEq<BigUInt>` header; the load-bearing readability unlock" moves, future "reading rmp's `cmp.rs` `&Self` form — `fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> ...` and `fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> ...`; the same reference parameter type spelled with `Self` instead of the named type; readable today modulo the alias-equivalence note in *What To Ignore For Now* but not centered" moves

```text
id: 114-generic-trait-parameter
move: extend lesson 113's trait declaration with one *type parameter* between angle brackets after the trait name — `trait AddRhs<RHS> { fn add(&self, rhs: RHS) -> u32; }` — and substitute that placeholder with a concrete type at the impl header — `impl AddRhs<u32> for Counter { fn add(&self, rhs: u32) -> u32 { self.count + rhs } }` — installing three coupled new tokens (`<RHS>` in the trait header, `RHS` as a type-position placeholder inside the trait body, `<u32>` in the impl header) under one centered move; concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } trait AddRhs<RHS> { fn add(&self, rhs: RHS) -> u32; } impl AddRhs<u32> for Counter { fn add(&self, rhs: u32) -> u32 { self.count + rhs } } fn main() { let c = Counter { count: 7 }; let total = c.add(35); println!("total = {}", total); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `total = 42` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe `no_arg.rs` (same source modulo dropping the `<u32>` from the impl header — `impl AddRhs for Counter` instead of `impl AddRhs<u32> for Counter`, trait declaration unchanged) fires `error[E0107]: missing generics for trait \`AddRhs\`` with `--> no_arg.rs:9:6`, single caret span under `AddRhs` at the impl header, the inline label `expected 1 generic argument`, a `note: trait defined here, with 1 generic parameter: \`RHS\`` block at `--> no_arg.rs:5:7` pointing back at the trait declaration with carets under `AddRhs` and dashes under `RHS`, a `help: add missing generic argument` block proposing the exact diff `impl AddRhs for Counter` → `impl AddRhs<RHS> for Counter` with five `+` markers under the inserted `<RHS>`, and the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0107` trailer; the auxiliary E0308 probe `wrong_type.rs` (changes only the call site from `c.add(35)` to `c.add(35u64)`) fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with caret under `35u64`, inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`u64\``, and a `note: method defined here` block pointing at the trait declaration line `fn add(&self, rhs: RHS) -> u32;` — empirical witness that the impl's chosen concrete type (`u32`) flows to the call site even though the trait declaration writes `RHS`
main_concept: a trait declaration may carry a *type parameter* between angle brackets after the trait name (Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md:73-84` verbatim "Type parameters can be specified for a trait to make it generic. These appear after the trait name." plus the `Seq<T>` example showing `T` used as a type inside the trait body in `fn elt_at(&self, n: u32) -> T;`); the angle-bracket grammar is `< ( GenericParam )? >` (Reference `items/generics.md:8-12, 24` plus the example `trait A<U> {}`) where `GenericParam → IDENTIFIER ( : TypeParamBounds? )? ( = Type )?` with the bounds and default-type clauses deferred today; inside the trait body the parameter name (`RHS`, `T`, ...) stands in any type position; the impl header substitutes the placeholder with a concrete type (`impl AddRhs<u32> for Counter`, Reference `items/implementations.md:18-24` `TraitImpl → unsafe? impl GenericParams? !? TypePath for Type ...` where the `TypePath` slot carries the `<ConcreteType>` after the trait name); after substitution the impl method's signature must match the trait method's signature *with the concrete type in place of the placeholder* — the contract-matching rule lesson 112 installed extends to the post-substitution form. Three coupled new tokens under one centered move. (1) *`<RHS>` in the trait header*: declares one type parameter, scoped to the trait body. (2) *`RHS` in the body*: a name in scope inside the trait declaration that may sit in any type position; today's `rhs: RHS` parameter slot exercises one such position. (3) *`<u32>` in the impl header*: substitutes the trait's type parameter with a concrete type for this particular impl block. The same trait can be impl'd for the same target type with *different* concrete substitutions (`std/ops/trait.Add.md:17-21` verbatim "`std::time::SystemTime` implements `Add<Duration>`, which permits operations of the form `SystemTime = SystemTime + Duration`"); that dispatch power is *the* reason traits are generic. Today's probe demonstrates only one substitution. The audience crosses from "every type inside a trait body is a specific named type; the impl reproduces those types verbatim" (lessons 111-113) to "a trait declaration may carry a type parameter between angle brackets that the impl chooses a concrete type for; the impl method body uses the concrete type, by the contract-matching rule applied to the post-substitution signature." Connection to earlier lessons: `Vec<T>` (lesson 107) and `Option<T>` (the prelude) are pre-existing *uses* of the angle-bracket shape on types declared in std (Book `ch10-00-generics.md:13-15` verbatim "we already used generics in Chapter 6 with `Option<T>`, in Chapter 8 with `Vec<T>` and `HashMap<K, V>`, and in Chapter 9 with `Result<T, E>`. In this chapter, you'll explore how to define your own"); today is the *declaration* side of the same syntax, applied to a trait. The contrast probe drops the `<u32>` from the impl header only; the trait declaration is unchanged and demands a one-argument substitution; rustc fires E0107 with `expected 1 generic argument` and points back at the trait declaration as the contract. The auxiliary probe demonstrates that the impl's chosen concrete type propagates to the call site (passing `35u64` to a method expecting `u32` after substitution fires E0308). Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic move into the generic-trait arc — one type parameter, one impl, one substitution. The named-form `<RHS>` (uppercase identifier) is the ordinary convention. Explicitly defer multi-impl with different RHS substitutions (the dispatch power of generic traits, named today not probed); multiple type parameters on one trait `trait T<A, B>` (same grammar slot extended); generic functions `fn f<T>(t: T)` (distinct mechanic, same `<T>` slot); generic struct types `struct S<T>` (distinct mechanic, same `<T>` slot); trait bounds `trait T<U: SomeBound>` and `where` clauses (constraints on substitutable types, blocked wholesale); default type parameters `trait T<U = Self>` (what `std::ops::Add` actually uses, named-deferred); associated types `type Output = ...;` (next mechanic on the path to rmp's Add); lifetime parameters `trait T<'a>` (deferred wholesale); the orphan rule for generic impls; `Self` in the type-parameter slot; trait method receivers other than `&self` (compose into generic traits identically); operator traits from `std::ops` (compose today's shape with default type parameters and associated types). This unlocks reading rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:4` `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` outer shape end-to-end (the `<BigUInt>` slot is exactly today's `<u32>` slot with a different concrete type; the rest is the lesson 113 method shape with `&BigUInt` parameter; the body's `self.limbs == other.limbs` uses `Vec` equality which composes onto today's machinery as a future move); and partially unlocks rmp's `src/biguint/add.rs:112-115` `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self: Self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` outer shape (the `<&BigUInt>` slot maps to today's mechanic with a reference type as the concrete substitution; the `for &BigUInt` impl-on-reference target type is a separate deferred mechanic; the `type Output = BigUInt` is an associated type, deferred; the `self: Self` consuming receiver is lesson 102).
depends_on: 113-reference-parameter-in-trait-method (load-bearing — installs the trait method shape with a non-receiver parameter slot that today fills with a *type variable* `RHS` instead of a concrete type; the parameter-list grammar `(&self, p: T)` is unchanged from 113, only the content of the type slot differs — concrete type from a primitive or reference in 113 vs. a name in scope from the trait's generic-parameter list today; the lesson body's framing "lessons 111-113 wrote concrete trait declarations: every type inside the trait body was specific" is a one-line summary of the trait arc up through 113), 112-trait-method-extra-parameter (load-bearing — installs the trait method signature with one parameter beyond `&self` and the contract-matching rule that the impl signature must reproduce the trait signature exactly; today extends the contract-matching rule to the post-substitution form: the impl method's signature must match the trait method's signature *after* the impl header's chosen concrete type is substituted for the trait's type parameter; today's working probe witnesses this with `fn add(&self, rhs: u32) -> u32` matching `fn add(&self, rhs: RHS) -> u32` after `RHS` becomes `u32`), 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing — installs the `trait Name { fn method(&self) -> T; }` declaration block and the `impl Trait for Type { ... }` block; today extends both headers with angle-bracket type parameters in the same syntactic positions 111 installed: between `trait` and `{` for the declaration and between `impl` and `for` for the impl), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — installs `struct Name { field: Type, }`, struct expression `Name { field: value }`, and field access `instance.field`; today reuses all three in the working probe — `Counter` is the trait-impl target, `Counter { count: 7 }` is the struct literal in `fn main`, and `self.count` is field access on the `&self` receiver inside the impl method body), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `&self` as the borrowing receiver and the inherent-vs-trait impl distinction; today reuses `&self` as the fixed first slot of the trait method's parameter list), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs the dot-call shape `value.method(arg)`; today's `c.add(35)` is exactly that shape, and the trait's genericity is invisible at the call site once the impl has chosen a concrete type), 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs the `(p1: T1, p2: T2)` parameter-list grammar; today's `(&self, rhs: RHS)` slots `RHS` (a name in scope from the trait header) into the type position; the parameter-list grammar itself is unchanged), 107-vec-basics (cited — installs the `Vec<u64>` shape, the learner's first encounter with the angle-bracket `<...>` syntax on a *type used*; the lesson body's connection paragraph "you have seen `Vec<u64>` and `Option<T>`; today shows the declaration side of the same syntax" is grounded here; the Book `ch10-00-generics.md:13-15` quote names this connection explicitly), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let c = Counter { count: 7 };` and `let total = c.add(35);` in `fn main`), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `+` (addition) is the operator used in the working probe's method body `self.count + rhs`; lesson 009 is the move that installed `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` on integers; today reuses `+` unchanged on `u32` values), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("total = {}", total)` consumes one `{}` slot for `total`), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: u32` annotations in `count: u32` and `rhs: u32`, and the `-> u32` return-type annotation; the type annotation slot is unchanged from lesson 019), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` as the named member of the integer family used in today's struct field type, impl method parameter type, and method return type; `u64` as another named member used in the auxiliary E0308 probe), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0107 transcript; the multi-`-->` shape — caret at impl header plus secondary `-->` line at trait declaration in the `note: trait defined here` block — is the same shape lessons 096, 100, 109, 111, 112, 113 already installed for E0603, E0599, E0603, E0599, E0053, E0308 respectively; today extends the diagnostic-pattern catalog with E0107), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)

the std example `Add<Duration>` for `SystemTime` is the corpus warrant; named today, not probed" moves, future "associated types `type Output = ...;` inside a trait or impl — the next mechanic on the path to fully reading rmp's `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; ... }` capstone; declares an associated type in the trait declaration and resolves it in the impl block" moves, future "default type parameters `trait T<U = Self> { ... }` — what `std::ops::Add` actually writes (`pub trait Add<Rhs = Self> { ... }`); composes today's no-default form with a default-providing form; the `= Self` shape composes with the alias rule lesson 100 installed" moves, future "multiple type parameters on one trait — `trait Pair<A, B> { ... }`; the same angle-bracket slot extended with a comma-separated list; Reference `items/generics.md:10` `< ( GenericParam ( , GenericParam )* ,? )? >` is the formal grammar" moves, future "trait bounds `trait T<U: SomeBound> { ... }` and `where U: SomeBound` clauses — constraints on which concrete types may substitute the placeholder; central machinery for the pay-off of generic traits, blocked on this arc and on associated types" moves, future "generic functions `fn f<T>(t: T)` — distinct mechanic from today's trait-declaration case but uses the same `<T>` angle-bracket slot, just on a function header instead of a trait header; Reference `items/generics.md:46` `fn foo<'a, T>() {}` is the formal example" moves, future "generic struct types `struct S<T> { field: T }` — distinct mechanic from today's trait-declaration case but uses the same `<T>` angle-bracket slot, just on a struct header; Reference `items/generics.md:48` `struct Ref<'a, T> where T: 'a { r: &'a T }` is the formal example; once installed, the `Vec<T>` and `Option<T>` declarations in std become readable" moves, future "operator traits from `std::ops` and the rmp Add capstone — `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `Rem`, `Shl`, `Shr`, `AddAssign`, `SubAssign`, `MulAssign`, `Neg`, `Not`; each composes today's generic-trait shape with default type parameters (`<Rhs = Self>`), associated types (`type Output`), and various receiver shapes; the eventual rmp capstone `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self: Self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` is the multi-axis composite end-state of the trait arc" moves, future "lifetime parameters `trait T<'a> { ... }` and `&'a Counter` — explicit lifetimes on reference types in trait declarations; deferred wholesale" moves, future "the rmp `cmp.rs:4` line `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` — directly readable end-to-end after today's lesson except for the body's `Vec` equality `self.limbs == other.limbs` which composes onto today's machinery as a future move; the load-bearing readability unlock" moves, future "the rmp `add.rs:112-115` outer shape `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self: Self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` — partially readable after today: the `Add<&BigUInt>` shape is the same generic-trait + concrete-substitution mechanic today installed with a reference type as the concrete substitution; the `for &BigUInt` impl-on-reference target type is a separate deferred mechanic; the `type Output = BigUInt` is associated types (deferred); the `self: Self` consuming receiver is lesson 102 (already installed); the body's carry-propagation algorithm is its own pedagogical sequence" moves, future "impl-on-reference target types — `impl Trait for &Type { ... }` where the `for` slot of the impl header carries a reference type rather than a named type; required for the rmp Add capstone's `for &BigUInt`" moves
