```text
id: 115-trait-associated-type
move: extend lesson 114's trait declaration with an *associated type* — declare `type Output;` as a required item inside the trait body (semicolon-terminated, the same shape as `fn foo(&self) -> T;` for an associated function declaration with no default), reference the path `Self::Output` in any type position inside the trait body (today: the trait method's return type), and resolve the associated type with `type Output = u32;` inside the impl block; installing three coupled new tokens (`type Output;` in the trait body, `Self::Output` as a type-position path, `type Output = u32;` in the impl body) under one centered move; concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } trait Doubled { type Output; fn doubled(&self) -> Self::Output; } impl Doubled for Counter { type Output = u32; fn doubled(&self) -> u32 { self.count * 2 } } fn main() { let c = Counter { count: 21 }; println!("doubled = {}", c.doubled()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `doubled = 42` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe `no_type.rs` (same source modulo dropping the `type Output = u32;` line from the impl block — trait declaration unchanged, impl method body unchanged) fires `error[E0046]: not all trait items implemented, missing: \`Output\`` with `--> no_type.rs:10:1`, two `-->` blocks: one underlining the trait body's `type Output;` line at line 6 with the label `\`Output\` from trait` (the contract item) and one underlining the impl header `impl Doubled for Counter {` with the inline label `missing \`Output\` in implementation`, plus the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0046` trailer; the auxiliary E0053 probe `wrong_type.rs` (impl resolves `type Output = u64;` while the impl method's signature still writes `-> u32`) fires `error[E0053]: method \`doubled\` has an incompatible type for trait` with caret on the impl method's `-> u32`, the inline label `expected \`u64\`, found \`u32\``, a `note: type in trait` block pointing at the trait declaration's `Self::Output` token, and a `= note: expected signature \`fn(&Counter) -> u64\` / found signature \`fn(&Counter) -> u32\`` summary — empirical witness that after the impl resolves the associated type, `Self::Output` *is* the resolved concrete type for contract-matching purposes (the post-resolution form of lesson 112's contract-matching rule); the alternate-spelling probe `self_output.rs` (impl method writes `-> Self::Output` instead of `-> u32`) compiles silently and runs to `doubled = 42`, witness that both forms are legal once the impl has resolved `Output`
main_concept: a trait declaration may include associated *types* as required items alongside associated functions and constants (Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md:19-23` verbatim "A *trait* describes an abstract interface that types can implement. This interface consists of associated items, which come in three varieties: functions, types, constants" — today centers the *types* variety after lessons 111-114 exercised the *functions* variety); the associated-type declaration grammar inside the trait body is `type IDENTIFIER;` (Reference `items/associated-items.md:248-261` admits the simplest form `type Assoc;` with no `Bounds`, no `Params`, no `WhereBounds`); the trait body cannot give the associated type a default — Reference `items/traits.md:43-46` verbatim "Associated types must never define the type, the type may only be specified in an implementation" — so the impl is required to resolve via `type IDENTIFIER = ConcreteType;` (Reference `items/associated-items.md:281-290` admits the simplest form `type Assoc = Type;`); inside the trait body the associated type is referenced via the path `Self::IDENTIFIER` (Reference `items/associated-items.md:292-298` "If a type `Item` has an associated type `Assoc` from a trait `Trait`, then `<Item as Trait>::Assoc` is a type that is an alias of the type specified in the associated type definition" — today uses the `Self::Assoc` shorthand form), which can sit in any type position; today's working probe puts `Self::Output` in the return-type position of the trait method (matching the `Container` trait example at `items/associated-items.md:367-373` which uses `Self::E` in a method signature). After resolution, `Self::Output` is just a type alias for the concrete type the impl chose; the contract-matching rule lesson 112 installed extends post-resolution — the impl method's signature must match the trait method's signature with `Self::Output` replaced by the resolved concrete type. The impl method's return type can be written either as `Self::Output` (Probe 4) or as the resolved concrete type (Probe 1, working probe); both compile and produce identical behavior; today's lesson body uses the concrete-type form because rmp's `add.rs:115` does (`fn add(self: Self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt` rather than `-> Self::Output`). Three coupled new tokens under one centered move. (1) *`type Output;` in the trait body*: declares one required associated type, contract item the impl must fill. (2) *`Self::Output` in the trait body*: a path-position reference to the associated type, usable in any type position. (3) *`type Output = u32;` in the impl block*: resolves the associated type. The audience crosses from "a trait may carry a generic *parameter* between angle brackets that the impl substitutes at the impl header" (lesson 114) to "a trait may *also* carry an associated type — declared as a required item *inside* the trait body and resolved *inside* the impl body." The mental-model contrast with lesson 114 is the central pedagogical bridge: generic trait parameters are filled at the impl *header* by the impl-site author and may differ across impls of the same trait for the same target type (114's dispatch power); associated types are filled inside the impl *body* and are unique per (trait, target type) pair (Book `ch20-02-advanced-traits.md:91-105` verbatim spelling out this contrast: "when a trait has a generic parameter, it can be implemented for a type multiple times, changing the concrete types of the generic type parameters each time" / "With associated types, we don't need to annotate types, because we can't implement a trait on a type multiple times"). The contrast probe drops the `type Output = u32;` line from the impl block; rustc fires E0046 with `not all trait items implemented, missing: `Output`` and points at the trait declaration as the contract — empirical witness for the central rule that the impl is required to resolve every associated type the trait declared. The auxiliary E0053 probe demonstrates the post-resolution contract-matching extension: when `type Output = u64;` resolves but the impl method still writes `-> u32`, rustc fires the same E0053 lesson 112 installed, framing the expected type as `u64` (the resolved concrete) — empirical witness that `Self::Output` is just an alias for the concrete type after resolution. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic associated-type machinery — one associated type, no default in the trait, no bounds, used only in the return-type position of one trait method. The associated type is named `Output` matching std's convention (`std/ops/trait.Add.md:6-13` shows `pub trait Add<Rhs = Self> { type Output; fn add(self, rhs: Rhs) -> Self::Output; }` — same name `Output`, same `Self::Output` path-position reference). Explicitly defer default associated types `type Output = u64;` *inside the trait body* (Reference `items/traits.md:43` covers the rule, today exercises the no-default form); multiple associated types in one trait (`type A; type B;`); associated types with trait bounds (`type Output: Display;`, blocked on the trait-bounds arc); generic associated types / GATs (`type Item<'a>;`, blocked on lifetimes); using the associated type as a parameter type (`fn foo(&self, x: Self::Output)`, same path syntax in a different slot); cross-impl access via qualified path (`<Counter as Doubled>::Output`, the fully-qualified form Reference `items/associated-items.md:292-298` describes); default type parameters on the trait (`trait T<U = Self>`, what `std::ops::Add` uses, named-deferred from 114); multiple impls dispatching different `Output` types (the payoff, e.g., std's `Iterator::Item` resolved differently per iterator); operator traits from `std::ops` (compose today's mechanic with 114's generic parameter and a default type parameter); the orphan rule for impls; trait bounds and `where` clauses; lifetime parameters; trait method receivers other than `&self` (compose into associated-type machinery identically). This unlocks reading rmp's `src/biguint/add.rs:113` `type Output = BigUInt;` line fully end-to-end (the line is exactly today's `type Output = ConcreteType;` form with `BigUInt` filling the concrete-type slot); and partially unlocks the rmp capstone `src/biguint/add.rs:112-115` `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self: Self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` outer shape — the `Add<&BigUInt>` slot is lesson 114's mechanic, the `type Output = BigUInt` line is today's mechanic, the `for &BigUInt` impl-on-reference-target type and the `self: Self` consuming receiver are 100/102 (the consuming receiver already installed; the impl-on-reference-target is a separate deferred mechanic), and the body's carry-propagation algorithm is its own future move.
depends_on: 114-generic-trait-parameter (load-bearing — installs the framing "trait declarations carry fillable type slots" with the generic-parameter-on-the-trait-header form `<RHS>` filled at the impl header `<u32>`; today extends the framing with a *dual* mechanic: the trait body declares `type Output;` and the impl body resolves `type Output = u32;`; the entire pedagogical bridge "generic parameters at the header, associated types in the body" rests on 114; the lesson's *Mental Model Delta* and *What Changed* both pivot on this dual structure), 112-trait-method-extra-parameter (load-bearing — installs the contract-matching rule "the impl method's signature must match the trait method's signature exactly" and the diagnostic E0053; today extends the rule to the post-resolution form: after `type Output = u32;` resolves, `Self::Output` is replaced by `u32` and the impl method's signature must match `fn doubled(&self) -> u32`; Probe 3 witnesses E0053 firing when the impl method's signature mismatches the resolved type, identical shape to lesson 112's E0053 only on a different mismatch axis), 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing — installs the basic `trait Name { ... }` declaration and `impl Trait for Type { ... }` block; today places one new associated item inside each block, namely the `type Output;` declaration and the `type Output = u32;` resolution; the block syntax is unchanged, only the variety of associated item is new — Reference `items/traits.md:19-23` verbatim "associated items, which come in three varieties: functions, types, constants" was named in 111's main_concept as the framing; today centers the *types* variety after 111-114 exercised the *functions* variety), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `Self` as a *type alias* inside an impl block and the inherent-vs-trait impl distinction; today's `Self::Output` is a path *through* that alias to a name the trait declared as an associated item, so the Reference's "Self refers to the implementing type" rule from lesson 100 is exactly what makes `Self::Output` resolvable — `Self` is the namespace prefix, `Output` is the name the trait declared inside that namespace, the impl resolves the binding), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — installs `struct Name { field: Type, }`, struct expression `Name { field: value }`, and field access `instance.field`; today reuses all three in the working probe — `Counter` is the trait-impl target, `Counter { count: 21 }` is the struct literal in `fn main`, and `self.count` is field access on the `&self` receiver inside the impl method body), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs the dot-call shape `value.method()`; today's `c.doubled()` is exactly that shape, and the trait's associated-type machinery is invisible at the call site once the impl has resolved `Output`), 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs the `(p1: T1, p2: T2)` parameter-list grammar and the `-> R` return-type slot; today's `fn doubled(&self) -> Self::Output;` (trait declaration) and `fn doubled(&self) -> u32 { ... }` (impl method) slot `Self::Output` and `u32` respectively into the return-type position), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let c = Counter { count: 21 };` in `fn main`), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `*` (multiplication) is the operator used in the working probe's method body `self.count * 2`; lesson 009 is the move that installed `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` on integers; today reuses `*` unchanged on `u32`; integer division `/` from the same lesson is used in Check Yourself's `tiny.rs` body `self.n / 2`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("doubled = {}", c.doubled())` consumes one `{}` slot for the trait method's return value), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: u32` annotation in `count: u32` and the `-> u32` return-type annotation; the type annotation slot is unchanged from lesson 019), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` as the named member of the integer family used in today's struct field type and resolved associated-type value; `u64` as another member used in the auxiliary E0053 probe), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 2's E0046 transcript; the multi-`-->` shape — caret at impl header plus secondary `-->` line at the trait declaration's `type Output;` — is the same shape lessons 100, 109, 111, 112, 113, 114 already installed for E0599 / E0603 / E0599 / E0053 / E0308 / E0107 respectively; today extends the diagnostic-pattern catalog with E0046, the "missing trait item" code), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "multi-impl with different `Output` types — *the* dispatch power of associated types, since one trait's `type Output;` slot can resolve to a different concrete type for each impl on each target type; std's `Iterator::Item` is the canonical example (each iterator implements `Iterator` with `type Item` resolved to whatever the iterator yields); named today, not probed" moves, future "multiple associated types in one trait — `type A; type B;` extends naturally; each requires its own resolution line in the impl" moves, future "associated types with trait bounds — `type Output: Display;` constrains which concrete types may resolve `Output`; blocked on the trait-bounds arc" moves, future "generic associated types (GATs) — `type Item<'a>;` and the impl side `type Item<'a> = ArrayLender<'a, T>;` per Reference `items/associated-items.md:333-359`; blocked on lifetime parameters" moves, future "using associated types as parameter types — `fn foo(&self, x: Self::Output)`; same path syntax as today's return-type position, just in a different slot; the `Container` trait example at Reference `items/associated-items.md:367-373` is the canonical instance (`fn insert(&mut self, elem: Self::E)`)" moves, future "cross-impl access via qualified path — `<Counter as Doubled>::Output` and `<Item as Trait>::Assoc` per Reference `items/associated-items.md:292-298`; the fully-qualified form for referring to an associated type from outside any impl block; today uses only the simpler `Self::Output` form inside the trait body" moves, future "default associated types in the trait declaration — though the Reference at `items/traits.md:43-46` says 'Associated types must never define the type, the type may only be specified in an implementation' for trait *declarations*, default associated types are a feature available via the `associated_type_defaults` feature gate per current Rust; today's stable-Rust probe correctly defers" moves, future "default type parameters on the trait — `trait T<U = Self>` is what `std::ops::Add` uses; composes today's associated type with 114's generic parameter and the default; named-deferred from 114" moves, future "operator traits from `std::ops` and the rmp Add capstone — `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `Rem`, `Shl`, `Shr`, `AddAssign`, `SubAssign`, `MulAssign`, `Neg`, `Not`; each composes today's associated-type shape with default type parameters (`<Rhs = Self>`), generic trait parameters (lesson 114), and various receiver shapes; the eventual rmp capstone `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self: Self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` is the multi-axis composite end-state of the trait arc, and after today its `type Output = BigUInt;` line is fully readable" moves, future "lifetime parameters `trait T<'a> { ... }` and `&'a Counter` — explicit lifetimes on reference types in trait declarations; deferred wholesale" moves, future "the rmp `add.rs:113` line `type Output = BigUInt;` — directly readable end-to-end after today; exactly today's `type Output = u32;` shape with `BigUInt` filling the concrete-type slot" moves, future "the rmp `add.rs:112-115` outer shape `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self: Self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` — partially readable after today: the `Add<&BigUInt>` slot is 114's mechanic, the `type Output = BigUInt;` is today's mechanic, the `for &BigUInt` impl-on-reference target type is a separate deferred mechanic, the `self: Self` consuming receiver is lesson 102 (already installed), and the body's carry-propagation algorithm is its own pedagogical sequence" moves, future "the rmp `cmp.rs:13-15` `impl PartialOrd for BigUInt` block — `PartialOrd` carries an associated type internally (its supertrait `PartialEq` plus the `partial_cmp` method's signature); composes today's mechanic with the supertrait machinery (deferred) and the `Option<std::cmp::Ordering>` return type (familiar, std's `Option` and `Ordering` use no new mechanics)" moves, future "impl-on-reference target types — `impl Trait for &Type { ... }` where the `for` slot of the impl header carries a reference type rather than a named type; required for the rmp Add capstone's `for &BigUInt`" moves, future "std's `Iterator` trait with `type Item` and `Option<Self::Item>` — the canonical introduction-of-associated-types example in Book ch20-02; composes today's associated-type mechanic with `Option<T>` (already installed via std prelude) and `&mut self` receiver (lesson 101)" moves
```

```text
id: 116-trait-default-method-body
move: extend lesson 111's trait method declaration with a *default body* — the body slot of `fn method(&self) -> T` may be either `;` (no default; impl must provide) or `{ ... }` (default the impl may accept or override) — and exercise both halves of the impl response: an impl that accepts the default by writing an empty `{}` body (`impl Trait for Type {}`, no `fn` line for the default-having method), and an impl on a second target type that overrides the default by providing its own `fn method(&self) -> T { override_body }`; concretely the working probe `struct Counter { count: u32 } trait Greeting { fn greet(&self) -> u32 { 100u32 } } impl Greeting for Counter {} fn main() { let c = Counter { count: 7 }; println!("c.count = {}", c.count); println!("greet   = {}", c.greet()); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `c.count = 7 / greet   = 100` and exits 0; the centered override contrast probe `override.rs` (adds a second struct `Tally { n: u32 }` and a second impl `impl Greeting for Tally { fn greet(&self) -> u32 { self.n * 2 } }`, trait declaration unchanged) compiles silently and prints `c.count = 7 / counter = 100 / tally   = 42`, witnessing both halves of the mechanic in one program — `Counter` accepts the default, `Tally` overrides it; the optional sharpening probe `no_default.rs` (drops the default body's `{ 100u32 }` so the trait method ends in `;` again, leaving the impl `{}` empty) fires `error[E0046]: not all trait items implemented, missing: `greet`` with `--> no_default.rs:9:1`, the multi-`-->` shape with one `-->` block underlining the trait body's `fn greet(&self) -> u32;` line at line 6 with the label `` `greet` from trait`` and a second `-->` block underlining the impl header `impl Greeting for Counter {}` with the inline label `missing `greet` in implementation`, plus the standard `error: aborting due to 1 previous error` and `--explain E0046` trailer — empirical witness that the default body in the trait is precisely what makes the empty `{}` impl legal: with the default body the same impl works, without it the same impl is incomplete and the same E0046 lesson 115 installed for a missing associated type fires for a missing associated function instead
main_concept: a trait method's body slot can be either `;` (Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md:43` verbatim "Trait functions may omit the function body by replacing it with a semicolon. This indicates that the implementation must define the function." — the form lessons 111-115 used) or `{ ... }` (Reference `items/traits.md:43-44` verbatim "If the trait function defines a body, this definition acts as a default for any implementation which does not override it." — the form today installs); the canonical reference example at `items/traits.md:48-58` shows both forms side-by-side as `fn method_without_default(&self);` and `fn method_with_default(&self) {}` inside one trait body; the impl block has a dual response to the default-body form — accept by leaving the method out entirely (Book `output/docs/rust/book/ch10-02-traits.md:207-208` verbatim "To use a default implementation to summarize instances of `NewsArticle`, we specify an empty `impl` block with `impl Summary for NewsArticle {}`." — exactly today's working probe shape) or override by providing the same `fn` line lessons 111-115 used (Book lines 235-239 verbatim "the syntax for overriding a default implementation is the same as the syntax for implementing a trait method that doesn't have a default implementation."); the diagnostic E0046 (Reference `output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0046.md:22-25` verbatim "you must, at minimum, provide implementations for all of `Foo`'s required methods (meaning the methods that do not have default implementations), as well as any required trait items like associated types or constants") fires precisely when an impl block is empty `{}` *and* the trait declared a method without a default body — empirical witness for the "required = no default body" clause; the audience crosses from "every trait method declaration ends in `;` and every impl block has at least one `fn` line per trait method" (lessons 111-115) to "the body slot has two shapes; `;` means impl must provide, `{ ... }` means default; an empty `{}` impl is legal exactly when every method the trait declared has a default body." Two coupled framings under one centered move. (1) *Body-slot dual form*: `fn method(&self) -> T;` vs. `fn method(&self) -> T { default_body }` inside a trait declaration. The curly-brace form is the same `fn name(&self) -> T { ... }` shape lesson 008 installed for free functions and lesson 100/111 installed for impl method bodies; the only new fact is that the shape is also legal *inside* the trait declaration. (2) *Impl response dual form*: an impl may either include a `fn method(&self) -> T { override_body }` line (the lesson 111-115 shape) or omit the line entirely. Omission is legal exactly for those methods the trait gave default bodies; for methods the trait declared with `;`, omission triggers E0046. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic default-body machinery — one trait, one method with a default body, one accepting impl in the working probe; the override demonstration uses a second struct in Probe 2 (auxiliary multi-type witness, not the centered teaching). The unlocked shape `impl Trait for Type {}` is read structurally — empty impl accepts every default the trait provided. The rmp target `/Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs:10` `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` is precisely this shape; std's `Eq` (`output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.Eq.md:7`) is `pub trait Eq: PartialEq { }` whose body has no required methods and one auto-default-bodied method — the canonical real-world instance of today's mechanic. Explicitly defer default *associated types* `type Output = u32;` *inside the trait body* (feature-gated; deferred from 115); *multi-type dispatch* as a centered teaching (Probe 2 demonstrates incidentally; the *point* of trait machinery still has its own future move; deferred since 111); *supertraits* `trait Eq: PartialEq` (the colon-after-trait-name shape; `Eq`'s actual std declaration uses this, but today reads `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` only structurally and defers the supertrait machinery; Reference `items/traits.md:235-258` covers the section); *marker traits as a category* (`Send`, `Sync`, `Copy`, `Sized`, `Eq` — named in passing as "traits with no methods or only default-bodied methods"; the formal category is deferred); *internal `Eq` details* (the implicit `assert_receiver_is_total_eq` method and the deeper PartialEq-supertrait rationale); `#[derive(Eq)]` and `#[derive(Debug)]` macros (deferred since 095/110); *default bodies that call other trait methods* (Book `ch10-02-traits.md:241-256` shows the `summarize` calling `summarize_author` example; composes today's shape with itself, named not exercised); *all deferrals from 111-115* remain deferred (associated constants, GATs, generic functions, generic struct types, trait bounds, default type parameters on trait, lifetime parameters, the orphan rule, multi-impl with different RHS or `Output`, operator traits). This unlocks reading rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:10` `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` line *structurally* (empty impl accepts every default the trait provided) and partially unlocks reading rmp's `cmp.rs:4-10` PartialEq + Eq capstone (the Eq line is fully readable structurally; the PartialEq line composes lessons 113/114, and its body's `self.limbs == other.limbs` uses Vec equality which composes onto today's machinery as a future move).
depends_on: 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing — installs the trait declaration `trait Name { fn method(&self) -> T; }` with the body slot filled by `;` and the impl block `impl Trait for Type { ... }` whose body provides the matching `fn method` line; today extends the body slot to admit `{ ... }` as a *default body* and the impl block to admit *empty* `{}` content; the lesson body's framing "lessons 111-115 wrote every trait method declaration with a semicolon body slot" is a one-line summary of the trait arc up through 115 grounded directly in 111's main_concept; lesson 111's What To Ignore For Now block named **default method bodies** explicitly verbatim "`fn method(&self) { ... }` (curlies, not `;`) inside the trait acts as a default the impl may override" — today is exactly the deferred move named there), 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs the `fn name(&self) -> T { ... }` shape with a body, the same shape today's default body uses inside the trait declaration; the body's syntax is unchanged from 008/100/111), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — installs `struct Name { field: Type, }`, struct expression `Name { field: value }`, and field access `value.field`; today's working probe uses `Counter { count: u32 }`, `Counter { count: 7 }`, and `c.count` — the field access in `main` keeps the field "used" so `rustc demo.rs` is silent on the `dead_code` warning), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `&self` as the borrowing receiver inside an impl block; today's default body inside the trait declaration uses `&self` as its receiver, structurally matching the impl method receivers from 111-115), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs the dot-call shape `value.method()`; today's `c.greet()` is exactly that shape, and the trait's default-body machinery is invisible at the call site once the impl has accepted or overridden), 115-trait-associated-type (cited — installs the diagnostic E0046 with the multi-`-->` shape, one `-->` underlining the trait body's missing-item line and another underlining the impl header with the label "missing `<item>` in implementation"; today's optional sharpening probe (Probe 3) reuses the exact same E0046 diagnostic on a different missing item kind — a trait *function* instead of an associated type — empirical witness that the same diagnostic frames "the trait declared an item the impl did not provide" across both function and type item kinds), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let c = Counter { count: 7 };`, `let t = Tally { n: 21 };` in `fn main`), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `*` (multiplication) is the operator used in Probe 2's override body `self.n * 2`; lesson 009 is the move that installed `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` on integers), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("c.count = {}", c.count)`, `println!("greet   = {}", c.greet())` each consume one `{}` slot), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: u32` annotations in `count: u32`, `n: u32`, and the `-> u32` return-type annotations; the type annotation slot is unchanged from lesson 019), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` as the named member of the integer family used in the struct field types and the trait method's return type; the literal `100u32` uses the `u32` suffix from the same lesson), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 3's E0046 transcript; the multi-`-->` shape is the same shape lessons 100, 109, 111, 112, 113, 114, 115 already installed for E0599 / E0603 / E0599 / E0053 / E0308 / E0107 / E0046 respectively; today reuses the E0046 shape from 115 on a function item kind instead of a type item kind), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "multi-type dispatch — one trait, multiple structs implementing it; the canonical demonstration that the *point* of trait machinery is shared interfaces across types; set aside in 111 and 113 with no new mechanic and remains the natural next centered teaching of why traits exist as a separate feature from inherent impls; today's Probe 2 demonstrates this incidentally as auxiliary witness for the override half of the mechanic" moves, future "supertraits — `trait Eq: PartialEq { }` and the `trait Sub: Super { ... }` shape generally; Reference `items/traits.md:235-258` covers the *Supertraits* section; the next mechanic to fully read rmp's `cmp.rs:10` `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` end-to-end (today reads it only structurally as 'empty impl accepts default') and to fully read std's `pub trait Eq: PartialEq { }` declaration line; the `where Self: Trait` alternative form is also covered in the section" moves, future "marker traits as a category — `Send`, `Sync`, `Copy`, `Sized`, `Eq`; the formal category of traits with no required methods (or only default-bodied methods) plus the orphan-rule and Send/Sync auto-trait mechanics; today names the category in passing but defers the formal definition" moves, future "derive macros `#[derive(Eq)]`, `#[derive(Debug)]`, `#[derive(Clone)]`, etc. — derive macros expand at compile time to `impl Trait for Type` blocks; some (like Eq) expand to empty `{}` impls today's mechanic makes structurally readable, others (like Debug) expand to non-empty impls that override default-bodied methods; today defers the macro machinery itself" moves, future "the rmp `cmp.rs:10` line `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` — structurally readable end-to-end after today modulo the supertrait `Eq: PartialEq` mechanic which is itself a pending future move; the structural reading 'empty impl accepts every default body the trait provided' is correct independent of the supertrait machinery" moves, future "the rmp `cmp.rs:4-10` PartialEq + Eq capstone — the two-impl block reading; the PartialEq impl on line 4 composes lessons 113 (reference parameter) and 114 (generic trait parameter `<BigUInt>`), the Eq impl on line 10 is today's empty-impl-accepts-default mechanic; the body's `self.limbs == other.limbs` (line 6) uses Vec equality which composes onto today's machinery as a future move" moves, future "default bodies that call other trait methods — the Book at `ch10-02-traits.md:241-256` shows the `summarize` default body calling `summarize_author` (a required method); composes today's mechanic with itself; the trait can offload the bulk of its behavior to a default body that delegates to a small required method, which is the central pay-off of default bodies in real code (the implementor writes only the smallest required surface)" moves, future "operator traits and the rmp Add capstone — the eventual `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` in `src/biguint/add.rs`; multi-axis composite end-state of the trait arc; today's mechanic is one more axis (some operator traits provide default bodies for compound forms like `add_assign` calling `add`) although the `Add` trait itself does not have a default body for `add`" moves, future "default bodies that take other receivers — `&mut self` defaults, `self` by value defaults; compose lesson 101/102 receivers with today's default-body shape" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md
```

```text
id: 117-vec-equality
move: extend lesson 013's `==` operator (installed there only on primitive integers) from integer operands to `Vec<T>` operands — concretely, write `vec_a == vec_b` between two `Vec<u64>` values and bind/print the resulting `bool`; the working probe `fn main() { let a: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let b: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let c: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 99]; let d: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20]; println!("a == b is {}", a == b); println!("a == c is {}", a == c); println!("a == d is {}", a == d); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `a == b is true / a == c is false / a == d is false` and exits 0; the single working probe carries both halves of the centered contrast in one program — `a == b` true (same length, agreeing elements at every index), `a == c` false (same length, one mismatched element at index 2), `a == d` false (different lengths, fails before any element comparison is logically required); no separate failing probe is needed; an attempted mismatched-element-types probe `Vec<u64> == Vec<u32>` was tried during preparation and fires `error[E0277]: can't compare u64 with u32` with the help line "the trait `PartialEq<u32>` is not implemented for `u64` but trait `PartialEq<u64>` is implemented for it" — the diagnostic exposes the deferred trait-bounds machine and is documented in evidence but not committed as a probe and is named-deferred under "What To Ignore For Now"
main_concept: `==` is *sugar* for a `PartialEq::eq` method call (Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/operator-expr.md` lines 508-516 verbatim "`a == b;` // is equivalent to `::std::cmp::PartialEq::eq(&a, &b);`" and lines 525-526 the table row "`==` Equal `std::cmp::PartialEq::eq`"); the std doc at `output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.PartialEq.md` lines 25-29 verbatim "Implementing this trait for types provides the `==` and `!=` operators for those types. `x.eq(y)` can also be written `x == y`"; std implements `PartialEq` for `Vec<T>` whenever the element type itself implements `PartialEq` (std `trait.PartialEq.md` line 1460 verbatim "impl<T, U, A1, A2> PartialEq<Vec<U, A2>> for Vec<T, A1> where A1: Allocator, A2: Allocator, T: PartialEq<U>"); the semantics are pairwise — `==` returns `true` exactly when the two vectors have the same length AND the same element value at every index; different length returns `false` before any element comparison is required; the mechanism connects directly to the trait machinery installed in lessons 111-116 — std's impl is exactly the `impl Trait for Type` shape from lesson 111 with the `<RHS>` generic-trait-parameter shape from lesson 114 substituting `<Vec<U, A2>>` for the trait's `<Rhs = Self>` slot; `T = U = u64` in today's probe, and lesson 013 already installed `u64 == u64`, so the bound is satisfied; the audience crosses from "`==` works on integer values and produces a `bool`" (lesson 013) to "`==` is sugar for a `PartialEq::eq` call; whoever implemented `PartialEq` for a type makes `==` work on that type; std implemented `PartialEq` for `Vec<T>` (when the element type itself supports `==`), so `vec_a == vec_b` returns `true` exactly when the two have the same length and the same element values pairwise." Two coupled framings under one centered move. (1) *`==` is overloadable, and std supplies the overload for `Vec<T>`*: `==` is not a primitive operator hard-wired only to integers — it is a method call dispatched through the `PartialEq` trait. The operator on the surface is identical between integer `==` and `Vec<T>` `==`; the dispatched body is different (std's integer impl vs. std's Vec impl). (2) *Pairwise semantics*: `Vec<T> == Vec<U>` is `true` iff `len_a == len_b` AND for every index `i` in range, `a[i] == b[i]`; the probe demonstrates both failure modes (one mismatched element, different length) alongside the success case. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic extension of `==` from integers to `Vec<T>`. The Reference desugar quote lets the learner read `==` mechanistically going forward, and the `impl PartialEq for Vec<T>` line is the canonical example of the trait machinery from lessons 111-116 used by std. Explicitly defer `!=` on `Vec<T>` (works alongside `==` per the std quote, but the formal exercise of `!=` is a sibling future move; lesson 013 installed both `==` and `!=` on integers, but today only exercises `==` for atomicity); the full `PartialEq` declaration with `<Rhs: ?Sized = Self>` (the `?Sized` bound and the *default type parameter* `= Self`); the `ne` method's default body as a defining feature of `PartialEq` (today notes only that `ne` is the structural reason `!=` is available wherever `==` is); trait bounds `T: PartialEq` and `where` clauses (the formal syntax std's impl line uses; the trait-bounds arc has been blocked since lesson 114 named it); comparing `Vec<T>` with mismatched element types (e.g., `Vec<u64> == Vec<u32>`) which fires `E0277` exposing the deferred trait-bounds machine; the `Allocator` parameter `<A1, A2>` in std's `Vec` impl (invisible while the default global allocator is used); `==` on other std collections (`HashMap`, `BTreeMap`, `VecDeque`) which use the same mechanism but are separate moves; `==` on user-defined types (requires writing your own `impl PartialEq for MyType { ... }`; rmp's `cmp.rs:4-8` is exactly such a manual impl, but today reads only the *operator* — the *manual impl* is a pending future move); `#[derive(PartialEq)]` (auto-generates the impl); E0277 (trait not implemented) as a distinct E-code from lesson 003's diagnostic catalogue. This unlocks reading rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:6` `self.limbs == other.limbs` end-to-end (`BigUInt::limbs` is a `Vec<u64>` field per lesson 107's reading of `src/biguint/basic.rs`, so the expression is exactly today's mechanic) and partially unlocks reading rmp's `cmp.rs:4-10` PartialEq + Eq capstone (the `eq` method body is fully readable after today; the surface `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` line itself, specifically the `<BigUInt>` generic argument substituting for std's `<Rhs = Self>` default type parameter, is the thin remaining mechanic for the next lesson 118).
depends_on: 013-comparison-operators (load-bearing — installs the `==` operator and the fact that `==` between two values produces a `bool`; lesson 013 used `==` only on primitive integer operands, and its What To Ignore For Now block named "`PartialEq` and `PartialOrd` *traits* in the appendix's 'Overloadable?' column. Deeper machinery for opting other kinds of values into comparison; traits are a much later move." — today is exactly the deferred move named there, taking the first concrete step from "primitive-integer-only `==`" to "`==` on a non-primitive type via std's `PartialEq` impl"; the operator and the result kind are unchanged from 013, only the operand type is new), 107-vec-basics (load-bearing — installs the `Vec<T>` shape with `vec![]`, `vec![v1, v2, ...]`, `.len()`, and `v[i]`; lesson 107's What To Ignore For Now block named "Equality `v1 == v2`" verbatim as deferred — today is exactly that deferred move; the working probe builds four `Vec<u64>` values with the lesson 107 `vec![10, 20, 30]` shape and the `: Vec<u64>` annotation slot; the lesson 107 capstone-trio reading of rmp's `BigUInt::limbs` as a `Vec<u64>` field is also load-bearing for the unlock claim), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs `u64` as a member of the integer family; combined with lesson 013, `u64 == u64` is the impl that satisfies the *element-type* `T: PartialEq<U>` clause inside std's `Vec` impl; without lesson 080's `u64` and lesson 013's integer `==`, the today's probe could not type-check), 114-generic-trait-parameter (cited — installs the trait `<RHS>` generic-parameter shape; today reads std's `pub trait PartialEq<Rhs = Self>` and the impl line `impl<T, U, A1, A2> PartialEq<Vec<U, A2>> for Vec<T, A1>` *structurally* using lesson 114's machine — the trait declaration's `<Rhs>` parameter and the impl line's `<Vec<U, A2>>` substitution are both lesson 114's shape; today does not exercise the full machine, only reads it), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — installs default method bodies; std's `PartialEq` has one provided (default-bodied) method `ne` alongside the required `eq`, so the default-body machinery from 116 is the structural reason `!=` is available wherever `==` is; today notes this in passing without exercising it), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` entry point of the probe), 005-let-binding (cited — `let a: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30];` etc.), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("a == b is {}", a == b)` with one positional `{}` slot; the substituted expression is the comparison expression itself), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: Vec<u64>` annotation slot; the type annotation slot is unchanged from lesson 019), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — not exercised today since the probe compiles silently and no failing probe is committed; named for completeness in case the learner experiments with mismatched element types and encounters E0277), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs` then `./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the rmp `cmp.rs:6` line `self.limbs == other.limbs` — readable end-to-end after today; `BigUInt::limbs` is a `Vec<u64>` field per lesson 107's capstone-trio reading of `src/biguint/basic.rs`, so the expression is exactly the `Vec<u64> == Vec<u64>` mechanic today installs; this is the unlock target named in the orchestrator brief" moves, future "the rmp `cmp.rs:4-10` PartialEq + Eq capstone — the two-impl block reading; the `eq` method body `self.limbs == other.limbs` is fully readable after today, lessons 113 (reference parameter `&BigUInt`) and 114 (generic trait parameter `<BigUInt>`) made the surface `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt { fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool { ... } }` signature readable, and lesson 116 made the `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` line structurally readable; the only remaining mechanic for the next lesson 118 capstone is the `<BigUInt>` generic argument substituting for std's `<Rhs = Self>` default type parameter on PartialEq's declaration" moves, future "`!=` on `Vec<T>` — works alongside `==` per std `trait.PartialEq.md` lines 25-26 verbatim 'Implementing this trait for types provides the `==` and `!=` operators for those types'; lesson 013 installed both `==` and `!=` on integers; today exercises only `==` on `Vec<T>` for atomicity, and the formal `!=` move is the sibling deferral" moves, future "`==` and `!=` on other std collections — `HashMap`, `BTreeMap`, `BTreeSet`, `HashSet`, `VecDeque`, `LinkedList`, etc.; same mechanism (std implements `PartialEq` for them whenever the element/key/value types do), but each is its own separate move because the pairwise-equality semantics differ subtly per collection (e.g., `HashMap == HashMap` is order-independent while `Vec == Vec` is order-sensitive)" moves, future "the full std `PartialEq` trait declaration — `pub trait PartialEq<Rhs: ?Sized = Self> { fn eq(&self, other: &Rhs) -> bool; fn ne(&self, other: &Rhs) -> bool { ... } }` from std `output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.PartialEq.md` lines 7-19; today reads structural fragments of this declaration but defers (a) the `?Sized` bound on `Rhs`, (b) the *default type parameter* `= Self` machinery (`<Rhs = Self>` is one of the two canonical sites where Rust's default-type-parameter shape appears alongside operator-overload traits like `Add<Rhs = Self>`), and (c) the `ne` method's default body as a defining feature of the trait (lesson 116's machinery applies but is not centered today)" moves, future "trait bounds `T: PartialEq` and `where T: PartialEq<U>` clauses — the formal 'T must implement PartialEq' syntax; the trait-bounds arc has been blocked since lesson 114 named it; today reads the clause in std's impl line for context only and does not exercise the syntax; lesson 013's What To Ignore For Now also named this as deferred ('traits are a much later move')" moves, future "E0277 — the trait-not-implemented error code; fires on `Vec<u64> == Vec<u32>` with the help line 'the trait `PartialEq<u32>` is not implemented for `u64` but trait `PartialEq<u64>` is implemented for it'; distinct from lesson 003's diagnostic catalogue so far (E0046, E0053, E0107, E0308, E0599, E0603 from the trait arc); E0277 is the canonical 'a trait bound was not satisfied' diagnostic and unlocks the trait-bounds arc" moves, future "manual `impl PartialEq for MyType { ... }` — write your own `PartialEq` impl on a user-defined type; the trait machinery from lessons 111-116 is sufficient to do this; rmp's `cmp.rs:4-8` is exactly such a manual impl on `BigUInt`; today reads only the *operator* and defers the *manual impl* as a pending future move" moves, future "`#[derive(PartialEq)]` — the derive macro that auto-generates an `impl PartialEq for MyType` for a struct/enum where every field is itself `PartialEq`; deferred since lesson 095 (struct-with-named-fields) named derive macros as a separate machine" moves, future "the `Allocator` parameter `<A1, A2>` in std's `Vec` impl — invisible while the default global allocator is used; the parameter exists because `Vec<T, A: Allocator = Global>` is the actual std declaration; covers custom allocators and unstable `allocator_api` features" moves, future "operator-trait analogues — `PartialOrd`, `Ord`, `Eq` (today gestured at via lesson 116's `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` reading), `Hash`, and the arithmetic operator traits `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `Rem`; `==` desugars to `PartialEq::eq` is one instance of the more general operator-trait pattern; the rmp `cmp.rs:18-33` `impl Ord for BigUInt` and `cmp.rs:12-16` `impl PartialOrd for BigUInt` blocks compose today's machinery with PartialOrd/Ord; the rmp `add.rs` capstone composes with `Add`" moves, future "`==` on user-defined types using the `Eq` marker trait — when a type implements both `PartialEq` and `Eq`, the `==` semantics are guaranteed to be a *full* equivalence relation (reflexive, symmetric, transitive), unlike `PartialEq` alone (e.g., floating-point `NaN != NaN` shows why `f32`/`f64` are `PartialEq` but not `Eq`); today exercises `Vec<u64>` whose elements *are* `Eq`, so `Vec<u64>` is also `Eq`, but the formal Eq-vs-PartialEq distinction is named-deferred" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.PartialEq.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/vec/struct.Vec.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/operator-expr.md
  - /Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/117-vec-equality.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/117-vec-equality.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 118-capstone-rmp-cmp-equality
move: capstone for the trait arc (lessons 111-117) — read end-to-end the rmp slice `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt { fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool { self.limbs == other.limbs } }` and `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` defined in `/Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs:4-10`. **No new mechanic.** Three understanding axes covered. (1) *The two impl headers token-by-token*: `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` composes 111 (`impl Trait for Type`) with 114 (`<BigUInt>` generic argument substituting into PartialEq's `<Rhs: ?Sized = Self>` slot — `?Sized` and `= Self` are explicit named deferrals); `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` composes 111 with 116 (empty body accepts every default the trait provides) and reads the supertrait `Eq: PartialEq` structurally as a named deferral (the colon-after-trait-name machinery is its own future move). (2) *The `eq` method body* `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool { self.limbs == other.limbs }` composes 008 (`fn`), 100 (`&self`), 113 (`other: &BigUInt` non-receiver reference parameter), 112 (the contract-matching rule applied post-substitution `Rhs = BigUInt`), 012 (`-> bool`), 095 (field access through `&self` / `&BigUInt`), and 117 (`Vec<u64> == Vec<u64>` pairwise equality on the field). (3) *The dispatch chain* from user code's `a == b` to a `bool`: Reference `expressions/operator-expr.md:512-516` desugars `a == b` to `::std::cmp::PartialEq::eq(&a, &b)`, trait resolution selects rmp's `cmp.rs:4-7` impl, the body computes `self.limbs == other.limbs` which dispatches into std's `Vec<T>` `PartialEq` impl, returning `true` iff same length and same element values pairwise; `a != b` rides std's default `ne` body (lesson 116 in real-world use). Six probes plus rmp's own test suite. Working probe — self-contained `.rs` mirror at `observations/118-capstone-rmp-cmp-equality.rs` (the field is plain `pub` because the single-file probe has no parent module on which to ride `pub(super)`) — compiled with `rustc 118-capstone-rmp-cmp-equality.rs -o probe` exits 0 silent and `./probe` prints exactly `a == b -> true / a == c -> false / a != b -> false / a != c -> true` and exits 0; the cross-crate driver via path dependency `bignum = { path = "/Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp" }` at `/tmp/eduratchet2-118/rmp_driver/` (mirroring lesson 110's driver layout) builds two `BigUInt` values via rmp's `From<u64>` impl in `convert.rs:16-20` and `cargo run` prints the same four equality lines plus three more confirming lesson 110's honest observation reused on the `==` axis (`z == from_zero -> false`, `z == z -> true`, `from_zero == from_zero -> true`); two contrast probes — drop the `impl PartialEq` block entirely and `a == b` fires `error[E0369]: binary operation \`==\` cannot be applied to type \`BigUInt\`` with `note: an implementation of \`PartialEq\` might be missing for \`BigUInt\`` (witness that the impl block is *what makes* `==` work — `==` is dispatched through the trait, not hardwired); drop only `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` (PartialEq kept) and the probe still compiles and runs identically (witness that today's `==`/`!=` calls need only PartialEq; Eq's load-bearing consequence appears at HashMap/BTreeMap key positions and certain generic bounds, deferred); a sixth `vec_zero.rs` probe directly witnesses `vec![] == vec![0] -> false / vec![] == vec![] -> true / vec![0] == vec![0] -> true` — the underlying `Vec<u64>` mechanic for the constructor-discipline observation; `cargo test --lib` against rmp itself prints `17 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out` confirming the impls compile and rmp's existing tests still pass.
main_concept: this is *Capstone Mode* — no new Rust mechanic, instead a witness that the trait arc (lessons 111-117, seven moves) composes into a real-world Rust slice readable end-to-end. The audience crosses from "I have installed every named ingredient for rmp's `cmp.rs:4-10` PartialEq + Eq pair, but I have not read it end-to-end" to "I can read the seven lines token-by-token, walk the dispatch chain from `a == b` to `bool` through std's desugar rule and the `Vec<T>` PartialEq impl, predict behavior on hand-built inputs, witness empirically with two probes (self-contained mirror plus cross-crate driver), and reason about three named-deferred mechanics: the `?Sized` bound on `Rhs`, the `= Self` *default type parameter* on `Rhs`, and the supertrait `Eq: PartialEq`." Three understanding axes covered explicitly: (1) *The two impl headers*: `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` reads as 111's `impl Trait for Type` shape with 114's `<BigUInt>` generic argument substituting into the trait declaration's `<Rhs: ?Sized = Self>` slot — std's actual declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.PartialEq.md:7-19` `pub trait PartialEq<Rhs = Self> where Rhs: ?Sized { fn eq(&self, other: &Rhs) -> bool; fn ne(&self, other: &Rhs) -> bool { ... } }` — the `?Sized` bound and the `= Self` default-type-parameter are explicit named deferrals (rmp's explicit `<BigUInt>` bypasses the default; the bare `impl PartialEq for BigUInt` form would also have worked because `Self` here is `BigUInt`); `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` reads as 111's shape with 116's empty-body-accepts-default rule applied to std's declaration `pub trait Eq: PartialEq { }` at `trait.Eq.md:7` (the supertrait `Eq: PartialEq` is named-deferred; the body has no required methods because std's doc states `trait.Eq.md:23-24` "This property cannot be checked by the compiler, and therefore `Eq` is a trait without methods"). (2) *The `eq` method body*: token-by-token mapping — `fn` (008), `eq` matching the trait declaration (112's contract-matching rule applied after substituting `Rhs = BigUInt`), `&self` (100, Reference shorthand for `self: &Self`), `other: &BigUInt` (113's non-receiver reference parameter, post-substitution form of the trait's `other: &Rhs`), `-> bool` (012), body `self.limbs == other.limbs` composing field access (095) on `&self` and `&BigUInt` with `Vec<u64> == Vec<u64>` (117) — the implicit return value (025). (3) *The dispatch chain* from `a == b` to a `bool`: Reference `expressions/operator-expr.md:512-516` verbatim "`a == b;` // is equivalent to `::std::cmp::PartialEq::eq(&a, &b);`" plus the operator-overload table at lines 525-526 "`==` Equal `std::cmp::PartialEq::eq` / `!=` Not equal `std::cmp::PartialEq::ne`" — `a == b` desugars to `<BigUInt as PartialEq<BigUInt>>::eq(&a, &b)`, trait resolution selects rmp's `cmp.rs:4-7` impl, the body runs `self.limbs == other.limbs`, this is the `Vec<u64> == Vec<u64>` mechanic from lesson 117, returns a `bool`; `a != b` rides std's *default* `ne` body which is `!self.eq(other)` (lesson 116's mechanic in real-world std use, named-not-exercised). The arc closes here on the equality halves; the remaining 21 lines of `cmp.rs` (the PartialOrd/Ord pair on lines 12-33) are unlocked but deferred. Three understanding-witnessing contrast probes: dropping the `impl PartialEq` block fires E0369 (witness the operator dispatches through the trait); dropping only `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` leaves the probe working (witness Eq's reflexivity-marker role is invisible to `==`/`!=`, load-bearing only at HashMap/BTreeMap key positions and certain generic bounds — deferred); the `vec![] == vec![0] -> false` direct witness shows the `Vec<u64>` PartialEq semantic that lesson 110's `BigUInt::from(0u64).is_zero() == false` defect rests on, replayed today on the `==` axis (rmp's `From<u64>` builds `BigUInt { limbs: vec![n] }` without trimming when `n == 0`, so `BigUInt::zero()` (`limbs: vec![]`) and `BigUInt::from(0u64)` (`limbs: vec![0]`) compare unequal under rmp's own PartialEq — same constructor-side discipline defect, on a new axis). The next moves close the supertrait deferral, the default-type-parameter deferral, and the rmp `cmp.rs:12-33` PartialOrd/Ord algorithmic pair; the rmp Add capstone composes today's machinery with `std::ops::Add`, default type parameters, and impl-on-reference-target-types.
depends_on: 117-vec-equality (load-bearing — installs `vec_a == vec_b` on `Vec<T>` returning `true` iff same length and same element values pairwise; the body `self.limbs == other.limbs` is exactly this mechanic on `Vec<u64>`; without lesson 117 the body would be uninterpretable; Probe 6's direct `vec![] == vec![0] -> false` witness reuses the same mechanic on hand-built inputs to ground the constructor-discipline observation in the cross-crate driver), 116-trait-default-method-body (load-bearing — installs the rule that an empty `{}` impl is legal exactly when every method the trait declared has a default body; `impl Eq for BigUInt {}` rides this rule, since std's `Eq` at `output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.Eq.md:7` is `pub trait Eq: PartialEq { }` with no required methods; lesson 116 also installed the framing for std's PartialEq `ne` default body which today rides without exercising), 114-generic-trait-parameter (load-bearing — installs `trait T<RHS> { ... }` declaration shape and the impl-header substitution `impl T<ConcreteType> for Target`; today reads `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` exactly as 114's substitution form, with `<BigUInt>` filling std's `<Rhs: ?Sized = Self>` slot; the `?Sized` and `= Self` mechanics are explicit named deferrals from this composition), 113-reference-parameter-in-trait-method (load-bearing — installs `other: &Type` as a non-receiver reference parameter on a trait method and the matching `&value` call-site form; today's `other: &BigUInt` parameter slot is exactly 113's shape, with `BigUInt` filling the type position), 112-trait-method-extra-parameter (load-bearing — installs the contract-matching rule "the impl method's signature must match the trait method's signature exactly"; today applies the rule post-substitution: after the impl header substitutes `Rhs = BigUInt`, the trait's `fn eq(&self, other: &Rhs) -> bool` becomes `fn eq(&self, other: &BigUInt) -> bool`, exactly what rmp wrote), 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing — installs the `impl Trait for Type { ... }` form distinct from inherent `impl Type { ... }`; both impl blocks today use this shape; lesson 111's *What To Ignore* explicitly named "Operator traits — `std::ops::Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `AddAssign`, `Display`, `Debug`, `PartialEq`, `Eq`, `PartialOrd`, `Ord`" as deferred — today is exactly the deferred move for the equality halves), 110-capstone-rmp-biguint-trio (load-bearing — installs the rmp module-path chain `bignum::biguint::BigUInt`, the `pub struct BigUInt { pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64> }` reading, the `impl BigUInt { pub fn zero() -> Self { ... } }` associated function used by Probe 2 to build `BigUInt::zero()`, and the honest observation that `From<u64>` does not normalize trailing-zero limbs so `BigUInt::from(0u64)` produces `vec![0]` rather than the canonical `vec![]`; today replays the latter defect on the `==` axis — `BigUInt::zero() != BigUInt::from(0u64)` because their `limbs` Vecs differ in length; the cross-crate driver's `use bignum::biguint::BigUInt;` reuses 110's path-readability conclusion verbatim), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `&self` as the borrowing receiver, Reference shorthand for `self: &Self`, and `Self` as a type alias inside an impl block; today's `&self` in the `eq` method rides this exactly; the `Self` alias is also what makes the named-deferred `<Rhs = Self>` default-type-parameter work — when the impl header bypasses the default and writes `<BigUInt>` explicitly, the Self alias in the impl is `BigUInt`, so both forms yield the same impl), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — installs `struct Name { field: Type, }` declaration, struct expression, and field access `value.field`; today's `self.limbs` and `other.limbs` are exactly 095's field-access shape on a reference receiver, plus Probe 1's local `BigUInt { limbs: vec![42] }` struct-literal in `fn main`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` entry point of all probes), 005-let-binding (cited — `let a: BigUInt = BigUInt::from(42u64);` etc. in Probe 2 and `let a = BigUInt { limbs: vec![42] };` etc. in Probe 1), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — `fn name(...) -> R { ... }` shape; the `eq` method reuses this with the `&self` receiver from 100), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("a == b -> {}", a == b)` etc. with one `{}` slot per call), 012-bool-and-bool-literals (cited — `bool` is the return type of `eq`; the literals `true` and `false` appear in probe transcripts as the resulting values), 013-comparison-operators (cited — `==` operator; lesson 013 installed it on integers, lesson 117 extended it to Vec, today reads it inside rmp's `eq` body), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: BigUInt` annotation in `let a: BigUInt = ...` in Probe 2 and the `-> bool` return-type annotation in `eq`), 025-implicit-return (cited — `self.limbs == other.limbs` is the implicit return value of `eq`, no trailing `;`), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — `value.method()` shape; not invoked at the surface today since `==` desugars through the operator, but the desugar target `::std::cmp::PartialEq::eq(&a, &b)` is the path-call form whose dot-call counterpart `a.eq(&b)` is grounded by lesson 040; rmp's other methods used in probes — `BigUInt::from(...)`, `BigUInt::zero()` — also use the dot/path-call shape), 044-use-declaration (cited — `use bignum::biguint::BigUInt;` in Probe 2's `src/main.rs`), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u64` as the element type of `BigUInt::limbs` and the suffix on literals `42u64`, `7u64`, `0u64`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — the four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 4's E0369 transcript; the `note: an implementation of \`PartialEq\` might be missing for \`BigUInt\`` block uses the same `note:` shape lessons 100, 109, 111, 112, 113, 114, 115 installed for E0599 / E0603 / E0599 / E0053 / E0308 / E0107 / E0046 respectively — today extends the diagnostic-pattern catalog with E0369), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs && ./demo` shape in Probe 1, 4, 5, 6), 032-cargo-new-and-run (cited — `cargo new --vcs none rmp_driver`, `cargo run`, `cargo test --lib` in Probes 2 and 3), 065-cargo-toml-dependencies-entry (cited — `[dependencies] / bignum = { path = "..." }` in Probe 2's `Cargo.toml`; the path-dependency form is named-but-deferred in 065 and reused here as in lesson 110's driver), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` and `cargo` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell, ability to `cd` into a directory)
unlocks: future "the rmp `cmp.rs:12-16` line `impl PartialOrd for BigUInt { fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Option<std::cmp::Ordering> { Some(self.cmp(other)) } }` — composes today's trait-impl shape with `Option<T>` (familiar via std prelude), `std::cmp::Ordering` (an enum with three unit variants — lessons 098/099 already installed the enum shape), the inherent `cmp` method on `BigUInt`, and PartialOrd's default-bodied comparison methods; the trait declaration `pub trait PartialOrd<Rhs: ?Sized = Self>: PartialEq<Rhs>` carries the same `<Rhs: ?Sized = Self>` shape as PartialEq plus the supertrait `: PartialEq<Rhs>` (today named-deferred)" moves, future "the rmp `cmp.rs:18-33` line `impl Ord for BigUInt { fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering { ... } }` — the algorithmic Ord impl with a `match` on `Ordering::Equal`, an iterator chain `self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())`, a `for` loop over the chained iterator, a nested `match` arm with `return ord;` from inside, and a final `return Ordering::Equal;` after the loop; each ingredient is its own future move (iterators, `.rev()`, `.zip()`, `for` over a tuple-yielding iterator, return-from-inside-a-match-arm, `match` on enum unit variants composing 098/099)" moves, future "the rmp Add capstone `impl Add<&BigUInt> for &BigUInt { type Output = BigUInt; fn add(self, rhs: &BigUInt) -> BigUInt { ... } }` in `src/biguint/add.rs:112-115` — multi-axis composite end-state of the trait arc; composes today's trait-impl shape with `std::ops::Add<Rhs = Self>`'s default type parameter (named-deferred today as the `= Self` mechanic), an associated type `type Output = BigUInt;` (lesson 115's mechanic), the *consuming* `self: Self` receiver (lesson 102), the impl-on-reference-target-type pattern `for &BigUInt` (its own future move), and the carry-propagation algorithm in the body (composes `overflowing_add` from lesson 108's family with mutable `Vec<u64>` operations)" moves, future "the supertrait machinery `Eq: PartialEq`, `Ord: PartialOrd + Eq`, `Copy: Clone` — colon-after-trait-name shape per Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/items/traits.md:235-258`; today reads `pub trait Eq: PartialEq { }` structurally (empty body accepts default) but defers the supertrait constraint mechanism that the compiler enforces" moves, future "default type parameters on a trait — `pub trait PartialEq<Rhs = Self> { ... }`, `pub trait Add<Rhs = Self>` etc.; the `= Self` shape lets the impl bypass the angle bracket entirely; today's `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` could equivalently be `impl PartialEq for BigUInt` because `Self` inside the impl is `BigUInt`; named-deferred from 114 and reaffirmed today" moves, future "the `?Sized` bound — `<Rhs: ?Sized>` opts out of the implicit `Sized` bound that all generic type parameters carry by default; per Reference `items/generics.md` the `?Sized` syntax is the only relaxation of an implicit bound currently allowed; today's reading defers the bound and confines its scope to 'when matters in std declarations'" moves, future "Eq's implicit `assert_receiver_is_total_eq` method — feature-gated lint internal in std (the unstable `structural_match` lang item that the compiler uses to enforce that `derive(Eq)`-generated impls only contain types whose equality is structural); deferred wholesale" moves, future "the `ne` method's default body in PartialEq — `fn ne(&self, other: &Rhs) -> bool { !self.eq(other) }` — lesson 116's mechanic at work in a real std trait; today rides it without exercising the body" moves, future "`#[derive(PartialEq)]` and `#[derive(Eq)]` macros — derive-macro expansion to `impl PartialEq for T` blocks where `eq` is generated structurally (every field's `==` is AND-combined); today reads only the manual-impl shape, which is what rmp uses" moves, future "the std 'never implement PartialEq for a foreign type' recommendation per `trait.PartialEq.md:67-83` — a coherence/orphan-rule consideration for cross-crate trait impls; today's rmp impl is on its own type so the rule is satisfied; the rule itself is its own topic" moves, future "the full `Allocator` parameter on `Vec<T, A: Allocator = Global>` — invisible while the default global allocator is used; named-deferred from 117" moves, future "E0369 (binary op cannot be applied) — diagnostic for missing operator-trait impls; today's Probe 4 transcript exhibits it; the formal E-code catalogue itself is its own arc" moves, future "E0277 (trait bound not satisfied) — diagnostic surfaced by mismatched-element-type Vec equality in 117's preparation; named there and unchanged today" moves, future "auto-deref through `&self` on field access — `self.limbs` works regardless of `self` being `&BigUInt` rather than `BigUInt`; the formal auto-deref rule remains deferred" moves, future "manual `impl PartialEq for MyType { ... }` on a user-defined type with multiple fields — generalizes today's single-field rmp impl to the multi-field case, where `self.field1 == other.field1 && self.field2 == other.field2 && ...` is the canonical body shape, and the `#[derive(PartialEq)]` macro expands to exactly this AND-chain" moves, future "operator-trait analogues — `Hash` (paired with `Eq`), `PartialOrd`, `Ord`, and the arithmetic operator traits `Add`, `Sub`, `Mul`, `Div`, `Rem`, `Shl`, `Shr`, `AddAssign`, etc.; today's mechanic of '`==` desugars to `PartialEq::eq`' is one instance of the more general operator-trait pattern" moves
evidence:
```
