```text
id: 119-option-some-none
move: install `Option<T>` as the standard library's two-variant generic enum, with the constructors `Some(T)` and `None`, and the `match` discrimination shape on `Some(value)` vs `None` arms; concretely, write the working probe `fn main() { let present: Option<i32> = Some(42); let absent: Option<i32> = None; let p_label = match present { Some(n) => n, None => -1, }; let a_label = match absent { Some(n) => n, None => -1, }; println!("present -> {}", p_label); println!("absent  -> {}", a_label); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `present -> 42` then `absent  -> -1` (exit 0); the centered contrast drops the `: Option<i32>` annotation off a bare `None` (`fn main() { let absent = None; }`) and rustc fires `error[E0282]: type annotations needed for \`Option<_>\`` with the help line `consider giving \`absent\` an explicit type, where the type for type parameter \`T\` is specified` and the suggestion `let absent: Option<T> = None;` — direct empirical witness of the Book ch06-01:389-395 prediction "the compiler can't infer the type that the corresponding `Some` variant will hold by looking only at a `None` value"; a corroborating probe captured in evidence shows `let present = Some(42);` *does* compile without an annotation, because `Some(42)`'s payload pins `T = i32`. Three coupled facts install together, mirroring lesson 052's three-fact shape for `Result<T, E>`. (1) *Type*: `Option<T>` is a generic enum from the standard library, declared `pub enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) }` per `output/docs/rust/std/option/enum.Option.md` lines 5-9 verbatim; the audience-level introduction at `output/docs/rust/book/ch06-01-defining-an-enum.md:314` "another enum defined by the standard library" and the declaration at lines 359-363 are reused; the prelude-membership claim at lines 366-370 verbatim "the `Option<T>` enum is so useful that it's even included in the prelude; you don't need to bring it into scope explicitly. Its variants are also included in the prelude: You can use `Some` and `None` directly without the `Option::` prefix" extends lesson 052's prelude framing to `Option`; the working probe needs no `use` line. (2) *Constructors*: `Some(value)` is a call expression that wraps `value` and produces an `Option<T>` (lesson 052 shape applied to one type parameter); `None` is a path expression (no parentheses, no payload) producing the same type's other variant (lesson 098 shape). For `Some(42)`, rustc infers `T = i32` from the `42` payload; for `None` alone, rustc cannot infer `T` because the variant carries nothing — Book ch06-01:387-395 verbatim makes this load-bearing for the contrast. (3) *Discrimination via `match`*: the lesson 058 payload-variant shape `Variant(name) => arm` carries over unchanged for `Some(n) => n`; the lesson 098 unit-variant shape `Variant => arm` carries over unchanged for `None => -1`. With both arms covered the match is exhaustive — `Option` has only two variants — and lesson 030's "all arms share a type" rule applies. The contrast probe's E0282 transcript empirically confirms what Book ch06-01:389-395 predicts in prose; the corroborating probe (in evidence) confirms the asymmetry that `Some(literal)` *can* pin `T` while bare `None` cannot.
main_concept: `Option<T>` is `Result<T, E>`'s prelude sibling — same generic-enum-with-call-expression-constructor shape lesson 052 installed, but with one type parameter instead of two and one of the two variants demoted to a unit variant (lesson 098 shape) rather than another payload variant. The audience crosses from "I have used `Result<T, E>` (052) — the prelude's two-payload-variant enum for success/failure — and matched on its variants (058); I have declared and matched user-defined unit-variant enums (098); `Option` is a name I have heard but never built or matched" to "`Option<T>` is the prelude's two-variant generic enum from the standard library: `Some(T)` is a tuple variant with a payload (052 shape), `None` is a unit variant (098 shape), both in the prelude (no `use` needed). `Some(value)` is a call-expression constructor that wraps `value` into an `Option<T>` and lets rustc infer `T` from the payload's type. `None` is a path-expression constructor that produces an `Option<T>` whose variant is `None` but cannot pin `T` on its own; without other context, the binding needs an annotation like `: Option<i32>` or rustc fires E0282. A `match` opens any `Option<T>` value with two arms — `Some(name) => arm` (058 shape) binds the payload to a local; `None => arm` (098 shape) matches the bare variant. Both arms must appear (exhaustiveness), and all arm bodies must share a type (lesson 030, unchanged). This is exactly the smallest move toward reading rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:12-15` `impl PartialOrd for BigUInt { fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Option<std::cmp::Ordering> { Some(self.cmp(other)) } }` — the return type `Option<std::cmp::Ordering>` and the constructor `Some(self.cmp(other))` are exactly the two surfaces today installs; the trait `PartialOrd`, the specific use of `Option<Ordering>`, and the `partial_cmp` method body all compose later." Today is *consumption* of std's generic Option, not authoring of generic enums (that move is still blocked on the generics-installation arc). The asymmetry in inference (`Some(literal)` infers `T`, bare `None` does not) is not a quirk — it follows mechanically from "the type variable `T` in `Option<T>` needs at least one constraint, and `None` provides none."
depends_on: 052-result-enum-and-is-ok (load-bearing — installs the *generic enum with payload variant* shape `pub enum Result<T, E> { Ok(T), Err(E) }` and the call-expression constructor `Variant(value)`. Today's `Some(T)` is a structural sibling of `Ok(T)` — same payload-variant shape with one type parameter instead of two. The call-expression constructor `Some(42)` is exactly lesson 052's `Ok(5)` shape. The prelude-membership claim rides 052's prelude framing for `Result`, extended to `Option` per Book ch06-01:366-370. Lesson 052 explicitly named "The `Option<T>` enum — sibling of `Result` with one type parameter" in its *What To Ignore For Now* — today picks that exact deferred move), 058-match-result-payload-variants (load-bearing — installs the `match` arm pattern `Variant(name) => arm` with payload binding. Today's `Some(n) => n` is exactly this shape with `Variant = Some`. Lesson 058 explicitly named "The `Option<T>` enum (`Some(T)` / `None`). Same payload-variant shape; deferred." in its *What To Ignore For Now* — today picks that exact deferred move and joins the payload-variant arm shape to the unit-variant arm shape from lesson 098 in one match), 098-enum-with-unit-variants (load-bearing — installs the unit-variant pattern `Variant => arm` with no parentheses and nothing to bind. Today's `None => -1` is exactly this shape with `Variant = None`. Lesson 098 also explicitly named "Generic enums like the shape of `Option<T>`" in its *What To Ignore For Now* — today is the *consumption* half of that deferral, using std's generic `Option<T>`; the *authoring* half remains deferred), 019-type-annotation-i32 (load-bearing — installs the `let name: TYPE = value;` binding-annotation slot. Today fills the TYPE slot with `Option<i32>`. The contrast probe's `help:` suggestion `let absent: Option<T> = None;` directly echoes this slot. Lesson 052 already exercised the multi-argument form `Result<i32, i32>`; today's one-argument form is a strict subset), 030-match-on-bool (cited — installs the `match` form, exhaustiveness, and the rule that all arms share a type. All three carry through unchanged today), 031-match-integer-and-wildcard (cited — installs the `_` wildcard pattern and the source-order arm-matching rule. Today does not exercise either; both arms appear by name), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — installs the four-part diagnostic map. Today's contrast probe transcript matches the map: headline `error[E0282]: type annotations needed for \`Option<_>\``, location `--> broken.rs:2:9`, source excerpt with caret under `absent` and underline `---- type must be known at this point` under `None`, `help:` block with the annotation suggestion), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("present -> {}", p_label)` etc. with one positional `{}` slot per call), 005-let-binding (cited — used four times in the working probe), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` entry point of all probes), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs && ./demo` shape; silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the rmp `cmp.rs:12-15` line `impl PartialOrd for BigUInt { fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Option<std::cmp::Ordering> { Some(self.cmp(other)) } }` — composes today's `Option<T>` machinery with the trait `PartialOrd` (named-deferred today; supertrait of `PartialEq` per std `output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.PartialOrd.md`), the specific instantiation `Option<std::cmp::Ordering>` (composes today's `Option<T>` with lesson 051's `Ordering` enum), and the `partial_cmp` method body which calls the inherent `cmp` method on `BigUInt` (deferred until the `Ord` arc lands)" moves, future "`Option<T>` as a function parameter or return type — `fn name(arg: Option<T>) -> Option<U> { ... }` shapes; composes today's mechanic with the lesson-021 `-> RTYPE` slot already exercised by lesson 052 for `Result<i32, i32>`; the rmp `cmp.rs:13` use site is exactly this composition" moves, future "`Option`'s method surface — `.is_some()`, `.is_none()`, `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, `.unwrap_or()`, `.map()`, `.and_then()`, `.ok_or()`, `?` — each is its own future move; the std page at `output/docs/rust/std/option/enum.Option.md:39-...` documents them in detail; today reads the declaration only" moves, future "`if let Some(x) = opt { ... }` — the single-arm shorthand for matching only one variant of an enum; deferred since lesson 058 named it; Reference `expressions/if-expr.md` covers it" moves, future "non-primitive `Option` payloads — `Option<&T>`, `Option<Box<T>>`, `Option<Vec<T>>`, `Option<String>`, etc.; each composes today's mechanic with a separate type machinery (references, `Box`, `Vec`, owned strings, etc.)" moves, future "authoring your own generic enum — `enum My<T> { ... }`, `enum Either<L, R> { Left(L), Right(R) }`; blocked on the generics-installation arc that lesson 052 first named and lesson 098 reaffirmed; today is on the *consumption* side, using std's existing `Option<T>`" moves, future "the `Option<T>` vs null framing — Book ch06-01:325-365 frames it; the audience-level motivation is deferred; today centers the mechanic, not the philosophy" moves, future "E0282 in general — the full type-annotations-needed catalogue (closures, integer-type defaults blocked by trait calls, generic function calls without enough constraints, etc.); today's contrast is one specific witness of E0282 (`Option<_>` placeholder); its full E-code arc is its own move" moves, future "`Option<T>` and `Result<T, E>` interconversion — `.ok()`, `.err()`, `.ok_or(...)`, `.ok_or_else(...)`; each is a sibling future move" moves, future "tuple-payload `Option` like `Option<(i32, i32)>` and struct-payload `Option<MyStruct>` — composes today's mechanic with lesson 099's tuple-variant shape and lesson 095's struct-with-named-fields, respectively; the construction `Some((a, b))` and `Some(MyStruct { ... })` use the same call-expression constructor as today's `Some(42)`, just with a more complex payload" moves
evidence:
```

```text
id: 120-self-in-non-receiver
move: write `other: &Self` in the non-receiver parameter slot of a trait *impl* method, as a stylistic alias for `&ImplTargetType`, while leaving the trait declaration's named-type spelling unchanged; concretely, take the lesson 113 working probe and replace `&Counter` *only in the impl method signature* (not the trait declaration) with `&Self`; the program `struct Counter { count: u32 } / trait Combine { fn combine(&self, other: &Counter) -> u32; } / impl Combine for Counter { fn combine(&self, other: &Self) -> u32 { self.count + other.count } } / fn main() { let a = Counter { count: 7 }; let b = Counter { count: 35 }; let result = a.combine(&b); println!("result = {}", result); println!("b.count still = {}", b.count); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `result = 42` then `b.count still = 35` (exit 0); the corroborating probe `named.rs` is the same source with the impl signature's `&Self` swapped back to `&Counter` (so both sites read `&Counter`, the lesson-113 form) and produces byte-identical output `result = 42 / b.count still = 35` (`diff <(./demo) <(./named)` exits 0); the centered contrast `outside.rs` places `&Self` in a free function `fn outside(a: &Self) -> u32 { 0 }` and rustc fires `error[E0411]: cannot find type \`Self\` in this scope` with the inline label `\`Self\` is only available in impls, traits, and type definitions` and a secondary inline label under the function name `\`Self\` not allowed in a function`
main_concept: `Self` inside any impl block (inherent `impl Type { ... }` per lesson 100 or trait `impl Trait for Type { ... }` per lesson 111) is a *type alias* for the impl-target type — the Book ch05-03 lines 62-63 verbatim "Within an `impl` block, the type `Self` is an alias for the type that the `impl` block is for" — and the Reference `paths.md` lines 316-322 confirm `Self` is allowed in implementations referring to the type being implemented; because `Self` is a *type*, not a special parameter, it can sit in any type position inside the impl block, including non-receiver parameters; lesson 113 installed `other: &Counter` in this slot and lesson 100 installed `Self` in a return-type slot of an inherent impl, so today's `other: &Self` in a non-receiver slot of a trait *impl* method composes the two — same mechanic as 113, different spelling in the impl. Lesson 112's contract-matching rule (impl signature must match trait declaration exactly) is satisfied *post-resolution*: inside `impl Combine for Counter`, the impl's `&Self` resolves to `&Counter`, matching the trait declaration's `&Counter` exactly. Today centers the impl-side use only; framing `Self` from the trait-declaration side is its own move (Reference `paths.md:316-318` covers it; deferred). The equivalence is empirically witnessed by Probe 2 (the named-type variant with both sites `&Counter` produces byte-identical runtime output to Probe 1); outside any impl/trait/type-definition the `Self` alias does not exist, and the centered contrast probe demonstrates rustc fires E0411 with the inline label naming today's allowed-sites rule verbatim. Today is *only* the impl-site spelling change; nothing else about lesson 113's mechanic moves. The audience crosses from "I have seen `Self` in a return-type slot of an inherent impl (100), and `&Self` as the explicit form of `&self` (Reference `items/associated-items.md:113-122`); I have always written non-receiver reference parameters with the explicit named type" to "`Self` inside any impl is the contextual alias — Reference's exact phrase `items/associated-items.md:122`; it can sit in any type position inside the impl, including non-receiver parameters; `other: &Self` and `other: &Counter` inside `impl Combine for Counter` are interchangeable spellings of the same type; lesson 112's contract-matching rule applies *after* `Self` resolution; the rmp source uses both spellings inside the same file (`cmp.rs:5` `&BigUInt`, `cmp.rs:13, 19` `&Self`); outside the three Reference-allowed sites (impl, trait, type-def) `Self` does not exist as a type and rustc fires E0411." This is the directly-named deferral from lesson 113's *What To Ignore For Now*: "**`&Self` vs `&Counter` inside the impl** — same meaning here (`Self` is the type alias from lesson 100). The rmp source uses both; today centers the named-type form because it is directly readable." Today closes that deferral, scoped to the impl-site use. The unlock is reading rmp's `cmp.rs:13` `fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Option<std::cmp::Ordering>` and `cmp.rs:19` `fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering` — the impl-side *signatures* become readable; the bodies (`Some(self.cmp(other))` for partial_cmp; an iterator-chain for cmp) compose later moves.
depends_on: 113-reference-parameter-in-trait-method (load-bearing — installs `&Type` in a non-receiver parameter slot on a trait method, the matching `&value` call-site shape, and the "caller still owns" property; today substitutes `Self` for the named type `Counter` *only in the impl signature*; the mechanic is unchanged; lesson 113's *What To Ignore For Now* explicitly named this exact move as "`&Self` vs `&Counter` inside the impl" deferral — today closes it scoped to the impl site), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `Self` as a type alias inside an impl block per Book ch05-03:62-63 "Within an `impl` block, the type `Self` is an alias for the type that the `impl` block is for"; lesson 100 used the alias in a return-type slot of an inherent impl (`fn new() -> Self`); today extends the alias to a parameter-type slot inside a trait impl; the Reference `paths.md:316-322` covers both extensions — implementations include both inherent `impl Type { ... }` and trait `impl Trait for Type { ... }`, and the alias is allowed in either), 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing — installs the `impl Trait for Type { ... }` scaffold distinct from inherent `impl Type { ... }`; today's working probe uses this scaffold and the Self-alias rule applies inside it the same way per Reference `paths.md:316-322`; without 111 today's probe could not declare and impl the `Combine` trait), 112-trait-method-extra-parameter (load-bearing — installs the rule that the impl method signature must match the trait declaration exactly; today's centered move uses this rule *post-`Self`-resolution*: the impl's `&Self` resolves to `&Counter`, matching the trait declaration's `&Counter`; without 112 the post-resolution match would not be the relevant question), 095-struct-with-named-fields (cited — `struct Counter { count: u32 }` declaration, struct expression, field access; reused unchanged from 113), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `value.method(arg)`; the call-site argument is `&b` per 113), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to Probe 3's E0411 transcript; today extends the diagnostic-pattern catalog with E0411), 011-println-positional-args (cited — two `println!("... {}", expr)` calls in the working probe), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs && ./demo`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` entry point), 005-let-binding (cited — three `let` bindings in the probe), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — type-annotation slot machinery; not exercised on a binding today, but the `-> u32` return-type annotation rides this slot), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` field type and method return type), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the rmp `cmp.rs:13` line `fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Option<std::cmp::Ordering>` and `cmp.rs:19` line `fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering` — signatures become directly readable after today; the bodies compose later moves" moves, future "`&mut Self` in a non-receiver slot — composes lesson 101's `&mut self` into a parameter slot with today's alias mechanic; e.g., `fn merge(&mut self, other: &mut Self) -> ()`" moves, future "`Self` as a return type inside `impl Trait for Type { ... }` — `fn clone(&self) -> Self` etc.; raises the `Self: Sized` constraint and trait-object dispatch questions" moves, future "`Self::method()` self-references inside an impl body — calls an associated function on the implementing type via the alias; distinct mechanic from today's type-position use" moves, future "`where Self: Bound` clauses — composes today's `Self` with the trait-bounds arc named since lesson 114" moves, future "`Self` inside a trait declaration itself — Reference `paths.md:316-318` says the trait-declaration `Self` refers to the not-yet-known implementing type; trait declarations like `pub trait PartialEq<Rhs = Self>` use `Self` as a default type parameter; framing it from the declaration side is a separate move" moves, future "`&'a Self` lifetimes-on-Self — composes today with the lifetime arc that has been wholesale deferred since lesson 113" moves, future "`Self::AssociatedType` projections — `fn new() -> Self::Output` etc.; composes today with lesson 115's associated types" moves, future "the full E0411 catalogue — `Self` outside the three allowed sites in various forms (`Self::method` in a free function, `<Self>::foo` in fn main, etc.); today's contrast is one specific witness; the full E-code arc is its own move" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch05-03-method-syntax.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/paths.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/associated-items.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0411.md
  - /Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/120-self-in-non-receiver.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/120-self-in-non-receiver.transcript.txt

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 121-equality-on-ordering
move: compare two `Ordering` values with `==` and `!=`; concretely, write the working probe `use std::cmp::Ordering; fn main() { let a: Ordering = Ordering::Less; let b: Ordering = Ordering::Less; let c: Ordering = Ordering::Equal; println!("a == b is {}", a == b); println!("a == c is {}", a == c); println!("a != c is {}", a != c); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `a == b is true / a == c is false / a != c is true` (exit 0); the centered contrast declares a fresh user-side enum `enum Color { Red, Blue }` (no `#[derive(PartialEq)]`) and tries `==` on its values, witnessing `error[E0369]: binary operation \`==\` cannot be applied to type \`Color\`` with the inline label `note: an implementation of \`PartialEq\` might be missing for \`Color\`` and the `help:` block `consider annotating \`Color\` with \`#[derive(PartialEq)]\``; a corroborating probe (in evidence) sweeps a 3x3 matrix over `[Ordering::Less, Ordering::Equal, Ordering::Greater]` and confirms the diagonal reads `true` and the six off-diagonal cells read `false`. Today extends lesson 117's "==/!= via std's PartialEq impl" framing from `Vec<T>` to a unit-variant std enum.
main_concept: `==` and `!=` on `Ordering` are provided by std's `impl PartialEq for Ordering` (Ordering page lines 327-340) — the same desugar as lessons 013 and 117 (`a == b ≡ ::std::cmp::PartialEq::eq(&a, &b)` per Reference `expressions/operator-expr.md:508-516`), with a third dispatched body (Ordering's `eq` and the inherited `ne` default). Two `Ordering` values compare equal exactly when they pick the same variant; `!=` is the negation. The audience crosses from "`==` works on integers (013) and on `Vec<u64>` (117) because std implements `PartialEq` for both; I have not yet seen it on a standard-library *enum*" to "`==`/`!=` work on `Ordering` for the same structural reason — a `PartialEq` impl exists. The desugar is unchanged; the dispatched body is std's `Ordering` impl. Whether *any given* enum supports `==` is not automatic — it depends on whether someone (std for `Ordering`, the user via `#[derive(PartialEq)]` for their own enum) wrote the impl. A fresh user-declared enum without that impl fires `error[E0369]: binary operation \`==\` cannot be applied to type \`Color\``; the diagnostic's `note:` and `help:` name the missing piece directly." Today is shallow operational: install only that `==`/`!=` work on `Ordering`. The ordering operators `<`/`<=`/`>`/`>=` (PartialOrd-driven), the `Eq` marker, the `#[derive(PartialEq)]` mechanic for user enums, manual `impl PartialEq` writing, and `Copy` on `Ordering` (implicit in the corroborating matrix probe) are all named-deferred. The unlock is reading rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:21` line `if ord == cmp::Ordering::Equal { ... }` — `ord` is an `Ordering` value, the right-hand side is `Ordering`'s `Equal` variant constructor, and `==` is exactly today's mechanic.
depends_on: 117-vec-equality (load-bearing — installs `==`/`!=` on a non-primitive type via std's `PartialEq` impl, plus the desugar `a == b ≡ ::std::cmp::PartialEq::eq(&a, &b)`. Today reuses the framing verbatim, with `Ordering` substituted for `Vec<u64>`; the desugar and `bool` result are unchanged; only the dispatched impl body differs — std's `Ordering` impl rather than std's `Vec` impl), 051-ordering-enum-and-variant-match (load-bearing — installs `Ordering` as the std three-variant unit-only enum at path `std::cmp::Ordering`, with the variant-construction shape `Ordering::Less` and the `use std::cmp::Ordering;` line. Today reuses all three to bind three `Ordering` values for comparison), 013-comparison-operators (load-bearing — installs `==`/`!=` between two values of the same kind producing a `bool`. Today extends the operand types again to `Ordering`; the operator surface and result type are unchanged), 098-enum-with-unit-variants (load-bearing for the contrast — installs the user-side declaration `enum Name { V1, V2 }` for unit-variant enums. The contrast probe `enum Color { Red, Blue }` is exactly this shape; today's contrast relies on the *absence* of any `PartialEq` impl on the user-declared enum to provoke E0369), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — installs the `let name: TYPE = value;` annotation slot. Today fills the TYPE slot with `Ordering`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("a == b is {}", a == b)` etc. with one positional `{}` slot per call; the substituted expression is a `bool`), 044-use-declaration (cited — `use std::cmp::Ordering;` brings the type into scope), 005-let-binding (cited — three `let` bindings in the probe), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` entry of all probes), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs && ./demo` shape), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to the contrast probe's E0369 transcript), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — `ne` is `PartialEq`'s default-bodied method; the structural reason `!=` works alongside `==`), 022-for-range (cited — used by the corroborating matrix probe in evidence: `for i in 0..3`), 076-array-literal-and-type (cited — used by the corroborating probe: `[Ordering::Less, Ordering::Equal, Ordering::Greater]`), 077-array-indexing-and-usize (cited — used by the corroborating probe: `xs[i]`), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:21` `if ord == cmp::Ordering::Equal { ... }` line — the `==` half is readable today; the surrounding `if` block, the iterator chain in `cmp.rs:22` and the `cmp` method body still compose later moves" moves, future "`<`/`<=`/`>`/`>=` on `Ordering` via `PartialOrd` — Ordering page line 344 lists `impl PartialOrd for Ordering`; the operator-to-trait wiring (Reference `operator-expr.md:527-530`) is its own move; ordering operators are a sibling of today's equality move" moves, future "the `Eq` marker trait on `Ordering` — Ordering page line 385 `impl Eq for Ordering`; `Eq: PartialEq` structurally per lesson 116 framing. Today reads `==`, not `Eq`" moves, future "`#[derive(PartialEq)]` on a user-declared enum — the `help:` suggestion in today's contrast diagnostic; lets `==` work on the user enum without writing the impl by hand" moves, future "manual `impl PartialEq for MyType` — wholesale deferred; the rmp `cmp.rs:4-8` `impl PartialEq<BigUInt> for BigUInt` is exactly this shape (lesson 117/118 already noted it)" moves, future "`==`/`!=` on `Result`, `Option`, generic enums beyond `Ordering` — each composes today's `PartialEq`-via-impl mechanic with a separate generic enum's PartialEq impl" moves, future "`Copy` on `Ordering` (and on user enums) — implicit in today's corroborating matrix probe, deferred; the marker that lets indexing-read-and-compare like `xs[i] == xs[j]` compile without moves" moves, future "the full `PartialEq<Rhs = Self>` machinery — `?Sized`, the default type parameter `= Self`, the `ne` default-body, and the trait-bounds arc — same span lesson 117 deferred, still deferred" moves, future "the full E0369 catalogue — `<<` on f32, `+` on user types without `Add`, `==` on user structs without PartialEq, etc.; today's contrast is one specific witness for `==` on a user enum without PartialEq" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/cmp/enum.Ordering.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.PartialEq.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/operator-expr.md

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 122-self-method-delegation
move: call a sibling method on the same type from inside a method body via the dot-form `self.method(args)`; concretely, in the working probe `struct Counter { n: u32 } impl Counter { fn doubled(&self) -> u32 { self.n * 2 } fn quadrupled(&self) -> u32 { self.doubled() * 2 } } fn main() { let c = Counter { n: 7 }; println!("doubled    = {}", c.doubled()); println!("quadrupled = {}", c.quadrupled()); }`, the line `self.doubled() * 2` inside `quadrupled`'s body fills the *receiver* slot of lesson 040's dot-call shape `value.method(args)` with the implicit `&self` parameter; compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo`, prints exactly `doubled    = 14 / quadrupled = 28` (exit 0); the centered contrast `broken.rs` drops the `fn doubled` definition entirely while keeping `quadrupled`'s body unchanged, and rustc fires `error[E0599]: no method named \`doubled\` found for reference \`&Counter\` in the current scope` with the inline label `method not found in \`&Counter\`` and the caret landing at line 5 column 14 *inside* `quadrupled`'s body (not at a `main`-side call); the corroborating probe `path.rs` swaps the dot form for the named-type path form `Counter::doubled(self) * 2` and produces byte-identical runtime output (`diff <(./demo | tail -1) <(./path)` exits 0). Today opens *one* new context for the existing dot-call grammar — no new mechanic.
main_concept: `self.method(args)` inside another method body on the same impl is exactly lesson 040's dot-call shape `value.method(args)` with the implicit `self` parameter filling the receiver slot — the Reference `expressions/method-call-expr.md:13-14` requires "an expression (the *receiver*)" in that slot, and the `self` parameter is an expression of type `&Self` / `&mut Self` / `Self` per the receiver shape (Book ch05-03:62 "The `&self` is actually short for `self: &Self`"). Method-name lookup is unchanged: rustc looks for `method` on the type of the receiver (the type of `self`), and if no such method exists on that type, the same E0599 lesson 100 installed fires — *pointing into the method body now*, not into `main`. The visible trace of the `&self` shorthand expansion is the diagnostic's choice of the type name `&Counter` (not bare `Counter`) in the inline label "method not found in \`&Counter\`". Today is the dot-form delegation only; the path-form analog `Counter::doubled(self)` is corroborated empirically (byte-identical output) but stays deferred — the named-type variant is one half of an old deferral from lesson 100, and the `Self::method()` shorthand stays deferred for the same lesson. The audience crosses from "I can call methods that std wrote on integer values (lesson 040), and I can call methods I authored from `main` via `c.method()` (lesson 100); I have not seen one method on a type call another method on the same type" to "Inside a method body, `self` is a value of type `&Self` (or `&mut Self`, or `Self`, depending on the receiver). It fits in the receiver slot of the dot-call exactly the way `c` did in `main`. One method calls a sibling method via `self.sibling(args)`; the lookup rule is unchanged; if the sibling does not exist on the type of `self`, the same E0599 fires with the diagnostic pointed inside the method body and the inline label naming `&Counter` (the `&self` shorthand expansion as a visible trace)." This makes rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:14` line `Some(self.cmp(other))` directly readable end-to-end as today's mechanic + lesson 119's `Option::Some` constructor: `self.cmp(other)` inside `partial_cmp`'s body calls the sibling method `cmp` on the same `self`, returning `Ordering`; `Some(...)` wraps it.
depends_on: 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — installs the dot-call shape `value.method(args)`: receiver expression, dot, method name, parenthesized argument list. Today fills the receiver slot with `self` instead of a `let`-bound name; the shape is unchanged), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs inherent `impl Type { ... }` with `&self` as shorthand for `self: &Self` and `Self` as the impl-target alias, plus the call-site `c.current()` from `main` and the E0599 diagnostic shape for missing `&self` methods. Today's impl block contains two methods, one calling the other; the contrast probe surfaces E0599 from inside the method body rather than from `main`), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — `struct Counter { n: u32 }` declaration, struct expression `Counter { n: 7 }`, and field access `self.n` are all reused inside the impl bodies), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `*` on integers; used in both bodies as `self.n * 2` and `self.doubled() * 2`), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` field type and method return type), 011-println-positional-args (cited — two `println!("... = {}", expr)` calls in the working probe), 005-let-binding (cited — `let c = Counter { n: 7 };`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` entry of the probes), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs && ./demo`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic four-part map applied to Probe 2's E0599 transcript), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — the `: TYPE` annotation slot, reused as the `-> u32` return-type slot, lesson 021's mechanic), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:14` `Some(self.cmp(other))` line — readable end-to-end after today + lesson 119's `Option::Some`; `self.cmp(other)` is exactly today's mechanic on the rmp `BigUInt` impl, with `cmp` as the sibling method declared by `impl Ord for BigUInt` and `other` as the parameter binding" moves, future "the path-form self-reference `Counter::method(self)` and its `Self::method(self)` shorthand — corroborated empirically in today's appendix; the named-type form follows from lesson 100 (named there as deferred); the `Self::` shorthand stays deferred for lesson 100" moves, future "delegation in `&mut self` method bodies (`self.foo_mut()` inside `&mut self`) — composes lesson 101 with today's mechanic" moves, future "delegation in `self` by-value method bodies — composes lesson 102; the receiver in the body has type `Self`, not `&Self`" moves, future "trait-method delegation — calling another trait method from a trait method body. Composes lessons 111-116 with today's mechanic" moves, future "recursive self-calls (`self.foo()` calling `foo` itself directly or through a cycle) — composes today with control-flow termination questions" moves, future "method delegation across multiple `impl` blocks on the same type — sibling methods declared in two different `impl Type { ... }` blocks. Same lookup rule, larger scope" moves, future "the auto-deref/auto-ref step in method-call resolution (Reference `method-call-expr.md:39`) — the rule that lets `c.doubled()` and `Counter::doubled(self)` both compile when `doubled` takes `&self`. Already deferred from lesson 100" moves, future "the chained method form `self.field.method().method()` (rmp `cmp.rs:20` `self.limbs.len().cmp(&other.limbs.len())`) — composes today's `self.field` access with lesson 049's chaining" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch05-03-method-syntax.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/method-call-expr.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/associated-items.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0599.md
  - output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/122-self-method-delegation.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/122-self-method-delegation.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/122-self-method-delegation.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 123-vec-iter
move: walk a `Vec<T>`'s elements with `v.iter()` consumed by a `for`-loop; concretely, in the working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; for x in v.iter() { println!("{}", x); } }`, the call expression `v.iter()` (the lesson-040 dot-call shape with `v` as receiver, `iter` as the method name, and an empty argument list) returns a value of type `std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>` — an iterator yielding each element of the vec in order — and the for-loop from lesson 079 (whose COLLECTION slot is now extended from arrays to iterators returned by `.iter()`) runs the body once per yielded element, binding `x` to a `&u64` reference; compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo`, prints exactly `10 / 20 / 30` (exit 0, three lines, in vec order); the centered contrast `broken.rs` (`fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let bad: u64 = v.iter(); }`) treats the iterator as if it were a `u64` and rustc fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with the inline label `expected u64, found Iter<'_, u64>` and the `note:` block `found struct \`std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>\``, witnessing that the call's actual type is the iterator struct, not a `u64` element; a corroborating probe in evidence runs the same shape on a different vec (`Vec<u64>` of length 2 with values `[100, 200]`) and confirms the mechanic generalizes; a type-witness probe `let _: &u64 = x;` inside the working loop is accepted by rustc, witnessing `x: &u64`; an alternative-shape probe `for x in v` (no `.iter()`) compiles and produces byte-identical output, but a follow-up `for x in v; println!("len after = {}", v.len());` fires E0382 with the diagnostic naming `for x in v` as "an implicit call to `.into_iter()` that moves `v`" — surfacing that `v.iter()` and bare `for x in v` are *different* shapes (the former borrows, the latter consumes; ownership semantics deferred). Today opens *one* iterator-producing method; the chained adapters and the trait machinery compose later moves.
main_concept: `Vec<T>::iter()` (inherited from `[T]` via `Deref<Target=[T]>` per `std/vec/struct.Vec.md:6970-6982`; the explicit method signature is `pub fn iter(&self) -> Iter<'_, T>` per `std/primitive.slice.md:1434` "Returns an iterator over the slice. The iterator yields all items from start to end.") is the canonical method for producing an iterator that walks a `Vec<T>` element-by-element. The return type is `std::slice::Iter<'_, T>` whose associated `Item` type is `&'a T` per `std/slice/struct.Iter.md:199`; consequently the for-loop binds the loop variable to a *reference* `&T`, not the element itself. For `T: Display` (lesson 080's `u64` qualifies), `println!("{}", x)` for `x: &T` works via std's blanket impl `impl<T: ?Sized + Display> Display for &T` (named-deferred; the appendix names the rule but does not re-derive). The for-loop machinery from lesson 079 accepts the iterator in its COLLECTION slot via the `IntoIterator` impl on `Iter<'_, T>` (every `Iterator` is `IntoIterator` via std's blanket impl; named-deferred). Reference `loop-expr.md:208` verbatim: "A `for` expression is a syntactic construct for looping over elements provided by an implementation of `std::iter::IntoIterator`." The audience crosses from "I have `vec![]`, `.len()`, `v[i]` (lesson 107) and `for x in array` (lesson 079); I have no installed shape that walks every element of a `Vec<T>`" to "I write `v.iter()` to get an iterator over the vec, drop it into the same `for X in COLLECTION { ... }` shape, and the loop runs once per element. The yielded value is a reference, not the element. The actual return type of `v.iter()` is named `Iter<'_, T>`; binding it where a `T` is expected fires E0308 with `expected T, found Iter<'_, T>` — the centered contrast." `.iter()` is one specific iterator-producing method on `Vec<T>`; the consuming `.into_iter()` and mutable-reference `.iter_mut()` siblings, the `for x in &v` and `for x in v` shorthand desugars, the `.next()` pull form, the `Iterator` trait machinery (`type Item;`, `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>;`), the `IntoIterator` trait, iterator lifetimes (`'_`, `'a`), and all iterator adapters (`.rev()`, `.zip()`, `.map()`, `.filter()`, `.collect()`, `.fold()`, `.sum()`, `.enumerate()`) are wholesale or named-deferred. The unlock is reading the *first link* of rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` line `self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())`: `self.limbs` is field access on a `Vec<u64>`, and `.iter()` is exactly today's mechanic; the chained `.rev()` and `.zip()` compose later moves.
depends_on: 107-vec-basics (load-bearing — installs `Vec<T>` construction with `vec![]` and the `Vec<u64>` annotation; lesson 107's "What To Ignore" explicitly named `v.iter()` as the deferred sibling, which today installs), 079-for-over-array (load-bearing — installs `for X in COLLECTION { ... }` over a runtime collection. Today extends the COLLECTION slot from arrays to the iterator returned by `.iter()`; the loop-runs-once-per-element rule and the binding semantics are unchanged), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — `v.iter()` is the dot-call shape with empty argument list. Today fills the receiver slot with a `Vec<u64>` value), 011-println-positional-args (load-bearing — `println!("{}", x)` with one positional `{}`; today's `x` has type `&u64` and prints via std's blanket Display-for-&T impl, named-deferred), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u64` element type), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — `: TYPE` annotation slot, used as `Vec<u64>`), 005-let-binding (cited — `let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30];` and the equivalents in every probe), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` entry of every probe), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs && ./demo`, silent on success), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic four-part map applied to Probe 2's E0308 transcript), 095-struct-with-named-fields (cited — only in the rmp-unlock framing for `self.limbs.iter()` where `self.limbs` is field access; not used in today's centered probe), 022-for-range (cited — original for-loop shape that 079 and now 123 progressively extend), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` `self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())` line — the first link `self.limbs.iter()` is exactly today's mechanic on a `Vec<u64>` field; the chained `.rev()` composes lesson 091's range-reversal mechanic on a different receiver type; the `.zip()` adapter is its own move; the `for (left, right) in ...` destructuring on iterator output composes lesson 073's tuple destructuring with iterator yield" moves, future "`.iter_mut()` — the mutable-reference sibling. Yields `&mut T`; usable in `for x in v.iter_mut()` to modify each element in place. Composes lesson 047's `&mut` references with today's mechanic" moves, future "`.into_iter()` — the consuming sibling. Yields `T` (taking ownership of the vec). The `for x in v` shorthand (Probe 5/6 in today's appendix) is the desugared form" moves, future "`for x in &v` and `for x in v` shorthand desugars — the `IntoIterator` impls on `&Vec<T>`, `&mut Vec<T>`, and `Vec<T>` make these three shapes compose with the for-loop directly, without an explicit `.iter()` / `.iter_mut()` / `.into_iter()` call. Today centers the explicit form" moves, future "`.next()` on an iterator — the explicit pull form. Each call returns `Option<&T>` (`Some(&first)`, `Some(&second)`, ..., `None`). Composes lesson 119's `Option::Some` with the iterator machinery" moves, future "the `Iterator` trait — `type Item;`, `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>;`, the contract that every iterator implements. Lessons 111-116 installed enough trait machinery to read this declaration; today names it as deferred" moves, future "the `IntoIterator` trait — the conversion trait that lets `for X in COLLECTION` accept ranges, arrays, vecs, references, and iterators uniformly. Reference `loop-expr.md:208` cites it; today reads only the rule, not the trait" moves, future "iterator adapters — `.rev()` on iterators (different receiver type than lesson 091's range), `.zip()`, `.map()`, `.filter()`, `.fold()`, `.sum()`, `.collect()`, `.enumerate()`. Each composes on the output of `.iter()` but is its own move" moves, future "`.iter()` on slices `&v[a..b]` and on arrays — same method shape, different receivers; arrays now have the same explicit `.iter()` form as vecs and slices. Today centers `Vec<T>` only" moves, future "iterator lifetimes — the `'_` and `'a` in `Iter<'_, T>`. Wholesale deferred; today's diagnostic surfaces `Iter<'_, u64>` only as a type-name fact, not exercised mechanically" moves, future "the `Iter<'a, T>` struct itself — surfaced only in today's diagnostic. Listing the fields, methods, and trait impls is its own move; today the type name is opaque" moves, future "ownership-and-iteration interaction — the structural difference between `for x in v.iter()` (borrows v), `for x in v.iter_mut()` (mutably borrows v), and `for x in v` (moves v). Today's appendix Probe 6 surfaces the move via E0382 but the lesson body does not center it" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/primitive.slice.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/vec/struct.Vec.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/slice/struct.Iter.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch13-02-iterators.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/loop-expr.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/123-vec-iter.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/123-vec-iter.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/123-vec-iter.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 124-iter-rev
move: walk a `Vec<T>`'s elements back-to-front by chaining `.rev()` on the iterator returned by `.iter()`; concretely, in the working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; for x in v.iter().rev() { println!("{}", x); } }`, the chain `v.iter().rev()` is the lesson-049 chained-dot-call shape — `v.iter()` (lesson 123, returns `std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>`) followed by `.rev()` on that iterator value (lesson 040 dot-call grammar, empty argument list) — and the for-loop from lesson 079 walks the resulting iterator binding `x: &u64`; compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo`, prints exactly `30 / 20 / 10` (exit 0, three lines, vec order *reversed*); the order-swap contrast probe drops `.rev()` from the same source, prints `10 / 20 / 30`, and `diff` between the two outputs surfaces only the ordering difference (same three elements); the centered diagnostic contrast `broken.rs` (`fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let r = v.rev(); println!("{:?}", r); }`) calls `.rev()` on the `Vec<u64>` directly and rustc fires `error[E0599]: no method named \`rev\` found for struct \`Vec<u64>\` in the current scope` with the inline label `\`Vec<u64>\` is not an iterator` and a `help: call \`.into_iter()\` first` line — verbatim witness that `.rev()` is a method on iterators, not on `Vec<T>`; a corroborating probe runs the same shape on `Vec<u64>` of length 5 (`[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`) and prints `5 / 4 / 3 / 2 / 1`, witnessing the mechanic generalizes. Today opens the *second* call site of the rmp `cmp.rs:22` chain (`.rev()` on `self.limbs.iter()`); `.zip(...)` and the `for (left, right) in ...` destructuring compose later moves.
main_concept: `.rev()` (the same method-name installed on a parenthesized range in lesson 091) is callable on the iterator returned by `Vec<T>::iter()` — concretely on `std::slice::Iter<'_, T>` — and produces a new iterator that yields the same elements in reversed order. The std signature is `fn rev(self) -> Rev<Self> where Self: Sized + DoubleEndedIterator` per `std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:2990`; the page's prose is verbatim "Reverses an iterator's direction. ... After using `rev()`, an iterator will instead iterate from right to left" (lines 2992-2995). The bound `where Self: Sized + DoubleEndedIterator` is satisfied for `Iter<'_, T>` via `std/slice/struct.Iter.md:131-133` `impl<'a, T> DoubleEndedIterator for Iter<'a, T>`. The `DoubleEndedIterator` trait is named-deferred today (consistent with lesson 091's deferral); the empirical handle is "the receiver type changed (range to slice-iterator); the semantics did not." The audience crosses from "I know `.rev()` reverses a *range* (lesson 091) and I know `.iter()` produces an iterator on a `Vec<T>` (lesson 123); I have not seen `.rev()` applied to anything except a range" to "`.rev()` is the same method name. It works on the iterator from `.iter()`. The chain `v.iter().rev()` is exactly the lesson-049 chained-dot-call shape with empty argument lists at each step; the for-loop walks the elements back-to-front; the binding `x` still has type `&T`. The boundary witness is E0599 on `v.rev()` directly: `\`Vec<u64>\` is not an iterator` — `.rev()` is on iterators, not on the vec." The return type `Rev<Iter<'_, T>>` (per `std/iter/struct.Rev.md`) is named-deferred; the `help:` line's `v.into_iter().rev()` is the consuming variant deferred from lesson 123. `.rev()` on iterator adapters that lose `DoubleEndedIterator` is wholesale deferred. The unlock is the *second link* of rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` line `self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())`: lessons 123 + 124 together make `self.limbs.iter().rev()` readable end-to-end; `.zip(...)` and `for (left, right) in ...` destructuring compose later moves.
depends_on: 123-vec-iter (load-bearing — installs `v.iter()` returning `Iter<'_, T>` whose iteration order is vec order yielding `&T`. Today chains `.rev()` on that result; the receiver of `.rev()` is the value lesson 123 named), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `.rev()` as a name-and-use atom that reverses iteration order, witnessed there on a parenthesized range. Today extends the *same method-name* to a different receiver type — slice iterator instead of range. The trait machinery deferred by 091 stays deferred today), 049-method-chaining (load-bearing — installs the chained-dot-call shape `expr.method1().method2()` where each call's return becomes the next call's receiver. `v.iter().rev()` fills the shape with empty argument lists at each step), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call grammar; reused at both calls in the chain), 107-vec-basics (cited — `Vec<T>` construction with `vec![]` and the `Vec<u64>` annotation), 079-for-over-array (cited — `for X in COLLECTION { ... }`; today's COLLECTION is the chained iterator), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (cited — installed the E0599 "no method named X found for type Y" diagnostic shape that Probe 3 reuses on a `Vec<u64>` receiver), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", x)` with one positional `{}`; `x: &u64` prints via the blanket `Display for &T` impl named-deferred in lesson 123), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u64` element type), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — `: TYPE` annotation slot, used as `Vec<u64>`), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic four-part map applied to Probe 3's E0599 transcript), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` `self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())` line — lessons 123 + 124 together make `self.limbs.iter().rev()` readable end-to-end; the `.zip(...)` adapter is its own move; the `for (left, right) in ...` destructuring on iterator output composes lesson 073's tuple destructuring with iterator yield" moves, future "`.zip(other.iter())` adapter — combines two iterators into an iterator of pairs. Composes today's mechanic with a binary adapter; rmp's chain uses it to align corresponding limbs of two BigUInt operands" moves, future "`for (a, b) in iter1.zip(iter2)` destructuring on iterator output — composes lesson 073's tuple destructuring with the for-loop binding slot when the iterator yields tuples" moves, future "the `DoubleEndedIterator` trait declaration — `fn next_back(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>` and the rule that `.rev()` requires this trait. Lessons 091 and 124 named it as deferred; lessons 111-116 installed enough trait machinery to read the declaration" moves, future "the `Rev<I>` adapter struct — the type returned by `.rev()`. Surfaced in the std signature only today" moves, future "`.rev()` on adapter chains that lose `DoubleEndedIterator` (e.g., a hypothetical `.filter().rev()`) — fires E0599 because the adapter's output type is not double-ended. Boundary case wholesale deferred" moves, future "other iterator adapters — `.map()`, `.filter()`, `.fold()`, `.sum()`, `.collect()`, `.enumerate()`, etc. Each composes on the output of `.iter()` but is its own move" moves, future "`v.into_iter().rev()` — the consuming variant suggested by today's E0599 `help:` line. Composes lesson 123's named-deferred `.into_iter()` with today's `.rev()`" moves, future "materializing a reversed `Vec<T>` — `v.iter().rev().collect()` or `.cloned().collect()`. Composes today with `.collect()`, deferred" moves, future "`.rev()` on slice iterators from `[T]` and from arrays — same trait, different receivers; today centers the `Vec<T>` case via 107" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
```

```text
id: 125-iter-zip
move: walk two `Vec<T>` values in lockstep by chaining `.zip(...)` on the iterator returned by `.iter()`, with the second iterator as argument; concretely, in the working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let w: Vec<u64> = vec![100, 200, 300]; for pair in v.iter().zip(w.iter()) { println!("{} / {}", pair.0, pair.1); } }`, the chain `v.iter().zip(w.iter())` is the lesson-049 chained-dot-call shape — `v.iter()` (lesson 123, returns `std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>`) followed by `.zip(...)` on that iterator value (lesson 040 dot-call grammar, *one* argument inside the parentheses, here itself the call expression `w.iter()` returning a second `Iter<'_, u64>`) — and the for-loop from lesson 079 walks the resulting iterator binding `pair` to a 2-tuple of references whose parts are read by lesson 072's tuple indexing `pair.0` and `pair.1` (each `&u64` per Probe 5's type-witness `let _: &u64 = pair.0;` accepted by rustc); compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo`, prints exactly `10 / 100`, `20 / 200`, `30 / 300` (exit 0, three lines, vec order, paired position-wise); the centered diagnostic contrast `broken.rs` (`fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let w: Vec<u64> = vec![100, 200, 300]; let z = v.zip(w); println!("{:?}", z); }`) calls `.zip()` on the `Vec<u64>` directly and rustc fires `error[E0599]: no method named \`zip\` found for struct \`Vec<u64>\` in the current scope` with the inline label `\`Vec<u64>\` is not an iterator` and a `help: call \`.into_iter()\` first` line — verbatim witness that `.zip()` is a method on iterators, not on `Vec<T>`, exactly mirroring lesson 124's `v.rev()` contrast with the method name swapped; semantic-contrast Probes 3 and 4 in the appendix witness the shortest-source rule (length 3 zipped against length 1 yields exactly one pair `10 / 100`; length 3 against length 0 yields zero pairs and the for-loop body never runs); a corroborating probe runs the same shape on `Vec<u64>` of length 2 with `pair.0 + pair.1` arithmetic on `&u64` and prints two paired sums, witnessing the mechanic generalizes. Today opens the *third* call site of the rmp `cmp.rs:22` chain (`.zip(...)` on `.iter().rev()`); the `for (left, right) in ...` tuple destructuring on iterator output is the next move.
main_concept: `.zip(other)` is a method on iterators (defined on the `Iterator` trait, name-deferred) that takes another iterator-like value as argument and returns a new iterator whose yielded values are 2-tuples paired position-wise from the two sources. The std signature is `fn zip<U>(self, other: U) -> Zip<Self, <U as IntoIterator>::IntoIter> where Self: Sized, U: IntoIterator` per `std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:664`; the page's prose is verbatim "'Zips up' two iterators into a single iterator of pairs ... returning a tuple where the first element comes from the first iterator, and the second element comes from the second iterator. ... If either iterator returns `None`, `next` from the zipped iterator will return `None`" (lines 666-675). For `v: Vec<u64>` and `w: Vec<u64>`, the chain `v.iter().zip(w.iter())` produces an iterator whose yielded tuple type is `(&u64, &u64)` (Probe 5's type-witness), and the `for`-loop from lesson 079 binds each yielded tuple to `pair`, with `pair.0` and `pair.1` reading the references via lesson 072's tuple indexing. The `where U: IntoIterator` bound permits arrays, ranges, vecs, and `Iterator` values themselves to appear in the argument slot; today's probe uses an `Iterator` directly (`w.iter()`), satisfying the bound trivially. The bound is named-deferred. The shortest-source rule is the load-bearing semantic claim: when sources differ in length, the zipped iterator yields min(|A|, |B|) tuples and stops; Probes 3 and 4 witness it at length 1 and length 0 respectively. The audience crosses from "I know `.iter()` produces an iterator on a `Vec<T>` (lesson 123) and I know `.rev()` chains as an empty-argument iterator method on that result (lesson 124); to walk two vecs together I would need an index loop with arithmetic on `v[i]` and `w[i]`" to "`.zip(...)` is another iterator method, but it takes one argument: a *second* iterator. The chain `v.iter().zip(w.iter())` produces an iterator whose yielded value is a 2-tuple — the next pair from the two sources. I read the elements with `pair.0` and `pair.1` (lesson 072). Iteration stops when *either* source runs out. The boundary witness is E0599 on `v.zip(w)` directly: `\`Vec<u64>\` is not an iterator` — `.zip()` is on iterators, not on the vec." The return type `Zip<Iter<'_, T>, Iter<'_, U>>` (per `std/iter/struct.Zip.md`) is named-deferred. For-pattern destructuring `for (left, right) in iter` (rmp's `cmp.rs:22` form) and the `Iterator` / `IntoIterator` trait machinery are wholesale or named-deferred. The unlock is the *third link* of rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` line `self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())`: lessons 123 + 124 + 125 together make the entire method-chain on the right of `for ... in` readable; the destructuring `for (left, right) in ...` composes future moves.
depends_on: 123-vec-iter (load-bearing — installs `v.iter()` returning `Iter<'_, T>` yielding `&T`. Today calls `.iter()` twice — once on each vec — and `.zip(...)` consumes the first iterator and takes the second as argument), 072-tuple-type-and-index (load-bearing — installs the tuple type `(A, B)` and field access by `.0` / `.1`. Today's `pair` is a 2-tuple value yielded by the zipped iterator; `pair.0` and `pair.1` read its parts via lesson 072's mechanic, with Probe 5's type-witness confirming both parts are `&u64`), 049-method-chaining (load-bearing — installs the chained-dot-call shape `expr.method1().method2(arg)` where each call's return becomes the next call's receiver. `v.iter().zip(w.iter())` fills the shape with empty argument list at the first call and one argument at the second), 124-iter-rev (cited — structurally parallel: both `.rev()` and `.zip()` are iterator methods chained after `.iter()`. Lesson 124's E0599 contrast on `v.rev()` directly is exactly today's contrast shape with the method name swapped from `rev` to `zip`; the diagnostic structure including the inline label "`Vec<u64>` is not an iterator" and the `help: call \`.into_iter()\` first` line is identical), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call grammar; reused at every call in today's chain), 107-vec-basics (cited — `Vec<T>` construction with `vec![]` and the `Vec<u64>` annotation), 079-for-over-array (cited — `for X in COLLECTION { ... }`; today's COLLECTION is the chained iterator returned by `.zip(...)`), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (cited — installed the E0599 "no method named X found for type Y" diagnostic shape that Probe 2 reuses on a `Vec<u64>` receiver with `zip` as the method), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{} / {}", pair.0, pair.1)` with two positional `{}` slots; same one-arg-per-slot rule lesson 011 installed), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u64` element type), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — `: TYPE` annotation slot, used as `Vec<u64>`), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic four-part map applied to Probe 2's E0599 transcript), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` `for (left, right) in self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev()) { ... }` line — lessons 123 + 124 + 125 together make the entire iterator chain on the right of `for ... in` readable; the remaining work is the for-pattern destructuring `for (left, right) in ...` that binds the yielded tuple's parts directly in the for-loop binding slot" moves, future "for-pattern destructuring `for (a, b) in iter` — composes lesson 073's tuple destructuring with the for-loop binding slot when the iterator yields tuples. The audience-level move is: instead of `for pair in iter { ... pair.0 ... pair.1 ... }`, write `for (a, b) in iter { ... a ... b ... }` and use the bound names directly" moves, future "the `Iterator` trait declaration including `.zip()` as a default method — `fn zip<U>(self, other: U) -> Zip<Self, ...>` lives on `Iterator` and is callable on every type that implements the trait. Lessons 111-116 installed enough trait machinery to read the declaration; today named it as deferred" moves, future "the `IntoIterator` trait — the `where U: IntoIterator` bound on `.zip()`'s argument lets you pass arrays, ranges, vecs, references, and iterators uniformly. Today's probe uses an `Iterator` directly which satisfies the bound trivially; the broader rule is named-deferred" moves, future "the `Zip<A, B>` adapter struct — the type returned by `.zip()`. Surfaced in the std signature only today" moves, future "`.zip(...)` chained after other iterator adapters (e.g., `.iter().rev().zip(...)`) — the rmp source chains three iterator methods. Composes 123 + 124 + 125 step by step; rmp's `cmp.rs:22` is the centered example" moves, future "other iterator adapters — `.map()`, `.filter()`, `.fold()`, `.collect()`, `.enumerate()`, etc. Each composes on the output of `.iter()` or `.zip(...)` but is its own move" moves, future "`std::iter::zip(a, b)` free function — the lesson `Iterator::zip` page references it as a more readable form when both arguments have similar syntax. Composes today with use-statement and free-function call form, deferred" moves, future "`.zip(...)` semantics on infinite iterators — the std doc page mentions zipping `(0..)` (infinite range) with a finite iterator as a common idiom equivalent to `.enumerate()`. Today does not exercise infinite iterators" moves, future "auto-deref / `Add for &u64` — Probe 6 uses `pair.0 + pair.1` (each `&u64`) and it compiles. Why this works at the trait-impl level is std machinery; today witnesses the empirical fact only" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Zip.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0599.md
  - output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/125-iter-zip.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/125-iter-zip.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/125-iter-zip.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 126-for-pattern-destructuring
move: destructure each yielded tuple in a for-loop binding slot by writing a tuple pattern `(a, b)` in place of a single identifier; concretely, in the working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let w: Vec<u64> = vec![100, 200, 300]; for (a, b) in v.iter().zip(w.iter()) { println!("{} / {}", a, b); } }`, the for-loop's binding slot holds the lesson-073 tuple pattern `(a, b)` rather than lesson 125's single name `pair`; the zipped iterator (lesson 125) yields three 2-tuples in vec order `(&10, &100)`, `(&20, &200)`, `(&30, &300)`, and each pass `a` is bound to the first part and `b` to the second (Probe 5's type-witness `let _: &u64 = a;` and `let _: &u64 = b;` accepted by rustc shows both bindings have type `&u64` directly, no `.0`/`.1` indexing); compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo`, prints exactly `10 / 100`, `20 / 200`, `30 / 300` (exit 0, three lines, byte-identical to lesson 125's working probe per Probe 4's `diff old.out new.out` exiting 0); the centered diagnostic contrast `broken_pair.rs` (`fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let w: Vec<u64> = vec![100, 200, 300]; for (a, b) in v.iter().zip(w.iter()) { println!("{:?}", pair); } }`) references the original tuple name `pair` in the loop body and rustc fires `error[E0425]: cannot find value \`pair\` in this scope` with caret under `pair` and inline label `not found in this scope` — verbatim witness that the destructuring pattern *replaces* the single-name binding; the parts `a` and `b` are bound but the original tuple is not. The alternative type-shape contrast `broken_type.rs` (`fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; for (a, b) in v.iter() { println!("{} / {}", a, b); } }`) applies a 2-tuple pattern to `v.iter()` directly which yields `&u64` and rustc fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with the inline label "this is an iterator with items of type `&u64`" and caret-pointed annotation `expected \`u64\`, found \`(_, _)\``. The count-mismatch corroborator (Probe 6) `triple_pattern.rs` applies a 3-tuple pattern to the 2-tuple-yielding iterator and rustc fires E0308 with the verbatim lesson-073 message `expected a tuple with 2 elements, found one with 3 elements` — same E-code, same inline annotation, applied at the for-binding slot rather than the let-binding slot. Today opens the *fourth* element of the rmp `cmp.rs:22` line — the for-binding pattern slot. With lessons 123 + 124 + 125 + 126 the entire `for (left, right) in self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())` head is readable end-to-end; the iterator chain on the right of `for ... in` parses by lessons 123, 124, 125, and the destructuring pattern on the left parses by today's move with `(a, b)` swapped to `(left, right)`.
main_concept: the for-loop's binding slot is grammatically a *pattern*, not just a single identifier; the Reference's iterator-loop syntax is `for [Pattern](../patterns.md#grammar-Pattern) in [Expression](../expressions.md#grammar-Expression)except [StructExpression] [BlockExpression]` per `output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/loop-expr.md:199-202`, and the runtime rule per line 212 is "If the iterator yields a value, that value is matched against the irrefutable pattern, the body of the loop is executed, and then control returns to the head of the `for` loop." When the iterator yields tuples, a tuple pattern `(a, b)` (lesson 073) fits the slot, destructuring each yielded tuple into one binding per part — `a` bound to the first, `b` to the second, both available as identifiers in the loop body. The pattern's name count must equal the tuple's field count (lesson 073's mechanic, unchanged), and the pattern must be *irrefutable* — always match the value (Reference patterns.md:137-146 with example `let (x, y) = (1, 2);  // "(x, y)" is an irrefutable pattern`). The word "irrefutable" is name-deferred today; the operational rule is "the for-binding pattern must be one rustc can match unconditionally." The Book's chapter 19 spells this out at audience level: "in `for x in y`, the `x` is the pattern" (`output/docs/rust/book/ch19-01-all-the-places-for-patterns.md:237-238`), and Listing 19-5 is precisely today's shape applied to `.enumerate()`: `for (index, value) in v.iter().enumerate() { println!("{value} is at index {index}"); }`. The audience crosses from "lesson 073 lets me write `let (a, b) = pair;` to split a tuple at the let-binding step; lesson 079's `for X in COLLECTION` puts a single name in `X`; to read 2-tuples from `.zip()` (lesson 125) I bind the whole tuple to one name and use `pair.0` / `pair.1`" to "the for-loop's binding slot is a *pattern*, just like the left of `let`. When the iterator yields tuples I can put a tuple pattern there: `for (a, b) in iter` binds `a` and `b` directly to the parts of each yielded tuple. The whole-tuple name is gone; only the parts are bound. The pattern's shape must match the yielded value — wrong shape fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types`, same as lesson 073's contrast, applied at the for-binding slot." Refutable patterns at the for-binding slot (e.g., `for Some(x) in iter`), other pattern shapes (struct, enum, slice), wildcard `_` inside the pattern (`for (_, b) in iter`), nested tuple patterns (`for ((a, b), c) in iter`), `mut` inside the pattern, and type annotations on the pattern (`for (a, b): (&u64, &u64) in iter`) are all wholesale or named-deferred. The unlock is the *fourth* element of rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` line `for (left, right) in self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev())`: lessons 123 + 124 + 125 + 126 together make the entire for-loop head readable end-to-end. The iterator chain (right of `in`) parses by 123/124/125; the for-binding pattern (left of `in`) parses by today's move.
depends_on: 073-let-tuple-destructure (load-bearing — installs the tuple pattern `(a, b)` parenthesized-and-comma-separated mirroring a tuple value's shape, with one binding per field, and the count-match rule with E0308 contrast `let (a, b, c) = (3, 7);` firing "expected a tuple with 2 elements, found one with 3 elements". Today moves the same pattern shape from the left of `let` to the for-loop binding slot; the pattern's mechanic is unchanged. Probe 6's count-mismatch transcript at the for-binding slot is structurally identical to lesson 073's at the let-binding slot — same E-code, same inline annotation), 079-for-over-array (load-bearing — installs `for X in COLLECTION { ... }` with the loop variable `X` bound to each yielded value, body running once per element. Today extends `X` from a single bare identifier to a tuple pattern; the per-pass / one-binding-per-element semantics are unchanged), 125-iter-zip (load-bearing — installs `v.iter().zip(w.iter())` as an iterator yielding 2-tuples `(&u64, &u64)` paired position-wise from two `Vec<u64>` sources. Today's working probe consumes the same iterator output through the new destructuring pattern slot; Probe 4's `diff` shows old form (`pair.0`/`.1`) and new form (`(a, b)`) produce byte-identical output), 072-tuple-type-and-index (cited — tuple type `(A, B)` and `.0` / `.1` indexing. Used by Probe 4's old-form program to corroborate equivalence with the new form; not used by today's centered probe), 123-vec-iter (cited — `v.iter()` yields `&T` (`&u64` here). Probe 3's contrast applies a 2-tuple pattern to `v.iter()` directly; rustc rejects with E0308 because `&u64` is not a tuple), 049-method-chaining (cited — `expr.method1().method2(arg)` shape used in `v.iter().zip(w.iter())`. Inherited from lesson 125 unchanged), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call grammar, used by `.iter()` and `.zip()` in the working probe), 107-vec-basics (cited — `Vec<T>` construction with `vec![]` and `Vec<u64>` annotation), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{} / {}", a, b)` two positional `{}` slots; same one-arg-per-slot rule), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u64` element type), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — `: TYPE` annotation slot, used as `Vec<u64>`), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic four-part map applied to Probe 2's E0425 transcript and Probes 3/6's E0308 transcripts), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:22` `for (left, right) in self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev()) { ... }` line — lessons 123 + 124 + 125 + 126 together make the entire for-loop head readable end-to-end. The iterator chain on the right of `in` parses by 123/124/125; the for-binding pattern on the left parses by today's move with `(a, b)` swapped to `(left, right)`. The match arm body inside (using `left.cmp(right)`, `Ordering::Equal`, `return ord`) is composed of separately installed concepts" moves, future "irrefutability of for-binding patterns — `for Some(x) in iter` is rejected because `Some(x)` may not match (it does not match `None`). Reference loop-expr.md:212 says the for-loop matches against an *irrefutable* pattern. Today name-defers the term; a future move installs the rule and its diagnostic" moves, future "wildcard `_` in tuple pattern at for-binding — `for (_, b) in iter` to ignore the first part. Composes 073's wildcard rule with today's binding slot" moves, future "nested tuple patterns at for-binding — `for ((a, b), c) in iter` for iterators yielding nested tuples (e.g., `.zip(...).zip(...)`). The parens-and-commas rule recurses; its own move" moves, future "`mut` inside the for-binding pattern — `for (mut a, b) in iter` to make one binding reassignable. Future move; today's bindings are immutable" moves, future "type annotation on the for-binding pattern — `for (a, b): (&u64, &u64) in iter`. Allowed by the grammar; not installed today" moves, future "struct / enum / slice patterns at for-binding — `for Point { x, y } in iter`, `for [a, b, c] in iter`, etc. Each pattern shape is its own move applied at the for-binding slot" moves, future "for-binding pattern with refutable subpattern wrapped in match arm — the audience-level alternative when an iterator yields refutable shapes (e.g., `Result<T, E>`); today's move only installs the irrefutable case" moves, future "patterns in match expressions, if-let, while-let — other places patterns appear besides `let` and for-binding. Each context has its own refutability rules" moves, future "`std::iter::zip(a, b)` free function — composes today's destructuring with the alternative free-function call form `for (a, b) in std::iter::zip(v.iter(), w.iter())`" moves, future "auto-deref / `Add for &u64` — Check Yourself uses `x + y` on `&u64`. Std machinery; named-deferred from 125" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/loop-expr.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/patterns.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch19-01-all-the-places-for-patterns.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0425.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md

  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/126-for-pattern-destructuring.transcript.txt

status: accepted
```
