```text
id: 127-cmp-on-usize-u64
move: extend `a.cmp(&b)` from lesson 061's `i32` case to two more typed receivers — `usize` and `u64` — by reusing the same `use std::cmp::Ordering;` import, the same dot-call argument-by-`&` shape, and the same three-arm match on the returned `Ordering`. The working probe `fn main() { let a: u64 = 100; let b: u64 = 200; match a.cmp(&b) { Ordering::Less => println!("u64: a < b"), Ordering::Greater => println!("u64: a > b"), Ordering::Equal => println!("u64: a == b") } let c: usize = 5; let d: usize = 5; match c.cmp(&d) { Ordering::Less => ..., Ordering::Greater => ..., Ordering::Equal => println!("usize: c == d") } }` (with the standard `use std::cmp::Ordering;` line) compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0), and `./demo` prints `u64: a < b` and `usize: c == d` reaching the `Ordering::Less` arm of the first match (`100 < 200`) and the `Ordering::Equal` arm of the second (`5 == 5`); two scrutinees in one program witness `.cmp` returning `Ordering` on both centered receiver types. The cross-type contrast probe `fn main() { let a: u64 = 100; let b: i32 = 5; let _ = a.cmp(&b); }` (mixing `u64` receiver with `&i32` argument) fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with the inline label `expected \`&u64\`, found \`&i32\`` plus the `note: expected reference \`&u64\` / found reference \`&i32\`` block and the standard `note: method defined here` dual-`-->` pointing into `core/src/cmp.rs:999:7` — empirical witness that `Self` in the `Ord::cmp` signature is the receiver's type, so the argument's reference type is `&u64` here, not `&i32`; both operands must be the same type. The corroborating probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let w: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20]; let result = v.len().cmp(&w.len()); match result { std::cmp::Ordering::Less => println!("v shorter"), std::cmp::Ordering::Greater => println!("v longer"), std::cmp::Ordering::Equal => println!("same length") } }` reads exactly rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:20` shape `self.limbs.len().cmp(&other.limbs.len())` (with `v` / `w` standing in for the two `BigUInt` operands' `limbs` fields); since `Vec::len()` returns `usize` (lesson 107), the chain is `usize.cmp(&usize)` which is today's centered case; compiles silently, prints `v longer` (since `v.len() == 3` and `w.len() == 2`), reaching the `Ordering::Greater` arm. Today's lesson opens *one* of the two `.cmp` call sites in rmp's `cmp.rs`: the `.len().cmp(&...len())` chain on line 20 (`.cmp` on `usize`) is fully readable end-to-end, and the `left.cmp(right)` shape on line 23 (`.cmp` on `&u64`) has its method-resolution piece installed (the `Self` rule applies through the `&u64` receiver too); the surrounding match on the rmp line composes lesson 061's match shape and lesson 121's `Ordering`-equality / 117's structural mechanics, deferred for today's narrow extension move.
main_concept: the `.cmp(&other)` method is declared on the `Ord` trait per `output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.Ord.md:9` with signature `fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering`, and the trait-page implementor list includes both `impl Ord for u64` (line 467) and `impl Ord for usize` (line 479) alongside `impl Ord for i32` (line 423, lesson 061). Each per-primitive page restates the specialized signature: `primitive.u64.md:4231-4237` reads `impl Ord for u64 / fn cmp(&self, other: &u64) -> Ordering / This method returns an Ordering between self and other`, and `primitive.usize.md:4538-4544` reads `impl Ord for usize / fn cmp(&self, other: &usize) -> Ordering / This method returns an Ordering between self and other`. The audience-level rule is: *the same method-name `.cmp`, the same two-argument shape `(&self, other: &Self)`, the same `Ordering` return type, with `Self` replaced by the receiver's concrete type.* Today extends from "I know `.cmp` works on `i32`" (lesson 061) to "I know `.cmp` works on `usize` and `u64`." The two-program centered probe witnesses the empirical move: same import line, same call shape, same match shape, only the operand type changes. The cross-type contrast captures the specialization rule: the argument slot expects `&Self` where `Self` is the receiver's type — concretely `&u64` for a `u64` receiver — and rustc's E0308 inline label `expected \`&u64\`, found \`&i32\`` is the verbatim source of that rule. The audience crosses from "lesson 061 installed `.cmp(&other)` on `i32` and I have not seen it on any other type" to "`.cmp(&other)` is the same method on `usize` and `u64`. The signature is `fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering` for every implementing type; `Self` is the receiver's concrete type, so both operands must match. Mixing types fires E0308 with the type-pair on the inline label." Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `Ord` trait declaration itself (supertraits `Eq + PartialOrd`, provided methods `max` / `min` / `clamp`, custom `impl Ord for ...`); the `PartialOrd` trait and `partial_cmp` returning `Option<Ordering>` (relevant for `f32` / `f64` whose values include `NaN`); `.cmp` on the other ten integer types (`i8` / `u8` / `i16` / `u16` / `i64` / `i128` / `u128` / `isize`, all listed as `Ord` implementors); `.cmp` on `f32` / `f64` (NOT `Ord`, only `PartialOrd`); `.cmp` on `&str`, `String`, `Vec<T>`, slices, tuples, user-defined structs / enums; trait-method dispatch in general (named-deferred since lesson 040). The unlock is the rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:20` line `self.limbs.len().cmp(&other.limbs.len())` end-to-end (the corroborating probe is exactly that shape transcribed onto a probe-level program) and the method-resolution piece of `cmp.rs:23` `match left.cmp(right) { ... }` (the `&u64`-receiver case; the inner match composes lesson 121).
depends_on: 061-i32-cmp-returns-ordering (load-bearing — installs `.cmp(&other)` on `i32` returning `Ordering`, with `use std::cmp::Ordering;` and the three-arm match. Today extends the *same* method-name to two more receiver types per the trait-page implementor list; the call shape, the import line, and the match shape are all unchanged. Lesson 061's `depends_on` chain (040, 044, 045, 051) carries through unchanged), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — names `usize` and `u64` as members of the twelve-name integer family by sign and width, plus the `: TYPE` annotation slot reused for `let a: u64 = 100;` and `let c: usize = 5;`. Today's centered receiver types are explicit annotations in the source, drawn from lesson 080's family), 051-ordering-enum-and-variant-match (cited — `Ordering` enum and three-variant match. Reused unchanged through lesson 061), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call grammar; reused at every `.cmp(...)` call), 044-use-declaration (cited — `use std::cmp::Ordering;` brings the variant names into scope), 045-shared-reference (cited — prefix-`&` operator; `&b` and `&d` build the `&u64` and `&usize` arguments), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — `let name: TYPE = value;` slot, used as `: u64` and `: usize`), 049-method-chaining (cited — the corroborating probe's `v.len().cmp(&w.len())` chain), 107-vec-basics (cited — appendix corroborator only; `Vec<T>` construction with `vec![]`, `.len()` returning `usize`, used to build the rmp `cmp.rs:20` shape), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", x)` shape, six invocations across the working probe and corroborator), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to the contrast E0308 transcript; same map as lesson 061), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:20` `let ord = self.limbs.len().cmp(&other.limbs.len());` line — today's corroborating probe is exactly that shape transcribed onto a probe-level program with `v` / `w` standing in for the `BigUInt` operands' `limbs` fields. With lesson 121 installed (equality on `Ordering`), the `if ord == cmp::Ordering::Equal { ... } else { ord }` outer shape of rmp's `Ord::cmp` impl is also readable" moves, future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:23` `match left.cmp(right) { Ordering::Equal => {} ord => return ord }` — today installs the method-resolution piece (`.cmp` on `&u64` follows the same `Self`-substitution rule); the match shape composes lesson 051 and the `ord` second arm composes lesson 060's binding pattern and lesson 086 / 087's `return` from inside a function" moves, future "`.cmp` on the other ten integer types (`i8`, `u8`, `i16`, `u16`, `i64`, `i128`, `u128`, `isize`) — same trait, same signature shape, only the receiver type changes per type. The mechanic carries; one batched extension lesson or a single 'integer family is `Ord`' move could install all ten" moves, future "`.cmp` on `&str` / `String` — same method-name on lexicographic-ordered string types; the std `Ord` page lists `impl Ord for str`. Different element semantics (lexicographic ordering), so the move is its own" moves, future "`.cmp` on `Vec<T>` and slices — also lexicographic, with `T: Ord` bound. The std `Ord` page lists `impl<A, T> Ord for Vec<T, A>` and similar for slices. Future moves" moves, future "`PartialOrd` and `partial_cmp` returning `Option<Ordering>` — for types like `f32` / `f64` whose values include `NaN`, comparison can be undefined. The corollary is `.cmp` does *not* exist on `f32` / `f64`; using it fires E0599 'no method named cmp found'. Future move" moves, future "the `Ord` trait declaration itself — supertraits `Eq + PartialOrd`, provided methods `max` / `min` / `clamp`, custom `impl Ord for ...`. Lessons 111-116 installed enough trait machinery to read the declaration; today named it as deferred" moves, future "trait-method dispatch in general — the rule by which `.cmp` is reachable on every implementing type. Named-deferred since lesson 040; today's extension is the empirical handle ('same method, more receivers') without naming the rule" moves, future "`.cmp` on user-defined structs / enums — requires `impl Ord for MyType` either by `#[derive(Ord)]` (which requires `Eq + PartialOrd + PartialEq`) or by manual implementation. The std `Ord` page's *How can I implement `Ord`?* section gives the worked Character example; future move" moves, future "`Ord::min`, `Ord::max`, `Ord::clamp` provided methods — `n.min(m)`, `n.max(m)`, `n.clamp(lo, hi)` directly on integer values. Sibling free functions `std::cmp::min` / `max` already installed (043); the dot-form provided methods are their own move" moves, future "lexicographic comparison rule — `Ord` on collections (`Vec<T>`, slices, tuples, `&str`) compares element-wise from the start until the first non-equal pair, returning that pair's `Ordering`; equal-prefix-with-different-length compares by length. The rmp `cmp.rs:22` for-loop reverses the limbs and compares pairwise — the *high-order limbs first* convention, audience-level non-trivial. Future move" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.Ord.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/primitive.u64.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/primitive.usize.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/127-cmp-on-usize-u64.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/127-cmp-on-usize-u64.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/127-cmp-on-usize-u64.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 128-empty-match-arm-body
move: write `Pattern => {}` as a match arm — the body is the empty block, type `()`, value `()` (lesson 029); the arm matches and runs nothing. Working probe `use std::cmp::Ordering; fn main() { let o: Ordering = Ordering::Equal; match o { Ordering::Equal => {} Ordering::Less => println!("less"), Ordering::Greater => println!("greater"), } println!("done"); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0), and `./demo` prints `done` on a single line: the `Equal` arm fires (matches the scrutinee), its `{}` body runs nothing, control falls through past the match to the unconditional `println!("done")`. All three arm bodies have type `()` (the empty block per block-expr.md:72; both `println!` calls return `()`), so the match's least upper bound (match-expr.md:138) is `()` and the whole match is used as a *statement* (no `let x = ...`). The contrast probe `use std::cmp::Ordering; fn main() { let o: Ordering = Ordering::Equal; let x = match o { Ordering::Equal => {}, Ordering::Less => 5, Ordering::Greater => 10, }; println!("{}", x); }` (binds the match to a `let`, mismatches arm types) fires `error[E0308]: \`match\` arms have incompatible types` with the inline labels `this is found to be of type \`()\`` (under `{}`) and `expected \`()\`, found integer` (under `5`) — empirical witness that rustc names `{}`'s type as `()` in source-spans, and that once the first arm fixes the match's type the all-arms-share-a-type rule from lesson 030 checks every other arm against `()`. The corroborating probe (same source as the working probe with `Ordering::Equal` replaced by `Ordering::Less`) compiles silently, prints `less` then `done` (two lines): witnesses that the empty arm fires only when the scrutinee equals `Ordering::Equal`, that non-empty arms run their bodies, and that control always reaches the post-match `println!`. Today's move opens rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:24` (`Ordering::Equal => {}`) end-to-end.
main_concept: a match arm body is any expression (match-expr.md:18-20). The smallest expression a learner can write there is the empty block `{}`, whose type is the unit type `()` and whose value is the unit value `()` (block-expr.md:70-82, restated by lesson 029). Writing `Pattern => {}` therefore makes the arm match and *do nothing*: no value beyond `()`, no side effect. Lesson 030's all-arms-share-a-type rule (Reference: match-expr.md:138 "the type of the overall `match` expression is the least upper bound of the individual match arms") still applies; when every arm has type `()` (or every other arm calls `println!`, which also returns `()`), the match's overall type is `()`, and the whole match is used as a *statement* — no `let x = match ...`, no value to bind, control just falls through to the next statement. The audience-level rule installed today is purely a composition: lesson 029 (`{}` is `()`) + lesson 030 (arm body is an expression and all arms must share a type) + lesson 051 (match on `Ordering`'s three variants). No new keyword, no new operator, no new trait. The contrastive failure mode — bind the match to `let x` and let arm types disagree — fires E0308 with the sub-form `\`match\` arms have incompatible types` and inline labels `this is found to be of type \`()\`` plus `expected \`()\`, found integer`; rustc itself names `{}`'s type. The audience crosses from "every match arm I have written so far carried a useful value or a side-effecting `println!`" to "an arm body is an expression, and `{}` is a perfectly good expression — it has type `()` and runs nothing. `Pattern => {}` is *match this case but do nothing here*." Wholesale or named-deferred today: diverging arm bodies (`continue` already installed in lesson 059; `break` / `return` / `panic!` named-deferred); the never type `!` and its arm-body merge rules (wholesale deferred — Reference: match-expr.md:142 "if there are no match arms, then the match expression is diverging and the type is `!`"); block expressions with statements inside (`{ side_effect(); 42 }` is a different mechanic — today's empty block has no statements at all); the `_` wildcard with an empty body `_ => {}` (composes today + lesson 031, not centered); `if let Pattern = expr { } else { ... }` (another way to "match one variant and do nothing", future move). The unlock is rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:24` (`Ordering::Equal => {}` — the empty arm whose body the corroborating probe demonstrates and whose type the contrast probe witnesses) and the surrounding match-as-statement pattern on lines 23-26.
depends_on: 029-unit-type (load-bearing — installs `{}` as an empty block whose type is `()` and whose value is `()`. Today's move is exactly that block placed in a match arm body slot; the centered claim "an empty arm body has type `()`" inherits from this), 030-match-on-bool (load-bearing — installs the match form with arms `pattern => arm_expression` and the rule that all arm expressions must share a type. Today's working probe lands the rule with all three arms at `()`; the contrast probe trips the rule with one `()` arm and two integer-literal arms), 051-ordering-enum-and-variant-match (load-bearing — installs the three-arm match on `Ordering` with `use std::cmp::Ordering;` and `Ordering::{Less,Greater,Equal}` variant patterns. Today's working probe is a tiny variation: same three arms, one body now empty), 031-match-integer-and-wildcard (cited — exhaustiveness with three concrete variants and no `_` needed; reused unchanged via 051), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!(...)` in arm bodies; each call has type `()`), 044-use-declaration (cited — `use std::cmp::Ordering;`), 019-type-annotation-i32 (cited — `let o: Ordering = Ordering::Equal;` annotation slot), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to the E0308 contrast walk in *Try It*), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:24` `Ordering::Equal => {}` — today's centered move. The `Ordering::Equal => {}` arm whose body is empty is fully readable; the surrounding match on line 23 (`match left.cmp(right) { ... }`) composes lesson 127's `.cmp` mechanic with today's empty-arm move, the named-binding pattern `ord =>` on line 25 composes lesson 060, and the `return ord` on line 25 composes function-`return` (already implicit through earlier lessons)" moves, future "named-binding match arm pattern `name => ...`" moves (lesson 060 installed it for stdin's `Result` payload variants but the *bare-name-on-the-left* arm form deserves its own narrow move; rmp `cmp.rs:25` uses it), future "match arm body is a multi-statement block `Pattern => { stmt; stmt; tail }`" moves (real Rust uses these constantly; today's empty `{}` is the limit case), future "`if let Pattern = expr { ... } else { ... }`" moves (another way to handle one variant specially), future "diverging arm body — `Pattern => return ...,` or `Pattern => panic!(...)` — and the never type `!` and its arm-merge rules" moves (named-deferred today), future "match with no arms — type `!`, only valid for uninhabited scrutinees" moves (deferred from match-expr.md:142), future "the `_` wildcard with an empty body `_ => {}` as the do-nothing default" moves (composes today + lesson 031)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/block-expr.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/expressions/match-expr.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - output/repos/rmp/src/biguint/cmp.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/128-empty-match-arm-body.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/128-empty-match-arm-body.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/128-empty-match-arm-body.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 129-return-in-match-arm
move: write `name => return name,` as a match arm — the pattern is a bare identifier (a binding pattern that matches any value of the scrutinee's type and binds it to `name`, per Reference patterns.md:216-228 "Patterns that consist of only an identifier ... match any value and bind it to that identifier"); the body is `return name;` (lesson 021's function-exit statement, per Reference return-expr.md:18 "Evaluating a `return` expression moves its argument into the designated output location for the current function call, destroys the current function activation frame, and transfers control to the caller frame"). Pair with lesson 128's empty arm to get the working probe `use std::cmp::Ordering; fn first_nonzero(a: Ordering, b: Ordering) -> Ordering { match a { Ordering::Equal => {} ord => return ord, } b } fn main() { let x = first_nonzero(Ordering::Less, Ordering::Greater); /* match-and-print */ println!("{}", label_of(x)); let y = first_nonzero(Ordering::Equal, Ordering::Greater); println!("{}", label_of(y)); }` — compiles silently (`rustc demo.rs` exit 0), prints `less` then `greater`. First call: `a = Less`, the bare-name arm fires, `ord` binds `Less`, `return ord;` exits the function with `Less`; caller prints `less`. Second call: `a = Equal`, the empty arm fires, `{}` runs nothing, control falls through past the match to the body's tail expression `b` (lesson 025), which is `Greater`; caller prints `greater`. Centered contrast probe (drop the bare-name arm entirely): `match a { Ordering::Equal => {} }` fires `error[E0004]: non-exhaustive patterns: \`std::cmp::Ordering::Less\` and \`std::cmp::Ordering::Greater\` not covered`, with `help:` text suggesting `std::cmp::Ordering::Less | std::cmp::Ordering::Greater => todo!()` — empirical witness that the `ord =>` arm is the wildcard that completes exhaustiveness. Secondary contrast probe (swap arm order so `ord =>` appears first): `warning: unreachable pattern` with inline labels `--- matches any value` (under `ord`) and `^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ no value can reach this` (under `Ordering::Equal`) — empirical witness that the bare-name arm catches every value (matching patterns.md:218) and that source-order matching (lesson 031) makes any subsequent specific arm unreachable. The corroborating probe varies inputs: `first_nonzero(Greater, Less)` → bare-name arm binds `Greater`, returns `Greater`; `first_nonzero(Equal, Less)` → empty arm fires, falls through, returns `Less`. Both early-return and fall-through paths witnessed for both non-`Equal` variants. Today's move opens rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:25` (`ord => return ord,`) end-to-end. Combined with lesson 128 (line 24: `Ordering::Equal => {}`), the full match shape on lines 23-26 is now readable.
main_concept: a match arm body can be `return value;` (Reference return-expr.md:14-22), and a match arm pattern can be a bare identifier `name` that matches any value and binds it to that local name (Reference patterns.md:216-218 "Patterns that consist of only an identifier ... match any value and bind it to that identifier"). The arm `name => return name;` therefore catches every remaining value, binds it to a usable local, and uses `return name;` to exit the enclosing function with that value. Three composable mechanics install together: (1) **Top-level bare-identifier pattern.** Lesson 058 installed bare identifiers inside payload constructors (`Ok(num)`); today extends to the top level of an arm. The same Reference rule (patterns.md:216-228) covers both placements; what changes is the surrounding structure — from inside a `Ok(...)` constructor to bare. (2) **`return value;` as arm body.** Lesson 021 installed `return value;` as a function-exit statement; lesson 059 installed `continue` as an arm body. Today combines: `return value;` works as an arm body (Reference match-expr.md grammar at lines 18-20 allows any expression after `=>`), and per return-expr.md:22 it is diverging with type `!`. The post-match code is unreachable for that call. (3) **Arm-type merge with diverging arms.** The Reference rule at match-expr.md:138 says the match's overall type is the least upper bound of the arm types; never.md:18 says `!` coerces into any other type. So a match with one `()` arm (empty `{}`) and one `!` arm (`return value;`) has overall type `()`. Lesson 059 installed this exemption for `continue`; today reuses it for `return value;` — same exemption, different diverging keyword. The audience-level framing today defers the *name* `!` ("diverging arms are exempt from the all-arms-share-a-type rule"). The audience crosses from "I know `return value;` exits a function and an arm body can be `continue`" to "I know `return value;` works in any expression position including a match arm body, and a bare identifier as an arm pattern catches everything for me to hand back." Wholesale or named-deferred today: the never type `!` and its formal arm-merge rules (named-deferred today; coerce-LUB rule explained in evidence appendix as red-team grounding); `break value;` and `panic!()` in arm bodies (named, not exercised); match guards `pattern if cond => arm` (deferred); `@`-bindings `name @ pattern` (deferred); the `_` wildcard with an arm body that uses the matched value — `_ => return ...` cannot use the value because `_` doesn't bind; today's bare-name form makes the value usable in the body (deferred-shape contrast); returning from inside a `for`-loop body — rmp's actual use site is `for { match { return } }` nested (today returns from a function's plain body; the for-loop composition is a future move). The unlock is rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:25` (`ord => return ord,` — today's centered move, the bare-name binding pattern paired with the diverging-`return` arm body) and the full match shape on lines 23-26 (paired with lesson 128's line-24 unlock).
depends_on: 058-match-result-payload-variants (load-bearing — installed bare-identifier binding pattern *inside* `Ok(num)`. Reference patterns.md:216-228 is the same rule for top-level use. Today extends from inside-payload to top-level), 021-function-return-value (load-bearing — `return value;` as a function-exit statement; sends `value` to the caller, destroys the activation frame, transfers control. Today places it inside a match arm body), 059-continue-in-match-arm (load-bearing — a match arm body can be a diverging control-flow expression; such arms are exempt from the all-arms-share-a-type rule. The exemption rule (Reference match-expr.md:161 + never.md:18) carries through unchanged; today is `return value;` in place of `continue`), 128-empty-match-arm-body (load-bearing — `Pattern => {}` arm body, type `()`. Today's working probe pairs the empty arm with the return-arm; the rmp `cmp.rs:23-26` shape requires both), 051-ordering-enum-and-variant-match (cited — `Ordering` enum and three-variant match shape; reused unchanged. Today's centered E0004 contrast names two `Ordering` variants as missing — same E-code, same enum, same diagnostic shape as lesson 051's original contrast), 025-implicit-return (cited — a function's tail expression is the return value. Today's working probe relies on this for the `b` tail when the empty arm fires and control falls through), 020-function-with-parameter (cited — function with two parameters `a: Ordering, b: Ordering`), 031-match-integer-and-wildcard (cited — source-order matching rule, used by *Check Yourself* (c) and the secondary contrast probe), 044-use-declaration (cited — `use std::cmp::Ordering;`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", x);` six invocations across working probe and corroborator), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to the E0004 contrast walk in *Try It* and the unreachable-pattern walk in *Check Yourself*), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "rmp `src/biguint/cmp.rs:23-26` (`match left.cmp(right) { Ordering::Equal => {} ord => return ord, }`) — today plus lesson 128 plus lesson 127 makes the full two-arm match readable end-to-end. Composed with `iter().rev().zip(...)` (lessons 124-126), the surrounding `for`-loop body's match-as-statement-with-early-return shape is now installed" moves, future "the never type `!` and its formal coercion rule (Reference never.md:18 'Expressions of type `!` can be coerced into any other type')" moves (named-deferred today; the audience-level framing 'diverging arms are exempt' carries the rule without naming it), future "diverging arm bodies — `break value;`, `panic!(...)`, other diverging library calls" moves (same exemption applies; named today, not exercised), future "match guards `pattern if cond => arm`" moves, future "`@`-bindings `name @ subpattern`" moves (composes today's bare-name pattern with a Reference-grade subpattern from patterns.md:230-244), future "returning from inside a `for`-loop body — `for x in iter { match { return value; } }` — composing today's diverging arm with lesson 022's `for`-loop body, exactly the rmp `cmp.rs:22-27` nesting" moves (natural composition; centered cycle when ready), future "named-binding pattern with subpattern guard — `n @ 1..=5` and friends" moves, future "the `_` wildcard arm body that uses the matched value — workaround patterns" moves (today's bare-name form is the canonical way; future move can name the asymmetry)
```

```text
id: 130-capstone-rmp-cmp-ordering
move: read rmp's `src/biguint/cmp.rs:12-33` PartialOrd + Ord impls end-to-end, plus the file-level `use std::cmp::{self, Ord, Ordering};` on line 2. Capstone-mode (no new centered Rust mechanic) — the trait-and-iterator arc (lessons 119-129) composes into one real-world Rust slice readable token-by-token. The slice is twenty-two lines: `impl PartialOrd for BigUInt { fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Option<std::cmp::Ordering> { Some(self.cmp(other)) } }` (lines 12-16) and `impl Ord for BigUInt { fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering { let ord = self.limbs.len().cmp(&other.limbs.len()); if ord == cmp::Ordering::Equal { for (left, right) in self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(other.limbs.iter().rev()) { match left.cmp(right) { Ordering::Equal => {} ord => return ord, } } return Ordering::Equal; } else { ord } } }` (lines 18-33). Each line maps to installed lessons: line 13 composes 100 (&self) + 120 (&Self) + 119 (Option) + 051 (Ordering) + 043 (qualified path); line 14 composes 122 (self.method delegation) + 119 (Some); line 19 composes 100 + 120 + 051; line 20 composes 005 (let) + 095 (field access through &self) + 100 + 107 (.len) + 049 (chain) + 127 (.cmp on usize) + 045 (prefix-&); line 21 composes 026 (if-as-expression) + 121 (== on Ordering) + the new grouped-`use`-with-`self` mechanic; line 22 composes 126 (for tuple destructuring) + 123 (.iter) + 124 (.rev) + 125 (.zip) + 095 (field access); line 23 composes 058 (match with payload) + 127 (.cmp on u64 through auto-deref); line 24 is exactly lesson 128's `Ordering::Equal => {}`; line 25 is exactly lesson 129's `ord => return ord,`; line 28 composes 021 (return value;) inside an if-then block; line 30 composes 025 (implicit tail-expression return) of the if-else; lines 29-31 compose 014 (if-else). Three new structural facts framed name-only with corpus citations: (A) PartialOrd's std declaration `pub trait PartialOrd<Rhs = Self>: PartialEq<Rhs> where Rhs: ?Sized` from `output/docs/rust/std/cmp/trait.PartialOrd.md` lines 7-21 — supertrait `: PartialEq<Rhs>` and default RHS `<Rhs = Self>` named-deferred matching 118's deferral pattern; required method `fn partial_cmp(&self, other: &Rhs) -> Option<Ordering>`; provided methods `lt` / `le` / `gt` / `ge` supplying `<` / `<=` / `>` / `>=` per Reference operator-expr.md:527-530. (B) Ord's std declaration `pub trait Ord: Eq + PartialOrd { fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering; ... }` from `trait.Ord.md` lines 7-19 — two supertraits joined by `+`, both named-deferred. (C) The grouped `use std::cmp::{self, Ord, Ordering};` form from Reference `items/use-declarations.md` lines 27-31 — brace-grouped paths with the `self` keyword for the parent module; lesson 044 extended. Three probes capture empirical witness: (1) self-contained mirror at `observations/130-capstone-rmp-cmp-ordering.rs` reproduces the slice with a small BigUInt-shaped struct and prints five lines including `a.cmp(&b) = Less`, `a.cmp(&c) = Equal`, `zero.cmp(&from0) = Less` (length-first short-circuit), `p.cmp(&q) (MSL 1 vs 2) = Less` (big-endian most-significant-first walk through `.iter().rev()`); (2) cross-crate driver under `/tmp/eduratchet2-130/cmp_capstone/` with `bignum = { path = "/Users/eli/InfoScraper/output/repos/rmp" }` confirms `partial_cmp` and `cmp` work on rmp's actual types and surfaces the canonical-zero defect (`zero().cmp(&from(0u64)) = Less`); (3) `cargo test --lib` against rmp itself reports `17 passed; 0 failed` — the impls compile and rmp's existing tests still pass. Three contrast probes capture trait-dispatch wiring: (4) drop `impl PartialOrd` → `error[E0277]: can't compare BigUInt with BigUInt` on `impl Ord` (witnesses Ord's `: ... + PartialOrd` supertrait), plus E0599 on `.partial_cmp()` and E0369 on `<`; (5) drop `impl Ord` → `error[E0599]: \`BigUInt\` is not an iterator ... method \`cmp\` not found ... candidate #1: \`Iterator\`, candidate #2: \`Ord\``; (6) drop `impl PartialEq` from a fresh struct → `error[E0277]: can't compare \`Score\` with \`Score\` ... the trait \`PartialEq\` is not implemented for \`Score\` ... required by a bound in \`PartialOrd\`` (witnesses PartialOrd's `: PartialEq<Rhs>` supertrait). Honest defect surfaced (third axis after 110 and 118): `BigUInt::zero().cmp(&BigUInt::from(0u64))` returns `Less` because `zero()` builds `vec![]` (length 0) and `from(0u64)` builds `vec![0]` (length 1), and the cmp body's first step is `usize.cmp` on the lengths — so `zero() < from(0u64) = true` even though both represent the mathematical value 0. Same constructor-side `From<u64>` discipline failure 110 surfaced via `is_zero` and 118 surfaced via `==`, now witnessed on the `<` axis. The whole file `cmp.rs` is now read end-to-end across two cycles (118 covered lines 4-10, today covers lines 12-33).
main_concept: lessons 119-129 (eleven moves: Option/Some/None, &Self in non-receiver, == on Ordering, self.method delegation, v.iter, .rev, .zip, for-tuple-destructuring, .cmp on usize/u64, empty arm body, return in arm) compose with lessons 095-118 to make `src/biguint/cmp.rs:12-33` token-by-token readable. Two new trait names enter the audience's vocabulary: `PartialOrd` (the trait `<` / `<=` / `>` / `>=` and `partial_cmp` dispatch through, std declaration `pub trait PartialOrd<Rhs = Self>: PartialEq<Rhs>`) and `Ord` (the trait `.cmp` dispatches through, std declaration `pub trait Ord: Eq + PartialOrd`). Four named deferrals: (1) the supertrait colon-form (`PartialOrd: PartialEq<Rhs>` and `Ord: Eq + PartialOrd`) — read structurally only, the formal supertrait machinery is its own move; (2) the default-type-parameter `<Rhs = Self>` and `?Sized` bound — same machinery 118 already named-deferred for `PartialEq`; (3) auto-deref on `left.cmp(right)` for `&u64` operands — surfaces today on line 23; (4) shadowing in nested scopes — line 25's pattern-bound `ord` shadows line 20's `let ord` only inside the match arm body. One new compositional `use` form: `use std::cmp::{self, Ord, Ordering};` — grouped braces with the `self` keyword bringing the module itself into scope alongside two of its items, per Reference `items/use-declarations.md` lines 27-31; lesson 044 extended. The audience-level installed mental model: every token in lines 12-33 of cmp.rs is now readable as a composition of installed lessons; the trait dispatch chain for `<` (and `partial_cmp`, `cmp`) is structurally traceable through the std declarations and the operator-to-trait table at Reference `expressions/operator-expr.md` lines 525-530; the rmp slice that the trait-and-iterator arc was assembled to make readable is now read; the canonical-zero defect identified in 110 and 118 manifests on the `<` axis through the same `From<u64>` discipline failure. The unlock is the rest of rmp (`add.rs`, `mul.rs`, `div.rs`, `shift.rs`, `format.rs`, the rest of `convert.rs`, `bigint.rs`) — each composes the trait arc through 130 with arithmetic, iteration, and formatting machinery yet to be installed.
depends_on: 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — installs `Option<T>` with `Some(t)` / `None` constructors and `Option<...>` as a return-type annotation slot. cmp.rs:13 fills that slot with `Option<std::cmp::Ordering>`; cmp.rs:14 uses `Some(...)` to wrap a `cmp` result), 120-self-in-non-receiver (load-bearing — installs `other: &Self` in a non-receiver trait-impl parameter slot; inside `impl ... for BigUInt` resolves to `&BigUInt`. cmp.rs:13 and cmp.rs:19 use this exact spelling), 121-equality-on-ordering (load-bearing — installs `==` on two `Ordering` values via std's `impl PartialEq for Ordering`. cmp.rs:21 `if ord == cmp::Ordering::Equal` is exactly this), 122-self-method-delegation (load-bearing — installs `self.method(args)` for sibling-method delegation. cmp.rs:14 `Some(self.cmp(other))` is the canonical example; lesson 122 named cmp.rs:14 explicitly as its unlock), 123-vec-iter (load-bearing — `v.iter()` returns a slice iterator yielding `&T`. cmp.rs:22 calls `.iter()` twice), 124-iter-rev (load-bearing — `.iter().rev()` reverses the iterator. cmp.rs:22 chains `.rev()` on each `.iter()` so the most-significant limb comes first), 125-iter-zip (load-bearing — `.zip(other_iter)` pairs two iterators. cmp.rs:22 zips the two reversed iterators), 126-for-pattern-destructuring (load-bearing — `for (left, right) in iter` tuple-pattern destructuring. cmp.rs:22 uses exactly `for (left, right) in ...`), 127-cmp-on-usize-u64 (load-bearing — `.cmp(&other)` on `usize` and `u64` returns `Ordering`. cmp.rs:20's `usize.cmp(&usize)` and cmp.rs:23's `u64.cmp(&u64)` (through auto-deref) both rely on this), 128-empty-match-arm-body (load-bearing — `Pattern => {}` empty arm body. cmp.rs:24 `Ordering::Equal => {}` is exactly this), 129-return-in-match-arm (load-bearing — `name => return name,` diverging arm with bare-name binding. cmp.rs:25 `ord => return ord,` is exactly this), 118-capstone-rmp-cmp-equality (load-bearing — capstone-mode template, named-deferred treatment of supertraits and `<Rhs = Self>` defaults, `From<u64>` non-canonicalization defect surfaced on the `==` axis that today extends to the `<` axis), 110-capstone-rmp-biguint-trio (load-bearing — rmp module-path chain `bignum::biguint::BigUInt`, the `pub struct BigUInt { pub(super) limbs: Vec<u64> }` declaration, the cross-crate driver pattern, and the limb-ordering invariants — little-endian, no trailing zeros, empty-vec-means-zero — that make `.iter().rev()` walk the most-significant limb first), 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing through 118 + 120 — `impl Trait for Type { ... }`. Both today's impl blocks use this shape), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — `&self` borrowing receiver, `Self` as impl-target alias, return-type slot; auto-deref named-deferred), 044-use-declaration (load-bearing — `use Path::Item;` import. Today extends with the grouped form `{...}` and the `self` keyword), 051-ordering-enum-and-variant-match (load-bearing — `Ordering` enum, `use std::cmp::Ordering;`, `Ordering::Less` etc. variant constructors), 026-if-as-expression (load-bearing — `if condition { ... }` as an expression that evaluates to its branch's value. cmp.rs:21-31 is the if-else expression that becomes `cmp`'s return), 014-if-else (load-bearing — `if-else` shape. cmp.rs:21+29 is exactly this), 025-implicit-return (load-bearing — tail expression as implicit return. cmp.rs:30 `ord` in else-block tail position becomes the if-else value, which is the function's return), 021-function-return-value (load-bearing — `return value;` as a function-exit statement. cmp.rs:25 `return ord;` and cmp.rs:28 `return Ordering::Equal;`), 049-method-chaining (load-bearing — chained dot-calls `expr.m1().m2()`. cmp.rs:20 `self.limbs.len().cmp(...)` and cmp.rs:22 `self.limbs.iter().rev().zip(...)`), 058-match-result-payload-variants (load-bearing — `match` with multiple arms, payload-binding patterns. cmp.rs:23-26 is exactly this with `Ordering` arms), 095-struct-with-named-fields (load-bearing — struct field access through `&`. cmp.rs:20 `self.limbs` and `other.limbs` rely on this), 107-vec-basics (load-bearing — `Vec<T>` with `.len() -> usize`. cmp.rs:20 calls `.len()`), 113-reference-parameter-in-trait-method (cited — `&u64` argument matches `&Self` parameter on line 23 without an extra `&`), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — provided trait methods on PartialOrd ride 116's mechanic; named in *What To Ignore*), 030-match-on-bool (cited — all-arms-share-a-type rule, exempted by line 25's diverging arm), 098-enum-with-unit-variants (cited — `Ordering::Equal` is a unit-variant constructor pattern, used by line 24), 045-shared-reference (cited — prefix-`&` operator builds `&other.limbs.len()` argument), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call grammar), 005-let-binding (cited — cmp.rs:20 `let ord = ...;`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to E0277 / E0369 / E0599 contrast walks in the *Empirical Witness* and *Honest Defect* sections), 008, 011, 012, 013, 019, 080, 032, 065 (cited; per-claim summaries in the evidence appendix), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` and `cargo` on `PATH`, ability to `cd` into a directory)
unlocks: future "the supertrait colon-form (`PartialOrd: PartialEq<Rhs>` and `Ord: Eq + PartialOrd`) — the `+`-joined supertrait list, compile-time enforcement, `where Self: Bound` equivalents" moves (named-deferred today), future "the default-type-parameter `<Rhs = Self>` and the `?Sized` bound" moves (named-deferred today and at lesson 118), future "provided trait methods on `PartialOrd` (`lt` / `le` / `gt` / `ge`) and `Ord` (`max` / `min` / `clamp`) — lesson 116's mechanic in std use" moves, future "the `<` / `<=` / `>` / `>=` operators on `BigUInt` — wired via `PartialOrd::lt` / `le` / `gt` / `ge` per Reference operator-expr.md:527-530" moves (witnessed today only via the contrast probe's E0369), future "auto-deref on method receivers — `left.cmp(right)` finds `u64::cmp` through `&u64`" moves (named-deferred since lesson 100; surfaces here on line 23), future "shadowing in nested scopes — line 25's pattern-bound `ord` vs line 20's `let ord`" moves (named-deferred since lesson 005), future "rmp `biguint/add.rs`, `mul.rs`, `div.rs`, `shift.rs`, `format.rs`, the rest of `convert.rs`, `bigint.rs` — composing the trait arc through 130 with arithmetic, iteration, and formatting machinery yet to be installed" moves, future "the `Reverse<T>` adapter, `cmp::reverse`, `cmp::min`, `cmp::max` — std `cmp` items unused by rmp's slice" moves, future "`#[derive(PartialOrd)]` and `#[derive(Ord)]` — derive-macro mechanics" moves (named only in contrast probes' `help:` text), future "the never type `!` — the formal name for the type of `return value;` and other diverging expressions, the arm-body coercion rule" moves (named-deferred since 129), future "`PartialOrd` for types that do not form a total order — `f64` with `NaN` returning `partial_cmp(&...) == None`" moves (today's `Some(self.cmp(other))` is structurally shaped for total-order types only)
```

```text
id: 131-iterator-next-call
move: pull elements from a slice iterator one at a time by binding it as `let mut iter = v.iter();` and calling `iter.next()` repeatedly. Four calls on `vec![10, 20, 30]` yields `Some(10)`, `Some(20)`, `Some(30)`, `None`; `rustc demo.rs` exits 0 silently and `./demo` prints exactly those four lines. Each call hands back the next element wrapped in `Some(_)` and advances the iterator's internal cursor; once every element has been yielded, calls return `None`. The wrapper is `Option<&u64>` (`std/slice/struct.Iter.md:195-207`: `impl<'a, T> Iterator for Iter<'a, T> { type Item = &'a T; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<&'a T>; }`). The `{:?}` Debug formatter (lesson 093) prints `Some(10)` not `Some(&10)` for primitive `T`; the actual return type is pinned by the appendix's positive type-witness probe `let first: Option<&u64> = iter.next();` (silent compile) and the negative-witness probe `let first: Option<u64> = iter.next();` which fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected Option<u64>, found Option<&u64>`. Centered contrast probe (drop `mut` from the binding): fires `error[E0596]: cannot borrow \`iter\` as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` with a `help:` line that literally writes the fix `let mut iter = v.iter();`, with `+++` marking where to insert `mut` — empirical witness for the `&mut self` receiver on `next`'s declaration at `std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:277`. Today's move opens the iterator arc per the audit at `experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/iterator-api-coverage.md` §5 step 1: it is the highest-priority ready-now Iterator move, composing 123 + 119 + 006 + 040 + 093.
main_concept: a slice iterator (the value `v.iter()` returns) has a method `.next()` with declaration `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>` (per `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:277`); for the slice iterator, `Self::Item = &T` (per `output/docs/rust/std/slice/struct.Iter.md:199` `type Item = &'a T`), so the specialized signature is `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<&T>`. Three coupled facts install: (1) **`.next()` is callable on the iterator** with the lesson-040 dot-call shape `iter.next()`; the receiver is the iterator object, not the vec. (2) **The receiver is `&mut self`**, so the binding must be `let mut iter = v.iter();`. Without `mut`, rustc fires `E0596 cannot borrow \`iter\` as mutable` and the `help:` line proposes the fix directly. The `&mut self` is empirically visible *only* through the diagnostic — it is otherwise hidden inside the trait declaration. (3) **The return wrapper is `Option<&T>`** (lesson 119 with `T = &u64` here): `Some(&value)` per element in vec order, then `None` when exhausted. For slice iterators, once `None` is returned every subsequent call returns `None` — the std doc at `trait.Iterator.md:281-284` explicitly notes "Individual iterator implementations may choose to resume iteration ... may or may not eventually start returning `Some(Item)` again at some point"; slice iterators do not resume. The named deferral "exhausted means `None` forever" carries today's operating rule without naming `Fuse<I>`. The audience-level mental model that installs: the iterator is an object with internal cursor state; calling `.next()` advances the cursor (which is why the binding has to be `mut`) and yields one element wrapped; the `for`-loop is one way to drive `.next()`, explicit calls are another. Composes lessons 119 (`Option<T>`) and 123 (slice iterator yielding `&T`) into one shape: `Option<&T>`. Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `Iterator` trait declaration itself (next in the iterator arc per audit §5 step 2); the `Self::Item` associated type slot in `Iterator::next`'s signature (lesson 115 installed associated-type *syntax*; the cross-trait composition is its own move); the `'a` lifetime in `&'a T` (wholesale deferred since 123); iterators that resume after `None` (`Fuse<I>` adapter); the `impl<T: Debug + ?Sized> Debug for &T` formatter rule that makes `{:?}` print `Some(10)` rather than `Some(&10)` for `Option<&u64>`; the `while let Some(x) = iter.next() { ... }` driver-loop pattern; `iter_mut()` and `into_iter()` siblings; `Option::copied` (surfaces in Probe 5's `help:` line). The unlock per audit §5: every later Iterator method (`count`, `last`, `nth`, `take`, `skip`, `enumerate`, `fuse`, `step_by`, `size_hint`) and the `Iterator` trait declaration itself, all of which are anchored to the `next` mechanic this lesson installs.
depends_on: 123-vec-iter (load-bearing — `v.iter()` returns a slice iterator yielding `&T`. Today binds it with `let mut iter` and calls `.next()` instead of routing it into a `for`. 123 explicitly named `.next()` as deferred in its *What To Ignore For Now* section: "the explicit pull form. Each call returns `Option<&T>`. Composes lesson 119 with the iterator machinery." — today executes that composition), 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — `Option<T>` with `Some` / `None`. Today's `T` is `&u64`, so the wrapper is `Option<&u64>`. The `Some(&u64)` slot is just 119's `Some(T)` with `T = &u64` — composition only), 006-mut-binding (load-bearing — `let mut name = value;`. `.next()`'s receiver is `&mut self`; calling it requires a mutable borrow of the iterator. Without `mut`, lesson-006's "mutating use is rejected" rule fires E0596 — the centered contrast probe), 040-method-call-syntax (load-bearing — `value.method(args)` dot-call shape with empty arg list. `iter.next()` is exactly that), 093-standard-library-prelude (cited — `{:?}` Debug placeholder; today's transcripts use `println!("{:?}", iter.next())` to show `Some(10)` / `None`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder; today fills the slot with `{:?}` per 093), 107-vec-basics (cited — `Vec<T>` / `vec![...]` literal), 080 (cited — `u64`), 019 (cited — `: TYPE` annotation slot), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part map applied to E0596 in the centered contrast and E0308 in the type-witness contrast in the appendix), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: 132-iterator-trait-declaration (next per audit §5 step 2 — read `pub trait Iterator { type Item; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>; }` structurally; today's `iter.next()` mechanic is the empirical anchor for the `next` row of that declaration), future "`Iterator::count` — `(self) -> usize`, calls `.next()` until `None` and counts" moves (audit §5 step 3), future "`Iterator::last` — `(self) -> Option<Self::Item>`, the infinite-iterator panic trigger" moves (audit §5 step 4), future "`Iterator::nth` — `(&mut self, n: usize) -> Option<Self::Item>`" moves (audit §5 step 5), future "`Iterator::take` — `(self, n) -> Take<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 6), future "`Iterator::skip` — `(self, n) -> Skip<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 7), future "`Iterator::enumerate` — `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>`, yields `(usize, Item)`" moves (audit §5 step 8), future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter — formal name for slice-iter's empirical 'exhausted means None forever' rule" moves (audit §5 step 9, named-deferred today), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`" moves (audit §5 step 10), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11), future "`while let Some(x) = iter.next() { ... }` driver-loop pattern" moves (composes today with `if let` / `while let`), future "the `impl<T: Debug + ?Sized> Debug for &T` formatter rule — why `{:?}` prints `Some(10)` not `Some(&10)` for `Option<&u64>`" moves, future "the `'a` lifetime in `&'a T`" moves (wholesale deferred since 123), future closure sub-arc moves per audit §4.4.1 (gates `map`/`filter`/`fold`/`reduce`/`for_each`/`all`/`any`/`find` and 19 more), future `IntoIterator` sub-arc per audit §4.4.2 (gates `chain`/`zip`-at-trait-depth/`cmp`/`partial_cmp`/`eq` etc. and the `for x in expr` desugar rule)

  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
```

```text
id: 132-iterator-trait-declaration
move: read the `std::iter::Iterator` trait declaration's required surface as a single structural unit. The synopsis box at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md` lines 6-13 collapses to `pub trait Iterator { type Item; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>; /* + 75 provided methods */ }`. Two required items only: the associated type `Item` (per `## Required Associated Types` at line 265) and the required method `next` (per `## Required Methods` at line 273). Empirical witness via a *user-defined* `impl Iterator for Counter` that fills only `type Item = u32;` and `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>` — `rustc demo.rs` exits 0 silently and `./demo` prints `Some(0)`, `Some(1)`, `Some(2)`, `None`, mirroring the std doc's `assert_eq!`-shaped example at `trait.Iterator.md:288-303`. Probe 2 sharpens the claim: with the impl supplying only the required surface, the provided method `c.count()` is callable and prints `count = 3` — empirical witness for "75 provided methods inherited from default bodies (lesson 116)". Probe 3 type-pin: `let first: Option<u32> = c.next();` compiles silently, witnessing `Self::Item` resolved to `u32` per the impl's `type Item =` line. Centered contrast Probe 4 (drop `type Item`): fires `error[E0046]: not all trait items implemented, missing: \`Item\`` with help line `implement the missing item: \`type Item = /* Type */;\``. Companion contrast Probe 5 (drop `fn next` instead): same E0046, `missing: \`next\``, help line writes `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<<Self as Iterator>::Item> { todo!() }`. Two contrasts together sandwich the *exactly two required items* claim; provided methods cannot trigger E0046 from absence (they are inherited). Today's lesson is step 2 of the iterator-API-coverage audit's §5 first-arc plan; today's structural read anchors every later "this method is on `Iterator`" claim (audit §5 steps 3-11 and beyond).
main_concept: there is one trait, `std::iter::Iterator`, declared in std at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md` lines 6-13 (synopsis box) and elaborated under `## Required Associated Types` (line 265) + `## Required Methods` (line 273). Its required surface is exactly two items: (1) the associated type `type Item;` (lesson 115's required-associated-type slot) — the per-impl element type; (2) the required method `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>` (lesson 111's method-signature shape, with lesson 101's `&mut self` receiver, lesson 119's `Option<T>` wrapper, and lesson 115's `Self::Item` path filling the return-type slot) — advance the iterator and return one element wrapped in `Some(_)` or `None` when exhausted. Every other Iterator method (`count`, `last`, `nth`, `take`, `skip`, `enumerate`, `map`, `filter`, `fold`, `collect`, `for_each`, `size_hint`, etc., 75 in total per the synopsis box's "Show 76 methods" minus the 1 required) sits in the trait body with a `{ ... }` *default* body (lesson 116), so an impl that fills only the required surface inherits them automatically. Three coupled facts install: (a) **the trait exists** as a single named entity `std::iter::Iterator` — every iterator the run has seen (slice iterator from 123 / 124 / 125 / 131, `rev`-iterator from 124, `zip`-iterator from 125) and every Iterator method the run will install is anchored to this one trait. (b) **The required/provided split** — `type Item` and `fn next` are required (drop either, E0046 fires); the other 75 are provided (drop them, no error possible because they were never required to be present). (c) **`Self::Item` composition with stdlib** — lesson 115 named `Self::IDENTIFIER` syntax in an introduction-only context with a user-defined trait; today is the first place `Self::IDENTIFIER` appears as a structural anchor for a real stdlib trait. The slice iterator's `type Item = &T` (per `struct.Iter.md:199`, lesson 131 evidence) is one impl's resolution of today's `type Item;` slot — the `&u64` lesson 131 saw was just `Self::Item` resolved. The audience-level mental model that installs: there is *one* iterator trait, with *two* required items, and reading any Iterator method's signature on the doc page means substituting the impl's `type Item =` line into every `Self::Item` slot. Composes lessons 131 (the empirical mechanic) + 111 (trait + impl shape) + 115 (associated-type slot + `Self::IDENTIFIER` path) + 116 (default-body mechanic licensing provided methods) + 119 (`Option<T>`) + 101 (`&mut self`) into one structural read of the trait declaration. Wholesale or named-deferred today: each of the 75 provided methods (each is its own future move per audit §5 steps 3-11 and beyond); stable-vs-nightly stability split (audit reports 60 stable + 15 nightly); `Sized` / `?Sized` / the `where Self: Sized` bound on most provided method signatures; lifetimes (the `'a` in slice iterator's `&'a T`); supertrait colon-form (`trait FusedIterator: Iterator`); generic associated types (GATs); the `IntoIterator` trait that licenses `for x in v` desugaring; closure machinery (gates `map`, `filter`, `fold`, `for_each`, `all`, `any`, `find`, etc.); the implementor list further down `trait.Iterator.md`; the formal definitions of "lazy adapter" vs "consumer".
depends_on: 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — `.next()` mechanic on a slice iterator returning `Option<&T>`. Today re-frames that mechanic as the *required method* of `std::iter::Iterator`, with the wrapper now generalized via `Self::Item` rather than per-impl-spelled `&T`. 131's *What To Ignore For Now* explicitly listed "the `Iterator` trait declaration itself (next in the arc)" as the deferred sibling — today executes that deferral per audit §5 step 2), 111-trait-decl-and-impl-for-type (load-bearing — `pub trait Name { ... } impl Name for Type { ... }`. Today reads the stdlib version of this shape; the working probe's `impl Iterator for Counter { ... }` is the same `impl Name for Type { ... }` shape from 111, just with a stdlib trait), 115-trait-associated-type (load-bearing — `type IDENTIFIER;` in trait body plus `Self::IDENTIFIER` in method signatures, resolved by `type IDENTIFIER = T;` in the impl. Today's `type Item;` and `Self::Item` reuse that mechanic; Probe 4's E0046 with `missing: \`Item\`` is the same diagnostic shape lesson 115 used for missing `Output`. 115 introduced the syntax in an intro-only context; today is the first time `Self::IDENTIFIER` appears as a structural anchor for a real stdlib trait), 116-trait-default-method-body (load-bearing — a trait method with `{ ... }` body in the trait declaration is a *default*; the impl may leave it out. Today this is the licensor for the 75 provided methods being available on the user-defined `Counter` impl without supplying their bodies. Probe 2 witnesses this empirically with `c.count()` callable on an impl supplying only the required surface), 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — `Option<T>` / `Some` / `None`. Today `T` is the `Self::Item` slot; the wrapper is `Option<Self::Item>`. The working probe's `Some(v)` and `None` arms are 119's constructors with `T = u32`), 101-mut-self-receiver (load-bearing — `&mut self` is the third receiver shape. Lesson 131 saw it via diagnostic; today sees it written into the trait declaration directly at `trait.Iterator.md:11` and `:277`), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `c.next()`, `c.count()`), 095-struct-with-named-fields (cited — `struct Counter { value: u32, limit: u32 }`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder; transcripts use `println!("{:?}", c.next())`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part map applied to E0046 in Probes 4 and 5), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::count` — `(self) -> usize`, calls `.next()` until `None` and counts" moves (audit §5 step 3 — anchors to today's "`count` is one of the 75 provided methods sitting in the trait body with a default `{ ... }` body"), future "`Iterator::last` — `(self) -> Option<Self::Item>`, infinite-iterator panic trigger" moves (audit §5 step 4 — `Self::Item` slot in `last`'s signature reads against today's structural anchor), future "`Iterator::nth` — `(&mut self, n: usize) -> Option<Self::Item>`" moves (audit §5 step 5 — same `&mut self` and `Self::Item` slots as `next`), future "`Iterator::take` — `(self, n) -> Take<Self>` lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 6), future "`Iterator::skip` — `(self, n) -> Skip<Self>` lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 7), future "`Iterator::enumerate` — `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>`, yields `(usize, Item)`" moves (audit §5 step 8), future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter" moves (audit §5 step 9 — formalizes lesson 131's "exhausted means `None` forever" empirical rule), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`" moves (audit §5 step 10), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11), future closure sub-arc moves per audit §4.4.1 (gates `map`/`filter`/`fold`/`reduce`/`for_each`/`all`/`any`/`find` and 19 more — each will read against today's "this method has a default body in the trait, here is what it does"), future `IntoIterator` sub-arc per audit §4.4.2 (gates the `for x in expr` desugar, `chain`, `zip`-at-trait-depth, `cmp`, `partial_cmp`, `eq`), future "the `where Self: Sized` bound on provided methods" moves (every provided method's signature carries this bound; today defers it), future "stable-vs-nightly Iterator API split" moves (audit §3 lists 15 nightly methods; today defers stability), future "lifetimes in `&'a T` for slice iterators' `type Item`" moves (wholesale deferred since 123), future "supertrait colon-form `trait FusedIterator: Iterator`" moves, future implementor-list-reading moves (the long list further down `trait.Iterator.md`)
```
