```text
id: 133-iterator-count
move: count a slice iterator's elements with `.count()`. The working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50]; let n = v.iter().count(); println!("{}", n); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints `5`; an empty vec (`vec![]`) yields `0`. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:416` spells `fn count(self) -> usize where Self: Sized,` — bare `self` (lesson 102's consuming receiver shape), return type `usize` (lesson 080), with the `where Self: Sized,` bound named-deferred. Centered contrast probe (use the iterator after `.count()`): the source `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let iter = v.iter(); let n = iter.count(); let _ = iter.count(); println!("{}", n); }` fires `error[E0382]: use of moved value: \`iter\`` with three annotations (`move occurs because \`iter\` has type \`std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>\`, which does not implement the \`Copy\` trait`; `\`iter\` moved due to this method call`; `value used here after move`) plus a `note:` block at the method-definition site reading verbatim `\`count\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`iter\`` — same E-code and same `note:` template lesson 102 captured for `Wrapper::into_inner`, but with the `-->` of the note pointing at core's `library/core/src/iter/traits/iterator.rs:225:13` (the std source for `Iterator::count`'s default body). The diagnostic literally proves the trait declaration's `fn count(self)` shape: if the receiver were `&self` or `&mut self`, the second call would not move-fail. Type-pin probes Probes 4-5 sandwich the return type: `let n: usize = v.iter().count();` compiles silently; `let n: u64 = ...` fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected \`u64\`, found \`usize\``. Empirical witness for "calls `next` until `None`" claim via a side-effect `Trace` impl supplying only the lesson-132 required surface (`type Item = u32;` + `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>` printing each call): for `limit: 3` the output is `next() -> Some(0)` / `Some(1)` / `Some(2)` / `None` / `count = 3` (four `next()` calls visible, three `Some(_)` + one `None`, count == 3); for `limit: 0` the output is a single `next() -> None` followed by `count = 0` (witnesses corpus claim "next has to be called at least once even if the iterator does not have any elements" at `trait.Iterator.md:421-422`). The `Trace` impl never wrote `fn count` itself, corroborating lesson 132's claim that `count` is one of the 75 provided methods inherited via default bodies (lesson 116). Today's move is step 3 of the iterator-API-coverage audit's §5 first-arc plan: smallest stable consumer, names self-by-value consuming and the call-`next`-until-`None`-and-count semantic. The `usize::MAX` overflow + panic note from `trait.Iterator.md:424-434` is named as a corpus fact and not probed (impractical to construct an iterator > 2^64-1 elements).
main_concept: `Iterator::count` is one of the 75 provided methods named-deferred at lesson 132. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:416` spells the signature `fn count(self) -> usize where Self: Sized,`; the synopsis-box line at `:19-20` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body shape) which is what licenses every iterator inheriting `count` for free as soon as it supplies the lesson-132 required surface. Three coupled facts install: (1) **Receiver `self` (no `&`)** is lesson 102's *consuming* shape; calling `iter.count()` *moves* the iterator into the method, and using the binding afterward fires E0382 with a `note:` block at the method-definition site naming the receiver shape (`\`count\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`iter\``). This is the first place in the run where lesson 102's rule fires on a *stdlib* method. The contrast diagnostic structurally aligns to lesson 102: same E-code, same `note:` template, but `-->` of the note points at core's `iter/traits/iterator.rs` instead of user source. (2) **Return type `usize`** (lesson 080's architecture-width unsigned integer, lesson 077's specific row of the integer family). The number of elements in a Rust collection is always reported in `usize` — same type `Vec::len` returned at lesson 107. Probe 4 silent compile + Probe 5 E0308 with `expected u64, found usize` sandwich this claim. (3) **Defining algorithm: call `next` until `None`, return the number of `Some(_)`s** (`trait.Iterator.md:420-421`). Probe 6's side-effect `Trace` impl witnesses this empirically — four `next()` calls visible (three `Some(_)` + one `None`), `count = 3`. Probe 7's empty Trace witnesses the corollary at `:421-422` ("Note that `next` has to be called at least once even if the iterator does not have any elements") — limit-0 records exactly one `next() -> None` before `count = 0`. The audience-level mental model that installs: `.count()` is the smallest concrete consumer; calling it moves the iterator (so the binding is gone afterward), the result is a `usize`, and internally it just walks `next` until `None` and tallies. The audience crosses from "lesson 132 told me there are 75 provided methods, I have not called one" to "I have called one — `count` — and I know it consumes its receiver, returns `usize`, and walks `next` to `None`. The receiver shape is the new operational fact today; the rest is composition." Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `where Self: Sized,` bound (appears on most provided methods, future move; the `Sized` / `?Sized` distinction is named in trait.Iterator.md:416 but its semantics are wholesale-deferred); the `Self::Item` link in provided-method signatures (composes 132 + 115; not exercised today since `count` does not return an `Item`); `usize::MAX` overflow / panic semantics (corpus fact at `:424-434`, not probed); `.clone()` on iterators (named in the contrast's `help:` line, gates many iterator workflows); the `for x in v` desugaring through `IntoIterator`; closures (gate 27 other Iterator methods per audit §4.4.1); the other 74 provided methods (audit §5 steps 4-11 and beyond). The unlock per audit §5 is every later consumer/adapter Iterator method that takes `self` by value (`last`, `take`, `skip`, `enumerate`, `step_by`, `fuse`) — each will reuse today's "self-by-value consumes" rule on the same `Iterator` trait declaration anchor.
depends_on: 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait declaration with 75 provided methods inherited via default bodies. `count` is one of them; today is the first concrete provided-method call. 132's unlock list explicitly named today's move: "future `Iterator::count` — `(self) -> usize`, calls `.next()` until `None` and counts" with audit §5 step 3 anchor), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `.next()` on a slice iterator returning `Option<&T>`; advances the cursor; `None` after exhaustion. `count`'s defining algorithm at `trait.Iterator.md:420-421` is *defined* as repeated `next` calls until `None`. Probe 6 and Probe 7 witness this empirically), 102-self-by-value-receiver (load-bearing — installs `self` (no `&`, no `mut`) as the third receiver shape; calling such a method consumes the value, and using the binding afterward fires E0382 with a `note:` block naming the receiver shape. Today is the first place this rule fires on a *stdlib* method; the centered contrast Probe 3 reuses lesson 102's E0382 + `note:` template verbatim, only with the method name and binding name substituted), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs the twelve integer types and the sign-and-width naming rule. `count`'s return type slot is `usize` specifically, not interchangeable with `u64`; Probes 4-5 sandwich this distinction empirically), 123-vec-iter (cited — `v.iter()` returns the slice iterator; the receiver in today's working probe), 119-option-some-none (cited — `Option<T>` / `Some` / `None`. `count` reads the `Some(_)` vs `None` returns from `next`), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — the `{ ... }` body marker on the synopsis-box line at `trait.Iterator.md:19-20` is the default-body mechanic that licenses inheritance), 107-vec-basics (cited — `Vec<T>` with `.len() -> usize`. Same return type as today's `count`; today notes both yield `5` for `vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50]` but compute differently — `len` reads a stored field, `count` walks `next`), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.count()`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder; today's transcripts use `println!("{}", n)` for `usize`), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to E0382 in the centered contrast and E0308 in the type-pin contrast), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::last` — `(self) -> Option<Self::Item>`, smallest consumer returning the last element; carries the infinite-iterator panic trigger" moves (audit §5 step 4 — reuses today's self-by-value consuming rule plus the `Self::Item` link from 132 + 115), future "`Iterator::take` — `(self, n) -> Take<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 6 — same self-by-value rule, but returns a new iterator instead of consuming end-to-end), future "`Iterator::skip` — `(self, n) -> Skip<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 7), future "`Iterator::enumerate` — `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>`, yields `(usize, Item)`" moves (audit §5 step 8), future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter" moves (audit §5 step 9), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`" moves (audit §5 step 10), future "`Iterator::nth` — `(&mut self, n: usize) -> Option<Self::Item>`" moves (audit §5 step 5 — `&mut self` instead of `self`, contrast against today), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — `&self` borrowing, contrast against today), future "the `where Self: Sized,` bound on provided methods — what `Sized` actually means, `?Sized` opt-out, dyn dispatch" moves (named on `count`'s signature today, deferred), future "`usize::MAX` overflow / panic semantics" moves (named as corpus fact today, not probed), future "`.clone()` on iterators — `iter.clone().count()` from today's `help:` line; the `Clone` trait for iterator adapters" moves, future "the `Iterator::count` source body itself — actually a trivial `self.fold(0, |count, _| count + 1)` per the std source — readable once closures + `fold` land" moves, future "`Vec::len` vs `iter().count()` — same `usize` return, different cost (O(1) vs O(n))" moves (composes today + 107)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0382.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/iterator-api-coverage.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/133-iterator-count.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/133-iterator-count.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/133-iterator-count.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 134-iterator-last
move: pull the final element of a slice iterator with `.last()`. The working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let result = v.iter().last(); println!("{:?}", result); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints `Some(30)`; an empty vec (`vec![]`) yields `None`. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:448` spells `fn last(self) -> Option<Self::Item> where Self: Sized,` — bare `self` (lesson 102's consuming receiver shape, same as `count` at `:416`), return type `Option<Self::Item>` (lesson 119 wrapper with `T = Self::Item`, the lesson 132/115 associated-type slot). For the slice iterator over `Vec<u64>` (lessons 123, 131), `Self::Item = &u64`, so `v.iter().last()` returns `Option<&u64>` empirically. Centered contrast probe (use the iterator after `.last()`): the source `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let iter = v.iter(); let _ = iter.last(); let _ = iter.last(); }` fires `error[E0382]: use of moved value: \`iter\`` with three annotations identical in template to lesson 133's E0382 (`move occurs because \`iter\` has type \`std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>\`, which does not implement the \`Copy\` trait`; `\`iter\` moved due to this method call`; `value used here after move`) plus a `note:` block at the method-definition site reading verbatim `\`last\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`iter\`` — same E-code and same `note:` template lesson 133 captured for `count`, only `last` substituted into the method-name slot, with the `-->` of the note pointing at core's `library/core/src/iter/traits/iterator.rs:258:12` (different line than `count`'s 225:13 because each provided method has its own definition site). The diagnostic literally proves the trait declaration's `fn last(self)` shape: if the receiver were `&self` or `&mut self`, the second call would not move-fail. Type-pin probes Probes 4-5 sandwich the new-fact return type: `let x: Option<&u64> = v.iter().last();` compiles silently; `let x: Option<u64> = v.iter().last();` fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected \`Option<u64>\`, found \`Option<&u64>\`` and a secondary `= note: expected enum \`Option<_>\`; found enum \`Option<&_>\`` plus a `help:` proposing `.copied()`. This is the FIRST place in the run where a *provided* Iterator method's return type is anchored to the `Self::Item` slot (lesson 133's `count` returned a primitive `usize` directly). Today's move is step 4 of the iterator-API-coverage audit's §5 first-arc plan: small consumer, names the infinite-iterator panic trigger from `trait.Iterator.md:456-458`. The Panics note ("This function might panic if the iterator is infinite") is named as a corpus fact and not probed (constructing a true infinite iterator like `(0u64..).last()` would hang the build).
main_concept: `Iterator::last` is one of the 75 provided methods named-deferred at lesson 132. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:448` spells the signature `fn last(self) -> Option<Self::Item> where Self: Sized,`; the synopsis-box line at `:21-22` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body shape) which is what licenses every iterator inheriting `last` for free. Three coupled facts install: (1) **Receiver `self` (no `&`)** is lesson 102's *consuming* shape — same as lesson 133's `count`. Calling `iter.last()` *moves* the iterator into the method, and using the binding afterward fires E0382 with a `note:` block at the method-definition site naming the receiver shape (`\`last\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`iter\``). The contrast diagnostic structurally aligns to lesson 133 exactly — same E-code, same body, same `note:` template, same `help:` proposing `.clone()`; only the method-name slot substitutes and the `-->` of the note points at a different line in core's `iter/traits/iterator.rs` because each provided method has its own definition site. (2) **Return type `Option<Self::Item>`** — *this is the new fact today.* Where lesson 133's `count` returned a primitive `usize`, `last` returns `Option<Self::Item>` — the lesson 115 associated-type slot (lesson 132's `Self::Item`) substituted by the impl. For the slice iterator over `Vec<u64>` (lessons 123, 131), `Self::Item = &u64`, so `v.iter().last()` returns `Option<&u64>`. This is the FIRST place in the run where a *provided* Iterator method's return is anchored to the `Self::Item` slot rather than to a primitive — opening the way for `nth`, `find`, `max`, `min`, and every other consumer whose return wrapper mentions `Self::Item`. Probe 4 silent compile of `let x: Option<&u64> = v.iter().last();` plus Probe 5 E0308 with `expected Option<u64>, found Option<&u64>` sandwich this claim from two sides; the secondary E0308 note `expected enum Option<_>; found enum Option<&_>` makes the inner `&` glyph rustc-visible. (3) **Defining algorithm: walk `next` until `None`, remembering the most recent `Some(_)`** (`trait.Iterator.md:450-454` reads "evaluate the iterator until it returns `None`. While doing so, it keeps track of the current element. After `None` is returned, `last()` will then return the last element it saw"). Same `next`-until-`None` template as lesson 133's `count`, different bookkeeping (track most recent `Some(_)` vs. tally count). Probe 1 (`Some(30)` for three-element vec) and Probe 2 (`None` for empty vec) sandwich both branches of the bookkeeping. The audience-level mental model that installs: `.last()` is the second concrete consumer; calling it moves the iterator (so the binding is gone afterward), the result is `Option<Self::Item>` — and for slice iterators that's `Option<&T>`, the same `Self::Item = &T` link lesson 131 caught through `.next()`. The audience crosses from "lesson 133's `count` returned a primitive `usize`; every consumer probably returns a primitive" to "no — the second consumer's return wrapper is anchored to the trait's associated-type slot. For slice iters that means `Option<&u64>` empirically. The substitution flows from `Self::Item = &u64` (lesson 131) through `Iterator::last`'s `Option<Self::Item>` return slot (today)." Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `where Self: Sized,` bound (still); the panic-if-infinite case (corpus fact at `:456-458`, not probed because constructing a true infinite iterator hangs the build); `.copied()` on `Option<&u64>` to obtain `Option<u64>` (named in Probe 5's `help:`); `Iterator::copied` and `Iterator::cloned` (audit §4.4.3); `.clone()` on iterators (named in Probe 3's `help:`, gates many iterator workflows); the default body of `Iterator::last` itself in core (a `fold(None, |_, x| Some(x))` one-liner, readable once closures + `fold` land); the other 73 provided methods (audit §5 steps 5-11 and beyond). The unlock per audit §5 is every later consumer Iterator method whose return is anchored to `Self::Item`: `nth` (`(&mut self, usize) -> Option<Self::Item>`), `find`, `max`, `min`, `reduce`. Today's lesson opens the path from "`Self::Item` is a slot in `next`'s required signature" (lesson 132) to "`Self::Item` flows through any provided method whose return wrapper mentions it" (today).
depends_on: 133-iterator-count (load-bearing — installs `(self) -> usize` consumer with the walk-`next`-until-`None` template, plus the centered E0382 + `note:` template for "this stdlib method consumes its receiver". Today extends to `(self) -> Option<Self::Item>` — same self-by-value rule, same walker; the new fact is the return-type slot anchored to `Self::Item` instead of a primitive. Probe 3 today is structurally isomorphic to lesson 133's centered E0382), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait declaration with 75 provided methods inherited via default bodies. `last` is one of them; its synopsis-box line at `trait.Iterator.md:21-22` ends in `{ ... }`. The `Self::Item` slot today resolves through the same lesson 132/115 mechanism), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `Self::Item = &u64` for the slice iterator over `Vec<u64>` empirically, through `.next()`'s `Option<&u64>` return. Today's `last` returns `Option<&u64>` for the same iterator — the substitution flows through any provided method whose return mentions `Self::Item`. Probes 4-5 today witness this resolution through `Iterator::last`'s return slot rather than `Iterator::next`'s), 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — installs `Option<T>` / `Some` / `None`. Today `T = Self::Item`, substituted by the impl; Probe 1 produces `Some(30)`, Probe 2 produces `None`, both empirically witnessing the lesson 119 variants on `last`'s return), 102-self-by-value-receiver (load-bearing — same self-by-value consuming rule lesson 133 fired on `count`. Probe 3 today reuses the E0382 + `note:` template), 115-trait-associated-type (load-bearing — installs `type IDENTIFIER;` in trait body, `Self::IDENTIFIER` in method signatures, resolved by `type IDENTIFIER = T;` in the impl. Today is the FIRST place in the run where a `Self::Item` slot appears in the *return* type of a *provided* (default-body) Iterator method that the audience *calls*; lesson 132 named the shape on the synopsis box, lesson 131 caught the `&u64` substitution through `next`'s required signature), 123-vec-iter (cited — `v.iter()` returns the slice iterator; the receiver in today's working probe), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — the `{ ... }` body marker on the synopsis-box line at `trait.Iterator.md:21-22` is the default-body mechanic that licenses inheritance), 093-standard-library-prelude (cited — `{:?}` Debug formatter; today's transcripts use `println!("{:?}", result)` for `Option<&u64>`; same convention lesson 131 captured for `Option<&u64>`), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.last()`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to E0382 in the centered contrast and E0308 in the type-pin contrast), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::nth` — `(&mut self, n: usize) -> Option<Self::Item>`, drops preceding elements" moves (audit §5 step 5 — reuses today's `Option<Self::Item>` return-slot rule but with `&mut self` instead of `self`; first contrast against today's self-by-value), future "`Iterator::find` — `(&mut self, predicate) -> Option<Self::Item>`" moves (gated on closure arc; reuses today's `Option<Self::Item>` return slot), future "`Iterator::max`, `min` — `(self) -> Option<Self::Item> where Self::Item: Ord`" moves (gated on Ord-bound arc; reuses today's `Option<Self::Item>` return-slot rule), future "`Iterator::reduce` — `(self, FnMut(Item, Item) -> Item) -> Option<Self::Item>`" moves (gated on closure arc), future "`Iterator::take` — `(self, n) -> Take<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 6 — reuses today's self-by-value rule, but returns a new iterator instead of consuming end-to-end), future "`Iterator::skip` — `(self, n) -> Skip<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 7), future "`Iterator::enumerate` — `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>`, yields `(usize, Item)`" moves (audit §5 step 8 — uses `Self::Item` inside the tuple yield-type), future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter" moves (audit §5 step 9), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`" moves (audit §5 step 10), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — `&self` borrowing, contrast against today's `self`), future "`Option::copied` — `Option<&T> -> Option<T> where T: Copy`" moves (named in Probe 5's `help:` line; today's `Option<&u64>` to `Option<u64>` peel), future "`Iterator::copied` — `Iterator<Item=&T> -> Iterator<Item=T> where T: Copy`" moves (audit §4.4.3, gated on `Copy` trait), future "the `where Self: Sized,` bound on provided methods" moves (still named-deferred), future "`std::iter::repeat` and the `(0..).last()` infinite-iterator panic trigger" moves (corpus fact at `:456-458` named today, not probed), future "`Iterator::last`'s default body — actually `self.fold(None, |_, x| Some(x))` per the std source — readable once closures + `fold` land" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
```

```text
id: 135-iterator-nth
move: index into a slice iterator with `iter.nth(n)`. The working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50]; let mut iter = v.iter(); println!("{:?}", iter.nth(1)); println!("{:?}", iter.nth(0)); println!("{:?}", iter.nth(0)); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints `Some(20)` / `Some(30)` / `Some(40)` (three lines). The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:507` spells `fn nth(&mut self, n: usize) -> Option<Self::Item>` — `&mut self` (lesson 101's third receiver shape, same shape lesson 131 caught on the *required* `next`), second parameter `n: usize` (lesson 080), return `Option<Self::Item>` (lesson 119 wrapper with `T = Self::Item`, lesson 132/115 associated-type slot). Synopsis-box line at `:24` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker, no `where Self: Sized` bound — structurally different from `count`/`last`). For the slice iterator over `Vec<u64>` (lessons 123, 131), `Self::Item = &u64`, so `iter.nth(1)` is `Option<&u64>`. Centered contrast Probe 2 (drop `mut` from `let mut iter`): fires `error[E0596]: cannot borrow \`iter\` as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` with `help: consider changing this to be mutable` proposing `let mut iter = v.iter();` and `+++` insert marker — same E-code and shape lesson 131 captured for `.next()` (because both methods take `&mut self`). Corroborating Probe 4 (`let mut iter = v.iter(); let _ = iter.nth(1); println!("{:?}", iter.nth(0));`) compiles silent and prints `Some(30)` — empirical witness that `&mut self` does not move the iterator (in contrast to lesson 133's `count` and lesson 134's `last`, both of which fire E0382 in the analogous reuse pattern). Past-end Probe 3 (`iter.nth(100)` on five-element iter): silent compile, prints `None`, exit 0 — witnesses corpus claim at `trait.Iterator.md:519-520` ("`nth()` will return `None` if `n` is greater than or equal to the length of the iterator"), no panic. Type-pin probes Probe 5-6 sandwich the return type: `let x: Option<&u64> = iter.nth(1);` compiles silent; `let x: Option<u64> = iter.nth(1);` fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected \`Option<u64>\`, found \`Option<&u64>\`` and secondary `note: expected enum \`Option<_>\`; found enum \`Option<&_>\`` plus `help:` proposing `.copied()` — same shape lesson 134 captured for `last`. Probe 7 (std doc replay `iter.nth(1); iter.nth(1)` on `vec![10, 20, 30]`): prints `Some(20)` then `None`, mirroring `trait.Iterator.md:533-540` exactly modulo `i32` vs `&u64` rendering. Walk-trace for the working probe: cursor starts before `&10`; `iter.nth(1)` consumes `&10` (preceding) and returns `&20` (also consumed from iter's POV per `:514-517`), cursor now before `&30`; `iter.nth(0)` returns `&30`, cursor before `&40`; `iter.nth(0)` returns `&40`. Each call advances the cursor by `n+1` positions — Probe 1's three-line output is the empirical witness of "preceding elements as well as the returned element will be consumed" + "`nth(0)` multiple times returns different elements". Today's move is step 5 of the iterator-API-coverage audit's §5 first-arc plan: first *provided* Iterator method with `&mut self` receiver, first *provided* method with a non-`self` parameter. The `nth_back` sibling on `DoubleEndedIterator` is named-deferred. The default body of `nth` in core (an internal `for`-loop over `self.next()`) is named-deferred (readable once the `for` desugar lands).
main_concept: `Iterator::nth` is one of the 75 provided methods named-deferred at lesson 132. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:507` spells the signature `fn nth(&mut self, n: usize) -> Option<Self::Item>`; the synopsis-box line at `:24` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body shape) which is what licenses every iterator inheriting `nth` for free. Three coupled facts install simultaneously: (1) **Receiver `&mut self`** (lesson 101's third receiver shape) — the FIRST *provided* Iterator method the run installs that takes `&mut self`. Lesson 131 caught the same shape on the *required* `next`; today is the first provided sibling. The call does not move the iterator: the binding stays usable for subsequent `nth` calls. Probe 1 demonstrates this with three sequential calls on the same binding; Probe 4 demonstrates it across a statement boundary (`let _ = iter.nth(1); iter.nth(0)`). The centered contrast (Probe 2: drop `mut`) is E0596 — *not* E0382 (lesson 133/134's contrast). The diagnostic kind is itself diagnostic of the receiver shape. (2) **Second parameter `n: usize`** — first *provided* Iterator method the audience sees with a non-`self` argument. Lessons 112/113 introduced multi-parameter trait methods generally; today specializes to `Iterator`. The type is `usize` (lesson 080) — the indexing integer. (3) **Return type `Option<Self::Item>`** — same wrapper as lesson 134's `.last()`. For the slice iterator over `Vec<u64>` (lessons 123, 131), `Self::Item = &u64`, so `iter.nth(1)` is `Option<&u64>` empirically (Probes 5-6 sandwich). The audience-level mental model that installs: `nth` is the *first* provided Iterator method that does *not* consume its receiver. The split between consuming (`count`, `last` — lesson 133/134's `self`) and mutably-borrowing (`nth` — today's `&mut self`) is the operational frame for every later Iterator method. The audience crosses from "every Iterator method I have called consumes the iterator; the binding is gone after one call" to "the receiver shape is *per method*. Some take `self` (consume), some take `&mut self` (borrow + cursor-advance), and the diagnostic kind on misuse (E0382 vs E0596) tells me which." Operational corollaries today: (a) **Each call advances the cursor by `n+1` positions** (`trait.Iterator.md:514-517`): preceding elements are dropped, the returned element is also consumed, multiple `nth(0)` calls return different elements. Probe 1 lines 2-3 (`Some(30)`, `Some(40)`) are the empirical witness. (b) **Past-end yields `None`, no panic** (`:519-520`): Probe 3 (`nth(100)`) and Probe 7 (second `nth(1)` on now-empty iter). The corpus prose hedges no panic; the corner case is a clean `None`. (c) **No `Self: Sized` bound** on `nth`'s signature — structurally different from `count` (`:19-20`) and `last` (`:21-22`), both of which carry `where Self: Sized`. Named-deferred either way today; named here for completeness because the synopsis-box line is structurally shorter than `count`/`last`. Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `where Self: Sized` bound (still); the default body of `nth` in core (an internal `for`-loop over `self.next()`, readable once the `for` desugar lands); `Option::copied` and `Iterator::copied` (named in Probe 6's `help:`); `nth_back` (sibling on `DoubleEndedIterator`); `usize::MAX` overflow corner cases on very long iterators; the other 72 provided methods (audit §5 steps 6-11 and beyond). The unlock per audit §5 is every later Iterator method that takes `&mut self` (`size_hint`'s sibling `&self`-form contrasts) — `take`, `skip`, `enumerate`, `fuse`, `step_by` (audit §5 steps 6-10) all take `self` and contrast back to today's `&mut self`; `find`, `all`, `any`, `position` (closure sub-arc, audit §4.4.1) all take `&mut self` and reuse today's "binding survives" rule; `size_hint` (audit §5 step 11) takes `&self` (third receiver shape on `Iterator`'s provided methods) and contrasts with both today and lesson 133/134.
depends_on: 134-iterator-last (load-bearing — installs `Option<Self::Item>` return slot on a *provided* Iterator method, with `Self::Item = &u64` for slice iterators over `Vec<u64>`. Today reuses that exact return slot; the new fact is the receiver shape and the second parameter. Probes 5-6 today are structurally isomorphic to lesson 134's Probes 4-5, only the method-name and the contrast diagnostic E0596-vs-E0382 substitute), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait with 75 provided methods inherited via default bodies. `nth` is one of them; today is the first concrete *provided* method with a non-`self` parameter. The synopsis-box line `nth` at `:24` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker)), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `.next()`'s `&mut self` receiver, the cursor-advance semantic, the `let mut iter = v.iter();` requirement, and the E0596 contrast for dropped `mut`. Today's `nth` is structurally `next` called `n+1` times: per `trait.Iterator.md:514-517` "all preceding elements, as well as the returned element, will be consumed". Probe 2 today is the same E0596 + `help:` shape lesson 131 captured for `.next()`, only with the method-name slot substituted), 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — installs `Option<T>` / `Some` / `None`. Today `T = Self::Item`, substituted by the impl; Probe 1 produces `Some(20)` / `Some(30)` / `Some(40)`, Probe 3 produces `None`, both empirically witnessing the lesson 119 variants on `nth`'s return), 115-trait-associated-type (load-bearing — installs `type IDENTIFIER;` in trait body, `Self::IDENTIFIER` in method signatures, resolved by `type IDENTIFIER = T;` in the impl. Today's `Self::Item` slot in `nth`'s return resolves the same way lesson 134's did; for slice iterators over `Vec<u64>`, `Self::Item = &u64`), 101-mut-self-receiver (load-bearing — installs `&mut self` as the third receiver shape, the receiver-shorthand for `self: &mut Self`. Today is the FIRST place this shape appears on a *provided* Iterator method. Probe 2 fires E0596 with `help:` proposing `let mut iter = v.iter();` — same diagnostic shape lesson 131 captured for `.next()` and lesson 101 captured for user-defined `&mut self` methods), 006-mut-binding (load-bearing — installs `let mut name = value;`. Without `mut` on the binding, the dot call on `&mut self` fires E0596 — Probe 2's centered contrast), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `usize` is one specific row of the twelve integer types. `nth`'s second parameter slot is `usize` specifically), 123-vec-iter (cited — `v.iter()` returns the slice iterator; the receiver in today's working probe), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — the `{ ... }` body marker on the synopsis-box line at `trait.Iterator.md:24` is the default-body mechanic that licenses inheritance), 093-standard-library-prelude (cited — `{:?}` Debug formatter; today's transcripts use `println!("{:?}", iter.nth(1))` for `Option<&u64>`), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.nth(n)`; the binding-then-method form is from lesson 131), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to E0596 in the centered contrast and E0308 in the type-pin contrast), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::take` — `(self, n) -> Take<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 6 — same `n: usize` second-parameter shape installed today, but receiver returns to `self` consuming and result is a new iterator), future "`Iterator::skip` — `(self, n) -> Skip<Self>`, lazy adapter" moves (audit §5 step 7 — sibling of `take` with the inverse semantic; same `n: usize` slot from today), future "`Iterator::enumerate` — `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>`, yields `(usize, Item)`" moves (audit §5 step 8 — `Self::Item` link inside a tuple yield-type, plus `usize` indexing slot today's `n` parameter), future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter" moves (audit §5 step 9), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`, panics on `step == 0`" moves (audit §5 step 10 — same `usize` second-parameter shape installed today, but with a panic precondition), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — `&self` borrowing, third receiver shape on `Iterator`'s provided methods, contrasts back against today's `&mut self` and lesson 133/134's `self`), future "`Iterator::find` — `(&mut self, predicate) -> Option<Self::Item>`" moves (gated on closure arc; reuses today's `&mut self` receiver and `Option<Self::Item>` return slot), future "`Iterator::all`, `any` — `(&mut self, predicate) -> bool`, short-circuit" moves (gated on closure arc; reuses today's `&mut self` receiver), future "`Iterator::position` — `(&mut self, predicate) -> Option<usize>`" moves (gated on closure arc; reuses today's `&mut self` receiver, returns `Option<usize>` with same `usize` indexing slot today's `n` parameter has), future "`Iterator::nth_back` — sibling on `DoubleEndedIterator`" moves (named-deferred today; needs the `DoubleEndedIterator` trait declaration), future "the `where Self: Sized` bound on provided methods — what `Sized` actually means, why `&mut self` methods omit it, `?Sized` opt-out, dyn dispatch" moves (today's `nth` is the first provided method without `Self: Sized`; the contrast against lesson 133/134's `Self: Sized` is now legible), future "`Iterator::nth`'s default body itself — actually a `for` loop over `self.next()` per the std source — readable once the `for` desugar lands" moves, future "`Option::copied` — `Option<&T> -> Option<T> where T: Copy`" moves (named in Probe 6's `help:`; today's `Option<&u64>` to `Option<u64>` peel)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0596.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/iterator-api-coverage.md

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 136-iterator-take
move: limit a slice iterator to its first `n` elements with `iter.take(n)`. The working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50]; let n = v.iter().take(2).count(); println!("{}", n); let last = v.iter().take(3).last(); println!("{:?}", last); for x in v.iter().take(2) { println!("{}", x); } }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints four lines `2` / `Some(30)` / `10` / `20`. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1376` spells `fn take(self, n: usize) -> Take<Self> where Self: Sized,` — bare `self` (lesson 102's consuming receiver shape, same as `count` at `:416` and `last` at `:448`), second parameter `n: usize` (lesson 080, same shape as `nth` at `:507`'s second parameter), return type `Take<Self>` — a wrapper struct documented at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Take.md:1-15` (`pub struct Take<I> { /* private fields */ }`) which itself implements `Iterator`. Synopsis-box line at `:66-67` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker). Centered contrast Probe 5 (call `.take(2)` twice on the same binding): fires `error[E0382]: use of moved value: \`iter\`` with `note:` block at the method-definition site reading verbatim `\`std::iter::Iterator::take\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`iter\`` and `help:` proposing `iter.clone().take(2)` — same E-code and same `note:` template lessons 133 and 134 captured for `count` and `last`, only `take` substitutes into the method-name slot, with the `-->` of the note pointing at core's `library/core/src/iter/traits/iterator.rs:1414:12` (different line than `count`'s 225:13 and `last`'s 258:12 because each provided method has its own definition site). The diagnostic literally proves the trait declaration's `fn take(self, ...)` shape: if the receiver were `&mut self` or `&self`, the second call would not move-fail. Lazy-walk witness Probe 2 (Trace iterator + take, three steps): step A builds `Trace { n: 0 }.take(2)` and binds the result without iterating — output prints zero `next:` lines between markers (empirical witness that building the `Take<Self>` value does not pull on the inner iterator); step B calls `.next()` once on a fresh wrapped value — output prints exactly one `next:0` line then `Some(0)` (one pull per `.next()`); step C calls `.count()` on a fresh wrapped value — output prints exactly two `next:` lines (`next:0`, `next:1`) then `count = 2` (`.count()` pulls `n` times not all). Past-end Probe 3 (`take(100).count()` on five-element vec): silent compile, prints `5` not `100` not panic, exit 0 — witnesses corpus claim at `trait.Iterator.md:1383-1385` ("The returned iterator is a prefix of length `n` if the original iterator contains at least `n` elements, otherwise it contains all of the (fewer than `n`) elements"). Iterator-witness Probe 4 (`let mut t = v.iter().take(2); t.next(); t.next(); t.next();`): prints `Some(10)` / `Some(20)` / `None` — `Take<Self>` participates in the `.next()` mechanic from lesson 131. Type-pin Probe 6 (`let _x: u32 = v.iter().take(2);`): fires E0308 with inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`Take<Iter<'_, u64>>\`` and secondary `note: expected type \`u32\`; found struct \`std::iter::Take<std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>>\`` — rustc spells the actual return type out, grounding "the result is itself an iterator" empirically. Today's move is step 6 of the iterator-API-coverage audit's §5 first-arc plan: first centered lazy-adapter lesson on the `Iterator` trait. The `Take<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body are referred to opaquely; the `Self: Sized` bound is named-deferred (still); the `by_ref` adapter named in the std doc at `:1423-1437` is named-deferred; the infinite-range example `(0..).take(n)` at `:1402` depends on `Range`'s `Iterator` impl (still deferred since 022); the default body of `take` in core is named-deferred (readable once closures + `unsafe` patterns land).
main_concept: `Iterator::take` is one of the 75 provided methods named-deferred at lesson 132. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1376` spells the signature `fn take(self, n: usize) -> Take<Self> where Self: Sized,`; the synopsis-box line at `:66-67` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body shape) which is what licenses every iterator inheriting `take` for free. Three coupled facts install simultaneously: (1) **Receiver `self`** (lesson 102's consuming shape) — same as `count` and `last`. The call moves the iterator into `take`. Probe 5 captures the E0382 + `note:` template lesson 133 captured for `count` and 134 captured for `last`, only with `take` in the method-name slot. (2) **Second parameter `n: usize`** — same shape lesson 135's `nth` installed: a non-`self` argument typed `usize`. (3) **Return type `Take<Self>` — *itself an iterator*.** *This is the new fact today.* Where lesson 133's `count` returned `usize`, lessons 134/135's `last`/`nth` returned `Option<Self::Item>` (a wrapper around an element), today's `take` returns *another iterator value* — the wrapper struct `Take<Self>` documented at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Take.md`. Its definition is `pub struct Take<I> { /* private fields */ }` (referred to opaquely; today does not unpack the struct). The struct implements `Iterator`, so `.next()`, `.count()`, `.last()`, and `for` all work on it (Probes 1, 4, 7 sandwich this from four directions). The audience-level mental model that installs: Iterator methods split into *consumers* (return a primitive or `Option`-wrapped element) and *adapters* (return another iterator). `take` is the smallest adapter. The split is the operational frame for every later Iterator method choice in audit §2's table. The audience crosses from "every Iterator method I have called returns a primitive or an `Option`; calling the method does the work" to "some Iterator methods (*adapters*) return another iterator value. They are *lazy*: building the wrapper does no work. The work happens only when something pulls on the wrapper — `.next()`, `.count()`, `.last()`, or a `for` loop. Adapters compose with consumers via lesson-049 method chaining: `v.iter().take(2).count()` reads 'take the slice iter, limit to the first two, then count'." Operational corollaries today: (a) **Building the `Take<Self>` does not call `next` on the inner iterator** — Probe 2 step A: zero `next:` lines after the build statement. The corpus's lazy framing is at `trait.Iterator.md:866-867` ("`map()` is lazy") and `:892` ("it won't even execute, as it is lazy") — applied to adapters generally; today's Trace probe is the empirical witness specifically for `take`. (b) **Pulling on the wrapper pulls on the inner iterator at most `n` times** — Probe 2 step B (one pull per `.next()`); step C (two pulls per `.count()` on `take(2)`). (c) **Past-end yields the full iterator's elements, no panic** — Probe 3 (`take(100)` on five-element vec → `5`). Matches `trait.Iterator.md:1383-1385`. (d) **Adapters compose with consumers** — Probe 1 chains `.take(2).count()`, `.take(3).last()`, and `for x in v.iter().take(2)`; all three produce the expected output. First chain in the run that puts a *consumer* after a *non-consumer* iterator method. Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `where Self: Sized` bound (still); the `Take<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body; the `by_ref` adapter named at `:1423-1437` ("take from the iterator without consuming it, and then continue using the original"); the infinite-range example `(0..).take(n)` at `:1402` (depends on `Range`'s `Iterator` impl, still deferred); the corpus prose's full "iterator adapter" / "consumer" terminology beyond the bare distinction installed today; the default body of `take` in core (readable once closures + unsafe patterns land); the other 71 provided methods (audit §5 steps 7-11 and beyond, plus closure / `IntoIterator` / Ord-bound / Try sub-arcs). The unlock per audit §5 is every later Iterator adapter whose return is another iterator: `skip` (audit §5 step 7), `enumerate` (step 8), `step_by` (step 10), `fuse` (step 9) — each reuses today's "result is itself an iterator" rule and laziness frame; once the closure arc lands, `map`, `filter`, `filter_map`, `inspect`, `take_while`, `skip_while` all reuse today's frame too.
depends_on: 135-iterator-nth (load-bearing — installs the `n: usize` second-parameter shape on a *provided* Iterator method, plus the receiver-shape contrast (consuming `self` vs borrowing `&mut self`). Today reuses the `n: usize` slot and returns to lesson 102's consuming-`self` receiver), 134-iterator-last (load-bearing — `.last()` is the consumer chained on `take(3).last()` in the working probe; output line 2 `Some(30)` is the empirical witness. The `Self::Item = &u64` substitution from 134 carries through), 133-iterator-count (load-bearing — `.count()` is the consumer chained on `take(2).count()` in the working probe; output line 1 `2` is the empirical witness. Probe 5's E0382 + `note:` template reuses lesson 133's verbatim with `take` substituted into the method-name slot), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait with 75 provided methods inherited via default bodies. `take` is one of them; the synopsis-box line at `:66-67` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker). Lesson 132's "What To Ignore" listed *"the formal 'lazy adapter' / 'consumer' definitions"* as deferred; today installs that frame for `take`), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — `Take<Self>` is itself an iterator; Probe 4 calls `.next()` on the `Take<Self>` value to show the binding is itself an iterator. The Trace iterator in Probe 2 reuses lesson 131's `&mut self`-on-`next` mechanic. Lazy-walk evidence depends on `next` being the underlying advance mechanism), 102-self-by-value-receiver (load-bearing — installs `self` (no `&`, no `mut`) as the consuming receiver shape; calling such a method consumes the value, and using the binding afterward fires E0382 with a `note:` block naming the receiver shape. Probe 5 today reuses the E0382 + `note:` template), 049-method-chaining (load-bearing — installs that the receiver in `value.method(args)` is *any expression*, including another method call. Today's `v.iter().take(2).count()` is the first chain in the run that puts a *consumer* after a *non-consumer* iterator method — `take` returns an iterator value that becomes the receiver of `.count()`), 080-integer-type-family (cited — installs the twelve integer types and the sign-and-width naming rule. `take`'s second parameter slot is `usize`), 123-vec-iter (cited — `v.iter()` returns the slice iterator; the receiver in today's working probe), 022-for-range (cited — `for x in iter` works on any Iterator; today reuses that on `for x in v.iter().take(2)`), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — the `{ ... }` body marker on the synopsis-box line at `trait.Iterator.md:66-67` is the default-body mechanic that licenses inheritance), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.take(n)`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — four-part diagnostic map applied to E0382 in the centered contrast and E0308 in the type-pin contrast), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::skip` — `(self, n) -> Skip<Self>`, lazy adapter (sibling of `take` with the inverse semantic)" moves (audit §5 step 7 — reuses today's `n: usize` slot, self-by-value rule, and lazy-adapter frame; the Skip wrapper is the structural sibling of Take), future "`Iterator::enumerate` — `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>`, yields `(usize, Item)`" moves (audit §5 step 8 — reuses today's lazy-adapter frame; first adapter today's audience meets that yields a *tuple* element), future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter" moves (audit §5 step 9 — reuses today's adapter frame), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`, panics on `step == 0`" moves (audit §5 step 10 — same `n: usize` second-parameter shape from today, but with a panic precondition), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — `&self` borrowing, contrast against today's consuming `self`), future "`Iterator::map` — `(self, f) -> Map<Self, F>`, lazy closure-adapter" moves (gated on closure arc, audit §4.4.1 — reuses today's lazy-adapter frame; the corpus's lazy passage at `:866-867,892` is *about* `map`), future "`Iterator::filter`, `filter_map`, `inspect`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while` — closure-driven adapters" moves (gated on closure arc — all reuse today's lazy-adapter frame), future "`Iterator::collect` — `(self) -> B where B: FromIterator<Item>`, the canonical consumer that drives an adapter chain" moves (gated on `FromIterator` + turbofish), future "`Iterator::by_ref` — `(&mut self) -> &mut Self`, partial-consumption pattern named in `take`'s std doc at `:1423-1437`" moves (gated on `&mut Self` reference shape), future "the `where Self: Sized` bound on provided methods" moves (still named-deferred — today's `take` carries the bound, contrasting against `nth` which omits it), future "the `Take<I>` struct's `Iterator` impl — `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<I::Item>`, decrement counter logic" moves (today refers to the struct opaquely; the impl details unlock a quizzed understanding of why `Take<I>::next` returns `None` after `n` calls), future "`(0..).take(n)` — the infinite-range adapter pattern named in `take`'s std doc at `:1401-1409`" moves (depends on `Range`'s `Iterator` impl, still deferred since 022), future "`Iterator::take`'s default body in core — `Take::new(self, n)` per the std source — readable once closures + unsafe patterns land" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Take.md
```

```text
id: 137-iterator-skip
move: drop the first `n` elements of a slice iterator with `iter.skip(n)`. The working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50]; let n = v.iter().skip(2).count(); println!("{}", n); let first_remaining = v.iter().skip(2).next(); println!("{:?}", first_remaining); for x in v.iter().skip(3) { println!("{}", x); } }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints four lines `3` / `Some(30)` / `40` / `50`. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1352` spells `fn skip(self, n: usize) -> Skip<Self> where Self: Sized,` — same three slots as `take` at `:1376`: bare `self` (lesson 102's consuming receiver shape), second parameter `n: usize` (lesson 080), return type `Skip<Self>` — a wrapper struct documented at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Skip.md:1-15` (`pub struct Skip<I> { /* private fields */ }`) which itself implements `Iterator`. Synopsis-box line at `:64-65` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker). Inverse-pair Probe 2 (the centered new contrast today): `let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50]; v.iter().take(2).count()` returns `2`; `v.iter().skip(2).count()` returns `3`; their sum is `5`, equal to `v.iter().count()` — empirical witness that `take(n)` and `skip(n)` partition the source iterator at position `n`. Empty-on-short Probe 3 (`v.iter().skip(100).count()` and `.next()` on the same five-element vec): silent compile, prints `count = 0` and `next  = None`, exit 0 — witnesses corpus claim at `trait.Iterator.md:1356-1359` ("if the original iterator is too short, then the returned iterator is empty"). Type-pin Probe 4 (`let _x: u32 = v.iter().skip(2);`): fires E0308 with inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`Skip<Iter<'_, u64>>\`` and secondary `note: expected type \`u32\`; found struct \`Skip<std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>>\`` — rustc spells the actual return type out, grounding "the result is itself an iterator" empirically. Appendix-only continuity Probe 5 (call `.skip(2)` twice on the same binding): fires `error[E0382]: use of moved value: \`iter\`` with `note:` block reading `\`skip\` takes ownership of the receiver \`self\`, which moves \`iter\`` (note format: shortened method name, not fully qualified — minor rustc-version cosmetic difference from lesson 136's `take` diagnostic which printed `\`std::iter::Iterator::take\` takes ownership`; semantic content identical) at `library/core/src/iter/traits/iterator.rs:1341:12` (different line than `take`'s 1414:12 because each provided method has its own definition site in core). Today's move is step 7 of the iterator-API-coverage audit's §5 first-arc plan: the inverse sibling of lesson 136's `take`. The lazy framing is *not* re-witnessed today (lesson 136's Trace + take Probe 2 installed it; today inherits by structural analogy). The `Skip<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body are referred to opaquely; the `Self: Sized` bound is named-deferred (still); the `skip_while` adapter at `:1284` is named-deferred (closure-driven, gated on the closure arc); the std-doc implementor advice "Rather than overriding this method directly, instead override the `nth` method" at `:1361` is named-deferred (implementor-facing, not user-facing).
main_concept: `Iterator::skip` is the structural inverse of lesson 136's `Iterator::take`. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1352` spells `fn skip(self, n: usize) -> Skip<Self> where Self: Sized,` — identical to `take`'s signature at `:1376` modulo the wrapper-struct name (`Skip<Self>` vs `Take<Self>`). All three structural slots carry from lesson 136 unchanged: (1) consuming `self` receiver (lesson 102); (2) `n: usize` second parameter (lesson 080); (3) wrapper struct that itself implements `Iterator`. The single new fact today is the **inverse semantic**: where `take(n)` keeps the first `n` elements and discards the rest, `skip(n)` discards the first `n` and yields the rest. Composed on the same source, the two adapters partition the iterator at position `n`: for any in-range `n`, `iter.take(n).count() + iter.skip(n).count() == iter.count()`. Probe 2 witnesses this empirically: `2 + 3 == 5`. The audience-level mental model that installs: `take` and `skip` are a complementary pair. They share an adapter shape; they differ only in which side of position `n` they keep. The lazy framing carries from 136 (building either wrapper does no work; pulling on it drives the inner iterator) — *not re-witnessed today* because the structural analogy is tight (both adapters are listed adjacent in the synopsis box at `:64-67`) and a fresh Trace probe would add appendix volume without much new fact value. Operational corollaries today: (a) **`skip(n)` past the end yields the empty iterator, no panic** — Probe 3 (`skip(100)` on five-element vec → `count = 0`, `next = None`); matches `trait.Iterator.md:1356-1359` ("if the original iterator is too short, then the returned iterator is empty"). Symmetric to lesson 136's `take(huge)` past-end behavior, but with the inverse outcome: where `take(huge)` truncates to the source's full length, `skip(huge)` truncates to zero. (b) **`Skip<Self>` is itself an iterator** — Probe 1 chains `.skip(2).count()`, `.skip(2).next()`, and `for x in v.iter().skip(3)`; Probe 4's E0308 names the type `Skip<Iter<'_, u64>>` from rustc's mouth. (c) **`skip` consumes its receiver** — Probe 5 (appendix-only continuity) reuses lesson 136's E0382 + `note:` template with `skip` in the method-name slot; the diagnostic literally proves the trait declaration's `fn skip(self, ...)` shape. Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `where Self: Sized` bound (still); the `Skip<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body; the `skip_while` adapter at `:1284` (closure-driven, gated on the closure arc); the implementor advice at `:1361` ("Rather than overriding this method directly, instead override the `nth` method"); the `Skip<I>: DoubleEndedIterator + ExactSizeIterator` impl bound at `struct.Skip.md:47-49`; the default body of `skip` in core (readable once `nth`-delegation patterns become explicit); the other 70 provided methods. Unlock per audit §5: the structural-pair frame opens the way for `enumerate` (step 8), `fuse` (step 9), `step_by` (step 10), and (once the closure arc lands) the closure-driven sibling pair `take_while` / `skip_while` whose semantics reuse today's "take and skip are inverse" frame.
depends_on: 136-iterator-take (load-bearing — installs the adapter shape `(self, n: usize) -> WrapperStruct<Self>`, the lazy framing for adapters, and the chain-with-consumer pattern. Today is the inverse sibling: every structural slot (consuming `self`, `n: usize`, wrapper-struct return) is reused, the lazy framing is inherited not re-witnessed, and the only new fact is the inverse semantic. Probe 2's inverse-sum identity (`take(n).count() + skip(n).count() == count()`) directly composes 136's installed `take(2).count() == 2` with today's `skip(2).count() == 3`), 134-iterator-last (load-bearing — installs `last`'s `(self) -> Option<Self::Item>` and the `Self::Item = &u64` substitution for slice iterators, which carries into Probe 1 line 2's `Some(30)` (Debug format hides `&` for primitive targets)), 133-iterator-count (load-bearing — `.count()` is the consumer chained on `.skip(2).count()` in Probe 1 (output line 1 `3`) and on the inverse-sum probe in Probe 2. Probe 5's E0382 + `note:` template reuses lesson 133's verbatim with `skip` substituted into the method-name slot), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait with 75 provided methods inherited via default bodies. `skip` is one of them; the synopsis-box line at `:64-65` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker)), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — `.next()` on a slice iterator returning `Option<&T>`. Probe 1 calls `.next()` on a `Skip<Self>` value to read `Some(30)` — the binding is itself an iterator), 102-self-by-value-receiver (load-bearing — installs `self` (no `&`, no `mut`) as the consuming receiver shape. Probe 5 reuses the E0382 + `note:` template with `skip` in the method-name slot), 049-method-chaining (load-bearing — installs that the receiver in `value.method(args)` is any expression. Today's `v.iter().skip(2).count()` parses as `((v.iter()).skip(2)).count()` — same chain shape lesson 136 installed for `take`), 080-integer-type-family (cited — installs `usize` as one row of the integer family. `skip`'s second parameter slot is `usize`), 123-vec-iter (cited — `v.iter()` returns the slice iterator; the receiver in today's working probe), 022-for-range (cited — `for x in iter` works on any Iterator; today reuses on `for x in v.iter().skip(3)`), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — the `{ ... }` body marker on the synopsis-box line at `trait.Iterator.md:64-65` is the default-body mechanic that licenses inheritance), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.skip(n)`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic map applied to E0308 in Probe 4 and E0382 in Probe 5), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::enumerate` — `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>`, yields `(usize, Item)`" moves (audit §5 step 8 — reuses today's adapter shape; first adapter today's audience meets that yields a *tuple* element), future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter" moves (audit §5 step 9 — reuses today's adapter frame; first adapter that does not change the yielded count or contents but only the post-`None` behavior), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`, panics on `step == 0`" moves (audit §5 step 10 — same `n: usize`-shaped second parameter but with a panic precondition), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — `&self` borrowing, contrast against today's consuming `self`), future "`Iterator::take_while` and `Iterator::skip_while` — closure-driven sibling pair with predicate-based stopping" moves (gated on closure arc, audit §4.4.1 — directly reuses today's "take and skip are inverse" frame, but with a `FnMut(&Item) -> bool` predicate replacing the `n: usize` argument), future "`Iterator::map` — `(self, f) -> Map<Self, F>`, lazy closure-adapter" moves (gated on closure arc — reuses today's adapter shape with closure replacing the index argument), future "`Iterator::filter`, `filter_map`, `inspect`, `map_while` — closure-driven adapters" moves (gated on closure arc — all reuse today's adapter shape), future "`Iterator::collect` — `(self) -> B where B: FromIterator<Item>`, the canonical consumer that drives an adapter chain" moves (gated on `FromIterator` + turbofish), future "the `Skip<I>` struct's `Iterator` impl — `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<I::Item>`, decrement-then-delegate logic" moves (today refers to the struct opaquely; the impl details unlock a quizzed understanding of why `Skip<I>::next` returns the inner iterator's elements only after `n` of them are consumed), future "the `nth`-delegation pattern named in `skip`'s std doc note at `:1361`" moves (implementor-facing, gated on a deeper-than-quiz lesson on default-body delegation chains in `core`), future "the `where Self: Sized` bound on provided methods" moves (still named-deferred — today's `skip` carries the bound, same as `take`)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Skip.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0382.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/iterator-api-coverage.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/137-iterator-skip.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/137-iterator-skip.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/137-iterator-skip.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 138-iterator-enumerate
move: pair each element of a slice iterator with its index using `iter.enumerate()`. The working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; for (i, x) in v.iter().enumerate() { println!("{} {}", i, x); } let n = v.iter().enumerate().count(); println!("count = {}", n); let last_pair = v.iter().enumerate().last(); println!("{:?}", last_pair); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints five lines `0 10` / `1 20` / `2 30` / `count = 3` / `Some((2, 30))`. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1041` spells `fn enumerate(self) -> Enumerate<Self> where Self: Sized,` — bare `self` (lesson 102's consuming receiver shape, same as `take`/`skip` at `:1376`/`:1352`), *no second parameter* (the leanest adapter signature seen so far — `take`/`skip` carried a `n: usize` argument; enumerate has only the receiver), return type `Enumerate<Self>` — a wrapper struct documented at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Enumerate.md:7` (`pub struct Enumerate<I> { /* private fields */ }`) which itself implements `Iterator` (per `:162`'s `impl<I> Iterator for Enumerate<I> where I: Iterator,`). Synopsis-box line at `:51-52` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker). The yielded element type per `struct.Enumerate.md:180` is `type Item = (usize, <I as Iterator>::Item)` — a 2-tuple of (iteration index, original element). For the slice iterator over `Vec<u64>` (lessons 123, 131), `Self::Item = &u64`, so the wrapper yields `(usize, &u64)`. Centered new contrast Probe 2 (`let _x: Option<u64> = v.iter().enumerate().last();`): fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected \`Option<u64>\`, found \`Option<(usize, &u64)>\`` and secondary `note: expected enum \`Option<u64>\`; found enum \`Option<(usize, &u64)>\`` — rustc spells the tuple-yield from its own mouth, anchoring "the yielded element changes shape from `Self::Item` to `(usize, Self::Item)`" empirically. Direct contrast against lesson 134's `Option<&u64>` for the same `.last()` chained on the bare iter: today's wrapper wraps a tuple instead of the bare element. Type-pin Probe 3 (`let _x: u32 = v.iter().enumerate();`): fires E0308 with inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`Enumerate<Iter<'_, u64>>\`` and secondary `note: expected type \`u32\`; found struct \`Enumerate<std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>>\`` — same forced-error type-pin technique lessons 134/135/136/137 used; today substitutes `Enumerate` for `Take`/`Skip`. Empty-iter Probe 4 (`vec![]` source): silent compile, prints `count = 0` and `next  = None`, exit 0 — corroborates clean null-case handling (no panic). The for-loop binding `for (i, x) in v.iter().enumerate()` composes lesson 126's tuple pattern in the for-binding slot directly: each yielded `(usize, &u64)` pair is destructured into `i` (the iteration index, `usize`) and `x` (the original element, `&u64`). Debug format on `Some((2, &30))` prints `Some((2, 30))` because Rust's `Debug` impl on `&T` delegates to `T`'s Debug for primitive `T` — same convention lessons 131/134/137 captured for `Option<&u64>`. Today's move is step 8 of the iterator-API-coverage audit's §5 first-arc plan: first tuple-yielding Iterator adapter. The lazy framing is *not* re-witnessed today (lesson 136's Trace + take Probe 2 installed it; today inherits by structural analogy as lesson 137's `skip` did). The consuming-`self` rule is *not* re-witnessed today via E0382 (lessons 102/133/134/136/137 captured the same template five times; sixth substitution would be appendix bloat). The `Enumerate<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body are referred to opaquely; the `Self: Sized` bound is named-deferred (still); the std-doc note "`zip` provides similar functionality" at `:1051-1052` is named-deferred (depends on `Range`'s `Iterator` impl, still deferred since 022); the `next_index` nightly method at `struct.Enumerate.md:23-25` is named-deferred (out of scope per audit §3); the `usize::MAX` overflow / panic semantics at `:1054-1063` are named as a corpus fact and not probed (impractical to construct an iterator with > 2^64 elements).
main_concept: `Iterator::enumerate` is the next adapter sibling of `Iterator::take` (lesson 136) and `Iterator::skip` (lesson 137). The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1041` spells `fn enumerate(self) -> Enumerate<Self> where Self: Sized,` — same lazy-adapter family `(self) -> Wrapper<Self>` shape, but with two new structural facts. The first fact: **no second parameter**. Where `take(n)` and `skip(n)` each carried a `n: usize` argument, today's signature is the leaner `(self) -> Enumerate<Self>` — only the receiver. This is the leanest adapter signature seen so far. The second fact: **the yielded element type changes shape**. Per `struct.Enumerate.md:180`, `type Item = (usize, <I as Iterator>::Item)`. Where `Take<I>` and `Skip<I>` inherit `type Item = I::Item` (the inner element unchanged), `Enumerate<I>` rewrites the associated type to a 2-tuple of (iteration index, original element). For the slice iterator over `Vec<u64>`, `Self::Item = &u64`, so the yielded element is `(usize, &u64)`. This is the FIRST place in the run where an iterator adapter changes the yielded element's *shape* (not just its filtering or count). Three structural slots inherited from 136/137 unchanged: (1) consuming `self` receiver (lesson 102) — same E0382 + `note:` template the receiver-shape rule fires for `take`/`skip` if reused; not re-witnessed today since five prior captures (102/133/134/136/137) suffice; (2) `Self: Sized` bound (named-deferred, same as `take`/`skip`); (3) wrapper-struct return whose impl makes it itself an iterator (per `struct.Enumerate.md:162`'s `impl<I> Iterator for Enumerate<I> where I: Iterator,`). The audience-level mental model that installs: an adapter can leave out the second parameter *and* rewrite the yielded element's shape. The canonical loop form is `for (i, x) in v.iter().enumerate()` — lesson 126's tuple pattern in the for-binding slot destructures each yielded pair at the binding step into `i` (the index, `usize`) and `x` (the original element). The audience crosses from "adapters carry a `n: usize` argument and yield the inner element unchanged" to "adapters can have no extra argument and *rewrite* the yielded element type. enumerate's rewrite is to wrap the original in `(usize, _)` — first slot is the iteration index, second is the original. The rewrite is rustc-visible at type-pin time: `Option<(usize, &u64)>` not `Option<&u64>`." Operational corollaries today: (a) **The first slot is the iteration index, starting at 0 and incrementing on each yield** (`trait.Iterator.md:1043-1048` "yields pairs `(i, val)`, where `i` is the current index of iteration"). Probe 1 lines 1-3 witness `0, 1, 2`. (b) **The index counter is a `usize`** (`:1050` "keeps its count as a `usize`"). Probe 2's E0308 names the type as `(usize, &u64)` from rustc's mouth. (c) **`Enumerate<Self>` is itself an iterator** — Probe 1 chains `.count()` and `.last()` on the wrapper, plus `for x in`. Probe 3's E0308 names the wrapper-struct type. (d) **Lazy** (inherited from 136/137 by structural analogy; not re-probed). (e) **Empty-source → empty enumerate, no panic** (Probe 4). (f) **Index overflow on > `usize::MAX` elements: wrong result or panic** — corpus fact at `:1054-1063`, not probed (impractical). Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `where Self: Sized` bound (still); the `Enumerate<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body (the increment-counter logic); the std-doc note "`zip` provides similar functionality" at `:1051-1052` (depends on `Range`'s `Iterator` impl, still deferred since 022); the `next_index` nightly method on `Enumerate<I>` at `struct.Enumerate.md:23-25`; the `Enumerate<I>: DoubleEndedIterator` impl bound at `:100`; the default body of `enumerate` in core; the other 70 provided methods (audit §5 steps 9-11 and beyond, plus closure / `IntoIterator` / Ord-bound / Try sub-arcs). Unlock per audit §5: future `fuse` (step 9), `step_by` (step 10), `size_hint` (step 11) — each reuses today's lazy-adapter frame. Once the closure arc lands, `map` reuses today's "yielded element type can be rewritten by the adapter" frame directly (closure-driven version of today's structural rewrite); `filter_map`, `scan` extend further.
depends_on: 137-iterator-skip (load-bearing — installs the lazy-adapter shape `(self, n: usize) -> WrapperStruct<Self>` and the chain-with-consumer pattern. Today is the next sibling: same lazy-adapter family, but with no second parameter and a yielded element that changes shape), 136-iterator-take (load-bearing — installs the lazy framing for adapters and the "wrapper is itself an iterator" claim. Today inherits both unchanged), 134-iterator-last (load-bearing — `.last()` is chained on `v.iter().enumerate().last()` in Probe 1 (output line 5 `Some((2, 30))`) and on the centered Probe 2 (E0308 with `Option<u64>` vs `Option<(usize, &u64)>`). Lesson 134 installed `last`'s `(self) -> Option<Self::Item>`; today `Self::Item = (usize, &u64)`, the substitution flowing through Enumerate's rewritten associated type), 133-iterator-count (load-bearing — `.count()` is chained on `v.iter().enumerate().count()` in Probe 1 (output line 4 `count = 3`); witnesses that adding the index slot does not change the element count), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait with 75 provided methods inherited via default bodies. `enumerate` is one of them; the synopsis-box line at `:51-52` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker)), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — `.next()` on a slice iterator returning `Option<&T>`. Today's Probe 4 calls `.next()` on the `Enumerate<Self>` wrapper to read `None` on an empty source; Probe 1's Debug-format `Some((2, 30))` reuses 131's convention that Debug hides the `&` glyph on `&u64` for primitive targets), 126-for-pattern-destructuring (load-bearing — installs `for (a, b) in iter` — tuple pattern in the for-binding slot — on lesson 125's `v.iter().zip(w.iter())` yields. Today reuses the *exact same shape* on a different tuple-yielding source: `for (i, x) in v.iter().enumerate()`. The yielded tuple is `(usize, &u64)` instead of `(&u64, &u64)`, but the binding mechanic is identical. This is the first non-`zip` source of tuple-yielding iterators in the run), 102-self-by-value-receiver (load-bearing — installs `self` (no `&`, no `mut`) as the consuming receiver shape. Today's `enumerate` carries the same shape per `:1041`. *Not re-witnessed* today via E0382 — five prior captures (102/133/134/136/137) of the same template suffice; sixth substitution would be appendix bloat), 072-tuple-type-and-index (load-bearing — installs the tuple type `(A, B)` and the `.0`/`.1` index mechanic. Today's yielded type `(usize, &u64)` is one specific instance. The Check Yourself part (b) explicitly invokes 072's index mechanic as the equivalent non-destructuring form for the yielded pair), 049-method-chaining (load-bearing — installs left-associative method-chain parsing. Today's `v.iter().enumerate().count()` and `v.iter().enumerate().last()` parse as `((v.iter()).enumerate()).count/last()` — same chain shape lessons 136/137 installed for `take`/`skip`), 073-let-tuple-destructure (cited — installs `let (a, b) = pair;` destructure mechanic. `for (i, x) in iter` is its loop variant via lesson 126), 080-integer-type-family (cited — installs `usize` as one row of the integer family. Enumerate's index slot is `usize` per `:1050`), 123-vec-iter (cited — `v.iter()` returns the slice iterator; the receiver in today's working probe), 022-for-range (cited — `for x in iter` works on any Iterator; today reuses on `for (i, x) in v.iter().enumerate()` with a tuple pattern in the binding slot), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — the `{ ... }` body marker on the synopsis-box line at `:51-52` is the default-body mechanic that licenses inheritance), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.enumerate()`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with positional placeholder), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic map applied to E0308 in Probes 2 and 3), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::fuse` — sticky-`None` rule and the `Fuse<I>` adapter" moves (audit §5 step 9 — reuses today's adapter shape with no second parameter; first adapter that does not change the yielded count or contents but only the post-`None` behavior), future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`, panics on `step == 0`" moves (audit §5 step 10 — same `n: usize`-shaped second parameter from 136/137 returns, but with a panic precondition), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — `&self` borrowing, contrast against today's consuming `self`; returns a 2-tuple of `(usize, Option<usize>)`, which composes today's tuple-yielding frame with lesson 119's `Option<T>` wrapper), future "`Iterator::map` — `(self, f) -> Map<Self, F>`, lazy closure-adapter that rewrites the yielded element type" moves (gated on closure arc, audit §4.4.1 — directly reuses today's "the yielded element type can be rewritten by the adapter" frame; `map` is the closure-driven generalization, today is the structural prototype with `(usize, _)` rewrite), future "`Iterator::filter_map`, `scan`, `flat_map` — closure-driven adapters that rewrite the yielded element type" moves (gated on closure arc — all reuse today's element-type-rewrite frame), future "`Iterator::zip` at trait depth — `(self, other: IntoIter) -> Zip<Self, U>`, yields `(Self::Item, U::Item)`" moves (gated on `IntoIterator` arc, audit §4.4.2 — generalizes lesson 125's slice-iter-only `zip`; the std doc at `:1051-1052` notes `zip(0..)` provides similar functionality to `enumerate`, today defers that linkage), future "the `Enumerate<I>` struct's `Iterator` impl — `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<(usize, I::Item)>`, increment-counter logic" moves (today refers to the struct opaquely; the impl details unlock a quizzed understanding of why the index always starts at 0 and increments by 1 each call), future "`Enumerate<I>::next_index` nightly method" moves (named in `struct.Enumerate.md:23-25`; nightly-only per audit §3, deferred), future "the `usize::MAX` overflow / panic semantics on enumerate" moves (corpus fact at `:1054-1063`, not probed today; gates a future `usize` overflow behavior lesson), future "the `where Self: Sized` bound on provided methods" moves (still named-deferred — today's `enumerate` carries the bound, same as `take`/`skip`)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
```
