```text
id: 139-iterator-fuse
move: wrap any iterator with `iter.fuse()` to make its `None` sticky — once the inner iterator returns `None`, every subsequent call to `.next()` on the wrapped value returns `None` regardless of what the inner would have done. Working probe `struct Stutter { n: u32 } impl Iterator for Stutter { type Item = u32; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> { if self.n % 2 == 0 { let v = self.n / 2; self.n += 1; Some(v) } else { self.n += 1; None } } } fn main() { let mut a = Stutter { n: 0 }; for _ in 0..6 { println!("{:?}", a.next()); } println!("---"); let mut b = Stutter { n: 0 }.fuse(); for _ in 0..6 { println!("{:?}", b.next()); } }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints thirteen lines: `Some(0)` / `None` / `Some(1)` / `None` / `Some(2)` / `None` / `---` / `Some(0)` / `None` / `None` / `None` / `None` / `None`. The contrast is internal to a single source file: same six `next()` calls on `Stutter` (which alternates `Some, None, Some, None, ...`) and on `Stutter.fuse()` — without `.fuse()`, six calls produce three `Some(_)` and three `None`; with `.fuse()`, the second call's `None` latches and calls 3-6 all return `None` without ever calling `Stutter.next()` again. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1737` spells `fn fuse(self) -> Fuse<Self> where Self: Sized,` — bare `self` (lesson 102's consuming receiver shape, same as 136-138), *no second parameter* (same as `enumerate` 138), return type `Fuse<Self>` — a wrapper struct documented at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Fuse.md:7` (`pub struct Fuse<I> { /* private fields */ }`) which itself implements `Iterator`. Synopsis-box line at `:81-82` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker). Type-pin Probe 3 (`let _x: u32 = v.iter().fuse();`): fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`Fuse<Iter<'_, u64>>\`` and secondary `note: expected type \`u32\`; found struct \`Fuse<std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>>\`` — same forced-error type-pin technique 134/135/136/137/138 captured. Probe 2 (slice-iter corroboration, `v.iter()` vs `v.iter().fuse()` over `vec![10, 20, 30]` for five calls each): both halves print `Some(10)` / `Some(20)` / `Some(30)` / `None` / `None` — empirical witness that `.fuse()` is a no-op on iterators that already implement `FusedIterator` (per `trait.FusedIterator.md:18-21` "the additional `Fuse` wrapper will be a no-op with no performance penalty"). Three corpus restatements of the centered semantic: `trait.Iterator.md:1739-1743` ("Creates an iterator which ends after the first `None` ... `fuse()` adapts an iterator, ensuring that after a `None` is given, it will always return `None` forever"); `struct.Fuse.md:12-13` ("An iterator that yields `None` forever after the underlying iterator yields `None` once"); `trait.FusedIterator.md:12-15` ("An iterator that always continues to yield `None` when exhausted. Calling next on a fused iterator that has returned `None` once is guaranteed to return `None` again"). The load-bearing corpus claim at `trait.Iterator.md:281-284`: "Individual iterator implementations may choose to resume iteration, and so calling `next()` again may or may not eventually start returning `Some(Item)` again at some point" — the bare `Iterator::next` contract does *not* require sticky-`None`; lesson 131 implicitly conflated "slice iterators stick" with "all iterators stick", today untangles them. Three-way distinction installed: (1) `FusedIterator` (`trait.FusedIterator.md`) is a *marker trait* an iterator can implement to declare it already sticks; (2) `Fuse<I>` (`struct.Fuse.md`) is the *adapter struct* that forces stickiness on whatever inner iterator it wraps; (3) `Iterator::fuse` (the method) builds a `Fuse<I>` from any iterator. The std-doc Examples block at `:1751-1787` features an `Alternate` iterator structurally identical to today's `Stutter` modulo `i32`/`u32` and `.then_some(...)` vs explicit `if/else`. The consuming-`self` rule is *not* re-witnessed today via E0382 (lessons 102/133/134/136/137/138 captured the same template six times; seventh substitution would be appendix bloat). The lazy framing is *not* re-witnessed today — today centers on post-`None` semantics, not laziness; lesson 136's Trace + take Probe 2 installed the family-level claim. The `Fuse<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body (latch-flag bookkeeping) are referred to opaquely; the `Self: Sized` bound is named-deferred (still); the implementor caveat at `:1745-1747` ("`fuse()` may therefore behave incorrectly if the `FusedIterator` trait is improperly implemented") is named-deferred (implementor advice, not user-facing); the `FusedIterator` implementor list at `trait.FusedIterator.md:23+` is named-deferred (long); the std-doc advice at `:18-21` ("you should not use `FusedIterator` in generic bounds if you need a fused iterator") is named-deferred (gates a future generic-bound lesson).
main_concept: `Iterator::fuse` is the lazy adapter that *enforces* sticky-`None` behavior on its inner iterator. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:1737` spells `fn fuse(self) -> Fuse<Self> where Self: Sized,` — same `(self) -> Wrapper<Self>` shape as `enumerate` (lesson 138), no second parameter. The new fact today is *behavioral*, not structural: where 136-138 installed adapters that change *what the wrapper yields* (count, count, element shape), `fuse` does not change the element count or type. It enforces a *post-`None` behavioral guarantee* that the bare `Iterator::next` contract at `:281-284` explicitly does not require — "Individual iterator implementations may choose to resume iteration, and so calling `next()` again may or may not eventually start returning `Some(Item)` again." Three-way distinction installed: (1) `FusedIterator` (a *marker trait*, `trait.FusedIterator.md:7` `pub trait FusedIterator: Iterator { }`) — declares "I already stick at `None`"; slice iterators implement it. (2) `Fuse<I>` (an *adapter struct*, `struct.Fuse.md:7` `pub struct Fuse<I> { /* private fields */ }`) — forces stickiness on whatever inner iterator it wraps. (3) `Iterator::fuse` (the *method*) — builds a `Fuse<I>` from any iterator. The audience-level mental model that installs: an adapter's purpose can be *purely behavioral* — same element type, same element count if the inner sticks, but a guarantee about post-`None` behavior. The audience crosses from "adapters change what the wrapper yields" to "adapters can also change *what happens after* the inner yields its first `None`." Operational corollaries today: (a) **Sticky-`None` enforcement requires a non-fused inner iterator to be witnessed.** The working probe builds `Stutter`, an iterator that alternates `Some, None, Some, None, ...` (uses lesson 132's user-defined-Iterator pattern + lesson 037's `%` + lesson 023's `+=`). Probe 1 left half: six calls produce three `Some(_)`/`None` interleavings. Probe 1 right half: with `.fuse()`, calls 3-6 all return `None`. (b) **`.fuse()` is a no-op on iterators that already implement `FusedIterator`.** Probe 2: `v.iter()` and `v.iter().fuse()` produce identical five-call sequences on `vec![10, 20, 30]`. (c) **`Fuse<Self>` is itself an iterator** — `for _ in 0..6 { ... b.next() }` works on the binding. Probe 3's E0308 names the wrapper-struct type `Fuse<Iter<'_, u64>>`. Three structural slots inherited from 136-138 unchanged: (i) consuming `self` receiver (lesson 102) — not re-witnessed today via E0382 since six prior captures (102/133/134/136/137/138) suffice; (ii) `Self: Sized` bound (named-deferred); (iii) wrapper-struct return whose impl makes it itself an iterator (per `struct.Fuse.md` Iterator impl). Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `Fuse<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body (the latch-flag bookkeeping); the `where Self: Sized` bound (still); the implementor caveat at `:1745-1747` ("`fuse()` may therefore behave incorrectly if the `FusedIterator` trait is improperly implemented") — implementor advice, not user-facing; the `FusedIterator` implementor list at `trait.FusedIterator.md:23+` (long); the std-doc advice "you should not use `FusedIterator` in generic bounds if you need a fused iterator" at `:18-21` (gates a future generic-bound lesson); the supertrait colon-form `pub trait FusedIterator: Iterator { }` (named-deferred since 132); the auto-`FusedIterator` impl on `Fuse<I>` itself; the lazy-adapter framing (carries by family analogy from 136-138; not re-probed since today centers on post-`None` semantics, not laziness). Unlock per audit §5: future `step_by` (step 10), `size_hint` (step 11) — each reuses today's adapter shape. Once the closure arc lands, `take_while` and `map_while` (audit §4.4.1) reuse today's "post-`None` behavior is itself a guarantee an adapter can install" frame: both short-circuit on a closure-driven condition and benefit from the same fused-or-not analysis. `FusedIterator` itself — as a standalone trait declaration to read structurally — is a future move (gates `Self: FusedIterator` generic-bound forms).
depends_on: 138-iterator-enumerate (load-bearing — installs the `(self) -> Wrapper<Self>` adapter shape with no second parameter. Today's signature `fn fuse(self) -> Fuse<Self> where Self: Sized,` is structurally the same modulo wrapper-type substitution. The new fact relative to 138 is the *behavioral* one: where `Enumerate<I>` rewrites the yielded element type to `(usize, I::Item)`, `Fuse<I>` does not change element type or count — it enforces a post-`None` guarantee), 137-iterator-skip (load-bearing — installs the lazy-adapter family, consuming `self`, wrapper that itself implements `Iterator`. Today inherits all three slots), 136-iterator-take (load-bearing — installs the lazy framing for adapters and the "wrapper is itself an iterator" claim. Today refers to the family-level laziness inheritance without re-probing), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs `pub trait Iterator { type Item; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>; /* + 75 provided */ }` and the user-defined-Iterator pattern. `Stutter` reuses 132's `Counter`-style template verbatim modulo the `next` body, and the 75 provided methods including `fuse` are inherited automatically by 116's default-body mechanic), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `.next()` on a slice iterator returning `Option<&T>` and the slice-iterator sticky-`None` claim. Today untangles 131's implicit conflation: that stickiness was *not* the bare `Iterator` contract — it came from the slice iterator implementing `FusedIterator`. 131 explicitly named "iterators that resume after `None` (`Fuse<I>`)" as deferred; today picks up that thread. Probe 1 left half is the first probe in the run that witnesses an iterator *resuming* after `None`), 102-self-by-value-receiver (cited — bare-`self` consuming receiver shape; not re-witnessed via E0382 today since well-installed by lessons 102/133/134/136/137/138), 119-option-some-none (cited — `Option<T>` / `Some` / `None`. Today's `T = u32` in Probe 1 and `T = &u64` in Probes 2-3), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` is one row of the integer family. Today's `Stutter::Item = u32`), 037-remainder-operator (cited — `%` for `self.n % 2 == 0` inside Stutter::next), 023-compound-add-assign (cited — `+=` for `self.n += 1` inside Stutter::next), 095-struct-with-named-fields (cited — `struct Stutter { n: u32 }`), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — the `{ ... }` body marker on the synopsis-box line at `:81-82` is the default-body mechanic that licenses `fuse`'s inheritance), 049-method-chaining (cited — left-associative method-chain parsing; `Stutter { n: 0 }.fuse()` parses as `(Stutter { n: 0 }).fuse()`), 022-for-range (cited — `for _ in 0..6` works on `Range<u32>`'s `Iterator` impl; reused six times in Probe 1 to drive sequenced `next()` calls), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.fuse()`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with `{:?}` Debug placeholder), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic map applied to E0308 in Probe 3), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::step_by` — `(self, step) -> StepBy<Self>`, panics on `step == 0`" moves (audit §5 step 10 — same `n: usize`-shaped second parameter from 136/137 returns, but with a panic precondition; reuses today's lazy-adapter frame), future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — `&self` borrowing, contrast against today's consuming `self`; returns a 2-tuple of `(usize, Option<usize>)`, composing 138's tuple-yielding frame with 119's `Option<T>` wrapper), future "`Iterator::take_while`, `map_while` — short-circuiting closure-driven adapters that stop pulling on the inner once a condition fails" moves (gated on closure arc, audit §4.4.1 — directly reuse today's "post-`None` behavior is itself a guarantee an adapter can install" frame; both adapters short-circuit and benefit from the same fused-or-not analysis), future "`FusedIterator` as a standalone trait declaration to read structurally" moves (untangled today as the marker-trait counterpart to `Iterator::fuse`; future move would install `pub trait FusedIterator: Iterator { }` as a structural fact — supertrait colon-form named-deferred since 132 — and gate `Self: FusedIterator` generic-bound forms), future "the `Fuse<I>` struct's `Iterator` impl — `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<I::Item>`, latch-flag bookkeeping" moves (today refers to the struct opaquely; the impl details unlock a quizzed understanding of why `fuse()` after the first `None` never calls the inner `next` again), future "the auto-`FusedIterator` impl on `Fuse<I>` itself" moves (recursion edge case — `iter.fuse().fuse()` is itself a no-op on the second wrap; named-deferred today)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Fuse.md
```

```text
id: 140-iterator-step-by
move: yield every `step`th element of an iterator with `iter.step_by(step)`. Working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50]; for x in v.iter().step_by(2) { println!("{}", x); } println!("---"); let n = v.iter().step_by(2).count(); println!("{}", n); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints five lines: `10` / `30` / `50` / `---` / `3`. The for-loop body runs three times (indices 0, 2, 4 of the vec); the chained `.count()` on a fresh `v.iter().step_by(2)` independently reports `3`. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:551` spells `fn step_by(self, step: usize) -> StepBy<Self> where Self: Sized,` — bare `self` (lesson 102's consuming receiver, same as 136-139), `step: usize` second parameter (same `n: usize` slot 135's `nth` / 136's `take` / 137's `skip` installed), return type `StepBy<Self>` — a wrapper struct documented at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.StepBy.md:7` (`pub struct StepBy<I> { /* private fields */ }`) which itself implements `Iterator`. Synopsis-box line at `:25-26` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker). Type-pin Probe 5 (`let _x: u32 = v.iter().step_by(2);`) fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`StepBy<Iter<'_, u64>>\`` and secondary `note: expected type \`u32\`; found struct \`StepBy<std::slice::Iter<'_, u64>>\`` — same forced-error type-pin technique 134/135/136/137/138/139 captured. Probe 2 (the centered new-fact probe — three sub-probes for `step_by(0)`): (a) `let _ = v.iter().step_by(0);` (no `.next()` call) — panics with `thread 'main' (...) panicked at /rustc/<hash>/library/core/src/iter/adapters/step_by.rs:35:9: assertion failed: step != 0` plus `note: run with RUST_BACKTRACE=1 environment variable to display a backtrace`, exit 101, stdout empty (`println!` after the `step_by(0)` line never runs); (b) `let mut it = ...; it.next();` — identical panic, identical line `step_by.rs:35:9`, exit 101 (the `.next()` call never runs); (c) bare-statement form `v.iter().step_by(0);` — same panic, plus rustc emits a `unused_must_use` warning at compile time ("iterators are lazy and do nothing unless consumed"). Conclusion: the panic fires at construction inside the `step_by` body itself, not at first `.next()`; the `assert!(step != 0)` lives in the `step_by` constructor — visible from the panic location pointing to `library/core/src/iter/adapters/step_by.rs:35:9`. The construction check is unconditional and binding-form-independent. This is the run's first iterator adapter with a documented runtime panic precondition. Panic shape exactly reproduces lesson 053's template (`thread 'main' ... panicked at <file>:<line>: <message>`, stderr, exit 101) — only difference: today's message has no human-written prefix because the std implementation uses a bare `assert!` with no message arg. Probe 3 (`step_by(1)` identity, `vec![10, 20, 30]`) prints `10`, `20`, `30` — every element yielded, corroborating that "stepping by the given amount" reduces to identity at `step = 1`. Probe 4 (`step_by(100)` past length, `vec![10, 20, 30]`) prints `10` then `count=1` — only the first element yielded; per `trait.Iterator.md:556-557` "The first element of the iterator will always be returned, regardless of the step given." No panic for large step; the `step == 0` case is the *only* panic precondition. Three corpus restatements of the centered semantic: `trait.Iterator.md:553-557` ("Creates an iterator starting at the same point, but stepping by the given amount at each iteration. Note 1: The first element of the iterator will always be returned, regardless of the step given."); `trait.Iterator.md:582-584` ("##### Panics — The method will panic if the given step is `0`."); `struct.StepBy.md:12-13` ("An iterator for stepping iterators by a custom amount."). The std-doc Examples block at `:589-595` uses `[0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5].into_iter().step_by(2)` and asserts `Some(0), Some(2), Some(4), None`; today's Probe 1 substitutes `Vec::iter()` (123) on a `Vec<u64>` literal — keeps the lesson within already-installed prereqs and produces `&u64` references rather than `u64` values, but the structural witness is identical. The consuming-`self` rule is *not* re-witnessed today via E0382 (lessons 102/133/134/136/137/138/139 captured the same template seven times). The lazy framing carries from 136 unchanged for *iteration*, but is broken by `step_by` for *argument validation* — that asymmetry is the new mental-model fact. Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `StepBy<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body (the doc at `:559-565` describes two equivalent pull strategies — `self.next(); self.nth(step-1); …` versus `advance_n_and_return_first(...)` — both compatible, choice is unobservable to the caller); the `Self: Sized` bound (still); the `unused_must_use` lint mechanic itself; the std-library source line `step_by.rs:35` named in the panic (internal path, not user-facing); the `into_iter()` form used by the std example (named-deferred since 022); the `assert!` macro itself as a writable construct (today treats `assert!` as observed mechanism, not user-side syntax — same as lesson 053 treated `panic!`).
main_concept: `Iterator::step_by` is the lazy adapter that yields every `step`th element of its inner iterator starting at index 0. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:551` spells `fn step_by(self, step: usize) -> StepBy<Self> where Self: Sized,` — same `(self, n: usize) -> Wrapper<Self>` shape as `take` (136) / `skip` (137). The new fact today is *preconditional*: `step_by` is the first iterator adapter in the run with a *documented runtime panic precondition*. Per the doc at `:582-584`, "The method will panic if the given step is `0`." The empirical refinement (Probe 2): the panic fires *at construction inside the `step_by` body itself*, not lazily at first `.next()` — `let _ = v.iter().step_by(0);` (with no subsequent iteration) panics identically to `let mut it = ...; it.next();` and to the bare-statement form. The std implementation runs an unconditional `assert!(step != 0)` (or equivalent) in the `step_by` body; the panic location `library/core/src/iter/adapters/step_by.rs:35:9` is the constructor body, not `StepBy::next`. Audience-level mental-model installed: **adapter laziness applies to *iteration*, not to argument validation.** Adapters in 136-139 succeeded silently regardless of argument value (`take(0)` → empty iterator, `skip(N)` past length → empty, `enumerate` → no constraints, `fuse` → no constraints). `step_by` is the first to break that pattern: the precondition check is *eager*, even though the wrapper itself is lazy about pulling elements. Operational corollaries today: (a) **`step_by(step)` yields elements at indices `0, step, 2*step, …` until past the end.** Probe 1: `vec![10, 20, 30, 40, 50].iter().step_by(2)` produces three lines `10, 30, 50` (indices 0, 2, 4). (b) **`step_by(1)` is the identity adapter** (Probe 3). (c) **`step_by(N)` with N greater than length yields just the first element, no panic** — per `:556-557` "The first element of the iterator will always be returned, regardless of the step given" (Probe 4: `step_by(100)` on a 3-element vec yields `&10` only). (d) **`step_by(0)` panics at construction** with exit 101 and the lesson 053 panic template (Probe 2, three sub-probes triangulate). (e) **`StepBy<Self>` is itself an `Iterator`** — `for x in iter.step_by(2)` and `iter.step_by(2).count()` both work; Probe 5's E0308 names the wrapper-struct type `StepBy<Iter<'_, u64>>`. Three structural slots inherited from 136-139 unchanged: (i) consuming `self` receiver (lesson 102) — not re-witnessed today via E0382 since seven prior captures (102/133/134/136/137/138/139) suffice; (ii) `Self: Sized` bound (named-deferred); (iii) wrapper-struct return whose `Iterator` impl makes it itself an iterator. Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `StepBy<I>` struct's private fields and its `next` body (two equivalent pull strategies described at `:559-565`); the `Self: Sized` bound; the `unused_must_use` lint that fires when a `StepBy` is built and discarded as a statement (separate lint mechanic — incidentally corroborates 136's laziness framing); the std-library source line `step_by.rs:35` named in the panic (internal path, not user-facing); the `into_iter()` form used by the std example (named-deferred since 022); the `assert!` macro itself as user-writable (treated as observed mechanism today, like lesson 053 treated `panic!`). Unlock per audit §5: `size_hint` (step 11) — the last closure-free non-consumer Iterator surface move; reuses today's adapter frame but with `&self` borrowing receiver (contrast against today's consuming `self`). Once the closure arc lands, `take_while` / `map_while` (audit §4.4.1) reuse today's "preconditions can be eager even when iteration is lazy" frame for closure-driven adapters. Future "general pattern of `assert!` in adapter constructors" lesson — gates a more abstract reading of std adapters' panic notes.
depends_on: 137-iterator-skip (load-bearing — installs the adapter shape `(self, n: usize) -> Wrapper<Self>`. Today reuses every slot structurally; the substitution is `step` for `n` and `StepBy` for `Skip`. The new fact relative to 137 is the panic precondition — 137's `skip(N)` past length silently produces an empty iterator, while today's `step_by(0)` panics), 136-iterator-take (load-bearing — installs the lazy framing for the adapter family and the "wrapper is itself an iterator" claim. Today inherits laziness for iteration, but breaks it for argument validation), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs `pub trait Iterator { type Item; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>; /* + 75 provided */ }` and the default-body inheritance mechanic from 116. `step_by` is one of the 75 provided methods; synopsis line at `:25-26` ends in `{ ... }`), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `.next()` on a slice iterator returning `Option<&T>` and stopping at `None`. Probe 1's for-loop and chained `.count()` both rely on the slice iterator stopping at `None` once index 6 is past the end of the 5-element vec), 053-result-expect-and-panic (load-bearing — installs the runtime panic shape `thread 'main' ... panicked at <file>:<line>: <message>`, output to stderr, exit status 101, with the `note: run with RUST_BACKTRACE=1` trailer. Today's `assertion failed: step != 0` panic (Probe 2) reuses that template exactly; no new panic mechanic is introduced. The only difference: today's message has no human-written prefix because the std implementation uses a bare `assert!`, where lesson 053's `expect("msg")` had a human-supplied prefix), 133-iterator-count (cited — `.count()` is the chained consumer on Probe 1's second half and Probe 4's count assertion), 102-self-by-value-receiver (cited — bare-`self` consuming receiver shape; not re-witnessed today via E0382 since well-installed by lessons 102/133/134/136/137/138/139), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `usize` parameter for `step`), 049-method-chaining (cited — `v.iter().step_by(2).count()` parses left-associative), 022-for-range (cited — `for x in iter` desugars through `IntoIterator`/`Iterator`; reused on Probe 1's first half), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.step_by(step)`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!` with `{}` placeholder), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic map applied to E0308 in Probe 5 and to the runtime panic message in Probe 2), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::size_hint` — `(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`" moves (audit §5 step 11 — last closure-free non-consumer Iterator surface move; reuses today's wrapper-as-iterator frame but with `&self` borrowing receiver — contrast against today's consuming `self`. Returns a 2-tuple of `(usize, Option<usize>)`, composing 138's tuple-yielding frame with 119's `Option<T>` wrapper. Each adapter has its own `size_hint` impl — `StepBy<I>::size_hint` divides by `step`), future "`Iterator::take_while`, `map_while`" moves (audit §4.4.1, gated on closure arc — reuse today's "post-`None` behavior" frame from 139 plus today's "preconditions can be eager" frame; both adapters short-circuit and benefit from the same fused-or-not analysis), future "general pattern of `assert!` in adapter constructors" moves (today is the first instance — `step_by(0)` panics in the constructor body. Future lessons can reuse this as a unified frame for adapter preconditions), future "the `StepBy<I>` struct's `Iterator` impl" moves (the doc at `:559-565` describes two equivalent pull strategies — `self.next(); self.nth(step-1); …` versus `advance_n_and_return_first(...)`; today refers to the impl opaquely. Future move would unpack which strategy this rustc version uses and whether the choice is observable through side effects on the inner iterator), future "the `unused_must_use` lint mechanic" moves (Probe 2c surfaced rustc's "iterators are lazy and do nothing unless consumed" warning — separate lint subsystem, not part of the iterator API)

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 141-iterator-size-hint
move: query an iterator's remaining-length bounds with `iter.size_hint()`. Working probe `fn main() { let v: Vec<u64> = vec![10, 20, 30]; let iter = v.iter(); println!("{:?}", iter.size_hint()); let mut iter = v.iter(); let _ = iter.next(); println!("{:?}", iter.size_hint()); let empty: Vec<u64> = vec![]; println!("{:?}", empty.iter().size_hint()); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints three lines: `(3, Some(3))` / `(2, Some(2))` / `(0, Some(0))`. Slice iters report exact remaining count: lower=upper for every line. The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:345` spells `fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)` — `&self` (lesson 100's third receiver shape, *not* `self`, *not* `&mut self`), no extra parameter, return is a bare 2-tuple `(lower_bound: usize, upper_bound: Option<usize>)`. The synopsis-box line at `:18` ends in `{ ... }` (lesson 116's default-body marker). Centered new-fact Probe 2 (twice-call witness, three sequential `iter.size_hint()` calls on a `let iter` with NO `mut`): all three calls succeed and return identical `(3, Some(3))` tuples — empirical witness that the receiver is the immutable borrow `&self` (cursor not advanced), no `mut` required on the binding. Direct contrast against lesson 131's `let mut iter` requirement for `.next()` (`&mut self`); Probe 6 (single-source negative contrast) compresses both halves: `iter.size_hint()` succeeds on `let iter`, `iter.next()` between two `size_hint` calls fires E0596 — the same diagnostic 131 captured. Type-pin Probe 3 (`let _x: u32 = v.iter().size_hint();`): fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`(usize, Option<usize>)\`` and secondary `note: expected type \`u32\`; found tuple \`(usize, Option<usize>)\`` — rustc spells the return type from its own mouth, naming the *kind* `tuple` (not struct, not Option-wrapped). Probe 4 (size_hint after exhaustion, 1-element source): `before: (1, Some(1))` / `after one .next(): (0, Some(0))` / `after second .next() (now None): (0, Some(0))` — slice iters track exact remaining length through state changes including post-`None`. Probe 5 (three receiver shapes coexist): `iter.size_hint()` (`&self`) → `iter.next()` (`&mut self`) → `iter.size_hint()` (`&self`) → `iter.count()` (`self` consumes) all chain successfully on one `let mut iter` binding. Three load-bearing corpus claims: (1) `:347-354` "first element is the lower bound, and the second element is the upper bound" + "second half is an `Option<usize>` ... `None` here means that either there is no known upper bound, or the upper bound is larger than `usize`"; (2) `:371-372` "The default implementation returns `(0, None)` which is correct for any iterator" — slice iters override and return exact counts; (3) `:358-369` "It is not enforced that an iterator implementation yields the declared number of elements ... `size_hint()` is primarily intended to be used for optimizations such as reserving space ... must not be trusted to e.g., omit bounds checks in unsafe code" — `size_hint` is a *hint*, not a guarantee. Today's move is step 11 of the iterator-API-coverage audit §5 first-arc plan: the LAST closure-free non-consumer Iterator surface move. After today, every method touched on the trait (10 total: `next`, `count`, `last`, `nth`, `take`, `skip`, `enumerate`, `fuse`, `step_by`, `size_hint`) has had a receiver-shape capture: `self` (consuming), `&mut self`, and (today) `&self`. The `None` upper-bound case is *not* probed today (slice iters always exact; `0..` example at `:407-411` requires `Range` iteration, still gated since 022); the default `(0, None)` impl is *not* probed (slice iters override; future move on a custom user-defined iterator could probe); the "hint, not guarantee" caveat at `:358-369` is named as a corpus fact and not probed (would require constructing a buggy iterator implementation, out of scope). The `where Self: Sized` bound is *absent* from `size_hint` (unlike take/skip/enumerate/fuse/step_by/count/last/nth — all consuming-`self` carry the bound; `size_hint` does not, because `&self` does not move out of `Self`); named-deferred today as a structural observation (future "`Self: Sized` bound on consuming-`self` provided methods" lesson). The `ExactSizeIterator` trait, which turns the upper-slot `Option<usize>` into a stronger (always `Some(n)`) contract, is named-deferred. The `(0, None)` / `extend` / `collect` reservation interaction is named-deferred. The closure sub-arc (audit §4.4.1) is the next major arc.
main_concept: `Iterator::size_hint` is the FIRST stable provided `Iterator` method whose receiver is `&self` (lesson 100). The trait declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:345` spells `fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>)`. Three new facts today: (1) **`&self` receiver — third shape on a stdlib Iterator method.** Lessons 102/133/134/136/137/138/139/140 captured `self` (consuming); lessons 131/135 captured `&mut self`; today is the first `&self`. The binding does not need `mut`; the cursor is not advanced; the call can be repeated and returns the same tuple. Probe 2 (twice-call witness on `let iter` no `mut`) and Probe 6 (E0596 contrast in the same source on `iter.next()` between two `iter.size_hint()` calls) ground this empirically. (2) **Return is a bare 2-tuple `(usize, Option<usize>)`** — composes lesson 072's tuple type with lesson 119's `Option<T>` wrapper, where `T = usize`. The first slot is the *lower bound*, always known (`usize`); the second slot is the *upper bound*, wrapped in `Option<usize>` because some iterators cannot give an exact answer — `Some(n)` if known, `None` if "either there is no known upper bound, or the upper bound is larger than `usize`" (`:352-354`). For slice iterators (Probes 1, 4, 5), the upper slot is always `Some(n)` with `n` matching the lower bound (exact-count iterators). Probe 3's E0308 confirms the return type from rustc's mouth: `(usize, Option<usize>)`, *kind* `tuple` — not `Option`-wrapped, not a named struct. (3) **The default impl returns `(0, None)`** per `:371-372`. Iterators inherit this conservative default unless they override it; slice iterators override. Audience-level mental model: "an iterator can answer 'how many elements remain?' without being consumed or mutated. The answer is two numbers: a lower bound (always known, possibly 0) and an upper bound (possibly unknown). For slice iterators the answer is exact. For others (especially closure-driven ones that come later) the answer can be partial or absent. The answer is a *hint* for optimizations, not a guarantee — buggy iterators may yield outside the declared range, and the trait does not enforce correctness." Operational corollaries today: (a) **Cursor is not advanced** (Probe 2: three identical reads on the same `let iter`). (b) **Empty iter → `(0, Some(0))`** (Probe 1 line 3). (c) **After exhaustion, size_hint stays at `(0, Some(0))`** even after additional `.next()` calls past `None` (Probe 4 line 3). (d) **Three receiver shapes coexist on one binding** (Probe 5: `iter.size_hint()` → `iter.next()` → `iter.size_hint()` → `iter.count()` — `&self`/`&mut self`/`&self`/`self` all chain successfully; the `mut` on the binding is required by `next` and `count`, not by `size_hint`). (e) **The `where Self: Sized` bound is absent** from `size_hint`'s declaration — unlike take/skip/enumerate/fuse/step_by/count/last/nth (all consuming-`self`); named-deferred. (f) **It is a hint, not a guarantee** (`:358-369`); used for optimizations such as reserving space (e.g., `Vec::extend` reserves based on the lower bound); do not rely on it for memory safety. Wholesale or named-deferred today: the `where Self: Sized` *absence* mechanic (gates a future "`Self: Sized` is required only when `Self` moves out of the receiver" lesson); the `(0, None)` default impl probe (would need a custom user-defined iterator that omits `size_hint`); the `None` upper-bound case for `0..` at `:407-411` (gated on `Range` iteration, deferred since 022) and for `.filter(...)` (gated on closure arc); the `ExactSizeIterator` trait at `:487` (mentioned in passing on the trait page); the `extend`/`collect`/`reserve` interaction; the buggy-iterator caveat probe (would need to write a buggy `Iterator` impl). Today closes the closure-free portion of audit §5 (the last of 11 steps) — every method touched on the trait has had a receiver-shape capture. Unlocks per audit §4.4: closure sub-arc, `IntoIterator` sub-arc, bounded-by-other-trait sub-arc, `Try` sub-arc — all can proceed in parallel where prereqs allow.
depends_on: 140-iterator-step-by (load-bearing — previous step in audit §5; closes the closure-free arc behind today. The arc 131-140 covered receivers `self` and `&mut self`; today adds `&self`), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs `pub trait Iterator { type Item; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>; /* + 75 provided */ }` and the default-body inheritance mechanic from 116. `size_hint` is one of the 75; synopsis line at `:18` ends in `{ ... }` — lesson 116's default-body marker), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `.next()` taking `&mut self`, requiring `let mut iter` and firing E0596 if `mut` is dropped. Today's centered contrast is the receiver-shape difference: `size_hint` takes `&self`, no `mut` required; Probe 2 (three calls on `let iter` succeed) and Probe 6 (single-source E0596 negative contrast on `iter.next()` between two `iter.size_hint()` calls) ground both halves), 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — installs `Option<T>` / `Some(_)` / `None`. Today's return type substitutes `T = usize` to give `Option<usize>` for the upper-bound slot; Probe 1 outputs `Some(3)`, `Some(2)`, `Some(0)` payloads. The `None` form is *not* probed today since slice iters always know exact remaining), 100-inherent-impl-and-self (load-bearing — installs `&self` as a receiver shape on user-defined inherent impls (`fn current(&self) -> u32` on `Counter`). Today is the first appearance of `&self` on a stdlib provided `Iterator` method — confirmed by walking the full 76-method declaration. Three structural slots inherited unchanged: `&self` is shorthand for `self: &Self`; the binding does not need `mut`; the receiver is not consumed), 072-tuple-type-and-index (cited — installs the tuple type `(A, B)`. Today's return type `(usize, Option<usize>)` is one specific 2-tuple shape; Probe 3's E0308 names the *kind* `tuple`), 073-let-tuple-destructure (cited — installs `let (a, b) = pair;`. Not used in today's probes; the lesson body keeps the tuple intact for `{:?}` debug printing. Future moves can destructure the bounds), 080-integer-type-family (cited — installs `usize`. Today's lower-bound slot and `Option<usize>` payload both use `usize`), 116-trait-default-method-body (cited — installs the `{ ... }` synopsis-box marker. `size_hint`'s synopsis line at `:18` carries it), 049-method-chaining (cited — `empty.iter().size_hint()` parses left-associative), 040-method-call-syntax (cited — dot-call shape `iter.size_hint()`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `{:?}` Debug placeholder; `Option<usize>` and `(usize, Option<usize>)` are both `Debug`), 005-let-binding (cited), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic map applied to E0308 in Probe 3 and to E0596 in Probe 6), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "`Iterator::map` — `(self, f) -> Map<Self, F>`, lazy closure-adapter that rewrites the yielded element type" moves (gated on closure arc, audit §4.4.1 — first closure-driven adapter; reuses 138's "yielded element type can be rewritten" frame and 140's "lazy adapter" frame), future "`Iterator::filter`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `filter_map`, `scan`, `inspect` — closure-driven adapters" moves (gated on closure arc, audit §4.4.1 — many of these have non-default `size_hint` impls that return partial bounds, e.g., `filter` returns `(0, original_upper)`), future "`Iterator::collect` — `(self) -> B where B: FromIterator<Item>`, the canonical consumer that drives an adapter chain" moves (gated on `FromIterator` + turbofish; uses `size_hint`'s lower bound to pre-allocate `Vec` capacity), future "`IntoIterator` trait sub-arc" moves (audit §4.4.2 — generalizes `for` desugaring, `Vec::iter` mechanics), future "`ExactSizeIterator` trait" moves (the `:487` mention — a sub-trait of `Iterator` whose `len()` returns the exact element count; turns the upper-slot `Option<usize>` into a stronger `(n, Some(n))`-with-equal-bounds contract. Slice iterators implement it; today's Probe 1/4/5 outputs all show the equal-bounds shape that licenses `ExactSizeIterator`), future "`size_hint` on a custom user-defined iterator (overriding the default `(0, None)`)" moves (composes lesson 132's user-defined `Iterator` pattern with a custom `size_hint` impl; out of scope today since the centered new fact is the `&self` receiver, not custom impls), future "`size_hint` `None` upper-bound case via `0..` (infinite range)" moves (gated on `Range` iteration, deferred since 022; `:407-411` shows `(usize::MAX, None)` for `0..`), future "the `where Self: Sized` *absence* on `&self`-receiver methods" moves (today's `size_hint` declaration carries no `where Self: Sized` bound — unlike all eight `self`-consuming methods seen so far. Future move could install "`Self: Sized` is required when `Self` moves out of the receiver, which `&self` does not"), future "`Vec::extend` and `Vec::reserve` capacity heuristics using `size_hint`" moves (the doc at `:362-364` names this as the canonical use — "reserving space for the elements of the iterator"), future "the `Try` sub-arc — `try_fold`, `try_for_each`, `try_find`" moves (audit §4.4.4)

  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/141-iterator-size-hint.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called
move: bind a closure literal to a `let` and call it with parens, e.g. `let add_one = |x: u32| x + 1; let a = add_one(5);`. Working probe `fn main() { let add_one = |x: u32| x + 1; let a = add_one(5); let b = add_one(10); println!("{}", a); println!("{}", b); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints two lines `6` then `11`. The closure literal `|x: u32| x + 1` reuses lesson 020's `name: TYPE` parameter slot inside pipe-bracket-pipe brackets `|...|`, then a single-expression body `x + 1` (no braces, no trailing `;`) — Book v4's brace-free body form. The Book's canonical four-form table at `output/docs/rust/book/ch13-01-closures.md:208-213` lines them up: v1 is the function form (lessons 008/020/021/025 shape), v2 fully annotates the closure (`|x: u32| -> u32 { x + 1 }`), v3 drops type annotations (`|x| { x + 1 }`), v4 drops braces (`|x| x + 1`). Today's hybrid keeps v2's parameter annotation with v4's brace-free body — explicitly mid-form per the audit §6 step-1 prompt: the parameter annotation gives rustc enough info to resolve the body type without entering "first-call-fixes-the-type" territory (deferred to step 2), and the brace-free body directly reuses lesson 025's implicit-return rule. The Book at `:215-219` says of v4: "we remove the brackets, which are optional because the closure body has only one expression" — verbatim source for the implicit-return-applies-to-closures claim. Negative-contrast Probe 2 (drop the call parens, try to assign the closure value to a `u32`): `let a: u32 = add_one;` fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with `expected u32, found closure` at the use site, plus `note: expected type u32, found closure {closure@noparens.rs:2:19: 2:27}` and `help: use parentheses to call this closure` with suggested edit `add_one(/* u32 */)`. Three grounded facts from rustc's mouth: (1) `add_one` is a value (rustc marks it `the found closure` under the closure literal); (2) its kind is `closure`, full type name `{closure@<file>:<line>:<col>: <line>:<col>}` — rustc's anonymous closure-type spelling, opaque to the user, tied to source location; (3) the call-with-parens shape is the missing step. The Book at `:6-9` defines closures as "anonymous functions you can save in a variable or pass as arguments to other functions" — today only covers the "save in a variable" half (passing to functions deferred to step 4). The alternative-suggested probe `let f = |x| x + 1; let n: u32 = f(5);` produced E0271 (`<i32 as Add>::Output == u32`) — rustc inferred `x: i32` from the body's `1` literal default, then failed at the use site. That's exactly the inference territory reserved for step 2, so the inferred-type probe was tested but explicitly deferred; `noparens.rs` (Probe 2) is the cleaner anchor for "closure is a value with a type" without entering inference territory. Wholesale or named-deferred today: closure capturing outer bindings (deferred to step 3); the `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` traits (steps 4-5); closure type-inference rules and the first-call-fixes-the-type diagnostic (step 2); closures over generic types; passing closures to functions; `impl Fn`/`Box<dyn Fn>` return forms; the opaque type name `{closure@<loc>}` itself as a syntactic category (rustc said it; what it *is* deferred); closure body as a brace-block (Book v2/v3 forms, today uses only v4); the unannotated parameter case (step 2). This is the FIRST move of the closure sub-arc per audit §6 step 1; gates 27 closure-taking Iterator methods.
main_concept: a *closure literal* `|param: T| body` is a value — written with pipes around a parameter list (same `name: TYPE` slot as a function's `(p: T)`, lesson 020) and a body after the closing pipe — that you bind with `let` like any other value (lesson 005) and *call* with the parens-call shape from lesson 008 to get the body's value out. Three new facts today: (1) **Pipe-bracket-pipe is the parameter-list bracket for closure literals** — Book v2/v3/v4 forms at `ch13-01-closures.md:208-213`. The shape `name: TYPE` lesson 020 used between function parens carries inside `|...|` unchanged. The *return* type is inferred from the body (Book v3/v4 omit `-> u32` because rustc reads it off `x + 1` once `x: u32`); writing it explicitly is the v2 form. (2) **A single-expression body with no braces is the most compact form** (Book v4): `|x: u32| x + 1`. Lesson 025's implicit-return rule applies — the body expression IS the closure's return value, no `return` keyword, no `;`. The Book at `:217-218` says the brackets "are optional because the closure body has only one expression." (3) **The closure is a value with its own anonymous type** — rustc spells it `{closure@<file>:<line>:<col>: <line>:<col>}` (Probe 2 E0308 `note:` block) and the `kind` is `closure`. That value is bound to a `let` like any other value, and calling it with the lesson-008 parens shape (`add_one(5)`) is what yields the value the body produces. Drop the parens (`let a: u32 = add_one;`) and rustc fires E0308 with `expected u32, found closure` at the use site, plus `help: use parentheses to call this closure`. Audience-level mental model: "the same call-with-parens shape works on a value bound to `let`, when that value is a closure literal `|param: T| body`. Pipes around the parameter list, single-expression body after. The closure has its own type — rustc names it `closure`, full type `{closure@<loc>}` — and you call it with parens to get the body's value out." Operational corollaries today: (a) **Pipe-bracket-pipe parameter list with one fully-annotated parameter** (Probe 1 `|x: u32|`); the Book v3/v4 unannotated parameter case is deferred to step 2. (b) **Brace-free single-expression body** (Probe 1 `x + 1`); the Book v2/v3 brace-block body form is named-deferred. (c) **Return type inferred from the body** (Probe 1 omits `-> u32`, compiles silently); writing it explicitly (Book v2) is supported but not centered. (d) **The closure value's type is `closure {closure@<loc>}`** (Probe 2 `note:` block from rustc's mouth); what kind of type that is, why it cannot be written by hand, whether two closures with identical bodies share a type — all named-deferred. (e) **Calling with parens is the lesson-008 shape** (Probe 1 `add_one(5)`, `add_one(10)` produce `6` and `11`; Probe 2's `help:` directly suggests the same `add_one(/* u32 */)` form). Wholesale or named-deferred today: closure capturing outer bindings (audit §6 step 3 — the load-bearing difference between closures and unnamed functions; today's closure does not reference any outer locals beyond its parameter); the `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` traits and how closures get auto-implementations of one or more (audit §6 steps 4-5); closure type-inference and "first call fixes the type" (audit §6 step 2 — the inferred-`x: i32` probe was tested and produced E0271, deferred); closures over generic types; passing closures to functions as parameters; `impl Fn` / `Box<dyn Fn>` return forms; the unannotated parameter case (Book v3/v4 forms — step 2). This is the FIRST move of the 5-step closure sub-arc that gates 27 closure-taking Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1 / §6).
depends_on: 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs the call-with-parens shape `name();`. Today reuses it on a `let`-bound closure value: `add_one(5)`. Probe 2's `help: use parentheses to call this closure` directly grounds this reuse), 020-function-with-parameter (load-bearing — installs the typed parameter slot `p: TYPE` between function parens. Today reuses the exact `name: TYPE` shape inside `|...|` brackets: `|x: u32|`. The new fact relative to 020 is that the brackets change and the parameter type is *optional* for closures (`ch13-01:141-143`), but today keeps the annotation to stay within step-1 scope), 021-function-return-value (load-bearing — installs that a function returns a value of a declared type, so a call expression `name(args)` carries the returned value. Today's closure returns a value too and the call expression `add_one(5)` carries it; the return type is *inferred* from the body for closures rather than written with `->`), 025-implicit-return (load-bearing — installs that a function body is a block, the block's value is its tail expression with no `;`, and `-> RTYPE` makes that value the function's return value. Today's closure body `x + 1` is exactly the bare-tail-expression form: no `;`, no `return`, no braces. The Book at `:217-218` says of v4: "we remove the brackets, which are optional because the closure body has only one expression" — verbatim source for the implicit-return-applies-to-closures claim), 005-let-binding (load-bearing — installs `let name = value;`. Today's `let add_one = |x: u32| x + 1;` reuses the shape on a closure literal value. The lesson-005 property "later statements use the name as that value" carries: later `let a = add_one(5);` *calls* the bound value with parens), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` is one of the integer types; today's parameter type and inferred return type both `u32`), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `+` on two integer values produces a new integer; today's body `x + 1` is one such expression), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", name)` formats with positional args; used in Probe 1), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (cited — diagnostic map applied to E0308 in Probe 2), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "closure type inference and the 'first call fixes the type' rule" moves (audit §6 step 2 — the next move in the closure sub-arc; reuses today's closure literal syntax with the parameter annotation *dropped* (Book v3/v4 forms) and shows E0308 / E0271 when an unannotated closure is called twice with different argument types. Book ch13-01:238-274 walks the canonical diagnostic. Today's deferred Probe with `let f = |x| x + 1; let n: u32 = f(5);` (E0271) is one piece of the empirical surface step 2 will use), future "closure capturing an outer binding" moves (audit §6 step 3 — closure references a local `let` from the enclosing scope; the binding is borrowed immutably or mutably depending on usage. Book ch13-01:286+ "Capturing References or Moving Ownership" anchors this. The load-bearing fact: closures can read or modify locals from the enclosing scope, which is what makes them more than just unnamed functions), future "`FnMut`-bound parameter on a function" moves (audit §6 step 4 — define `fn apply<F: FnMut(u32) -> u32>(f: F, x: u32) -> u32 { f(x) }`, pass a closure to it. First exposure to the `FnMut` trait bound on a generic; local doc at `output/docs/rust/std/ops/trait.FnMut.md`), future "`Fn` / `FnMut` / `FnOnce` distinction" moves (audit §6 step 5 — capture-by-shared-ref / capture-by-mut-ref / capture-by-move maps to the three traits; local docs at `trait.Fn.md`, `trait.FnMut.md`, `trait.FnOnce.md`. With this lesson installed, the closure-arc prereqs are complete), future "`Iterator::for_each` — `(self, F) where F: FnMut(Self::Item)`" moves (gated on closure arc; consumer with no return — simplest closure-driven Iterator method per audit §4.4.1), future "`Iterator::map` — `(self, F) -> Map<Self, F> where F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> B`" moves (gated on closure arc; lazy adapter that rewrites the yielded element type; directly composes lesson 138's enumerate frame with closure-driven element rewriting), future "`Iterator::filter`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `filter_map`, `scan`, `inspect` — closure-driven adapters" moves (gated on closure arc; many have non-default `size_hint` impls returning partial bounds — composes with lesson 141), future "the opaque type name `{closure@<loc>}` as a syntactic category" moves (today rustc said it; what it *is* deferred — gates a future understanding of "closure types are anonymous, one-per-source-location, and you cannot write them by hand; you constrain them with `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` trait bounds")
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch13-01-closures.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/iterator-api-coverage.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/142-closure-literal-bound-and-called.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/142-closure-literal-bound-and-called.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/142-closure-literal-bound-and-called.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 143-unannotated-closure-first-use
move: drop the parameter annotation from a closure literal and witness rustc inferring the parameter type from the *first* call site, then locking the closure to that one type. Working probe `fn main() { let id = |x| x; let a = id(5_u32); let b = id(10_u32); println!("{}", a); println!("{}", b); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints two lines `5` and `10`. The closure literal is `|x| x` — the `name: TYPE` slot lesson 142 wrote inside `|...|` is *gone*, leaving just `|x|`. Rustc accepts the literal cleanly; no error, no warning. Both call sites pass `u32`-suffixed integer literals (lesson 081's `5_u32` form). Rustc resolves `x: u32` from the first call site, then checks the second call against that. Negative-contrast Probe 2 (`twocalls.rs`: same source but second call `id(5_i32)`): fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` headlined at the *second* call (line 4), with inline label `expected u32, found i32`. The load-bearing diagnostic feature today is the `note:` block: `note: expected because the closure was earlier called with an argument of type \`u32\`` plus its own `--> twocalls.rs:3:16` location pointing at the *first* call site. A second `note: closure parameter defined here` underlines the `x` inside `|x|`. The `help:` block suggests changing `5_i32` to `5_u32` (i.e., changing the *argument*, not the closure) — rustc does not offer making the closure accept both types. Directionality Probe 3 (`orderswap.rs`: same source minus the order of the two calls — `5_i32` first, `5_u32` second): same E0308 shape, but with the headline's labels flipped (`expected i32, found u32`) and the `note:` saying "earlier called with an argument of type `i32`". Probe 2 + Probe 3 together rule out reading the asymmetry as anything other than first-call-wins. The Book at `output/docs/rust/book/ch13-01-closures.md:225-284` walks the canonical version of this exact diagnostic — lines 225-232 state the rule ("the compiler will infer one concrete type for each of their parameters"), lines 236-243 give Listing 13-3 (`let example_closure = |x| x; let s = example_closure(String::from("hello")); let n = example_closure(5);`), lines 252-279 reproduce the rustc diagnostic verbatim (same `note: expected because the closure was earlier called with an argument of type X` block, same `note: closure parameter defined here` block), lines 281-284 close with "Those types are then locked into the closure ... and we get a type error when we next try to use a different type". Today's probes substitute the integer-suffix form `5_u32`/`5_i32` for the Book's `String::from(...)`/`5` shape — `String::from` is named-deferred since lesson 042 — but the diagnostic structure is identical. The headline's label for `&str`-typed string-literal arguments and the `note:`'s use of `&'static str` (visible in the Check-Yourself side-probe `q.rs`) are named in the lesson body; the `&str`/`&'static str` types themselves are named-deferred. Probe-not-needed (documented in evidence): the body shape `|x| x + 1` (lesson 142's body) produces the same diagnostic structure with the same `earlier called` `note:` — body shape is not load-bearing for today's centered fact, identity body `|x| x` is the cleaner anchor and is also the Book's exact form. The unannotated closure case is the canonical setup for "first-call-fixes-the-type"; the Book cites this rule as analogous to `let v = Vec::new()` needing later `.push(...)` to give the elements a concrete type (`:222-223`) — generalized type inference, applied to the closure's parameter slot and return slot. Wholesale or named-deferred today: closure capturing outer bindings (audit §6 step 3); the `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` traits (steps 4-5); generic functions over closures (step 4); passing closures to functions (step 4); `impl Fn`/`Box<dyn Fn>` return forms; the `&str` type (mentioned in Check-Yourself, deferred); the `{closure@<loc>}` opaque-type spelling as a category (lesson 142 saw rustc say it; today does not deepen — note that today's diagnostic does NOT surface the `{closure@<loc>}` spelling because the mismatch is at a call-argument site naming integer types directly, not at a closure-vs-non-closure site); the closure return-type annotation (Book v2's `-> RTYPE`); default-integer inference inside closure bodies (the lesson 080 `i32` default would seed `x: i32` from a body like `|x| x + 1` if no suffix was supplied, separately from call-site inference; today's identity body bypasses this). This is the SECOND move of the closure sub-arc per audit §6 step 2; the next step (§6 step 3) installs closures capturing outer bindings.
main_concept: an *unannotated* closure literal `|x| body` has its parameter type (and return type) *inferred from the first call site*. Three new facts today: (1) **The closure parameter annotation is optional** — `|x|` is legal alongside lesson 142's `|x: T|`. The Book at `:208-213` lined up four forms; today's `|x| x` is Book v3's parameter slot with Book v4's brace-free body. The Book at `:141-143` framed this earlier: "Closures don't usually require you to annotate the types of the parameters or the return value like `fn` functions do." (2) **The first call site fixes the parameter type for the lifetime of the closure** — rustc infers ONE concrete type from the first call, then locks the closure to that type. The Book at `:281-284`: "The first time we call `example_closure` with the `String` value, the compiler infers the type of `x` and the return type ... Those types are then locked into the closure ... and we get a type error when we next try to use a different type." Closures are NOT generic over the parameter type — `|x| x` is one closure with one parameter type, not a fresh callable per type. (3) **The diagnostic for a disagreeing second call is `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with a load-bearing `note:` block carrying its own `-->` location** pointing at the first call site, with text "expected because the closure was earlier called with an argument of type X". This is the first time in the run that a `note:` block's `-->` location is *different* from the headline's `-->` location — the diagnostic structurally cross-references two source lines. A second `note: closure parameter defined here` underlines the `x` inside `|x|`. The `help:` block fixes the *argument*, not the closure. Audience-level mental model: "a closure with `|x|` (no annotation) is not generic — it commits to ONE parameter type and return type at the first call. Later calls have to match. If they don't, rustc fires E0308 at the later call and the `note:` block tells you which earlier call locked the type in." Operational corollaries today: (a) **Both calls must agree on the parameter type** (Probe 1: both `u32`). (b) **A second call with a different type fails at the second call site** (Probe 2: `expected u32, found i32`). (c) **The `note:` block ties back to the first call** (Probe 2 + Probe 3 transcripts). (d) **Order matters — first call wins regardless of which type that is** (Probe 2 vs Probe 3: same source, swapped order, headline labels flip from "expected u32, found i32" to "expected i32, found u32"). (e) **The closure literal compiles cleanly** (the error is at the call site, not the literal). (f) **Rustc does not offer making the closure generic** (the `help:` block fixes the argument, not the closure). Wholesale or named-deferred today: closure capturing outer bindings (audit §6 step 3 — the load-bearing difference between closures and unnamed functions); the `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` traits and how they classify closures by capture mode (audit §6 steps 4-5); generic functions over closures with `Fn`-bounds (step 4); passing closures to functions; `impl Fn` / `Box<dyn Fn>` return forms; the `&str` type (mentioned in Check-Yourself diagnostic but deferred); closure return-type annotation `-> RTYPE` (Book v2 form); default-integer inference inside closure bodies; the `{closure@<loc>}` opaque-type spelling as a category. This is the SECOND move of the 5-step closure sub-arc; with steps 1-2 installed, the closure literal can be read end-to-end as syntax and as type-inference behavior. Step 3 (capturing outer bindings) opens after this lesson lands.
depends_on: 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs the closure literal `|x: T| body` bound to a `let` and called with parens, plus the closure-as-value framing. Today drops the `: T` annotation from the parameter slot, leaving `|x|`; everything else carries unchanged. The new fact relative to 142 is what the type *becomes* when no annotation is written), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map (headline + `-->` + source excerpt with caret + `note:` / `help:`). Today's new feature is a second `-->` location inside a `note:` block, at a different line from the headline — rustc using `note:` to cross-reference two source lines), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — installs the integer literal type suffix `57u8` (or `5_u32`). Today's call sites use this form to force two distinct integer types into two call sites without a separate `let`-binding-with-annotation indirection. Without lesson 081's suffix form, bare unsuffixed `5` would default to `i32` and the negative contrast would not fire), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs `u32` and `i32` as distinct integer types in the family. Today's contrast rests on rustc treating them as incompatible — the diagnostic confirms `expected u32, found i32`), 005-let-binding (load-bearing — `let name = value;`. Today's three `let` lines per probe reuse this), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — call-with-parens shape `name(args)`. Today reuses lesson 142's reuse of 008 on a `let`-bound closure value), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", name)` for the working probe; `println!("{} {}", a, b)` for Probe 2/3 surface — two `{}` placeholders, already installed), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "closure capturing an outer binding" moves (audit §6 step 3 — the next move in the closure sub-arc; closure references a local `let` from the enclosing scope, the binding is borrowed immutably or mutably depending on usage. Book ch13-01:286+ "Capturing References or Moving Ownership" anchors this. Load-bearing fact: closures can read or modify locals from the enclosing scope, which is what makes them more than just unnamed functions), future "`FnMut`-bound parameter on a function" moves (audit §6 step 4 — first exposure to the `FnMut` trait bound on a generic; gates passing closures to functions), future "`Fn` / `FnMut` / `FnOnce` distinction" moves (audit §6 step 5 — three traits classifying closures by capture mode; with this lesson installed the closure-arc prereqs are complete), future "`Iterator::for_each`, `Iterator::map`, `Iterator::filter`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `filter_map`, `scan`, `inspect` — closure-driven Iterator adapters" moves (gated on closure arc completion; today's "first call fixes the type" rule explains why these methods' parameter signatures use `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B` patterns where `B` is also inferred — same mechanism applied to a generic function bound), future "the cross-reference `note:` block as a diagnostic feature" moves (today is the first time a `note:` block's `-->` points at a different line than the headline; the same multi-location diagnostic structure also surfaces in borrow-checker errors and lifetime errors, which are gated on later borrowing/lifetimes lessons), future "default-integer inference inside closure bodies" moves (today's identity body bypasses this; a closure body like `|x| x + 1` with no suffix at the call would seed `x: i32` from the body literal `1`, distinct from the call-site mechanism today's lesson centers — e.g. lesson 142's evidence appendix recorded an E0271 instance), future "closure return-type annotation `-> RTYPE`" moves (Book v2 form `|x: T| -> R { body }` — today and 142 both omit it, letting rustc infer the return type from the body)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch13-01-closures.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/iterator-api-coverage.md
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/143-unannotated-closure-first-use.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/143-unannotated-closure-first-use.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/143-unannotated-closure-first-use.md
status: accepted
```
