```text
id: 144-closure-captures-outer-let
move: write a closure literal whose body references a name from the enclosing scope and witness rustc accepting the literal silently. Working probe `fn main() { let n: u32 = 10; let add_n = |x: u32| x + n; let a = add_n(5); let b = add_n(7); println!("{}", a); println!("{}", b); }` compiles silently with `rustc demo.rs` (exit 0) and `./demo` prints two lines `15` then `17`. The closure literal `|x: u32| x + n` declares one parameter slot `x: u32` (lesson 142's annotated form) and a single-expression body `x + n` (Book v4 brace-free body, lesson 142). The body references *two* names: `x` (the parameter) and `n` (the outer `let n: u32 = 10;` binding declared one statement earlier in `main`). Rust's word for this is *capture* (Book at `output/docs/rust/book/ch13-01-closures.md:6-9` "Unlike functions, closures can capture values from the scope in which they're defined." Verified at lines 6-9.) Negative-contrast Probe 2 (`fnitem.rs`: `fn add_n(x: u32) -> u32 { x + n }` declared *inside* `main`, same body, replacing the closure binding): fires `error[E0434]: can't capture dynamic environment in a fn item` at line 3 column 35 (caret on the `n` token in the `fn` body), with `help: use the \`|| { ... }\` closure form instead`. The signature parses fine; rustc rejects the *body's mention* of `n`. The corpus `output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0434.md:21-23` puts the rule plainly: "Inner functions do not have access to their containing environment. To fix this error, you can replace the function with a closure." This is the load-bearing closure/`fn` asymmetry — Probe 1 and Probe 2 differ in one line (closure-on-let vs nested-fn-item), same body `x + n`, opposite outcomes. The Book section "Capturing References or Moving Ownership" at corpus lines 286+ (verified: heading at line 286, section runs to line 415, Listing 13-4 at lines 300-311 is the canonical immutable-capture example) names three capture modes — immutable borrow, mutable borrow, move — but today centers ONLY immutable capture. Side-probe A (`coexist.rs`): after the closure is defined and called, the outer `n` is still printable (`println!("{}", n)` prints `10`); witnesses that immutable capture does not consume the captured binding (Book lines 319-322 "list is still accessible from the code before the closure definition, after the closure definition but before the closure is called, and after the closure is called"). Side-probe B (`nobody.rs`: closure body does NOT mention `n`): compiles silently regardless; witnesses that capture is opt-in by mention, not automatic. Check-Yourself probe `q.rs` (closure form prints `36`) and `q-fn.rs` (nested fn fires E0434 on `g`) generalize the centered claim past the specific names used in Probe 1/2. Wholesale or named-deferred today: mutable capture (`let mut count = 0; let mut bump = || count += 1;` — future move); the `move` keyword (`move |...| ...` — deferred); capture mode classification (by-shared-ref / by-mut-ref / by-move per Book :286-292 "The closure will decide which of these to use based on what the body of the function does with the captured values" — closure sub-arc step 5); the `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` traits (steps 4-5); borrow-checker interactions (e.g. attempting to mutate the captured binding through the original name while the closure is alive — deferred); passing a capturing closure to a function (step 4); the `static`/`const` work-around for `fn` items at E0434.md:36-52 (deferred); the unused-variable warning that incidentally fires in Probe 2 on `n` (`#[warn(unused_variables)]`, separate lint subsystem — appears in transcript but not load-bearing); a top-level-fn alternative form (would fire E0425 not E0434 because at top level `n` doesn't exist anywhere; the prompt's nested-fn form is preferred precisely because it isolates the capture question — `n` is in scope but unreachable from the inner fn body). This is the THIRD move of the closure sub-arc per audit §6 step 3; gates closure sub-arc steps 4-5 (`FnMut`-bound parameter, `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` distinction) and the deferred mutable-capture / `move` / capture-mode-classification subtopics.
main_concept: a closure body can reference a name *from the enclosing scope* — Rust calls this *capturing* the binding — and a `fn` item declared at the same nesting level cannot. Three new facts today: (1) **A closure body may mention a name not declared inside the closure literal.** Lessons 142/143 wrote bodies that used only the closure's own parameter (`x + 1`, `x`); today's body `x + n` reaches outside to read `n` from the enclosing `fn main`. The closure literal does not declare `n`; rustc reaches into the surrounding lexical scope to resolve it (lesson 005 + 068's let-binding scope rule extended to inside the closure body). The Book at `output/docs/rust/book/ch13-01-closures.md:6-9` frames this directly: "Unlike functions, closures can capture values from the scope in which they're defined." (2) **A nested `fn` item declared in the same place CANNOT do this.** Probe 2 substitutes `fn add_n(x: u32) -> u32 { x + n }` for the closure with the same body `x + n`. Rustc fires `error[E0434]: can't capture dynamic environment in a fn item` with the caret pinpointing the `n` token in the `fn` body and a `help:` line "use the `|| { ... }` closure form instead". The corpus E0434.md:21-23 names the rule: "Inner functions do not have access to their containing environment. To fix this error, you can replace the function with a closure." This is the load-bearing closure/`fn` asymmetry — what makes a closure genuinely different from a `fn` item, not just a lighter syntax. (3) **Capture is opt-in by body mention.** Side-probe B (closure with body `|x: u32| x + 1`, no reference to `n`) compiles silently regardless of whether an outer `n` exists. Only closures whose body mentions an outer name capture it. Audience-level mental model installed: "closures and `fn` items are NOT the same form. A closure body may reference names from the enclosing scope (capture); a `fn` body may not. The capture is opt-in by writing the name in the body. The closure is the only one of the two that can do this." Operational corollaries today: (a) **The captured binding is not consumed.** Side-probe A: after the closure is defined and called, the outer `n` is still readable and printable. The Book at lines 319-322 makes the corresponding claim for Listing 13-4 ("list is still accessible ... after the closure is called"). The lesson does NOT centre this co-existence as a separate rule because it would preempt the deferred capture-mode classification (the `Copy` case for `u32` makes "still readable" trivially true; the more interesting cases for non-`Copy` types are deferred). (b) **The error code is `E0434`, distinct from lesson 005's E0425.** E0425 fires when no binding exists anywhere in scope; E0434 fires when a binding exists in the enclosing scope but a `fn` body cannot reach it. The codes are not interchangeable — E0425 means "missing"; E0434 means "out of reach for this kind of definition". (c) **The signature of the rejected `fn` parses cleanly.** Probe 2's `fn add_n(x: u32) -> u32` is well-formed; rustc only objects to the body's mention of `n`. (d) **The `help:` line proposes the closure form by name.** Rustc's suggested fix is `|| { ... }` — the closure literal — directly grounding the asymmetry from rustc's own mouth. Wholesale or named-deferred today: mutable capture (`let mut x = ...; let f = || x += 1;`) — future closure sub-arc move; the `move` keyword forcing ownership-by-move capture mode (Book :373-415, gates the cross-thread-closure use case from Listing 13-6) — deferred; capture mode classification by shared-ref / mut-ref / move (Book :286-292 names all three; today probes only the shared-ref case for a `Copy` type) — deferred to step 5; the `Fn` / `FnMut` / `FnOnce` traits that classify closures by capture mode (steps 4-5); borrow-checker interactions when the captured binding is borrowed mutably or moved into the closure — deferred; passing a capturing closure to a function with a generic `Fn`-bound parameter (step 4); the `static`/`const` work-around for `fn` items shown at E0434.md:36-52 — named in the lesson but deferred; the unused-variable warning that incidentally fires on `n` in Probe 2 (`#[warn(unused_variables)]` — separate lint subsystem); the `fn` item *as a value* (closures-as-values was lesson 142's centered fact; the `fn` form's value-vs-item distinction is named-deferred); the precise definition of "scope" the closure reaches into (lesson 068 installed `let`-binding scope; today's body inherits it). Unlock per audit §6: closure sub-arc steps 4 and 5. Once steps 4-5 install, the first closure-driven Iterator method (`for_each` or `map` per audit §4.4.1) becomes teachable.
depends_on: 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs the closure literal `|param: T| body` bound with `let` and called with parens. Today reuses the exact closure literal shape `|x: u32| body` from 142; the new fact relative to 142 is what the body is *allowed to mention*. 142's bodies referenced only the closure's own parameter; today's body `x + n` adds a reference to an outer-scope name. Today's negative contrast `fn add_n(x: u32) -> u32 { x + n }` versus the closure form is structurally the closest-possible substitution to lesson 142's working probe), 005-let-binding (load-bearing — installs `let name = value;` and the property "later statements in the same body can use the bound name". Today's outer `let n: u32 = 10;` is the binding the closure captures, and the closure binding `let add_n = |x: u32| x + n;` is a second `let`. The new fact relative to 005 is that the lesson-005 reach extends *inside* a closure literal's body, not just to outer-scope statements that mention the bound name), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. E0434 is a *new* error code today; the diagnostic shape is unchanged), 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs `fn name() { ... }` defined and `name();` called. Today's negative-contrast Probe 2 reuses the shape with one parameter and a return type, declared *inside* `main` instead of at file top level. The novel structural fact relative to 008 is *placement* — nested `fn` items parse but cannot capture), 020-function-with-parameter (load-bearing — installs `fn name(p: TYPE) { ... }` with one typed parameter. Probe 2's `fn add_n(x: u32) -> u32 { x + n }` reuses the exact `name: TYPE` parameter slot from 020. Lesson 020's hard rule "in function signatures parameter types are mandatory" carries; the contrast in lesson 142 was that closure parameter types are *optional* — different axis from today's contrast), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` is one of the integer types; today's outer binding `n: u32 = 10` and parameter `x: u32` both use it), 062-u32-unsigned-integer (cited — installs `u32` as an explicit type annotation form for an integer binding; today's `let n: u32 = 10;` reuses it), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (cited — `+` on two integer values produces a new integer; today's body `x + n` is one such expression. Both operands are `u32`), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", name)` formats with positional args; used in both probes), 021-function-return-value (cited — `fn name(p: T) -> R { ... }` declares a return type; Probe 2's `fn add_n(x: u32) -> u32 { x + n }` reuses the shape, but the `-> u32` is incidental to the centered fact — the rejection is the body, not the signature), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "closure mutable capture" moves (deferred subtopic — `let mut count = 0; let mut bump = || count += 1;`. Rules and diagnostics differ from today's immutable-read case; the closure binding itself needs `mut` and the captured local needs `mut` too, with borrow-checker interactions. Anchored at Book ch13-01-closures.md Listing 13-5 lines 340-372), future "closure capture mode classification" moves (closure sub-arc step 5 — by-shared-reference vs by-mutable-reference vs by-move; Book :288-292 names all three. Today centers ONLY the shared-ref case empirically; the classification itself is named-deferred), future "the `move` keyword on closures" moves (Book :373-415, Listing 13-6 — `move |...| ...` forces ownership transfer of captured values, useful for cross-thread closures. Deferred to closure sub-arc), future "`FnMut`-bound parameter on a function" moves (audit §6 step 4 — first exposure to `FnMut` trait bound on a generic; gates passing a capturing closure to a function. Local doc at `output/docs/rust/std/ops/trait.FnMut.md`), future "`Fn` / `FnMut` / `FnOnce` distinction" moves (audit §6 step 5 — the three traits that classify closures by capture mode. With this lesson installed the closure-arc prereqs are complete; the first closure-driven Iterator method becomes teachable), future "`Iterator::for_each` — `(self, F) where F: FnMut(Self::Item)`" moves (gated on closure arc; consumer with no return — simplest closure-driven Iterator method per audit §4.4.1; the closure may capture from the enclosing scope thanks to today's lesson), future "`Iterator::map`, `filter`, `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `inspect`, `find`, `find_map`, `position`, `rposition`, `all`, `any`, `fold`, `reduce`, `partition`" moves (gated on closure arc; all 27 closure-taking Iterator methods per audit §4.4.1. Today's lesson is what makes "the closure passed to .filter() can reference an outer threshold" make sense), future "borrow-checker interactions with captured bindings" moves (deferred subtopic — what happens if you try to mutate the captured binding through the original name while the closure is still alive; gates `let mut x = ...; let f = || x; x += 1; f();` style probes), future "the `static`/`const` work-around for `fn` items" moves (E0434.md:36-52 documents this — `static mut X` or `const Y` declared at the same level as the `fn` body can be reached from inside it. Named-deferred today)
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch13-01-closures.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0434.md

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 145-generic-function-type-parameter
move: extend lesson 008/020/021's `fn` declaration with a *type parameter* between angle brackets after the function name — `fn id<T>(t: T) -> T { t }` — and call the same declaration from two distinct call sites with different concrete types. Working probe `fn id<T>(t: T) -> T { t } fn main() { let a: u32 = id(5_u32); let b: i32 = id(7_i32); println!("{} {}", a, b); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `5 7` and exits 0; the centered contrast probe `nodecl.rs` (same source modulo dropping `<T>` from the declaration — `fn id(t: T) -> T { t }` instead of `fn id<T>(t: T) -> T { t }`, body and call sites unchanged) fires `error[E0425]: cannot find type \`T\` in this scope` *twice* — once with `--> nodecl.rs:1:10` (caret on the `T` in the parameter type slot `t: T`) and once with `--> nodecl.rs:1:16` (caret on the `T` in the return type slot `-> T`); each error carries an inline label `not found in this scope` and a `help: you might be missing a type parameter` block proposing the exact diff `fn id(t: T) -> T { t }` → `fn id<T>(t: T) -> T { t }` with three `+` markers under the inserted `<T>`; the diagnostic ends with `error: aborting due to 2 previous errors` and `--explain E0425` trailer; the auxiliary E0308 side-probe `mismatch.rs` (changes only the call site's annotation from `let a: u32 = id(5_u32);` to `let a: i32 = id(5_u32);`) fires `error[E0308]: mismatched types` with caret on `5_u32`, inline label `expected \`i32\`, found \`u32\``, plus a sub-help at column 18 reading "the return type of this call is \`u32\` due to the type of the argument passed" with the further label "this argument influences the return type of \`id\`" and a `note: function defined here` block pointing at the original declaration `fn id<T>(t: T) -> T { t }` — empirical witness for the per-call substitution mechanic with rustc's own words.
main_concept: a `fn` declaration may carry a *type parameter* between angle brackets after the function name (Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/items/functions.md:128-130` verbatim "A *generic function* allows one or more *parameterized types* to appear in its signature. Each type parameter must be explicitly declared in an angle-bracket-enclosed and comma-separated list, following the function name." plus the canonical example `fn foo<A, B>(x: A, y: B) {}` at lines 132-140); inside the function's signature and body, the parameter name is in scope and may sit in any type position (Reference `items/functions.md:144` verbatim "Inside the function signature and body, the name of the type parameter can be used as a type name"); each call site picks a concrete type for `T` independently — rustc instantiates `T` based on the context of the reference (Reference `items/functions.md:159-176` verbatim "When a generic function is referenced, its type is instantiated based on the context of the reference"); the same source declaration covers all call sites; rustc generates one specialized version per concrete substitution at compile time (Book `ch10-01-syntax.md:442-448` verbatim "Rust accomplishes this by performing monomorphization of the code using generics at compile time"). Three new tokens under one centered move. (1) *`<T>` after the function name*: declares one type parameter, scoped to the function's signature and body; placement is "between the name of the function and the parameter list" (Book `ch10-01-syntax.md:80-81` verbatim). (2) *`T` in the parameter type slot or the return type slot*: same `name: TYPE` and `-> RTYPE` slots from lessons 020/021, with the placeholder filling the type position. (3) *Per-call substitution at the call site*: at `id(5_u32)` rustc picks `T = u32`, at `id(7_i32)` rustc picks `T = i32`; the call-site context fixes the substitution. The audience crosses from "every type written inside a `fn` signature is a specific named concrete type" (lessons 020, 021, 080) to "a `fn` declaration may also carry a type parameter, and the same source declaration handles multiple concrete types via per-call substitution." Connection to lesson 114: lesson 114 installed the `<T>` slot on a *trait* header — between the trait name and the body. Today's `<T>` is on a *function* header — between the function name and the parameter list. Same angle-bracket grammar, different host item. Lesson 114's `move:` field in the graph entry verbatim names today as deferred: "generic functions `fn f<T>(t: T)` (distinct mechanic, same `<T>` slot)." Today opens that distinct mechanic. The convention is uppercase identifiers, often single-letter (Book `ch10-01-syntax.md:71-74` verbatim "we'll use `T` because, by convention, type parameter names in Rust are short, often just one letter"). The contrast probe drops the `<T>` declarator only; the body and call sites are unchanged. Without the declarator, `T` in the signature is a name out of scope; rustc fires E0425 — the same E-code lesson 005 installed for `cannot find value` and lesson 008 reused for `cannot find function`, with the noun rotating to `type` — and the `help: you might be missing a type parameter` block proposes the exact missing `<T>` diff. The auxiliary E0308 probe witnesses per-call substitution with rustc's own narration: at the mismatch call site, rustc's sub-help reads "the return type of this call is `u32` due to the type of the argument passed" — the substitution machinery made visible. Today is *shallow operational*: install only the smallest atomic move into the generic-function arc — one type parameter, one declaration, two call sites with different concrete types. Decomposition note: the closure sub-arc audit (`iterator-api-coverage.md` §6 step 4) sketches "FnMut-bound parameter on a function" as one move; in execution it is split into three lessons — today (generic function syntax), 146 (trait bound on a generic function parameter), 147 (the `FnMut(T) -> R` parenthesized sugar plus closure-as-argument). Explicitly defer trait bounds `<T: Trait>` and `where T: Trait` (lesson 146; without a bound the body can do almost nothing with `T` except move it around, which is exactly today's `id` body `{ t }`); the parenthesized `Fn(...)` / `FnMut(...) -> R` sugar (lesson 147); multiple type parameters `fn f<T, U>(...)` (same grammar slot extended); lifetime parameters `fn f<'a>(...)` (deferred wholesale); const generics `fn f<const N: usize>(...)`; generic struct types `struct Point<T> { x: T, y: T }` (same `<T>` slot on a struct header, separate mechanic, Book ch10-01 next subsection); turbofish `id::<u32>(5)` for explicit substitution at the call site (Reference `items/functions.md:178-181`); monomorphization at depth (code-bloat trade-off, generic function instantiation rules, Book ch10-01 lines 436-495 — named lightly today); generic methods inside an `impl` `impl<T> Point<T> { ... }` (composite of today with the trait-and-impl arc); trait objects `&dyn SomeTrait` (different machinery — dynamic dispatch — for "function takes a value of various types"; today's mechanic is static dispatch). This unlocks lesson 146 (trait bound on a generic function parameter — required because the body of a generic function with no bound can do almost nothing); lesson 147 (the `Fn(T) -> R` / `FnMut(T) -> R` parenthesized trait sugar plus closure-as-argument — completes the closure sub-arc); future `for_each` / `map` / `filter` / `filter_map` / `take_while` / `skip_while` / `map_while` / `scan` / `inspect` / `find` / `find_map` / `position` / `rposition` / `all` / `any` / `fold` / `reduce` / `partition` Iterator method moves (audit §4.4.1, all gated on the full closure-and-generic arc); future generic struct types `struct Point<T>` move (same `<T>` slot, different host); future generic methods inside an `impl<T>` move; future multi-parameter generics `fn f<T, U>` and the `where` clause form; future explicit-substitution / turbofish move.
depends_on: 008-define-and-call-function (load-bearing — installs `fn name() { ... }` defined and `name();` called; today extends the *header* grammar by inserting `<T>` between the function name and the opening `(`; the call shape `id(5_u32)` and `id(7_i32)` reuses 008's `name(arg);` form unchanged), 020-function-with-parameter (load-bearing — installs the `(p: TYPE)` parameter-list grammar and the rule that parameter types are mandatory in function signatures; today fills the `TYPE` slot with the type parameter `T`; lesson 020's hard rule extends — the `<T>` declarator must be present *before* `T` can sit in the type slot, otherwise rustc fires E0425; lesson 020's parse-error "if this is a parameter name, give it a type" is *not* what fires today because `t: T` is a syntactically complete parameter), 021-function-return-value (load-bearing — installs `-> RTYPE` as the return-type slot and `name(args)` as a value-carrying expression usable on the right of `let`; today fills `RTYPE` with `T` and uses both calls on the right of `let` with annotated bindings `let a: u32 = id(5_u32);` and `let b: i32 = id(7_i32);`), 114-generic-trait-parameter (load-bearing — installs the `<T>` slot on a *trait* header, with `T` in scope inside the trait body and usable in any type position; today is the *function* version of the same `<T>` slot — the "distinct mechanic, same `<T>` slot" that lesson 114's `move:` field named-deferred verbatim; the key conceptual difference from 114 is that the *call site* chooses the concrete substitution per call rather than the impl header choosing one substitution per impl block), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map; today's E0425 is read with the same map, including the `help:` block at the bottom proposing the exact diff that makes `T` resolvable), 081-integer-literal-forms (cited — installs the literal-suffix form `5_u32`, `7_i32` used at the call sites; the suffix fixes the argument type unambiguously), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` and `i32` are distinct named members of the integer family; today's lesson rests on their distinctness so the two call sites pick different concrete `T`), 005-let-binding (cited — `let a: u32 = id(5_u32);` and `let b: i32 = id(7_i32);` are the bindings that hold the call results), 025-implicit-return (cited — the body `{ t }` is a single-expression body that returns `t` without a `return` keyword; lesson 025's implicit-return form), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{} {}", a, b)` consumes two `{}` slots), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited — `fn main` is the entry point of the probe), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited — `rustc demo.rs && ./demo`, silent on success), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "trait bound on a generic function parameter — `fn f<T: SomeTrait>(...)` and the `where T: SomeTrait` form; required because the body of a generic function with no bound can do almost nothing with `T` except move it around; lesson 146 in the closure sub-arc decomposition" moves, future "`Fn(...)` / `FnMut(...) -> R` parenthesized trait sugar plus closure-as-argument — `fn apply<F: FnMut(u32) -> u32>(f: F, x: u32) -> u32`; lesson 147 in the closure sub-arc decomposition; completes the closure sub-arc and unlocks the first closure-driven Iterator method" moves, future "multiple type parameters on one function — `fn f<T, U>(t: T, u: U)`; same grammar slot extended with a comma; Reference `items/generics.md:10` `< ( GenericParam ( , GenericParam )* ,? )? >` is the formal grammar; the canonical example is the Book's `Point<X1, Y1>::mixup<X2, Y2>` at `ch10-01-syntax.md:394-417`" moves, future "the `where` clause form of trait bounds — `fn f<T>(t: T) where T: Display { ... }`; Reference `items/functions.md:148-156` shows this; same mechanic as the inline `<T: Display>` form; useful when bounds get long" moves, future "lifetime parameters `fn f<'a>(...)` and `fn f<'a, T>(...)` — explicit lifetimes on reference types in function signatures; Reference `items/generics.md:46` `fn foo<'a, T>() {}` is the formal example; deferred wholesale" moves, future "const generics `fn f<const N: usize>(...)` — generic over constant values rather than types; Reference `items/generics.md:66+`; deferred" moves, future "generic struct types `struct Point<T> { x: T, y: T }` — same `<T>` slot on a struct header instead of a function header; once installed, `Vec<T>` and `Option<T>` declarations in std become readable" moves, future "explicit substitution at the call site (turbofish) — `id::<u32>(5)`; Reference `items/functions.md:178-181`; the explicit form for cases where rustc cannot infer the substitution from arguments" moves, future "monomorphization at depth — code-bloat trade-off, generic function instantiation rules; Book `ch10-01-syntax.md:436-495` covers this; named lightly today, depth deferred" moves, future "generic methods inside an `impl<T>` block — `impl<T> Point<T> { fn x(&self) -> &T { ... } }`; composite of today's mechanic with the trait-and-impl arc from lessons 100/111-115; gates the rmp Add capstone" moves, future "trait objects `&dyn SomeTrait` — a different machinery (dynamic dispatch) for the same audience-level question 'function takes a value of various types'; today's mechanic is the static-dispatch alternative" moves, future "all 27 closure-taking Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `for_each`, `map`, `filter`, `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `inspect`, `find`, `find_map`, `position`, `rposition`, `all`, `any`, `fold`, `reduce`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_fold`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; all gated on the full closure-and-generic arc 145+146+147" moves

  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/generics.md
```

```text
id: 146-trait-bound-on-type-parameter
move: extend lesson 145's `fn name<T>(t: T)` with a *trait bound* on the type parameter — `fn say<T: std::fmt::Display>(t: T) { println!("{}", t); }` — and call the same declaration from two distinct call sites with different concrete types whose values `Display` accepts. Working probe `fn say<T: std::fmt::Display>(t: T) { println!("{}", t); } fn main() { say(5_u32); say(7_i32); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints two lines `5` and `7`, exit 0; the centered contrast probe `nobound.rs` (same source modulo dropping `: std::fmt::Display` from the angle brackets — `fn say<T>(t: T)` instead of `fn say<T: std::fmt::Display>(t: T)`, body and call sites unchanged) fires `error[E0277]: \`T\` doesn't implement \`std::fmt::Display\`` with `--> nobound.rs:2:20` (caret on `t` in `println!("{}", t)`), inline label `\`T\` cannot be formatted with the default formatter`, plus a `--` underline marking the `{}` placeholder as the requirement source ("required by this formatting parameter"), a sibling `= note: in format strings you may be able to use \`{:?}\` (or {:#?} for pretty-print) instead`, and the load-bearing `help: consider restricting type parameter \`T\` with trait \`Display\`` block proposing the exact diff `fn say<T: std::fmt::Display>(t: T)` with 19 `+` markers under the inserted `: std::fmt::Display` segment; the diagnostic ends with `--explain E0277` trailer; side-probes A and B (`extra.rs` shows the bound accepts `bool` and `char` alongside `u32`, all three implementing Display per std doc lines 163/167/223; `notdisplay.rs` passes a custom `struct Widget;` to `say(...)` and fires E0277 at the *call* site with `note: required by a bound in \`say\`` pointing at the bound itself in the function signature — symmetric contrast to Probe 2: Probe 2 = "no bound, body fails"; Side-probe B = "bound exists, call site fails"; together they witness both halves of the bound's two-things-at-once role).
main_concept: a `fn` declaration with a type parameter `<T>` may carry a *trait bound* — a colon and a trait path appended after the parameter name, inside the same angle brackets — and the bound does two things at once: (1) it restricts the call site so only types that implement the trait can be substituted for `T`, and (2) it grants the body the right to use the trait's methods/capabilities on a value of type `T`. Reference `output/docs/rust/reference/trait-bounds.md:38-50` is the formal source: line 40 verbatim "Bounds written after declaring a generic parameter: `fn f<A: Copy>() {}` is the same as `fn f<A>() where A: Copy {}`" — the inline `<T: Trait>` and the `where T: Trait` form are the same mechanic; line 48 verbatim "In the body of a generic function, methods from `Trait` can be called on `Ty` values" — the body-side enabling rule. Reference `items/generics.md:16` gives the formal grammar `TypeParam → IDENTIFIER ( : TypeParamBounds? )?` — the optional `: TypeParamBounds` slot today's lesson installs. Book `ch10-02-traits.md:384-397` is the textbook treatment: line 397 verbatim "We place trait bounds with the declaration of the generic type parameter after a colon and inside angle brackets." Today's body uses `println!("{}", t)` — the `{}` placeholder dispatches to `Display::fmt` (Display trait page `output/docs/rust/std/fmt/trait.Display.md:6-15`: line 8-9 give `fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_>) -> Result<(), Error>` as Display's required method; line 15 verbatim "Format trait for an empty format, `{}`." — the audience-level reading of the trait). The bound is what makes the placeholder legal for a value of generic type `T`; without it, rustc rejects the body at *definition* time (E0277.md:65-67 verbatim "the error here is in the definition of the generic function ... It must work with all possible input types"). Three new tokens under one centered move. (1) *`: TRAIT` after the type parameter name*: the trait bound, written inline inside the angle brackets after the parameter identifier and a colon. (2) *Body uses a method or capability that the trait provides*: today's body `println!("{}", t)` uses Display's empty-format placeholder; without the bound this body would fail. (3) *Per-call-site enforcement*: at each call rustc still substitutes the argument's concrete type for `T` (the lesson 145 mechanic, unchanged) AND now also verifies the substituted type satisfies the bound; non-implementors are rejected at the call site with E0277 plus a `note: required by a bound in <fn>` block (Side-probe B). The audience crosses from "a generic `fn` accepts any type for `T`; the body can do almost nothing with `t` except move it around (lesson 145's `id` body `{ t }`)" to "adding `: TRAIT` after the type parameter constrains `T` to types that implement `TRAIT`, and inside the body you can use `TRAIT`'s methods on `t`." E0277 is a *new* error code today; the diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged. The `help:` block proposes the exact missing bound — rustc proposes today's centered fix from rustc's own mouth. Today is *shallow operational*: one type parameter, one bound, one trait (Display), Probe 1 with two distinct concrete substitutions in the working probe. Decomposition note: this is the second move of the closure sub-arc step 4 decomposition (145 = generic function syntax; today = trait bound; 147 = the `Fn(...)` / `FnMut(...) -> R` parenthesized sugar plus closure-as-argument). Without today's lesson, lesson 147 cannot install the parenthesized `FnMut(T) -> R` form because that form is itself a special-case grammar for trait bounds whose target is one of the `Fn`-family traits; the inline `<T: Trait>` shape today installs is the prerequisite grammar. Explicitly defer: the `where` clause form (same mechanic, Reference `trait-bounds.md:40` verbatim equivalence; Book `ch10-02-traits.md:443-466` covers it); multiple bounds with `+` (`<T: A + B>`); multiple type parameters with separate bounds (`<T: A, U: B>`); the `Fn(...)` / `FnMut(...) -> R` parenthesized sugar (lesson 147); `use` declarations as a syntactic mechanic (today writes `std::fmt::Display` as a fully-qualified path inline; `use` is named-deferred in this run); Display's full surface (only that the bound makes `{}` work for `t: T`); other formatting traits (`Debug`/`{:?}`, `LowerHex`, `Binary`, ...); implementing `Display` for your own type (`impl Display for MyType`); trait objects `&dyn Trait` (different machinery, dynamic dispatch); generic struct types and generic methods inside `impl<T>` (same `<T: Trait>` slot on different host items); turbofish (`say::<u32>(5_u32)` for explicit substitution). This unlocks lesson 147 (the `Fn(T) -> R` / `FnMut(T) -> R` parenthesized trait sugar plus closure-as-argument — completes the closure sub-arc); future generic struct types `struct Point<T: Trait>` move; future multi-bound move; future `where` clause move; future closure-driven Iterator method moves (audit §4.4.1, all gated on the closure-and-generic arc completing at 147).
depends_on: 145-generic-function-type-parameter (load-bearing — installs `fn name<T>(t: T)` with the `<T>` slot between the function name and the parameter list, plus per-call substitution of concrete types for `T` at the call site. Today appends `: TRAIT` after `T` inside the same angle brackets; everything else carries unchanged. The new fact relative to 145 is that the body can now do more than move `t` around — with the bound it can use `TRAIT`'s methods on `t` — and the call site is now restricted to types satisfying the bound. Lesson 145 explicitly named today as deferred under `unlocks`: "trait bound on a generic function parameter — `fn f<T: SomeTrait>(...)` and the `where T: SomeTrait` form; required because the body of a generic function with no bound can do almost nothing with `T` except move it around; lesson 146 in the closure sub-arc decomposition"), 011-println-positional-args (load-bearing — installs `println!("{}", arg)`, the positional placeholder consumed by one extra argument left-to-right. Today's body is exactly this form. The `{}` placeholder is the call site of the `Display` trait — formatted output is what `Display` provides per Display page line 15 verbatim "Format trait for an empty format, `{}`." The trait bound `<T: std::fmt::Display>` is what makes the placeholder usable on a value of generic type `T`; Probe 2's E0277 diagnostic — caret on `t`, inline label `\`T\` cannot be formatted with the default formatter` — is rustc enforcing this requirement), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. E0277 is a *new* error code today; the diagnostic shape is unchanged. The new feature is the *kind* of fix the `help:` block proposes — a missing trait bound, written inline with `+` markers under the inserted text. The sibling `= note:` line proposing `{:?}` (the Debug placeholder) as an alternative is captured in the evidence appendix but not centered in the lesson body, because today's centered fact is "add the bound," not "switch placeholders"), 081-integer-literal-forms (cited — `5_u32`, `7_i32` literal-suffix forms used at Probe 1's two call sites to fix the argument types unambiguously without a separate `let`-binding indirection), 043-nested-module-paths (cited — the `std::fmt::Display` path reuses 043's `::`-separated multi-segment grammar; today's trailing segment is a trait name rather than a function name), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` and `i32` are distinct named integer types in the family. Today's lesson rests on both implementing `Display` so both call sites compile under the same bound), 020-function-with-parameter (cited — `(p: TYPE)` parameter slot. Today reuses `t: T`; the novelty is in the angle brackets, not the parameter list), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — call shape `name(arg);`. Today reuses it for `say(5_u32)` and `say(7_i32)`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the parenthesized `Fn(...)` / `FnMut(...) -> R` trait sugar plus closure-as-argument — `fn apply<F: FnMut(u32) -> u32>(f: F, x: u32) -> u32 { f(x) }`; lesson 147 in the closure sub-arc decomposition; the parenthesized form is itself a special-case grammar for the trait bound today's lesson installs, with `F: Fn(...)`/`F: FnMut(...) -> R` as a sugar over the underlying `F: Fn<(Args,)>` / `F: FnMut<(Args,), Output = R>` desugaring; once installed, closure-as-argument and the first closure-driven Iterator method become teachable; audit §6 step 4 closes after 147 lands" moves, future "the `where` clause form of trait bounds — `fn name<T>(t: T) where T: Trait { ... }`; same mechanic as today's inline form per Reference `trait-bounds.md:40` verbatim equivalence and Book `ch10-02-traits.md:443-466` 'Clearer Trait Bounds with where Clauses'; useful when bounds get long" moves, future "multiple bounds with `+` — `<T: A + B>` requires `T` to implement both `A` and `B`; same comma-and-plus grammar extended; Book `ch10-02-traits.md:423-441` covers it" moves, future "multiple type parameters with separate bounds — `<T: A, U: B>`; same comma-separated grammar with one bound segment per parameter" moves, future "trait objects `&dyn Trait` — different machinery (dynamic dispatch) for the same audience-level question 'function takes a value of various types implementing `Trait`'; today's mechanic is the static-dispatch alternative" moves, future "generic struct types with bounds — `struct Pair<T: Display> { ... }`; same `<T: Trait>` slot on a struct header instead of a function header" moves, future "generic methods inside an `impl<T: Trait>` block — composite of today with the trait-and-impl arc from lessons 100/111-115" moves, future "implementing `Display` for your own type — `impl std::fmt::Display for MyType { fn fmt(...) { ... } }`; bridges today's bound-side use of `Display` to the implementor side" moves, future "turbofish with bounds — `say::<u32>(5_u32)` for explicit substitution where rustc cannot infer from arguments" moves, future "all 27 closure-taking Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `for_each`, `map`, `filter`, `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `inspect`, `find`, `find_map`, `position`, `rposition`, `all`, `any`, `fold`, `reduce`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_fold`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; all gated on the full closure-and-generic arc 145+146+147" moves, future "the `?Sized` opt-out — `<T: ?Sized>` relaxes the implicit `Sized` bound; Reference `trait-bounds.md:113-116`; deferred wholesale" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/reference/trait-bounds.md
  - output/docs/rust/reference/items/generics.md
  - output/docs/rust/book/ch10-02-traits.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/fmt/trait.Display.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0277.md

  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/146-trait-bound-on-type-parameter.rs
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/146-trait-bound-on-type-parameter.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/146-trait-bound-on-type-parameter.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound
move: extend lesson 146's inline trait bound `<T: TRAIT>` with the *parenthesized* sugar specific to the `Fn`-family of traits — `<F: Fn(u32) -> u32>` — and pass a closure literal as the argument to a generic function carrying that bound. Working probe `fn apply<F: Fn(u32) -> u32>(f: F, x: u32) -> u32 { f(x) } fn main() { let add_one = |n: u32| n + 1; let r = apply(add_one, 5); println!("{}", r); }` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly `6`, exit 0; centered contrast Probe 2 `wrong_ret.rs` (same source modulo the closure body — `|n: u32| n as i32` instead of `|n: u32| n + 1`, so the closure's return type becomes `i32` rather than `u32`) fires `error[E0271]: expected \`{closure@wrong_ret.rs:6:23}\` to return \`u32\`, but it returns \`i32\`` with `--> wrong_ret.rs:6:32` (caret on the closure body `n as i32`), inline label `expected \`u32\`, found \`i32\``, plus a `note: required by a bound in \`apply\`` block at `--> wrong_ret.rs:1:24` whose caret falls on the *return slot* `u32` (three characters) inside the parenthesized bound `Fn(u32) -> u32` — the structural witness that rustc matches the closure's shape against the bound's parens-and-arrow shape segment by segment; Probe 3 `wrong_param.rs` (closure parameter changed to `i32`) fires `error[E0631]: type mismatch in closure arguments` with the `= note:` block reading verbatim `expected closure signature \`fn(u32) -> _\`` / `found closure signature \`fn(i32) -> _\`` — different E-code from Probe 2 because rustc surfaces parameter-shape mismatches with E0631 and return-shape mismatches with E0271; Probe 4 `non_closure_arg.rs` (call site passes `7` instead of a closure) fires `error[E0277]: expected a \`Fn(u32)\` closure, found \`{integer}\`` — same E-code as lesson 146 with rustc spelling the trait name in the parenthesized form; side-probes A (fully-qualified `std::ops::Fn(u32) -> u32` is equivalent — compile-exit=0), B/C (Check Yourself answers verified — `Fn(i64) -> i64` over `|n: i64| n * 3` prints 21; the same-bound-i32-return contrast fires E0271), and D (`FnMut(u32) -> u32` bound with the same body fires `error[E0596]: cannot borrow \`f\` as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` with `help: 1 | fn apply<F: FnMut(u32) -> u32>(mut f: F, x: u32) -> u32 {` and `+++` markers under the inserted `mut ` — the empirical witness that picking `FnMut` would force `mut`-on-a-function-parameter as a side-fact lessons 006/020/021 have not installed, justifying the lesson's choice of `Fn` over `FnMut`).
main_concept: a `fn` declaration carrying a type parameter `<F>` may bound `F` with the *parenthesized* sugar `Fn(T1, T2, ...) -> R` (or `FnMut(...)` or `FnOnce(...)`), where the parameter types of the callable live inside parens after the trait name and the return type follows `->`. This is a special-case grammar for trait bounds whose target is one of the `Fn`-family traits. The colon, the angle brackets, and per-call substitution (lesson 145 + 146) carry unchanged; the new fact is the parenthesized-arg form on the trait name. With this bound, the call site can pass any *closure literal* (lesson 142) whose parameter and return shape matches; the body can call the parameter with parens (lesson 008's call shape on the parameter name). The std-library `Fn` trait page at `output/docs/rust/std/ops/trait.Fn.md:45-47` verbatim names this: "Also of note is the special syntax for `Fn` traits (e.g. `Fn(usize, bool) -> usize`)." `FnMut.md:45-47` carries the identical sentence — the special syntax applies to the whole family. The canonical example at `Fn.md:60-68` is structurally identical to today's working probe: `fn call_with_one<F>(func: F) -> usize where F: Fn(usize) -> usize { func(1) } let double = |x| x * 2; assert_eq!(call_with_one(double), 2);` — generic function with one type parameter constrained by `Fn(T) -> R`, closure literal bound to `let`, closure passed as argument. The Reference's formal grammar at `output/docs/rust/reference/paths.md:193-197` is the formal source: `TypePathSegment → PathIdentSegment ( ::? ( GenericArgs | TypePathFn ) )?` admits *either* the angle-bracketed `GenericArgs` (the `<T, U>` form lesson 146 used) *or* the parenthesized `TypePathFn → ( TypePathFnInputs? ) ( -> TypeNoBounds )?` form (today). The Book at `ch13-01-closures.md:449-482` is the textbook treatment via `unwrap_or_else`'s signature `pub fn unwrap_or_else<F>(self, f: F) -> T where F: FnOnce() -> T` — same parens-and-arrow shape, on `FnOnce` rather than `Fn`, in the `where` form rather than the inline form; per Reference `trait-bounds.md:38-42` (lesson 146's evidence) the inline and `where` forms are the same mechanic, and today's choice of `Fn` over `FnOnce` is part of the same family-of-traits picture lesson 148 will install. Three new tokens under one centered move. (1) *Parens after the trait name*: `Fn(...)` instead of just `Fn`, with the parameter types of the callable inside. (2) *`-> R` after the parens*: the return type of the callable. The Reference grammar makes both `( inputs )` and the `->` segment optional in the formal sense — `Fn()` is a valid no-input bound, and `Fn(T)` is a valid no-return bound (return type defaults to `()`); today uses both with one parameter for surface minimality. (3) *Closure literal as an argument to a generic function*: lesson 142 installed closure literals bound to `let` and called locally; today lifts that exact value across a function boundary, with the parenthesized bound naming what the receiving function expects. The audience crosses from "trait bounds use the inline `: Trait` form, with type arguments inside angle brackets" (lesson 146) to "for the `Fn` family of traits there is a *parenthesized* bound form `: Fn(T) -> R` that carries the parameter and return types of the callable inside parens, instead of inside angle brackets — and once a function carries this bound, you can pass any closure literal of matching shape as the argument." This is the syntax all 27 closure-taking Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) use. E0271 (return-type mismatch) and E0631 (parameter-type mismatch) are *new* error codes today; E0277 reappears with a new payload format that spells the trait name in the parenthesized form. The diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged; the new feature is the *kind* of mismatch surfaced — closure shape vs bound shape, segment by segment, with `note: required by a bound in <fn>` pointing at the offending segment of the parenthesized bound inside the function declaration. Today is *shallow operational*: one type parameter, one Fn-family trait (`Fn`), one parameter inside the parens, one return type, Probe 1 with one call site. Decomposition note: this is the third move of the closure sub-arc step 4 decomposition (145 = generic function syntax; 146 = trait bound; today = parenthesized Fn-family bound + closure-as-argument). After today, audit §6 step 4 closes; lesson 148 (Fn/FnMut/FnOnce distinction = audit §6 step 5) closes the closure sub-arc entirely, after which the 27 closure-taking Iterator methods become teachable. Choice of `Fn` over `FnMut`: empirical (Side-probe D — `FnMut` requires `mut f: F`; rustc fires E0596 without `mut` and `help:` proposes `mut f: F`). Picking `FnMut` would compound `mut`-on-a-function-parameter as a side-fact; lessons 006/020/021 installed `let mut` and parameter slots but not their composition. Lesson 148 will install the Fn/FnMut/FnOnce distinction and the `mut f: F` consequence together, motivated by capture rules. The std-library `FnMut.md:65-66` corpus example itself writes `fn do_twice<F>(mut func: F) where F: FnMut()` — corpus precedent for the `mut func: F` necessity. Explicitly defer: the `Fn`/`FnMut`/`FnOnce` distinction (lesson 148; today uses only `Fn`); closure capture rules (lesson 148); Iterator methods that take closures (post-148); function pointers `fn(u32) -> u32` (lowercase `fn`, a *type* not a trait, separate mechanic); `impl Fn(...)` and `Box<dyn Fn(...)>` / `&dyn Fn(...)` (sugar and dynamic dispatch alternatives); multiple parameters `Fn(T, U) -> R` (same mechanic, comma-separated; `Fn.md:45-47` example uses two parameters); no-return form `Fn(T)` with implicit `()` return (same mechanic, return arrow omitted; `FnMut.md:65-66` corpus example uses `FnMut()`); higher-ranked trait bounds `for<'a> Fn(&'a T) -> R` (Reference `trait-bounds.md:144-195`; deferred wholesale); the desugaring `Fn(T) -> R` ≡ `Fn<(T,), Output = R>` (implementor-side; named in Fn.md's link to the Rustonomicon). This unlocks lesson 148 (the Fn/FnMut/FnOnce distinction, closing the closure sub-arc); future `for_each` / `map` / `filter` / `filter_map` / `take_while` / `skip_while` / `map_while` / `scan` / `inspect` / `find` / `find_map` / `position` / `rposition` / `all` / `any` / `fold` / `reduce` / `partition` / `flat_map` / `try_fold` / `try_for_each` / `max_by` / `max_by_key` / `min_by` / `min_by_key` / `is_sorted_by` / `is_sorted_by_key` Iterator method moves (audit §4.4.1, all gated on lesson 148); future multi-parameter parenthesized-bound move; future no-return parenthesized-bound move; future `impl Fn(...)` parameter-position sugar move; future `Box<dyn Fn(...)>` / `&dyn Fn(...)` dynamic-dispatch move; future function-pointer type `fn(u32) -> u32` move (separate mechanic); future higher-ranked trait bound `for<'a> Fn(&'a T) -> R` move.
depends_on: 146-trait-bound-on-type-parameter (load-bearing — installs the inline trait-bound shape `<T: TRAIT>`: a colon and a trait path appended after the type parameter name, inside the same angle brackets, with the dual effect "restricts call site to types implementing TRAIT, enables body to use TRAIT's methods/capabilities on T-values." Today extends this exact shape with one new fact: the trait name may be followed by *parens* and an optional `-> R`. The colon-and-angle-brackets placement and per-call substitution carry unchanged. The lesson body's "same colon, same angle brackets" sentence references 146's `<T: std::fmt::Display>` directly. Without 146, today's `<F: Fn(u32) -> u32>` shape has no prerequisite grammar to extend), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (load-bearing — installs `fn name<T>(t: T)` with the `<T>` slot on the function header and per-call substitution at the call site. Today fills the type parameter slot with `F` and the bound slot with the parenthesized form; the per-call substitution at `apply(add_one, 5)` picks the closure's anonymous type for `F`), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs the closure literal `|param: T| body` bound to `let` and called locally with parens. Today's argument `let add_one = |n: u32| n + 1; apply(add_one, 5)` is exactly this shape; the closure's parameter type and return value are what rustc matches against the bound's parens and `-> R`. Without 142 the audience cannot type the argument), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. E0271 (Probe 2) and E0631 (Probe 3) are *new* error codes today; E0277 reappears (Probe 4) with a new payload that spells the trait name in the parenthesized form `Fn(u32)`. The four-part shape is unchanged; the new feature is the *kind* of mismatch surfaced — closure shape vs bound shape, segment by segment, with `note: required by a bound in <fn>` pointing at the offending segment of the parenthesized bound), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — call shape `name(arg);` reused twice today: `f(x)` in the body and `apply(add_one, 5)` at the call site. The body's parens-call shape on the parameter name `f` is what the bound *enables*), 020-function-with-parameter (cited — `(p: TYPE)` parameter-list grammar; today fills it with `f: F` and `x: u32`. The novelty is in the angle brackets, not the parameter list), 021-function-return-value (cited — `-> RTYPE` return-type slot. Today fills it with `-> u32`; the parenthesized bound's `-> u32` segment is structurally similar but lives inside the trait bound, not on the function signature itself), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32`, `i32`, `i64` as distinct named integer types; today's working probe uses `u32`, the contrast probes mix `u32` with `i32`, the Check Yourself uses `i64`. The bare `5` at the call site is inferred to `u32` from the function's `x: u32` parameter slot — no literal suffix needed), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", r)` to print the result), 005-let-binding (cited — `let add_one = ...` and `let r = ...`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Fn` / `FnMut` / `FnOnce` distinction — three traits in the family with different rules on call-once-vs-repeated and capture mutation; lesson 148, the closure sub-arc closer (audit §6 step 5); installs together with the `mut f: F` consequence for `FnMut`-bound parameters (Side-probe D witnesses why) and the capture-rules-determine-which-trait-the-closure-implements rule (Book `ch13-01-closures.md:431-447`); after 148 lands, audit §6 closes and the 27 closure-taking Iterator methods become teachable" moves, future "all 27 closure-taking Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `for_each`, `map`, `filter`, `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `inspect`, `find`, `find_map`, `position`, `rposition`, `all`, `any`, `fold`, `reduce`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_fold`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; all gated on lesson 148; each method's bound uses today's parenthesized form, with one of the three Fn-family traits picked based on what the method needs to do with the closure" moves, future "multiple parameters on the parenthesized bound — `Fn(T, U) -> R`; same mechanic extended with comma; Reference `paths.md:197` formal grammar `TypePathFnInputs → Type ( , Type )* ,?` admits this; `Fn.md:45-47` example uses two parameters" moves, future "no-return form `Fn(T)` with implicit `()` return — same mechanic, return arrow omitted; ```text
```
```text
id: 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction
move: extend lesson 147's single-trait `<F: Fn(T) -> R>` parenthesized bound with the *three-trait Fn-family distinction* — `Fn`, `FnMut`, `FnOnce` — and the supertrait relationship that layers them. Working probe declares three call helpers `fn call_fn<F: Fn(u32) -> u32>(f: F, x: u32) -> u32 { f(x) }`, `fn call_fnmut<F: FnMut(u32) -> u32>(mut f: F, x: u32) -> u32 { f(x) }`, `fn call_fnonce<F: FnOnce(u32) -> u32>(f: F, x: u32) -> u32 { f(x) }`, and three closures matching one trait each: `let pure = |n: u32| n + 1` (captures nothing → Fn), `let mut counter: u32 = 0; let tick = |n: u32| { counter += n; counter }` (mutates capture → FnMut), `let s = String::from("hello"); let consume = move |_: u32| { drop(s); 0_u32 }` (moves capture out → FnOnce); compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0) and run as `./demo` prints exactly three lines `Fn:     6` / `FnMut:  5` / `FnOnce: 0`, exit 0; centered contrast Probe 2 `wrong_trait.rs` (pass `tick` to `call_fn` instead of `call_fnmut`) fires `error[E0525]: expected a closure that implements the \`Fn\` trait, but this closure only implements \`FnMut\`` with `--> wrong_trait.rs:10:16` (caret on the closure literal `|n: u32| { counter += n; counter }`), an annotated subspan on the captured `counter` token labelled `closure is \`FnMut\` because it mutates the variable \`counter\` here`, plus `note: required by a bound in \`call_fn\`` block at `--> wrong_trait.rs:4:15` whose caret falls on the entire `Fn(u32) -> u32` bound segment of the function declaration; centered contrast Probe 3 `no_mut.rs` (drop the `mut` from `call_fnmut`'s parameter slot, leaving `f: F`) fires `error[E0596]: cannot borrow \`f\` as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` with caret on the body's `f(x)` and `help: consider changing this to be mutable` block proposing `mut f: F` with `+++` markers under the inserted `mut ` (same shape as lesson 006's E0384 `+++` markers but at function-parameter position, not `let` position); supertrait Probe 4 `supertrait.rs` (pass `pure` (Fn) to all three call helpers; pass `tick` (FnMut) to `call_fnmut` and `call_fnonce`) compiles silently and prints five lines confirming the corpus claim from `Fn.md:33-35` and `FnMut.md:33-35` empirically; complementary contrast Probe 5 `once_to_mut.rs` (pass `consume` (FnOnce-only) to `call_fnmut`) fires E0525 with payload `expected ... \`FnMut\`, ... only implements \`FnOnce\`` and annotated subspan naming `s` as the variable moved out; structural Probe 6 `once_twice.rs` (call `f(x)` twice in `call_fnonce`'s body) fires `error[E0382]: use of moved value: \`f\`` with `note: \`FnOnce\` closures can only be called once` — rustc's verbatim restatement of the once-only rule; side-probe 7 `no_move.rs` (drop the `move` keyword from `consume`) still fires E0525 with the same FnOnce-only payload, witnessing that the body's `drop(s)` (not the `move` keyword) is what forces FnOnce; Check Yourself Probe 9 `q_a.rs` (counter-mutating closure passed to a Fn-bounded function — `let mut total: u32 = 100; let bump = |n: u32| { total += n; total }; call_fn(bump, 7)`) fires E0525 with `total` named as the captured-and-mutated variable; Check Yourself Probe 10 `q_b.rs` (same closure, bound changed to `FnMut(u32) -> u32` and parameter to `mut f: F`) compiles silently and prints `107`; side-probe 8 `check_yourself.rs` (Vec-pushing closure passed to a Fn-bounded function — `let mut v: Vec<u32> = Vec::new(); let push_n = |n: u32| { v.push(n); v.len() as u32 }`) fires the same E0525 with `v` named, witnessing that the mechanism is type-agnostic across captured types but not used in the lesson body's Check Yourself because `Vec::new()` and `.push` are uninstalled mechanics.
main_concept: there are *three* traits in the Fn-family — `Fn`, `FnMut`, `FnOnce` — and they form a layered supertrait hierarchy. The std-library trait headers `output/docs/rust/std/ops/trait.Fn.md:7` (verbatim `pub trait Fn<Args>: FnMut<Args>`), `trait.FnMut.md:7` (verbatim `pub trait FnMut<Args>: FnOnce<Args>`), and `trait.FnOnce.md:7-17` (no supertrait, declares `type Output;`) are the formal source. The `:` after each trait name names a *supertrait*: every type implementing `Fn` also implements `FnMut` and `FnOnce`; every `FnMut` also implements `FnOnce`. Three structural consequences. (1) *Receiver shape*: each trait's required method has a different `self` shape — `Fn::call(&self, args)` takes a shared borrow, `FnMut::call_mut(&mut self, args)` takes a mutable borrow, `FnOnce::call_once(self, args)` takes self by value (verified at `Fn.md:14`, `FnMut.md:14-17`, `FnOnce.md:16`). The receiver shape determines what calling the closure does: shared borrow can repeat without state changes, mutable borrow can repeat with state changes, by-value consumes the closure. (2) *Auto-implementation rule based on capture mode*: a closure auto-implements one or more of the three traits based on what its body does with captured values. The Book at `ch13-01-closures.md:431-447` packages this rule in a three-bullet summary — "in an additive fashion" (verbatim line 434) is the Book's word for the supertrait layering. The trait pages corroborate: `Fn.md:27` verbatim "implemented automatically by closures which only take immutable references to captured variables or don't capture anything at all"; `FnMut.md:27` verbatim "implemented automatically by closures which take mutable references to captured variables"; `FnOnce.md:28` verbatim "implemented automatically by closures that might consume captured variables." So: capture-nothing or shared-ref-read → Fn (and so all three); mutate captures → FnMut + FnOnce; move captures out → FnOnce only. The body's actions decide; the `move` keyword forces ownership transfer at capture time but is not itself the FnOnce-trigger (Probe 7 witnesses this — the body's `drop(s)` is what forces FnOnce regardless of `move`). (3) *Bound-side acceptance rule*: `Fn.md:33-35` verbatim "any instance of `Fn` can be used as a parameter where a `FnMut` or `FnOnce` is expected"; `FnMut.md:33-35` verbatim same with FnOnce; so a `<F: FnOnce(...)>` bound accepts any closure (most permissive), `<F: FnMut(...)>` accepts FnMut and Fn closures, `<F: Fn(...)>` accepts only Fn closures (strictest). Plus a *side-fact* required for FnMut bounds: when a generic function carries `<F: FnMut(...)>` and the body calls `f(x)`, the parameter binding *must* be declared `mut` (i.e. `mut f: F`, not `f: F`). The structural reason is that calling a FnMut closure invokes `call_mut(&mut self, ...)`, which takes a mutable borrow of `f`; without `mut` on the parameter binding, the body's `f(x)` cannot acquire that borrow. rustc fires `error[E0596]: cannot borrow \`f\` as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` with `+++` markers proposing the missing `mut`. The std-library FnMut page at `FnMut.md:65-66` itself writes `fn do_twice<F>(mut func: F) where F: FnMut()` — corpus precedent for the `mut func: F` form. This composes lesson 006 (`let mut x = ...` reassignable binding) with lesson 020's parameter slot `f: F`: the same `mut` keyword, slotted into a parameter position instead of a `let` position. The audience crosses from "there is one Fn trait used in the parenthesized-bound form (lesson 147)" to "there are three Fn-family traits, layered as supertraits; a closure auto-implements one or more based on what its body does with captures (read / mutate / move-out); the bound on the receiving function constrains which closures are accepted; FnMut bounds need `mut f: F` so the body can call `f(x)`." E0525 is *new* today (closure trait mismatch); E0596 reappears (lesson 131 first witnessed it on `&mut self` for `next()`); E0382 reappears (lesson 133 first witnessed it on `count(self)` consuming the iterator) with payload now tying the move to the trait via `note: \`FnOnce\` closures can only be called once`. The diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged. Today is the *closure sub-arc closer* — audit §6 step 5 (`iterator-api-coverage.md:313-365`). After this lesson lands, the closure sub-arc prereqs are complete; the 27 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1: `for_each`, `map`, `filter`, `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `inspect`, `find`, `find_map`, `position`, `rposition`, `all`, `any`, `fold`, `reduce`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_fold`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`) become teachable. Likely first: `for_each` (consumer, `FnMut(Self::Item)`, no return — simplest closure-driven Iterator method) or `map` (lazy adapter, `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B`). Today is *shallow operational*: three traits, three closures, one parameter inside the parens, one return type, supertrait relation read off the trait headers, side-fact grounded in Probe 3's E0596. Decomposition note: the orchestrator's prompt anticipated lesson 148 might be slightly larger than usual since it's a sub-arc closer; today's lesson body lands at ~1250 learner-facing words, slightly over the 1200 target but with a clear reason (three traits + supertrait + auto-impl rule + side-fact, all centered as today's single move). Explicitly defer: the `move` keyword as its own mechanic (used lightly today; Book `ch13-01-closures.md:286+` covers it; full thread/closure ownership pattern is a separate move); the desugaring `Fn(T) -> R` ≡ `Fn<(T,), Output = R>` (implementor-side; FnOnce.md declares `type Output;`); `impl Fn(...)` parameter or return position (separate sugar); `Box<dyn Fn(...)>` / `&dyn Fn(...)` (dynamic dispatch alternative); function pointers `fn(u32) -> u32` (lowercase `fn`, a *type* not a trait, separate mechanic; non-capturing closures coerce to it but capturing closures do not); higher-ranked trait bounds `for<'a> Fn(&'a T) -> R` (deferred wholesale); AsyncFn / AsyncFnMut / AsyncFnOnce (async-closure family, deferred wholesale); multiple parameters `Fn(T, U) -> R` and no-return form `Fn(T)` with implicit `()` return (same mechanic extended); the std-library implementations on `&F` / `&mut F` / `Box<F>` (each Fn-family trait page lists implementors). This unlocks: future `for_each` move (likely first closure-driven Iterator method); future `map` move; the remaining 25 closure-driven Iterator methods enumerated above; future `move` keyword move; future `impl Fn(...)` parameter or return position move; future `Box<dyn Fn(...)>` / `&dyn Fn(...)` dynamic-dispatch move; future function-pointer type `fn(u32) -> u32` move; future higher-ranked trait bound `for<'a> Fn(&'a T) -> R` move; future async-closure family move; future multi-parameter parenthesized-bound move; future no-return parenthesized-bound move.
depends_on: 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the `<F: Fn(T) -> R>` parenthesized trait bound and closure-as-argument. Today reuses that exact grammar three times, once with each of `Fn`, `FnMut`, `FnOnce`. The colon, the angle brackets, the parenthesized argument list, the optional `-> R`, and per-call substitution all carry unchanged. The three new facts relative to 147 are: there are three Fn-family traits not just one, the traits are layered as supertraits, and the auto-implementation rule maps capture mode to trait. Lesson 147 explicitly named today as deferred under `unlocks`: "the `Fn` / `FnMut` / `FnOnce` distinction — three traits in the family with different rules on call-once-vs-repeated and capture mutation; lesson 148, the closure sub-arc closer (audit §6 step 5)"), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (load-bearing — installs the closure-capture mechanic. Today's centered fact is which of the three traits a closure auto-implements based on *how* the body uses what it captured (read, mutate, or move). Lesson 144's `add_n` closure read its captured `n` by shared reference — implements Fn. Today's `tick` closure mutates its captured `counter` — implements FnMut. Today's `consume` closure moves its captured `s` out — implements FnOnce. Lesson 144's *What To Ignore For Now* explicitly named today as "Capture mode classification — by shared reference vs mutable reference vs move ... Closure sub-arc steps 4-5"), 006-mut-binding (load-bearing — installs `let mut name = value;` as the reassignable-binding form. Today extends `mut` to a *function parameter* slot: `mut f: F` is the same `mut` keyword, slotted into lesson 020's parameter position. The probe also uses lesson 006's binding form directly: `let mut counter: u32 = 0;` is exactly lesson 006's shape. Probe 3 (drop the `mut`) fires E0596 with `+++` markers proposing the missing `mut` — same `+++`-marker shape as lesson 006's E0384 contrast, but at function-parameter position. Lesson 144's *What To Ignore For Now* named "Mutable capture — `let mut count = 0; let mut bump = || count += 1;`" as deferred to the closure sub-arc; today is where the composition lands), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. E0525 is *new* today (closure trait mismatch on the bound side); E0596 reappears at function-parameter position (lesson 131 first witnessed it on `&mut self` for `next()`); E0382 reappears with the closure-specific payload `note: \`FnOnce\` closures can only be called once` (lesson 133 first witnessed it on `count(self)` consuming the iterator). The four-part diagnostic shape is unchanged), 146-trait-bound-on-type-parameter (cited — installs the inline trait-bound shape `<T: TRAIT>` and the dual call-site-restriction / body-enabling effect; today reuses the same colon-and-angle-brackets shape three times with each of the Fn-family traits), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — installs `fn name<T>(t: T)`; today reuses the `<F>` slot three times), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (cited — installs the closure literal `|param: T| body` bound to `let` and called locally with parens. Today's three closures `pure`, `tick`, `consume` are all closure literals), 042-string-new (cited — `String::from("hello")` for the FnOnce probe, which needs a non-`Copy` value so `drop(s)` actually moves `s`. Lesson 042 installed `String::new()`; today's `String::from("hello")` is the same `Type::function(args)` shape with one argument instead of zero. Lesson 042 explicitly named `String::from` in its *What To Ignore For Now* section as a deferred alternative constructor; today's use is the smallest application), 081-integer-literal-forms (cited — `0_u32` literal suffix form in the FnOnce probe's body provides a `u32` return value that matches the `Fn(u32) -> u32` shape of `call_fnonce`'s bound), 094-unused-must-use-result (cited — the `_` in `move |_: u32|` (lesson body line 39 plus Probes 5/6/7) reuses lesson 094's `_` wildcard meaning at a closure-parameter host: the parameter slot exists but the body doesn't use it), 020-function-with-parameter (cited — `f: F` parameter slot. Today extends with `mut f: F` for the FnMut bound), 008-define-and-call-function (cited — `f(x)` parens-call shape reused inside all three call helpers' bodies), 080-integer-type-family (cited — `u32` named integer type), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", value)`), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
```
