```text
id: 149-iterator-for-each
move: call `Iterator::for_each` on a `Range<u32>` with one closure literal as the argument — `(1..4_u32).for_each(|x| println!("{}", x));` plus a second call `let mut sum: u32 = 0; (1..4_u32).for_each(|x| sum += x); println!("sum = {}", sum);` showing the bound is `FnMut` (capture-mutating closure accepted). Source choice is `Range<u32>` (lesson 091 `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` + lesson 081 `4_u32` integer-suffix form + lesson 080 u32) so the iterator yields owned `u32` elements — `Self::Item = u32`, no `&u32`, no AddAssign-on-reference, no deref-read. Working probe `demo.rs` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0); `./demo` prints four lines `1` / `2` / `3` / `sum = 6`, exit 0; centered contrast Probe 2 `break_in_closure.rs` (replace the first closure body with `if x == 2 { break; } println!("{}", x);` — `x == 2` is plain `u32 == u32` per lesson 013, no deref) fires `error[E0267]: \`break\` inside of a closure` with `--> break_in_closure.rs:7:13`, source excerpt with `--- enclosing closure` annotation pointing at `|x|`, caret on the offending `break` token, inline label `cannot \`break\` inside of a closure` — empirical witness for the std-doc claim "`break` and `continue` are not possible from a closure" (`trait.Iterator.md:905-906`); Probe 3 `non_unit_bind.rs` (`let r: u32 = (1..4_u32).for_each(|x| println!("{}", x));`) fires E0308 with `expected \`u32\`, found \`()\`` — direct empirical witness that `for_each` returns `()`; Probe 4 `non_closure_arg.rs` (pass `7` instead of a closure to `(1..4_u32).for_each`) fires E0277 with rustc spelling the expected type as `FnMut(u32)` — direct empirical witness that `Self::Item` resolves to `u32` (owned) for `Range<u32>`; Probe 5 `for_each_consumes.rs` (bind `let it = 1..4_u32;` then call `it.for_each(...)` then `it.count()`) fires E0382 with rustc spelling the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>` and `note:` reading verbatim "`for_each` takes ownership of the receiver `self`" — direct empirical witness for the consuming-`self` receiver and for `1..4_u32` being a `Range<u32>` value; Probe 6 `q.rs` (`(1..6_u32).for_each(|x| if x % 2 == 0 { count += 1; })` with `let mut count: u32 = 0;` — `x % 2` is plain `u32 % u32` per lesson 037, `count += 1` is plain `u32 += u32` per lesson 023) and Probe 7 `q_no_mut.rs` are the Check Yourself probes (Probe 6 compiles silently and prints `2`; Probe 7 fires E0594 with `+++` markers proposing `let mut count: u32 = 0;` at the outer binding site — composes lesson 006's mutable-binding rule with today's captured-mutation mechanic); Probe 8 `type_pin.rs` (annotate the closure parameter `|x: u32|` explicitly) compiles silently and prints the same three lines as Probe 1's first call — empirical witness that the closure parameter type is `u32` (owned, not `&u32`).
main_concept: `Iterator::for_each` is the simplest closure-driven Iterator method on the trait. The signature, verbatim from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:902`, reads `fn for_each<F>(self, f: F) where Self: Sized, F: FnMut(Self::Item),`. Three structural facts. (1) *Receiver `self` (consuming)*: like lessons 133/134's `count(self)` and `last(self)`, `for_each` takes the iterator by value; the iterator binding cannot be used after the call (Probe 5's E0382 with `note:` "takes ownership of the receiver `self`" is the structural witness). (2) *No return-type slot*: `for_each` returns `()`; Probe 3's E0308 (`expected u32, found ()`) is the structural witness. This makes `for_each` a *non-short-circuiting* consumer — every element is visited, no value is produced for the caller to inspect. (3) *Closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item)`*: composes lesson 147's parenthesized bound grammar with lesson 148's `FnMut` trait choice and lesson 132's `Self::Item` assoc-type slot. For `Range<u32>` (lesson 091's `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step`), `Self::Item = u32` — the iterator yields owned values — so rustc spells the bound `FnMut(u32)` in diagnostics (Probe 4 verbatim). The choice of `FnMut` over `Fn` is what licenses Probe 1's second call `|x| sum += x` — a closure mutating a captured binding implements `FnMut` (lesson 148's auto-impl rule), not `Fn`. The semantics, per `trait.Iterator.md:904-906` verbatim, are "Calls a closure on each element of an iterator. This is equivalent to using a `for` loop on the iterator, although `break` and `continue` are not possible from a closure." Probe 2 empirically witnesses the no-`break` rule: E0267 with `--- enclosing closure` annotation pointing at `|x|` and caret on `break` — the closure body is its own scope, not a loop body. The audience crosses from "I have closures, parenthesized-bound generic functions, and three iterator-method shapes (`.next()`, lazy adapters, consumers); I have not yet called an Iterator method that takes a closure" (lesson 148 closer) to "`for_each` is the leanest closure-driven Iterator method: consume `self`, take one `FnMut(Self::Item)` closure, call it once per element, return `()`. The bound is `FnMut` so the closure can mutate captured state across calls. The closure body is not a loop body — `break`/`continue` do not reach into the surrounding code." E0267 is *new* today; E0277 reappears with a payload spelling the trait in the parenthesized form `FnMut(u32)`; E0308 reappears with payload `expected <T>, found ()` confirming the unit return; E0382 reappears with payload tying the move to `for_each`'s `self` receiver and the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>`; E0594 reappears in the Check Yourself probe (lesson 006 + 144 composition — captured binding still has to be `mut`). The diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged. Today is *shallow operational*: one closure-driven Iterator method, one trait (`FnMut`), no wrapper struct return, no element-type rewriting. Source choice — `Range<u32>` rather than `Vec::iter()` — is deliberate: `Range<u32>` yields owned `u32`, so the accumulator probe `sum += x` is plain `u32 += u32` per lesson 023 (not `u32 += &u32` invoking `impl AddAssign<&u32> for u32`, which is trait machinery lesson 023 deferred), and the body comparisons `x == 2` and `x % 2 == 0` are plain integer operations per lessons 013/037 (not `*x == 2` requiring deref-read, which lessons 047/048 deferred). Stabilized at 1.21.0 (`trait.Iterator.md:901`); local toolchain 1.95.0. Decomposition note: this is the first move of the closure-driven Iterator methods sub-arc (audit §4.4.1, 27 methods total). `for_each` is named first because per audit §7 (lines 417-446) it is the leanest of the 27: consumes `self` (no `Self::Item` rewriting like `map`), takes exactly one closure, returns `()`, and the closure bound is `FnMut(Self::Item)` — the simplest possible parenthesized bound for an Iterator method (no `&Self::Item` like `filter`, no `-> B` like `map`). Likely next moves per audit §4.4.1: `map` (lazy adapter, `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B`, returns `Map<Self, F>` — rewrites yielded element type, composes today's mechanic with lesson 138's enumerate frame), or `filter` (lazy adapter, `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool`, returns `Filter<Self, P>` — surfaces the *double-reference* situation rustc warns about at `trait.Iterator.md:962-967`). Explicitly defer: internal-iteration / `Chain` performance note (`trait.Iterator.md:909-911`, implementor-side); `try_for_each` (Try sub-arc, audit §4.4.4); `where Self: Sized` (still); `for x in (1..4_u32)` desugaring (gates on IntoIterator sub-arc); the other 26 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1, each its own move); `v.iter()` and `v.into_iter()` source shapes (the former composes a `Self::Item = &u32` resolution, the latter gates on the IntoIterator sub-arc).
depends_on: 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction (load-bearing — installs the Fn-family three-trait distinction with the layered supertrait shape `Fn: FnMut: FnOnce` and the auto-implementation rule mapping closure-body actions to traits. Today's bound is `FnMut`; the second probe's `|x| sum += x` mutates a captured binding and so implements `FnMut` per lesson 148's rule. Without 148, the audience cannot read the bound `FnMut(Self::Item)` segment-by-segment or understand why this specific trait — not `Fn` — is the right choice for an Iterator method called once per element. Lesson 148's *unlocks* explicitly named today: "the `Iterator::for_each` consumer with `FnMut(Self::Item)` bound — likely first closure-driven Iterator method"), 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the parenthesized form `<F: FnMut(T) -> R>`. Today's bound `FnMut(Self::Item)` is exactly this grammar in the no-return-segment form (return defaults to `()`). The lesson body's "Read it segment by segment" walk applies lesson 147's grammar to `for_each`'s declaration line. Without 147 the audience cannot read `FnMut(Self::Item)` as a parenthesized bound rather than a function type or method call), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (load-bearing — installs the closure-capture mechanic. The second probe's closure body `|x| sum += x` captures `let mut sum` from the enclosing scope and mutates it. Lesson 144's *What To Ignore For Now* named "Mutable capture — `let mut count = 0; let mut bump = || count += 1;`" as deferred to the closure sub-arc. Lesson 148 already installed the structural classification; today is the first place in the run where capture-and-mutate composes with an Iterator method bound), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs the closure literal `|param: T| body` and parens-call shape. Today the closure is the *argument* to `for_each` rather than bound to `let` and called locally; the literal grammar `|x| println!("{}", x)` and `|x| sum += x` is otherwise lesson 142 unchanged), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait synopsis and the `Self::Item` associated-type slot. Today's bound `FnMut(Self::Item)` reads the closure's parameter type from this slot. Lesson 132's *unlocks* named the 75 provided methods as future moves; today closes one of those slots), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` (091's evidence quotes `output/docs/rust/std/ops/struct.Range.md:139-141` verbatim) and the parens-rule for method calls on a range value (`(1..4).rev()` — parens force the range to be the receiver, otherwise rustc tries `4.rev()` first and emits E0689). Today reuses both: `1..4_u32` is a `Range<u32>` (Probe 5 empirical: rustc spells the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>`), and `(1..4_u32).for_each(...)` is the parens-rule applied to a different method name. Replaces the slice-iter source shape that earlier draft used), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — installs the `4_u32` type-suffix form. Today's `1..4_u32` makes the upper bound a `u32` literal without a separate annotation, fixing the range to `Range<u32>` directly. Without 081 the audience would need a separate `let upper: u32 = 4;` line), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs the twelve integer type names. Today's `u32` is one of them; combined with 081 and 091 this fixes `Self::Item = u32` for the iterator), 023-compound-add-assign (load-bearing — installs `n += value;` shorthand for `n = n + value;` on a `let mut`-declared integer binding. Today's `sum += x` and `count += 1` are exactly this shape with both sides owned `u32` values — pure lesson 023 mechanic, no AddAssign-on-reference trait machinery (which lesson 023's *What To Ignore For Now* explicitly deferred). Source-shape choice (`Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()`) ensures `x: u32` so this stays inside lesson 023's installed mechanic), 013-comparison-operators (cited — installs `==` between two integer values producing a boolean. Today's `x == 2` (Probe 2 body) and `x % 2 == 0` (Check Yourself) are plain integer-vs-integer-literal comparisons. With `x: u32` (owned), no deref needed), 037-remainder-operator (cited — installs `%` between two integer values. Today's Check Yourself uses `x % 2` where both sides are `u32`. Lesson 037's *unlocks* explicitly named "future `even/odd checks via `n % 2 == 0`` moves" — today's Check Yourself realizes that exact shape inside an iterator closure body), 143-unannotated-closure-first-use (cited — installs the no-annotation grammar `|x|`. Today's two probe closures both use this form; the type-binding mechanism today is structurally different — rustc reads the expected closure shape from the bound `FnMut(Self::Item)` = `FnMut(u32)`, so `x` is fixed to `u32` from the bound rather than a first call site. Probe 8 confirms by accepting the explicit `|x: u32|` annotation), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. E0267 is *new* today (Probe 2 — `break` inside closure); reappearing with the lesson 003 shape: E0277 (Probe 4) with payload spelling the trait in parenthesized form `FnMut(u32)`; E0308 (Probe 3) with payload `expected u32, found ()`; E0382 (Probe 5) with payload tying the move to `for_each`'s `self` receiver and the moved-value type to `std::ops::Range<u32>`; E0594 (Probe 7, Check Yourself) — captured binding not declared `mut`), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — `<F>` generic slot in the signature), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", x)` formats an owned `u32` directly), 006-mut-binding (cited — `let mut sum: u32 = 0;` and `let mut count: u32 = 0;`; the Check Yourself contrast (Probe 7) demonstrates that even with `FnMut` bound the captured binding still must be `mut`), 005-let-binding (cited), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Iterator::map` lazy adapter with `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B` bound — likely second closure-driven Iterator method; lazy because it returns `Map<Self, F>` rather than consuming; rewrites yielded element type via the closure; composes today's mechanic with lesson 138's enumerate-shape adapter framing; std doc at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:38-43` synopsis-box and main entry" moves, future "the `Iterator::filter` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` bound — surfaces the *double-reference* situation `trait.Iterator.md:962-967` warns about (closure receives `&Self::Item`, which for slice-iter is `&&T`); returns `Filter<Self, P>`" moves, future "the remaining 25 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `inspect`, `find`, `find_map`, `position`, `rposition`, `all`, `any`, `fold`, `reduce`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_fold`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; each method's bound uses lesson 147's parenthesized form with one of the three Fn-family traits (lesson 148) picked based on what the method needs to do with the closure" moves, future "`try_for_each` — short-circuiting variant of `for_each` gated on the `Try` trait sub-arc (audit §4.4.4); same closure shape but different return type" moves, future "the `for x in (1..4_u32)` desugaring — std doc says `for_each` is 'equivalent to using a `for` loop'; the exact desugaring pulls in `IntoIterator` (audit §4.4.2)" moves, future "`Iterator::for_each` with `v.iter()` source — composes today's mechanic with lesson 131's `Self::Item = &T` resolution; bound becomes `FnMut(&u32)`; closure body needs deref-read (lessons 047/048) or reference-RHS arithmetic to operate on the element values" moves, future "`Iterator::for_each` with `into_iter()` source — `vec![1,2,3].into_iter().for_each(...)`; gates on the IntoIterator sub-arc; closure receives `T` rather than `&T`" moves, future "`Iterator::for_each` performance via internal iteration on `Chain` and similar adapters — implementor-side note from `trait.Iterator.md:909-911`" moves, future "intermediate iterator-pipeline capstone — `(1..N_u32).filter(...).map(...).for_each(...)` end-to-end after 2-4 closure-driven methods land (audit §7)" moves

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 150-iterator-map
move: call `Iterator::map` on a `Range<u32>` with one closure literal that rewrites each owned `u32` element to a new owned `u32`, then chain the result into `Iterator::for_each` (lesson 149) to consume — `(1..4_u32).map(|x| x * 10).for_each(|y| println!("{}", y));`. Working probe `demo.rs` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0); `./demo` prints three lines `10` / `20` / `30`, exit 0. Lazy-witness Probe 2 `lazy.rs` (closure body contains `println!("called: {}", x)`, wrapper bound to `let _m` and never consumed) compiles silently and prints just `end` — empirical witness that `.map(...)` does not call the closure on any element when nothing iterates the wrapper. Probe 3 `lazy_consume.rs` (same closure body, `.for_each(|y| println!("got: {}", y))` chained on) compiles silently and prints six interleaved lines (`called: 1` / `got: 10` / `called: 2` / `got: 20` / `called: 3` / `got: 30` / `end`) — empirical witness for pull-style one-element-at-a-time iteration through the wrapper. Probe 4 `expr.rs` (bare expression-statement form `(1..4_u32).map(|x| x * 10);`) emits `warning: unused \`Map\` that must be used` with `note: iterators are lazy and do nothing unless consumed` and `help: use \`let _ = ...\`` proposing the silencing form — direct empirical witness that the corpus claim at `trait.Iterator.md:867,887-892` ("as `map()` is lazy ... it won't even execute, as it is lazy. Rust will warn you about this") is delivered by rustc itself; this is the first `must_use` lint the run has surfaced. Probe 5 `non_unit_bind.rs` (`let r: u32 = (1..4_u32).map(|x| x * 10);`) fires E0308 with rustc spelling the actual return type as `Map<std::ops::Range<u32>, {closure@non_unit_bind.rs:6:33: 6:36}>` — direct empirical witness that `map` returns a wrapper struct named `Map<I, F>` parameterized by the source iterator's type and the closure's anonymous type. Probe 6 `non_closure_arg.rs` (pass `7` instead of a closure to `(1..4_u32).map(7)`) fires E0277 with rustc spelling the expected type as `FnMut(u32)` — direct empirical witness that `Self::Item = u32` for `Range<u32>` and the bound's parens segment resolves to `FnMut(u32)`. Probe 7 `map_consumes.rs` (`let it = 1..4_u32; let _m = it.map(|x| x * 10); let _again = it.count();`) fires E0382 with rustc spelling the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>` and `note:` reading verbatim "`map` takes ownership of the receiver `self`, which moves `it`" — direct empirical witness for `map`'s consuming `self` receiver, with rustc spelling `map` by name. Probe 8 `type_pin.rs` (annotate both closure parameter and return type explicitly: `|x: u32| -> u32 { x * 10 }`) compiles silently and prints the same three lines — empirical witness that the closure parameter is `u32` (owned) and the closure return type is `u32` (so `B = u32`). Check Yourself Probe 9 `q.rs` (`(1..5_u32).map(|n| n + 100).for_each(|y| println!("{}", y))`) compiles silently and prints four lines `101` / `102` / `103` / `104` — exclusive upper bound rule (lesson 091/022) means `5` is not yielded. Side-probe 10 `no_parens.rs` (drop the parens around the range) fires E0689 — rehearses lesson 091's parens-rule with `.map(...)` rotated in for `.rev()`.
main_concept: `Iterator::map` is the first *lazy closure-driven* Iterator adapter in the run. The signature, verbatim from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:852`, reads `fn map<B, F>(self, f: F) -> Map<Self, F> where Self: Sized, F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> B,`. Five structural facts under one centered move. (1) *Two type parameters*: `<B, F>` — `F` for the closure's anonymous type (same as `for_each`), `B` for the closure's *return type*. Today exercises lesson 145's `<F>` slot extended to two slots in the same angle brackets; `B` is purely inferred from the closure's body. (2) *Receiver `self` (consuming)*: like lessons 133/134/149's `count(self)`, `last(self)`, `for_each(self)`, `map` takes the iterator by value (Probe 7's E0382 with `note:` "`map` takes ownership of the receiver `self`" is the structural witness). (3) *Returns a wrapper struct*: `-> Map<Self, F>` makes `map` an *adapter* (lesson 136-140 family) rather than a consumer. The wrapper has two type parameters (today's first wrapper with two), parameterized by the source iterator's type and the closure's anonymous type. The corpus declaration at `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Map.md:7` reads verbatim `pub struct Map<I, F> { /* private fields */ }` — private fields are implementor-side, deferred per lesson 136-140 discipline. (4) *Closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B`*: composes lesson 147's parenthesized bound grammar (with both the parens segment and the `-> R` segment exercised at once for the first time on a closure-driven Iterator method — lesson 149's `for_each` had `FnMut(Self::Item)` with no arrow), lesson 148's `FnMut` trait choice, and lesson 132's `Self::Item` slot. For `Range<u32>` (lesson 091 + 081), `Self::Item = u32`; today's closure `|x| x * 10` returns `u32` so `B = u32`. (5) *Wrapper itself implements `Iterator`*: from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Map.md:150-154` verbatim `impl<B, I, F> Iterator for Map<I, F> where I: Iterator, F: FnMut(<I as Iterator>::Item) -> B,` followed by `type Item = B`. The wrapper's `Item` is `B` — the closure's return type — so the yielded element type is whatever the closure returns. This composes lesson 138's enumerate-frame ("yielded element shape can be rewritten by an adapter" — then by tuple-wrapping, today by closure-return-type) and explains why chaining `.for_each(|y| ...)` onto `.map(...)` works (the wrapper is an iterator; `y` is `B = u32`). The semantics, per `trait.Iterator.md:854-855` verbatim, are "Takes a closure and creates an iterator which calls that closure on each element"; per `trait.Iterator.md:867` verbatim "`map()` is lazy" and lines 887-892 verbatim "it won't even execute, as it is lazy. Rust will warn you about this." Probe 2's empty output and Probe 3's interleaved output are the empirical witnesses for the lazy + pull-style framing; Probe 4's `must_use` lint surfaces the corpus claim verbatim ("iterators are lazy and do nothing unless consumed"). The audience crosses from "I have one closure-driven Iterator method (`for_each`) — it consumes `self`, returns `()`, calls the closure once per element" (lesson 149) to "I have two closure-driven Iterator methods. `map` is the *lazy adapter* form: takes one `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B` closure, returns a `Map<Self, F>` wrapper struct that itself implements `Iterator` with `Item = B`, and does not call the closure until something iterates the wrapper." E0308 reappears today with payload spelling the wrapper-struct return type verbatim; E0277 reappears with payload `FnMut(u32)` (matches lesson 149 Probe 4); E0382 reappears with payload tying the move to `map`'s `self` receiver and the moved-value type to `std::ops::Range<u32>`. The diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged. Today is *shallow operational*: one closure-driven adapter, one trait (`FnMut`), one wrapper struct, B = u32 (so no new type enters the picture), no closure capture (today's closures capture nothing — lesson 144 cited but not load-bearing). Source choice — `Range<u32>` rather than `Vec::iter()` — carries lesson 149's discipline forward: `Range<u32>` yields owned `u32`, so the closure parameter is `u32` (not `&u32`), the body uses plain integer multiplication (`*`, lesson 009) and addition (`+`, lesson 009) on owned `u32` values, no AddAssign-on-reference, no deref-read, no `as` casting (which lesson 081 named-deferred for integer-to-integer direction). Stabilized at 1.0.0 (`trait.Iterator.md:850`); local toolchain 1.95.0. Decomposition note: this is the second move of the closure-driven Iterator methods sub-arc (audit §4.4.1, 27 methods total). Picked second per audit §7's leanness ordering — `for_each` first because no `Self::Item` rewriting and no wrapper struct; `map` second because the rewriting and the wrapper are the smallest non-trivial extension. Likely next moves per audit §4.4.1: `filter` (lazy adapter, `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool`, returns `Filter<Self, P>` — surfaces the *double-reference* situation `trait.Iterator.md:962-967` warns about), `inspect` (lazy version of `for_each`, `FnMut(&Self::Item)` no return, returns `Inspect<Self, F>`), or `find`/`position`/`any`/`all` (short-circuiting consumers with predicate-shape closures). The audit's "intermediate iterator-pipeline capstone" (§7) becomes more plausible once `filter` lands — a chain `(1..N_u32).filter(...).map(...).for_each(...)` would then read end-to-end in the audience's installed mechanics. Explicitly defer: `Map<I, F>` internal fields and other trait impls (`DoubleEndedIterator`, `ExactSizeIterator`, `FusedIterator`, `TrustedLen` — `struct.Map.md` lines 88, 132, 688, 692, all implementor-side); `flat_map` (gates on IntoIterator, audit §4.4.2); `map_while` (separate method, `trait.Iterator.md:1274`); `filter_map` (separate method, `trait.Iterator.md:1004`); the "iterator chain" pipeline as a centered concept (defer to future capstone); the `Notes about side effects` section of `struct.Map.md` (lines 17-56, implementor-side); `map` with a closure that returns a different type from the source's `Self::Item` (today's body keeps `B = u32` for surface minimality; the mechanic is the same); `v.iter()` / `v.into_iter()` source shapes (each composes a different `Self::Item` resolution); the unused-must-use attribute itself as a separate mechanic (today's lesson uses the warning's payload but does not install `#[must_use]` as a centered concept); the closure's anonymous type as a nameable thing (`{closure@FILE:L:C: L:C}` in Probe 5's diagnostic — implementor-side rendering, not a teachable type form).
depends_on: 149-iterator-for-each (load-bearing — installs `Iterator::for_each` as the first closure-driven Iterator method: `self` consuming, `FnMut(Self::Item)` bound, returns `()`. Today uses `for_each` as the *consumer at the end of the chain* — `.map(...).for_each(|y| ...)` is the full Probe 1 line. Without 149, the audience cannot consume the `Map` wrapper to observe its yielded values. Lesson 149's *unlocks* explicitly named today: "the `Iterator::map` lazy adapter with `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B` bound — likely second closure-driven Iterator method; lazy because it returns `Map<Self, F>` rather than consuming; rewrites yielded element type via the closure"), 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction (load-bearing — installs the Fn-family three-trait distinction with the layered supertrait shape `Fn: FnMut: FnOnce` and the auto-implementation rule mapping closure-body actions to traits. Today's bound is `FnMut`; today's closures `|x| x * 10` and `|n| n + 100` capture nothing and so implement `Fn` (and so all three traits per the supertrait relation). The bound `FnMut` accepts them via the supertrait acceptance rule. Without 148, the audience cannot read the bound's `FnMut` segment as one of three Fn-family traits), 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the parenthesized form `<F: FnMut(T) -> R>` with both the parens segment and the optional `-> R` return-type segment. Today's bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B` exercises both segments at once for the first time on a closure-driven Iterator method — lesson 149's `for_each` had `FnMut(Self::Item)` with no arrow. The lesson body's "Read it segment by segment" walk applies lesson 147's grammar to today's signature line. Without 147 the audience cannot read `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B` as one parenthesized bound), 138-iterator-enumerate (load-bearing — installs the frame "yielded element shape can be rewritten by an adapter." Lesson 138's `Enumerate<Self>` rewrote `Self::Item = T` to `Item = (usize, T)` — wrapping in a tuple. Today's rewrite is *closure-driven* and chooses any type `B` per the closure's return; the wrapper's `Item` is whatever the closure returns. The lesson body's "yielded element type is whatever the closure returns" bullet is exactly this composition. Without 138 the audience cannot read today's `B` segment as a *yielded-element-type rewrite*), 136-iterator-take (load-bearing — installs the wrapper-struct frame: `.take(n)` returns a fresh iterator value (a `Take<Self>` struct), not a number / `Option` / `()`. Lessons 137-140 extended the family with single-parameter wrappers `Skip<Self>`, `Enumerate<Self>`, `Fuse<Self>`, `StepBy<Self>`. Today's `Map<Self, F>` is the same family with two type parameters instead of one (Probe 5's E0308 spells the wrapper as `Map<std::ops::Range<u32>, {closure@...}>` — direct empirical witness)), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait synopsis and the `Self::Item` associated-type slot. Today's bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> B` reads the closure's parameter type from this slot; today's wrapper's impl reads the closure's return type into the wrapper's own `Item = B`), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` (091's evidence quotes `output/docs/rust/std/ops/struct.Range.md:139-141` verbatim) and the parens-rule for method calls on a range value. Today's `(1..4_u32).map(...)` reuses both: the source is a `Range<u32>` (Probe 7 empirical: rustc spells the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>`), and the parens-rule keeps the range as the receiver (Probe 10 empirical: dropping the parens fires E0689)), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — installs the `4_u32` and `5_u32` type-suffix forms used at the upper bound of the working probe and Check Yourself, fixing the range to `Range<u32>` directly. Without 081 the audience would need a separate `let upper: u32 = 4;` line), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs the twelve integer type names. Today's `u32` is one of them; combined with 081 and 091 this fixes `Self::Item = u32`. The closure return type `B = u32` reuses the same name), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (load-bearing — installs `+ - * /` between two integer values producing a new integer value. Today's closure body `x * 10` is `u32 * u32` producing `u32`; the Check Yourself body `n + 100` is `u32 + u32` producing `u32`. No new arithmetic mechanic; no `as` cast (lesson 034 installed only `i32 as f64`; lesson 081 named integer-to-integer `as` as deferred). Source-shape choice (`Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()`) ensures both sides of `*` and `+` are owned `u32` values — pure lesson 009 mechanic, no AddAssign-on-reference, no deref-read), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. No new error codes today; reappearing with the lesson 003 shape: E0308 (Probe 5) with the most informative `found struct \`Map<std::ops::Range<u32>, {closure@...}>\`` payload yet seen in the run; E0277 (Probe 6) with payload `FnMut(u32)` matching lesson 149 Probe 4; E0382 (Probe 7) with payload tying the move to `map`'s `self` receiver. New top-level diagnostic *kind* today: a `must_use` lint warning (Probe 4) with the same four-part shape as an error but headline `warning:` instead of `error[E####]:` — the lint surface introduced today), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — `<F>` slot, today extended trivially to `<B, F>` with two type parameters in the same angle brackets), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (cited — closure literal grammar), 143-unannotated-closure-first-use (cited — `|x|` no-annotation form; type from bound), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (cited — capture mechanic, today's closures capture nothing so 144 is named but not load-bearing), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", x)`), 005-let-binding (cited — `let _m = ...`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Iterator::filter` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` bound — surfaces the *double-reference* situation `trait.Iterator.md:962-967` warns about (closure receives `&Self::Item`, which for slice-iter is `&&T`); returns `Filter<Self, P>`; with this method, an end-to-end pipeline `.filter(...).map(...).for_each(...)` becomes teachable" moves, future "the `Iterator::inspect` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item)` bound (no return) — like `for_each` but lazy; returns `Inspect<Self, F>`; lighter than `filter` because no element-type rewrite, no predicate decision" moves, future "short-circuiting consumers with predicate-shape closures — `find` (`FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool`, returns `Option<Self::Item>`), `position` (returns `Option<usize>`), `any` (returns `bool`), `all` (returns `bool`); first place the audience meets short-circuit semantics on closure-driven Iterator methods" moves, future "the `Iterator::fold` consumer with `FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> B` bound — *two-parameter* parenthesized bound (the next sub-step beyond today's one-parameter form); accumulator pattern; takes an initial accumulator value plus a closure; returns the final accumulator" moves, future "the remaining 23 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `find_map`, `rposition`, `reduce`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_fold`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; each composes today's wrapper-or-consumer + closure-bound mechanic with method-specific shape" moves, future "iterator-pipeline capstone — `(1..N_u32).filter(...).map(...).for_each(...)` end-to-end after `filter` lands; audit §7 names this as the natural integration point" moves, future "`map` with a closure that returns a different type from `Self::Item` — e.g. `u32 → bool` via `|x| x % 2 == 0`, or `u32 → (u32, u32)` via tuple construction; the mechanic is the same as today's `B = u32`, but now `B` differs from `Self::Item` empirically. Probably needs lesson 138's tuple shape to be re-exercisable" moves, future "the `Map<I, F>` wrapper's other trait impls — `DoubleEndedIterator` (`struct.Map.md:88`), `ExactSizeIterator` (`struct.Map.md:132`), `FusedIterator` (`struct.Map.md:688`), `TrustedLen` (`struct.Map.md:692`); each its own move composing with the corresponding upstream trait" moves, future "the `#[must_use]` attribute as a separate mechanic — today's Probe 4 surfaces the attribute's effect (the warning + lazy-framing note) but `#[must_use]` itself is a separate language feature; future move covers when to apply it, what types/functions carry it in std, etc." moves, future "side effects under `.rev()` on a `Map` wrapper — `struct.Map.md:17-56` corpus example shows the closure's mutated-state ordering can surprise; gated on a future `move`-keyword + capture-state lesson plus `DoubleEndedIterator::rev()` semantics on adapters" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/struct.Map.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/ops/struct.Range.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/ops/trait.FnMut.md

  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0308.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0382.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0689.md

status: accepted
```

```text
id: 151-iterator-fold
move: call `Iterator::fold` on a `Range<u32>` with an initial accumulator value and a two-parameter closure that threads the accumulator through the iteration — `let s = (1..4_u32).fold(0_u32, |acc, x| acc + x); println!("{}", s);`. Working probe `demo.rs` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0); `./demo` prints `6`, exit 0. Probe 2 `init_offset.rs` (`init = 100_u32` with explicit `|acc: u32, x: u32|` annotations) compiles silently and prints `106` — empirical witness that `init` is genuinely the value `acc` starts at, plus that the explicit-annotation form composes lesson 020's typed-parameter shape twice. Probe 3 `wrong_arity.rs` (one-parameter closure `|x| x` where the bound expects two) fires `error[E0593]: closure is expected to take 2 arguments, but it takes 1 argument` — *new* error code today; the diagnostic has two annotations on the same source line (`^^^^` under the method name labelled `expected closure that takes 2 arguments`, `---` under the closure literal labelled `takes 1 argument`). Probe 4 `init_type_mismatch.rs` (`init = 0_i32` against `(1..4_u32)`-yielding source) fires both E0308 (`expected i32, found u32`) and E0277 (`cannot add u32 to i32`) — direct empirical witness that `init`'s type fixes `B`, and that closure parameter types and return type must agree with `B`. Probe 5 `non_closure_arg.rs` (pass `7` instead of a closure to `(1..4_u32).fold(0_u32, 7)`) fires E0277 with rustc spelling the expected closure type as `FnMut(u32, u32)` — direct empirical witness for the *two-parameter* parenthesized bound shape, both slots visible in source order. Probe 6 `fold_consumes.rs` (bind `let it = 1..4_u32;` then call `it.fold(...)` then `it.count()`) fires E0382 with rustc spelling the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>` and `note:` reading verbatim "`fold` takes ownership of the receiver `self`, which moves `it`" — direct empirical witness for `fold`'s consuming `self` receiver, with rustc spelling `fold` by name. Check Yourself Probe 7 `q.rs` (`(1..4_u32).fold(1_u32, |acc, x| acc * x)`) compiles silently and prints `6` — multiplicative fold starting from the multiplicative identity; iteration walks `1*1=1`, `1*2=2`, `2*3=6`. Source choice: `Range<u32>` (lesson 091 + 080 + 081) yields owned `u32` elements, so the closure parameters after `B = u32` from `init` are both `u32` (no `&u32`, no deref-read). Closure bodies are plain `u32 + u32` and `u32 * u32` per lesson 009; no AddAssign-on-reference, no `as` cast.
main_concept: `Iterator::fold` is the third closure-driven Iterator method in the run, and the *first multi-parameter* one. The signature, verbatim from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:2365`, reads `fn fold<B, F>(self, init: B, f: F) -> B where Self: Sized, F: FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> B,`. Five structural facts under one centered move. (1) *Two type parameters*: `<B, F>` — same shape as `map` (lesson 150). `F` for the closure's anonymous type, `B` for the accumulator type. (2) *Receiver `self` (consuming) plus two non-receiver arguments*: `(self, init: B, f: F)` — the *first* Iterator method in the run with a non-self argument list of length two. (3) *Returns `B`*: `-> B`, not `()` (lesson 149) and not a wrapper struct (lesson 150). The return type is whatever the accumulator type is. Lesson 025's implicit-return rule composes naturally — the closure body's last expression *is* its return value, which feeds back into `B`. (4) *Closure bound `FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> B`*: composes lesson 147's parenthesized bound grammar (with both the parens segment and the `-> R` segment), lesson 148's `FnMut` trait choice, and lesson 132's `Self::Item` slot. The novel sub-fact today is *inside the parens*: **two parameter slots, comma-separated**, instead of one. This is the closure analogue of lesson 036's comma-separated function parameter list — same `,` separator, same positional matching. First slot is `B` (accumulator), second slot is `Self::Item` (element). (5) *Threading semantics*: rustc starts with `init` as the accumulator, and on each step calls `f(acc, next_element)` with the *return value of the prior call* (or `init` for the first call) as the first argument. After all elements are consumed, the final accumulator is `fold`'s return value. The corpus prose at `trait.Iterator.md:2370-2378` is the source: "`fold()` takes two arguments: an initial value, and a closure with two arguments: an 'accumulator', and an element. The closure returns the value that the accumulator should have for the next iteration. The initial value is the value the accumulator will have on the first call. After applying this closure to every element of the iterator, `fold()` returns the accumulator." The corpus walkthrough table at `trait.Iterator.md:2421-2427` mirrors the lesson body's iteration table for Probe 1's `(1..4_u32).fold(0_u32, |acc, x| acc + x)`: step 1 `(0, 1) → 1`, step 2 `(1, 2) → 3`, step 3 `(3, 3) → 6`. The audience crosses from "I have two closure-driven Iterator methods (`for_each`, `map`); their closures take *one* parameter (the element)" (lesson 150) to "Some Iterator methods take an initial accumulator value plus a closure with **two** parameters — accumulator and element, comma-separated inside the bars. `fold` is the canonical one. It consumes `self`, threads the accumulator through (each closure call's return becomes the next call's first argument), and returns the final accumulator." E0593 is *new* today (Probe 3 — one-parameter closure passed where a two-parameter closure is expected); E0308 reappears with payload tying the body's value to `B` (Probe 4); E0277 reappears in two payloads — one with `cannot add u32 to i32` (Probe 4) and one with the load-bearing `expected an FnMut(u32, u32) closure, found {integer}` (Probe 5) — direct empirical witness for the *two-parameter* parenthesized bound shape; E0382 reappears with payload tying the move to `fold`'s `self` receiver. The diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged. Today is *shallow operational*: one closure-driven consumer with multi-parameter closure, one trait (`FnMut`), B = u32 = Self::Item (so no new type enters the picture), no closure capture (today's closures capture nothing — lesson 144 cited but not load-bearing). Source choice — `Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()` — carries lessons 149/150's discipline forward: `Range<u32>` yields owned `u32`, so the closure parameters are `u32` (not `&u32`), the bodies use plain `u32 + u32` (lesson 009) and `u32 * u32` (lesson 009), no AddAssign-on-reference, no deref-read, no `as` cast. Stabilized at 1.0.0 (`trait.Iterator.md:2363`); local toolchain 1.95.0. Decomposition note: this is the third move of the closure-driven Iterator methods sub-arc (audit §4.4.1, 27 methods total). Picked third per audit §7's leanness ordering — `for_each` first because no return type; `map` second because the *return-type segment* of the parenthesized bound is the smallest extension; `fold` third because the *parens-segment* of the bound generalizes from one slot to two (the next sub-step beyond `map`'s shape) and because the return type is now non-trivial (`B`). Likely next moves per audit §4.4.1: `inspect` (lazy version of `for_each`, `FnMut(&Self::Item)` no return — first place the audience meets the *reference closure parameter* `&Self::Item`); `filter` (lazy adapter, `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` — surfaces the double-reference situation `trait.Iterator.md:962-967` warns about); `find`/`any`/`all`/`position` (short-circuiting consumers with predicate-shape closures); or `reduce` (fold variant that uses the first element as init; returns `Option<Self::Item>` for the empty-iterator case). Explicitly defer: `try_fold` (Try sub-arc, audit §4.4.4); `rfold` (DoubleEndedIterator sub-arc, audit §4.4.5); `reduce` (separate move); accumulator type different from element type (today keeps `B = Self::Item = u32` for surface minimality; the mechanic itself permits any `B`); right-fold / "fold from the right" (gated on `DoubleEndedIterator`); internal-iteration "implement other forward methods in terms of `fold`" note (`trait.Iterator.md:2397-2404`, implementor-side); `v.iter()` / `v.into_iter()` source shapes.
depends_on: 150-iterator-map (load-bearing — installs the `<B, F>` two-type-parameter shape and the `-> B` arrow segment of the parenthesized bound. Today's `fold<B, F>` reuses the same two slots in the same angle brackets; today's `FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> B` reuses the arrow segment. Lesson 150's "rustc reads `B` from somewhere" frame (then: from the closure's body) is what makes today's "`init`'s type fixes `B`" sentence land — the audience already accepts that `B` is determined by the surrounding context. Lesson 150's *unlocks* explicitly named today: "the `Iterator::fold` consumer with `FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> B` bound — *two-parameter* parenthesized bound (the next sub-step beyond today's one-parameter form); accumulator pattern; takes an initial accumulator value plus a closure; returns the final accumulator"), 149-iterator-for-each (load-bearing — installs `Iterator::for_each` as the first closure-driven Iterator method with consuming `self`. Today's `fold` also takes `self` by value (Probe 6 empirical: E0382 with `note:` "`fold` takes ownership of the receiver `self`, which moves `it`" — payload identical in shape to lesson 149 Probe 5 and lesson 150 Probe 7 with the method name rotated)), 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction (load-bearing — installs the Fn-family three-trait distinction with the layered supertrait shape `Fn: FnMut: FnOnce` and the auto-implementation rule mapping closure-body actions to traits. Today's bound is `FnMut`; today's closures `|acc, x| acc + x` and `|acc, x| acc * x` capture nothing and so implement `Fn` (and so all three traits per the supertrait relation). The bound `FnMut` accepts them via the supertrait acceptance rule), 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the parenthesized form `<F: FnMut(T) -> R>` with both the parens segment and the optional `-> R` return-type segment. Today extends the parens segment from one slot to two: `FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> B`. The lesson body's "Read it with the lesson 147 grammar" walk applies lesson 147's rules — the new piece is *inside the parens*: comma-separated multiple slots), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs the closure literal `|param: T| body` and the parameter-list-inside-pipes shape. Today's `|acc, x|` extends the parameter list from one slot to two, with comma separation (composes with lesson 036). Lesson 142's prereq chain explicitly grounded the parameter-list shape in lesson 020's typed-parameter `p: TYPE` form), 036-multiple-parameters (load-bearing — installs the comma-separated *function* parameter list `fn add(a: i32, b: i32)`. Today's `|acc, x|` is the closure analogue of this list — same `,` separator, same positional matching at the call site (here the "call site" is rustc invoking the closure on each element with two argument values: the threaded accumulator and the next element). The annotations are inferred today; Probe 2 exercises the explicit form `|acc: u32, x: u32|` and confirms the function-parameter analogy is exact, twice, comma-separated inside the closure pipes), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait synopsis and the `Self::Item` associated-type slot. Today's bound `FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> B` reads the closure's *second* parameter type from this slot. For `Range<u32>`, `Item = u32`. Probe 5 empirically confirms via rustc's `FnMut(u32, u32)` spelling — second slot is the resolved `Self::Item`), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` (091's evidence quotes `output/docs/rust/std/ops/struct.Range.md:139-141` verbatim) and the parens-rule for method calls on a range value. Today's `(1..4_u32).fold(...)` reuses both: the source is a `Range<u32>` (Probe 6 empirical: rustc spells the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>`)), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — installs the `4_u32`, `0_u32`, `1_u32`, `100_u32`, `0_i32` type-suffix forms. Today's `init` literals (`0_u32`, `100_u32`, `1_u32`) all rely on this form to pin `B = u32`; the range upper bound `4_u32` pins `Self::Item = u32`; Probe 4's `0_i32` pins `B = i32` for the contrast), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs the twelve integer type names. Today's `u32` and `i32` are two of them; combined with 081 and 091 this fixes `Self::Item = u32` and `B = u32` (or, in Probe 4, `B = i32`)), 025-implicit-return (load-bearing — installs the rule "a body's last expression *is* the return value when there is no `;`." Today's closure body `acc + x` (no `;`) is a single expression whose value is the closure's return — that is what makes `B` flow back through the closure correctly), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (load-bearing — installs `+ - * /` between two integer values producing a new integer value. Today's working closure body `acc + x` is `u32 + u32` producing `u32`; Check Yourself body `acc * x` is `u32 * u32` producing `u32`. No new arithmetic mechanic; no `as` cast (lesson 034 installed only `i32 as f64`; lesson 081 named integer-to-integer `as` as deferred). Source-shape choice (`Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()`) ensures both sides of `+` and `*` are owned `u32` values — pure lesson 009 mechanic, no AddAssign-on-reference, no deref-read), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. **E0593** is *new* today (Probe 3 — closure has wrong number of arguments); reappearing with the lesson 003 shape: E0308 (Probe 4) with payload `expected i32, found u32` on the body's value flowing back into `B`; E0277 (Probes 4 and 5) with two payloads, the load-bearing one being Probe 5's `expected an FnMut(u32, u32) closure, found {integer}`; E0382 (Probe 6) with payload tying the move to `fold`'s `self` receiver. Same lesson 003 four-part shape unchanged), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — `<F>` slot, today extended trivially to `<B, F>`), 143-unannotated-closure-first-use (cited — `|x|` no-annotation form; type from bound), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (cited — capture mechanic, today's closures capture nothing so 144 is named but not load-bearing), 020-function-with-parameter (cited — typed parameter `p: TYPE` shape; Probe 2 explicit annotations reuse this form twice, comma-separated, inside the closure pipes), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{}", x)`), 005-let-binding (cited — `let s = ...`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Iterator::inspect` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item)` bound (no return) — first place the audience meets the *reference closure parameter* shape `&Self::Item`; lighter than `filter` because no predicate decision; returns `Inspect<Self, F>`" moves, future "the `Iterator::filter` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` bound — surfaces the *double-reference* situation `trait.Iterator.md:962-967` warns about; returns `Filter<Self, P>`; with this method, an end-to-end pipeline `.filter(...).map(...).fold(0, ...)` becomes teachable" moves, future "short-circuiting consumers with predicate-shape closures — `find` (`FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool`, returns `Option<Self::Item>`), `position` (returns `Option<usize>`), `any` (returns `bool`), `all` (returns `bool`); first place the audience meets short-circuit semantics on closure-driven Iterator methods" moves, future "the `Iterator::reduce` consumer with `FnMut(Self::Item, Self::Item) -> Self::Item` bound — variant of `fold` that uses the *first* element as the initial accumulator; returns `Option<Self::Item>` because the iterator might be empty; first place the audience meets `Option` as a return type from a closure-driven consumer; signature at `trait.Iterator.md:2469`" moves, future "the `Iterator::try_fold` consumer with `FnMut(B, Self::Item) -> R where R: Try<Output = B>` bound — short-circuiting variant of `fold`; gates on the `Try` trait sub-arc (audit §4.4.4); signature at `trait.Iterator.md:2233`" moves, future "the `DoubleEndedIterator::rfold` consumer — right-associative version of `fold`; gates on `DoubleEndedIterator` trait sub-arc (audit §4.4.5); `trait.Iterator.md:2392-2395` names it" moves, future "`fold` with an accumulator type *different from* `Self::Item` — e.g. fold a `Range<u32>` into a `String` via `|acc: String, x: u32| format!(\"{}{}\", acc, x)`; the corpus has the canonical example at `trait.Iterator.md:2434-2443`; the mechanic is the same as today's `B = u32 = Self::Item` but with `B != Self::Item`; gates on `String` and `format!` (or some other multi-type accumulator)" moves, future "the remaining 24 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `find_map`, `rposition`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; each composes today's wrapper-or-consumer + closure-bound mechanic with method-specific shape" moves, future "iterator-pipeline capstone — `(1..N_u32).filter(...).map(...).fold(0, ...)` end-to-end after `filter` lands; audit §7 names this as the natural integration point — three closure-driven Iterator methods chained, two of which carry `&Self::Item` in their bound" moves, future "multi-parameter closures bound to `let` — generalize today's two-parameter form back to lesson 142's bind-and-call shape; e.g. `let add = |a: u32, b: u32| a + b; add(2, 3)` — composes today's parameter-list-with-commas inside `|...|` with lesson 142's parens-call mechanic; today defers because the iterator-method context exercises it concretely" moves
```

```text
id: 152-iterator-reduce
move: call `Iterator::reduce` on a `Range<u32>` with a single two-parameter closure that threads the accumulator through the iteration — `let s = (1..4_u32).reduce(|acc, x| acc + x); println!("{:?}", s);`. Working probe `demo.rs` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0); `./demo` prints `Some(6)`, exit 0. Probe 2 `empty.rs` (`(1..1_u32)` — half-open range with lower==upper, yields no elements) compiles silently and prints `None` — load-bearing empirical witness for the `Option` return type, since the empty case is the structural reason `reduce` cannot just return `Self::Item` directly. Probe 3 `single.rs` (`(5..6_u32)`, single element) compiles silently and prints `Some(5)` — sharpens the threading rule: the first element *becomes* the initial accumulator without passing through the closure (closure runs zero times for a one-element source). Probe 4 `type_pin.rs` (annotation `let s: Option<u32> = ...`) compiles silently and prints `Some(6)` — empirical witness that the inferred return type is exactly `Option<u32>` for `Range<u32>`. Probe 5 `non_closure_arg.rs` (pass `7` instead of a closure to `(1..4_u32).reduce(7)`) fires E0277 with rustc spelling the expected closure type as `FnMut(u32, u32)` — direct empirical witness for the *homogeneous* parens-bound shape (both parameter slots and the return are all `Self::Item`, no separate `B`). Probe 6 `extra_init.rs` (pass *two* arguments as if `reduce` were `fold` — `(1..4_u32).reduce(0_u32, |acc, x| acc + x)`) fires E0061 "this method takes 1 argument but 2 arguments were supplied" with `help: remove the extra argument` showing `(1..4_u32).reduce(0_u32);` — direct structural witness that `reduce`'s argument list has length one, not two; alongside fires E0277 with payload `expected an FnMut(u32, u32) closure, found u32`. Probe 7 `reduce_consumes.rs` (bind `let it = 1..4_u32;` then call `it.reduce(...)` then `it.count()`) fires E0382 with rustc spelling the moved-value type as `std::ops::Range<u32>` and `note:` reading verbatim "`reduce` takes ownership of the receiver `self`, which moves `it`" — direct empirical witness for `reduce`'s consuming `self` receiver, with rustc spelling `reduce` by name. Check Yourself Probe 8 `q.rs` (`(1..4_u32).reduce(|acc, x| acc * x)`) compiles silently and prints `Some(6)` — multiplicative reduce; first element `1` becomes acc, closure walks `(1, 2) → 2`, `(2, 3) → 6`. Source choice: `Range<u32>` (lesson 091 + 080 + 081) yields owned `u32` elements, so the closure parameters are both `u32` (no `&u32`, no deref-read). Closure bodies are plain `u32 + u32` and `u32 * u32` per lesson 009; no AddAssign-on-reference, no `as` cast.
main_concept: `Iterator::reduce` is the fourth closure-driven Iterator method in the run, and the *first Option-returning* one. The signature, verbatim from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:2469`, reads `fn reduce<F>(self, f: F) -> Option<Self::Item> where Self: Sized, F: FnMut(Self::Item, Self::Item) -> Self::Item,`. Five structural facts under one centered move. (1) *One type parameter*: `<F>` only — *no* `B` slot. The accumulator's type *is* `Self::Item`; there is no separate accumulator-type slot like fold's `<B, F>`. (2) *Receiver `self` (consuming) plus exactly one non-receiver argument*: `(self, f: F)` — no `init: B`. Probe 6 empirical witness via E0061 "this method takes 1 argument but 2 arguments were supplied" when an extra argument is supplied as if `reduce` were `fold`. The `help: remove the extra argument` line writes the single-argument form `reduce(0_u32);` — structural about argument count. (3) *Returns `Option<Self::Item>`*: not a bare value (`fold`'s `B`) and not `()` (`for_each`'s) and not a wrapper struct (`map`'s `Map<Self, F>`). It is `Option<T>` (lesson 119) with `T = Self::Item`. The empty-iterator case is the load-bearing reason: with no first element, `reduce` cannot start, so it returns `None`. Probe 2 empirical: `(1..1_u32).reduce(...)` prints `None`. Probe 1 empirical: `(1..4_u32).reduce(...)` prints `Some(6)`. Probe 3 empirical: `(5..6_u32).reduce(...)` prints `Some(5)` — single element becomes acc, closure runs zero times. (4) *Closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item, Self::Item) -> Self::Item`*: composes lesson 147's parens-bound, lesson 148's `FnMut`, lesson 132's `Self::Item` slot, and lesson 151's two-parameter list inside the parens. Novel sub-fact today: every position in the bound is the *same* type, `Self::Item`. There is no separate `B`. The closure takes two elements and returns one element of the same type. Probe 5 empirical: rustc spells `FnMut(u32, u32)` — both slots resolved to `u32 = Self::Item`. (5) *Threading semantics, with first-element-as-init*: rustc takes the iterator's first element as the initial accumulator (no closure call for that element), then on each subsequent step calls `f(acc, next_element)` with the return value of the prior call (or the first element for the first call) as the first argument. After all remaining elements are consumed, the final accumulator wrapped in `Some(_)` is `reduce`'s return value. The corpus prose at `trait.Iterator.md:2477-2480` is the source: "For iterators with at least one element, this is the same as `fold()` with the first element of the iterator as the initial accumulator value, folding every subsequent element into it." The corpus also says (`:2474-2475`): "If the iterator is empty, returns `None`; otherwise, returns the result of the reduction." Three elements → 2 closure calls (Probe 1); one element → 0 closure calls (Probe 3); zero elements → 0 closure calls and `None` (Probe 2). The audience crosses from "I have three closure-driven Iterator methods (`for_each`, `map`, `fold`); the consumer ones (`for_each`, `fold`) take an explicit closure shape with whatever return type" (lesson 151) to "Some closure-driven consumers compute their initial accumulator from the iterator itself rather than from an explicit `init` argument; if the iterator is empty there is nothing to start from, so the return type wraps in `Option`. `reduce` is the canonical one. It consumes `self`, takes one closure with two parameters of type `Self::Item` and returns `Self::Item`, and yields `Option<Self::Item>` — `Some(final_acc)` for non-empty input, `None` for empty input." E0061 fires today only on the wrong-argument-count contrast (Probe 6); no new error code is introduced. E0277 reappears in Probes 5 and 6 with payload tying the FnMut bound to `(u32, u32)` (homogeneous shape — direct empirical witness for the homogeneous bound); E0382 reappears in Probe 7 with payload tying the move to `reduce`'s `self` receiver. The diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged. Today is *shallow operational*: one closure-driven consumer with multi-parameter closure, one trait (`FnMut`), all three bound positions = Self::Item = u32 (so no new type enters the picture relative to lesson 151), no closure capture (today's closures capture nothing — lesson 144 cited but not load-bearing). Source choice — `Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()` — carries lessons 149/150/151's discipline forward: `Range<u32>` yields owned `u32`, so the closure parameters are `u32` (not `&u32`), the bodies use plain `u32 + u32` (lesson 009) and `u32 * u32` (lesson 009), no AddAssign-on-reference, no deref-read, no `as` cast. Stabilized at 1.51.0 (`trait.Iterator.md:2467`); local toolchain 1.95.0. Decomposition note: this is the fourth move of the closure-driven Iterator methods sub-arc (audit §4.4.1, 27 methods total). Picked next per audit §7's leanness ordering — fold (151) installed the threading rule and the multi-parameter parens-bound; reduce reuses both unchanged but introduces (a) the *no-init* signature variant and (b) the *Option-wrapped* return for the empty-iterator case. First time `Option` appears as the return type of a closure-driven Iterator method. Likely next moves per audit §4.4.1: `find`, `position`, `find_map`, `any`, `all` (the predicate-consumer family — short-circuiting consumers with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` or similar bounds, returning `Option<Self::Item>`, `Option<usize>`, `Option<B>`, or `bool`); or `inspect` / `filter` (the lazy-adapter family). Explicitly defer: `try_reduce` (nightly-only, audit §4.4.4); `fold` vs `reduce` design-choice rule; `Option::unwrap` / `unwrap_or` / `map` / `?` (separate `Option`-API moves); numerical edge cases on signed integers; `match` on `Option<T>` *as a centered concept* (installed by 119 but not re-centered today); `v.iter()` / `v.into_iter()` source shapes.
depends_on: 151-iterator-fold (load-bearing — installs the threading semantics: each closure call's return becomes the next call's first argument. Today reuses that rule unchanged for the *remaining* elements after the first; what differs is *only* the source of the *initial* accumulator value (first element vs. explicit `init`). Also installs the two-parameter parens-bound segment `FnMut(_, _) -> _` and the consuming `self` shape; today's bound `FnMut(Self::Item, Self::Item) -> Self::Item` reuses the segment with all three positions filled by the same type, and Probe 7 today empirically witnesses E0382 with `note:` "`reduce` takes ownership of the receiver `self`" — payload identical in shape to lesson 151 Probe 6 with the method name rotated), 150-iterator-map (load-bearing — installs the `<B, F>` two-type-parameter shape and the wrapper-aware framing. Today's signature has `<F>` only — *no* `B`. The contrast in the lesson body's signature walk depends on the audience already knowing `<B, F>` from 150 / 151), 149-iterator-for-each (load-bearing — installs `Iterator::for_each` as the first closure-driven Iterator method with consuming `self`. Today's `reduce` is the fourth in the same family), 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction (load-bearing — installs the Fn-family three-trait distinction with the auto-implementation rule. Today's bound is `FnMut`; today's working closures `|acc, x| acc + x` and `|acc, x| acc * x` capture nothing and so implement `Fn` (and so all three traits per the supertrait relation)), 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the parenthesized form `<F: FnMut(T) -> R>` with both the parens segment and the optional `-> R` return-type segment. Today reuses both segments; the two-parameter form inside the parens was installed by 151), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs the closure literal `|param| body` and the parameter-list-inside-pipes shape. Today's `|acc, x|` reuses lesson 151's two-parameter form, with comma separation), 036-multiple-parameters (load-bearing — installs the comma-separated *function* parameter list. Today's `|acc, x|` is the same closure analogue as lesson 151's; same `,` separator, same positional matching), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Iterator` trait synopsis and the `Self::Item` associated-type slot. Today's bound `FnMut(Self::Item, Self::Item) -> Self::Item` reads *all three* positions from this slot. For `Range<u32>`, `Item = u32`. Probe 5 empirically confirms via rustc's `FnMut(u32, u32)` spelling), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `{:?}` Debug-printing of `Option<&u64>` from `iter.next()`. Today reuses the same Debug-format mechanic for `Option<u32>` from `reduce`'s return — same `Some(_)` / `None` shape, with the inner type substituted), 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — installs `Option<T>` as the prelude's sibling of `Result<T, E>` with `Some(T)` / `None` variants, both in the prelude. Today is the first place the run encounters `Option<T>` as a *return type* from a closure-driven Iterator method. Probe 1 → `Some(6)`; Probe 2 → `None`; Probe 3 → `Some(5)`), 093-standard-library-prelude (load-bearing — installs `{:?}` Debug formatter as a `println!` slot for built-in types including `Option`. Today's `println!("{:?}", s)` for `s: Option<u32>` reuses this formatter), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` and the parens-rule for method calls on a range value. Today's `(1..4_u32).reduce(...)`, `(1..1_u32).reduce(...)`, `(5..6_u32).reduce(...)`, `(7..8_u32).reduce(...)` all reuse: source is a `Range<u32>` (Probe 7 empirical: rustc spells `std::ops::Range<u32>`). The half-open semantics of `Range` — `1..1` yields nothing because lower==upper — is what makes Probe 2 produce `None`), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — installs the `_u32` type-suffix forms. Today's range bounds (`1_u32`, `4_u32`, `5_u32`, `6_u32`, `7_u32`, `8_u32`, plus `0_u32` in Probe 6's contrast) all rely on this form to pin `Self::Item = u32`), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs the twelve integer type names. Today's `u32` is one of them; combined with 081 and 091 this fixes `Self::Item = u32`), 009-arithmetic-on-integers (load-bearing — installs `+ - * /` between two integer values producing a new integer value. Today's working closure body `acc + x` is `u32 + u32` producing `u32`; Check Yourself body `acc * x` is `u32 * u32` producing `u32`. No new arithmetic mechanic), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. Today's diagnostics: E0061 (Probe 6, *load-bearing* contrast — "this method takes 1 argument but 2 arguments were supplied"), E0277 (Probes 5 and 6, payload spelling `FnMut(u32, u32)` — direct empirical witness for the homogeneous parens-bound shape), E0382 (Probe 7, payload tying the move to `reduce`'s `self` receiver). No new error code today; the move is shape-only relative to existing diagnostics), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — `<F>` slot, today's only generic), 143-unannotated-closure-first-use (cited — `|x|` no-annotation form; type from bound), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (cited — capture mechanic, today's closures capture nothing), 011-println-positional-args (cited — `println!("{:?}", x)`), 005-let-binding (cited — `let s = ...`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the predicate-consumer family — `find` (`FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool`, returns `Option<Self::Item>`), `position` (`FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool`, returns `Option<usize>`), `any` / `all` (returns `bool`), `find_map` (`FnMut(Self::Item) -> Option<B>`, returns `Option<B>`); first place the audience meets *short-circuit semantics* on closure-driven Iterator methods; first place the closure parameter is `&Self::Item` (a reference)" moves, future "the `Iterator::inspect` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item)` bound (no return) — first place the audience meets the *reference closure parameter* shape `&Self::Item` outside a predicate context; lighter than `filter` because no decision; returns `Inspect<Self, F>`" moves, future "the `Iterator::filter` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` bound — surfaces the *double-reference* situation `trait.Iterator.md:962-967` warns about; returns `Filter<Self, P>`; with this method, an end-to-end pipeline `.filter(...).map(...).fold(0, ...)` becomes teachable" moves, future "`Option::unwrap`, `Option::unwrap_or`, `Option::map`, `?` operator — separate `Option`-API moves that compose with today's `reduce` return; the corpus example uses `.unwrap_or(0)` to extract the inner value, today the lesson keeps `{:?}` instead" moves, future "the `Iterator::min` and `Iterator::max` consumers with `Ord` bound — same `Option<Self::Item>` return shape as today's `reduce`, but with no closure (the Ord-based comparison is implicit); composes today's empty-iterator-returns-None pattern with a different (non-closure-driven) signature" moves, future "the `Iterator::min_by` / `Iterator::max_by` consumers with `FnMut(&Self::Item, &Self::Item) -> Ordering` closure-bound; combines today's two-parameter-of-same-type bound shape with `Ordering`-returning closure" moves, future "the `Iterator::try_reduce` consumer — short-circuiting variant; gates on the `Try` trait sub-arc (audit §4.4.4); signature at `trait.Iterator.md:2493`; nightly-only experimental in 1.95.0 toolchain" moves, future "fold-vs-reduce design choice rule — when to pick one over the other in real code; deferred today because the question is design, not signature" moves, future "the remaining 23 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `rposition`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; each composes today's wrapper-or-consumer + closure-bound mechanic with method-specific shape" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/option/enum.Option.md
```
