```text
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 153-iterator-any
move: call `Iterator::any` on a `Range<u32>` with a single-parameter predicate closure that returns `bool` — `let r = (1..10_u32).any(|x| x == 5); println!("{}", r);`. Working probe `demo.rs` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0); `./demo` prints `true`, exit 0. Probe 2 `reusable.rs` (`let mut it = 1..10_u32; let found = it.any(|x| x == 5); let next = it.next(); println!("{} {:?}", found, next);`) compiles silently and prints `true Some(6)` — *load-bearing* probe witnessing two structural facts in a single execution: (a) `it` is still bound after `.any(...)` (direct empirical witness for `&mut self` receiver, NOT `self`; with any of lessons 149-152's methods that line would have fired E0382), and (b) the iterator's next yielded value is `Some(6)`, not `Some(1)` or `None` — `any` short-circuited on `5` and stopped advancing the iterator past the matching element. Probe 3 `empty.rs` (`(1..1_u32)` half-open range with lower==upper, yields no elements) compiles silently and prints `false` — empirical witness for the empty-iterator-returns-`false` rule (corpus `trait.Iterator.md:2612`); dual to lesson 152 Probe 2's `None` return for empty `reduce`. Probe 4 `no_match.rs` (`(1..5_u32).any(|x| x == 100)`) compiles silently and prints `false` — closure called for every element (4 times), no match found, `any` returns `false`; load-bearing contrast that makes short-circuit visible *by absence* relative to Probe 5. Probe 5 `count_calls.rs` (`let mut count = 0_u32; let r = (1..10_u32).any(|x| { count += 1; x == 3 }); println!("{} {}", r, count);`) compiles silently and prints `true 3` — *direct numeric* witness for short-circuit: closure was called exactly 3 times for `x = 1, 2, 3` and stopped, not 9 times for the full range; composes lesson 144 capture (`count` from outer scope) with lesson 023 mutation (`+= 1`) and the regular Rust block-as-closure-body shape (`{ statement; expression }` where `count += 1;` is the statement and `x == 3` is the trailing expression that becomes the closure's return value). Probe 6 `non_closure_arg.rs` (`(1..4_u32).any(7)`) fires E0277 with rustc spelling `expected an FnMut(u32) closure, found {integer}` — direct empirical witness for the *single-parameter* parens-bound shape (no comma, contrast with lesson 151's `FnMut(u32, u32)` and lesson 152's `FnMut(u32, u32)`); rustc's `note: required by a bound in any` points at `library/core/src/iter/traits/iterator.rs:2863:4`. Probe 7 `needs_mut.rs` (`let it = 1..10_u32; let _r = it.any(|x| x == 5);` — binding without `mut`) fires E0596 with rustc spelling `cannot borrow `it` as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` and `help: consider changing this to be mutable` proposing `let mut it = 1..10_u32;` at the binding site with `+++` markers — direct empirical witness that `any`'s receiver is `&mut self` (only an `&mut self` method requires the binding to be mutable; same code shape as lesson 131's `next()` E0596 contrast with the method name rotated). Probe 8 `type_pin.rs` (`let r: bool = (1..10_u32).any(|x| x == 5);`) compiles silently and prints `true` — empirical witness that the inferred return type is exactly `bool`. Source choice: `Range<u32>` (lesson 091 + 080 + 081) yields owned `u32` elements; closure parameter is `u32` by value (no `&u32`, no deref-read — that mechanic is reserved for `find`). Closure bodies are plain `u32 == u32 -> bool` per lesson 013; no `as` cast, no AddAssign-on-reference.
main_concept: `Iterator::any` is the fifth closure-driven Iterator method in the run, the *first* with `&mut self` receiver (does NOT consume the iterator), and the *first* with short-circuit semantics. The signature, verbatim from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:2599`, reads `fn any<F>(&mut self, f: F) -> bool where Self: Sized, F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool,`. Five structural facts under one centered move. (1) *One type parameter*: `<F>` only. (2) *Receiver `&mut self` (mutable borrow), plus exactly one non-receiver argument*: `(&mut self, f: F)`. The iterator binding is *still usable* after the call returns. Probe 2 empirical: `let mut it = 1..10_u32; let _ = it.any(...); it.next()` produces `Some(6)`. Probe 7 empirical: omitting `mut` on the binding fires E0596 with `cannot borrow `it` as mutable` — same code shape as lesson 131's `next()` contrast, with the method name rotated to `any`. The borrow rule (binding must be `let mut`) is the same rule installed by lesson 131; what's new today is that this rule applies to a *closure-driven* method for the first time. (3) *Returns `bool`*: not `()` (lesson 149's `for_each`), not a wrapper struct (lesson 150's `Map`), not a bare value `B` (lesson 151's `fold`), not `Option<Self::Item>` (lesson 152's `reduce`). Just the primitive `bool` from lesson 013. Probe 8 type-pins. The empty-iterator case returns `false` directly — corpus `trait.Iterator.md:2612` "An empty iterator returns `false`." This is the dual of lesson 152's empty-`reduce` returning `None`: there the empty-case forced an `Option<_>` wrapper because no first element existed; here the empty case is just unambiguously "no element matched", which is `false`. (4) *Closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool`*: composes lesson 147's parens-bound, lesson 148's `FnMut`, lesson 132's `Self::Item` slot, and a `bool` return type slot. Single-parameter form (no comma inside the parens, contrast with lessons 151/152's two-parameter forms). For `Range<u32>`, `Self::Item = u32`. Probe 6 empirical: rustc spells `FnMut(u32)` — single slot. (5) *Short-circuit semantics*: `any` calls the closure on each element in source order; as soon as the closure returns `true`, `any` stops calling and returns `true`. If the closure never returns `true` (or the iterator is exhausted with no `true`), `any` returns `false`. Corpus `trait.Iterator.md:2608-2610`: "`any()` is short-circuiting; in other words, it will stop processing as soon as it finds a `true`, given that no matter what else happens, the result will also be `true`." Probe 5 empirical: closure called exactly 3 times for `x == 3` match in `1..10_u32` (counts via lesson 144 capture + lesson 023 `+=`); not 9 times for the full range. Probe 4 empirical contrast: with no match, closure runs once per element (full walk). Probe 2 empirical: iterator position after short-circuit lands just past the matching element (`Some(6)` after matching on `5`). The audience crosses from "all four closure-driven Iterator methods I know consume `self`; once I call them, the iterator is gone" (lesson 152) to "some closure-driven Iterator methods take `&mut self` instead — they borrow the iterator mutably, advance it as far as needed to answer their question, and return, leaving the iterator binding still usable. `any` is the simplest one: bound is `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool`, return is `bool`, short-circuits on first `true` from the closure, returns `false` for empty iterator. Iterator's position after `any` is just past the first match (or fully exhausted if no match)." E0596 fires today only on the `&mut self`-without-`mut` contrast (Probe 7); E0277 reappears in Probe 6 with payload `FnMut(u32)` (single-slot — direct empirical witness for the single-parameter bound shape). The diagnostic shape is the lesson 003 four-part map unchanged. Today is *shallow operational*: one closure-driven consumer with single-parameter closure, one trait (`FnMut`), `Self::Item = u32` (no new type enters the picture relative to lesson 152), Probe 5 captures + mutates (composes 144 + 023 + 148). Source choice — `Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()` — carries lessons 149-152's discipline forward: `Range<u32>` yields owned `u32`, so the closure parameter is `u32` (not `&u32`), the body uses plain `u32 == u32` (lesson 013) producing `bool`, no AddAssign-on-reference, no deref-read, no `as` cast. Stabilized at 1.0.0 (`trait.Iterator.md:2597`); local toolchain 1.95.0. Decomposition note: this is the fifth move of the closure-driven Iterator methods sub-arc (audit §4.4.1, 27 methods total) and the first move of the predicate-consumer arc named in audit §8 / lesson 152's run-context handoff. Picked next per the leanness ordering — opens with two distinct structural facts simultaneously witnessable in a single probe (Probe 2: `&mut self` reusability + short-circuit position). `any` is opened *before* `all` because (a) the empty-case identity reads naturally as the English word "any" (`false` for "did any of zero elements match? no"), and (b) the existential-versus-universal duality is best taught with `any` installed first. Likely next moves per audit §4.4.1: `all` (sibling — same signature shape, inverted semantics, empty case `true`); `position` (sibling with `Option<usize>` return); `find` / `find_map` (predicate consumers with `&Self::Item` parameter — gates on deref-read sub-arc). Explicitly defer: `all`, `position`, `find`, `find_map` (sibling moves); the "why `&mut self` not `self`?" design rule (named, deferred to position or capstone); `try_for_each` / `try_fold` (short-circuit-with-`?`, gated on Try sub-arc); `v.iter()` / `v.into_iter()` source shapes; `match` on `bool` *as a centered concept*.
depends_on: 152-iterator-reduce (load-bearing — installs the closure-driven Iterator method *family* shape with one non-receiver argument and a `FnMut(...)` bound, plus the consuming-`self` shape that today contrasts against. Today's `any` rotates the receiver from `self` to `&mut self` and the return from `Option<Self::Item>` to `bool`, while keeping the single-non-receiver-argument shape and the by-value closure parameter shape unchanged. The empty-case contrast — 152's empty `reduce` returns `None`, today's empty `any` returns `false` — is the dual of 152's empty case; both lessons name the empty-iterator behavior as load-bearing for the return-type design choice. Probe 2 today witnesses the receiver-rotation directly: `it` is still usable after `.any(...)`, where with 152's `reduce` the analogous line would have fired E0382), 151-iterator-fold (load-bearing — installs the two-parameter parens-bound segment `FnMut(_, _) -> _`. Today's bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool` is the single-parameter form. Probe 6 empirical: rustc spells `FnMut(u32)` — no comma, single slot — direct contrast with lesson 151 Probe 5's `FnMut(u32, u32)`), 150-iterator-map (load-bearing — installs the closure-driven Iterator method family with the `-> B` arrow segment. Today reuses the arrow segment with `bool` in place of `B`), 149-iterator-for-each (load-bearing — installs the first closure-driven Iterator method with consuming `self`. Today is the fifth in the same family but the first with non-consuming `&mut self` receiver), 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction (load-bearing — installs the Fn/FnMut/FnOnce three-trait family with auto-implementation. Today's bound is `FnMut`. Probes 1, 2, 3, 4, 8 use closures that capture nothing — under 148's rule they implement `Fn` (and so all three traits). Probe 5 uses a closure that captures `count` and mutates it — under 148's rule it implements `FnMut` and `FnOnce` but not `Fn`. The bound `FnMut` accepts both shapes, direct empirical witness that `FnMut` is the correct choice for `any`'s bound), 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the parens-bound grammar `<F: FnMut(T) -> R>`. Today reuses this grammar in single-parameter form), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (load-bearing only for Probe 5 — closure captures `count` from enclosing scope), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs the closure literal `|param| body`. Today's `|x|` reuses this form), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Self::Item` associated-type slot. Today's bound has `Self::Item` in the single parameter slot. For `Range<u32>`, `Item = u32`. Probe 6 empirical: rustc spells `FnMut(u32)`), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `&mut self` receiver shape on `next()` and the borrow rule "binding must be `let mut`" via E0596 contrast. Today reuses *both* directly: Probe 2 calls `it.next()` after `.any(...)` returns (direct mechanic reuse — call `.next()` on a `let mut` binding to advance the iterator one step); Probe 7 fires E0596 with the same payload shape as lesson 131's contrast, with the method name rotated from `next` to `any`. Lesson 131 is the structural anchor for today's lesson; the only thing that changes is that a closure-driven method now also exhibits the `&mut self` receiver shape that 131 first installed on `next`), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` and the parens-rule for method calls on a range value. Today's `(1..10_u32).any(...)` etc. all reuse. The half-open semantics `1..1` yields nothing — drives Probe 3's `false`), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — installs `_u32` suffix forms. Today's range bounds and Probe 6's `0_u32` rely on this form to pin `Self::Item = u32`), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — installs twelve integer type names. Today's `u32` is one of them; combined with 081 + 091 fixes `Self::Item = u32`), 023-compound-add-assign (load-bearing only for Probe 5 — `count += 1` is `u32 += u32` per lesson 023, with `count` declared `let mut count = 0_u32;`), 013-comparison-operators (load-bearing — installs `==` on `u32` produces `bool`. Today's closure bodies `x == 5`, `x == 100`, `x == 3` are `u32 == u32 -> bool` per lesson 013 — return type matches the bound's return slot `-> bool`), 011-println-positional-args (load-bearing — `println!("{}", r)` and `println!("{} {:?}", ..., ...)`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. Today: E0277 (Probe 6, *load-bearing* — payload `FnMut(u32)` direct empirical witness for single-slot bound); E0596 (Probe 7, *load-bearing* — payload `cannot borrow it as mutable` direct empirical witness for `&mut self` receiver). No new error code today; the move is shape-only relative to existing diagnostics), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — `<F>` slot, today's only generic), 143-unannotated-closure-first-use (cited — `|x|` no-annotation form), 005-let-binding (cited — `let r = ...`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Iterator::all` consumer — sibling with same signature shape `<F>(&mut self, f: F) -> bool where F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool` but inverted semantics; short-circuits on first `false`; empty iterator returns `true` (the existential-versus-universal duality)" moves, future "the `Iterator::position` consumer — sibling with same closure shape `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool` but `Option<usize>` return; combines today's short-circuit with a different return-type wrapper; first time the audience meets `Option<usize>` as a payload type" moves, future "the `Iterator::find` consumer — predicate consumer whose closure parameter is `&Self::Item`, not `Self::Item`; first place the audience meets the *reference closure parameter* shape; pulls in the deref-read mechanic; surfaces the double-reference situation when the source iterator already yields references" moves, future "the `Iterator::find_map` consumer — predicate consumer with `FnMut(Self::Item) -> Option<B>` bound; composes today's short-circuit with `Option`-returning closure bodies; returns `Option<B>`" moves, future "the `Iterator::filter` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` bound — surfaces the double-reference situation; returns `Filter<Self, P>`; with this method the end-to-end pipeline `.filter(...).map(...).fold(0, ...)` becomes teachable" moves, future "the `Iterator::inspect` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item)` bound (no return); first place the reference closure parameter shape `&Self::Item` appears outside a predicate context; lighter than `filter`; returns `Inspect<Self, F>`" moves, future "the `Iterator::try_for_each` and `Iterator::try_fold` consumers — short-circuit-with-`?` variants; gate on the `Try` trait sub-arc (audit §4.4.4)" moves, future "the design rule 'why `&mut self` and not `self`?' for predicate consumers — named today, deferred to `position` or a later capstone; the answer is that predicate consumers often want to keep using the iterator after the search (resume, ask another question, etc.)" moves, future "the existential-versus-universal duality between `any` and `all` — empty case identities `false` (any) and `true` (all); short-circuit polarities; design choice rule for predicate selection" moves, future "the remaining 22 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `rposition`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; each composes today's wrapper-or-consumer + closure-bound mechanic with method-specific shape" moves
evidence:
  - output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/ops/struct.Range.md
  - output/docs/rust/std/ops/trait.FnMut.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0277.md
  - output/docs/rust/error_codes/E0596.md

  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/observations/153-iterator-any.transcript.txt
  - experimental/eduratchet2/runs/rust-moves/evidence/153-iterator-any.md
status: accepted
```

```text
id: 154-iterator-all
move: call `Iterator::all` on a `Range<u32>` with a single-parameter predicate closure that returns `bool` — `let r = (1..10_u32).all(|x| x < 100); println!("{}", r);`. Working probe `demo.rs` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0); `./demo` prints `true`, exit 0 — every element of `1..9` is less than `100`, full walk, returns `true` (universally-quantified positive case). Probe 2 `short_circuit.rs` (`(1..10_u32).all(|x| x < 5)`) compiles silently and prints `false` — closure returns `true` for `x = 1, 2, 3, 4` and `false` on `x = 5`; `all` short-circuits on the first `false`. Probe 3 `empty.rs` (`(1..1_u32).all(|x| x < 0)`) — *load-bearing empty-case probe* — compiles with `unused_comparisons` warning (`comparison is useless due to type limits` because `x < 0` is impossible for `u32`) and prints `true`; the closure is *never called* even though the predicate would be impossible if it were; runtime answer comes from the empty-iterator-returns-`true` rule (corpus `trait.Iterator.md:2570`); direct **dual** of lesson 153 Probe 3 where `(1..1_u32).any(|x| x == 5)` returned `false`; the warning is informative — rustc agrees the predicate is impossible, yet the program still prints `true`, sharpening the vacuous-truth point. Probe 4 `count_calls.rs` (`let mut count = 0_u32; let r = (1..10_u32).all(|x| { count += 1; x < 5 }); println!("{} {}", r, count);`) compiles silently and prints `false 5` — *direct numeric* witness for short-circuit on `false`: closure called exactly 5 times for `x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5` (`true` for first four, `false` on fifth), not 9 times for the full range; composes lesson 144 capture + lesson 023 mutation + lesson 148's FnMut auto-impl; same shape as lesson 153 Probe 5 with closure body `count += 1; x == 3` swapped to `count += 1; x < 5`. Probe 5 `reusable.rs` (`let mut it = 1..10_u32; let r = it.all(|x| x < 5); let n = it.next(); println!("{} {:?}", r, n);`) compiles silently and prints `false Some(6)` — same shape as lesson 153 Probe 2 with `any` rotated to `all`: (a) `it` still bound after `.all(...)` (direct empirical witness for `&mut self` receiver, NOT `self`), (b) iterator's next yielded value is `Some(6)`, *not* `Some(1)` or `None` — `all` short-circuited on `5` and stopped advancing past the failing element. Probe 6 `type_pin.rs` (`let r: bool = (1..10_u32).all(|x| x < 100);`) compiles silently and prints `true` — empirical witness that the inferred return type is exactly `bool`. Probe 7 `non_closure_arg.rs` (`(1..4_u32).all(7)`) fires E0277 with rustc spelling `expected an FnMut(u32) closure, found {integer}` — same shape as lesson 153 Probe 6 with method name rotated; rustc's `note: required by a bound in all` points at `library/core/src/iter/traits/iterator.rs:2809:4` (*before* lesson 153's `:2863:4` for `any`, matching the corpus order where `all` is declared at `trait.Iterator.md:2557` and `any` at `:2599`). Probe 8 `needs_mut.rs` (`let it = 1..10_u32; let _r = it.all(|x| x < 100);`) fires E0596 with rustc spelling `cannot borrow it as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` — same shape as lesson 153 Probe 7 with method name rotated; direct empirical witness that `all`'s receiver is `&mut self`. Source choice: `Range<u32>` (lessons 091 + 080 + 081); closure parameter is `u32` by value; closure bodies are plain `u32 < u32 -> bool` (or `>`) per lesson 013; no `&u32`, no deref-read, no `as` cast, no `Vec`, no `.iter()` / `.into_iter()`.
main_concept: `Iterator::all` is the sibling of `Iterator::any` (lesson 153) with **inverted polarity** — same signature shape, dual short-circuit and dual empty-case. The signature, verbatim from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:2557`, reads `fn all<F>(&mut self, f: F) -> bool where Self: Sized, F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool,` — *identical* shape to `any`'s signature at `:2599`. Five structural facts under one centered move, all but two carry from lesson 153 unchanged. (1) *Same signature shape as `any`*: one type parameter `<F>`, receiver `&mut self`, exactly one non-receiver argument `f: F`, return type `bool`, closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool` (single-parameter parens-bound). All these were installed by lesson 153 and are reused without re-derivation. (2) *Same iterator-still-usable rule*: `all` borrows the iterator mutably and leaves it usable; iterator's position lands just past the decisive element. Probe 5 empirical: `false Some(6)` — same structure as lesson 153 Probe 2 (`true Some(6)` for `any` matching on `5`; today `false Some(6)` for `all` failing on `5`). Probe 8 empirical: omitting `mut` on the binding fires E0596 with same payload as lesson 153 Probe 7. (3) *Same diagnostic shapes for misuse*: E0277 on non-closure arg (Probe 7, payload `FnMut(u32)`) and E0596 on non-mut binding (Probe 8) are identical to lesson 153 with method name rotated. (4) **NEW: short-circuit polarity is inverted.** `any` stops on first `true`; `all` stops on first `false`. Corpus `trait.Iterator.md:2566-2568`: "`all()` is short-circuiting; in other words, it will stop processing as soon as it finds a `false`, given that no matter what else happens, the result will also be `false`." Probe 4 empirical: closure called exactly 5 times for `x < 5` failing in `1..10_u32` (4 trues then 1 false), not 9 times for the full range. Probe 2 empirical: returns `false` on the first failing element. Probe 1 empirical contrast: with all-true predicate (`x < 100`), closure runs once per element (full walk) and returns `true`. (5) **NEW: empty-iterator identity is dual.** `any` on empty returns `false`; `all` on empty returns `true`. Corpus `trait.Iterator.md:2570`: "An empty iterator returns `true`." Probe 3 empirical: `(1..1_u32).all(|x| x < 0)` prints `true` even though `x < 0` is impossible for any `u32` — the closure is *never called*, so its logic is irrelevant. The `unused_comparisons` warning rustc emits is the load-bearing detail: rustc agrees at compile time that the predicate is impossible, yet the program prints `true` at runtime — the empty-case rule is the deciding factor. The two empty-case identities (`false` for `any`, `true` for `all`) match the existential-versus-universal duality: `any` of zero things is trivially "no element matched"; `all` of zero things is trivially "every element passed (because none failed)". The audience crosses from "`any` short-circuits on `true` and returns `false` for empty" (lesson 153) to "`any` and `all` are siblings with the same signature; `any` short-circuits on `true` / empty-`false`; `all` short-circuits on `false` / empty-`true`. Both leave the iterator usable, with its position landing just past the decisive element. The empty-case identities match the existential-versus-universal rule." No new error code today; the move is shape-only relative to lesson 153. Today is *shallow operational*: one closure-driven consumer with single-parameter closure, one trait (`FnMut`), `Self::Item = u32`, Probe 4 captures + mutates (composes 144 + 023 + 148). Source choice — `Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()` — carries lessons 149-153's discipline forward unchanged. Stabilized at 1.0.0 (`trait.Iterator.md:2555`); local toolchain 1.95.0. Decomposition note: this is the sixth move of the closure-driven Iterator methods sub-arc (audit §4.4.1) and the second move of the predicate-consumer arc (lesson 153 was first). `all` is opened immediately after `any` because (a) the signature shape is *identical*, so the move is the smallest possible composition with lesson 153 — only the SEMANTICS differ, (b) the existential-versus-universal duality is best taught with both siblings adjacent, and (c) the empty-case dual ($\forall$ identity = `true`, $\exists$ identity = `false`) is empirically witnessable with one new probe (Probe 3) by changing the method name. Likely next moves per audit §4.4.1: `position` (sibling with `Option<usize>` return; first time `Option<usize>` is a payload type); `find` / `find_map` (predicate consumers with `&Self::Item` parameter — gates on deref-read sub-arc). Explicitly defer: `position`, `find`, `find_map` (sibling moves); the De Morgan duality at the closure-body level (`!cond`) — named, formal treatment deferred; the "why have both `any` and `all`?" design rule — named, deferred; `try_for_each` / `try_fold` (short-circuit-with-`?`, gated on Try sub-arc); `v.iter()` / `v.into_iter()` source shapes; `match` on `bool` *as a centered concept*.
depends_on: 153-iterator-any (load-bearing — direct sibling. Today reuses signature shape (`<F>(&mut self, f: F) -> bool`), receiver `&mut self`, closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool`, return `bool`, iterator-still-usable rule, the `let mut`-required rule for `&mut self` receivers, and the diagnostic shapes for E0277 and E0596 — all installed by lesson 153 on `any` and reused today on `all` without re-derivation. Today inverts only two facts: (1) short-circuit polarity (first `true` → first `false`) and (2) empty-case identity (`false` → `true`). Probe 5 empirical: same structure as lesson 153 Probe 2 with `any` rotated to `all`; Probe 7 same as lesson 153 Probe 6; Probe 8 same as lesson 153 Probe 7. The duality at the empty case is the centered new fact: lesson 153 Probe 3 returned `false` for empty `any`; today's Probe 3 returns `true` for empty `all`), 152-iterator-reduce (named only via 153 — sibling closure-driven consumer; cited for the empty-case-as-design-choice contrast which 153 already established), 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction (load-bearing — bound is `FnMut`. Probes 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 use closures that capture nothing — under 148's auto-impl rule they implement `Fn` and so all three traits. Probe 4 uses a closure that captures `count` and mutates it — under 148's rule it implements `FnMut` and `FnOnce` but not `Fn`. The bound `FnMut` accepts both shapes; same direct empirical witness as lesson 153 Probe 5), 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the parens-bound grammar `<F: FnMut(T) -> R>`; today reuses single-parameter form, identical to `any`), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (load-bearing only for Probe 4 — closure captures `count` from enclosing scope), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs `|param| body`; today's `|x|` reuses the form), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Self::Item` associated-type slot. For `Range<u32>`, `Item = u32`. Probe 7 empirical: rustc spells `FnMut(u32)`), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `&mut self` receiver shape on `next()` and the `let mut`-required rule via E0596 contrast. Today reuses both via the rotation chain established by lesson 153: Probe 5 calls `it.next()` after `.all(...)` returns; Probe 8 fires E0596 with the same payload shape as lessons 131 and 153 with method name rotated to `all`), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` and the parens-rule for method calls. The half-open semantics drives Probe 3's empty case `1..1_u32`), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — `_u32` suffix), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — `u32`), 023-compound-add-assign (load-bearing only for Probe 4 — `count += 1`), 013-comparison-operators (load-bearing — installs `<`, `==` on `u32` produce `bool`. Today's closure bodies use `<` (Probes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) and `>` (Check Yourself); both produce `bool` per lesson 013), 011-println-positional-args (load-bearing — `println!("{}", r)` and `println!("{} {:?}", ..., ...)`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. Today: E0277 (Probe 7, rotation-confirmation only); E0596 (Probe 8, rotation-confirmation only); `unused_comparisons` warning (Probe 3, load-bearing for vacuous-truth point). No new error code today; the `unused_comparisons` warning is a built-in lint, not a separate corpus citation), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — `<F>` slot), 005-let-binding (cited — `let r = ...`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Iterator::position` consumer — sibling with same closure shape `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool` (single-parameter parens-bound, by-value `Self::Item`) but `Option<usize>` return; combines today's short-circuit pattern with a different return-type wrapper; first time the audience meets `Option<usize>` as a payload type" moves, future "the `Iterator::find` consumer — predicate consumer whose closure parameter is `&Self::Item`, not `Self::Item`; first place the audience meets the *reference closure parameter* shape; pulls in the deref-read mechanic; surfaces the double-reference situation when the source iterator already yields references" moves, future "the `Iterator::find_map` consumer — predicate consumer with `FnMut(Self::Item) -> Option<B>` bound; composes today's short-circuit with `Option`-returning closure bodies; returns `Option<B>`" moves, future "the `Iterator::filter` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` bound — surfaces the double-reference situation; returns `Filter<Self, P>`; with this method the end-to-end pipeline `.filter(...).map(...).fold(0, ...)` becomes teachable" moves, future "the `Iterator::inspect` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item)` bound (no return); first place the reference closure parameter shape `&Self::Item` appears outside a predicate context" moves, future "the `Iterator::try_for_each` and `Iterator::try_fold` consumers — short-circuit-with-`?` variants; gate on the `Try` trait sub-arc (audit §4.4.4)" moves, future "the De Morgan duality between `any` and `all` at the closure-body level (`!cond` inversion); named today, deferred for formal treatment" moves, future "the design rule 'why have both `any` and `all`?' — both exist because predicate-direction (existential vs universal) is a real modeling choice; named today, deferred" moves, future "the remaining 22 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `rposition`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; each composes today's wrapper-or-consumer + closure-bound mechanic with method-specific shape" moves
```

```text
id: 155-iterator-position
move: call `Iterator::position` on a `Range<u32>` with a single-parameter predicate closure that returns `bool` — `let r = (10..20_u32).position(|x| x == 15); println!("{:?}", r);`. Working probe `demo.rs` compiled with `rustc demo.rs` (silent, exit 0); `./demo` prints `Some(5)`, exit 0 — `15` matches at index 5 in the yielded sequence `10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, ...`; *load-bearing* index/value distinction (matched value `15` vs returned index `5` are different numbers). Probe 2 `no_match.rs` (`(1..10_u32).position(|x| x == 100)`) compiles silently and prints `None` — closure called for every element of `1..=9`, never returns `true`; corpus correspondence `trait.Iterator.md:2769-2770` "If all of them return `false`, it returns `None`." Probe 3 `first_element.rs` (`(5..10_u32).position(|x| x == 5)`) compiles silently and prints `Some(0)` — first call returns `true`; index is `0`; direct empirical witness for zero-based-index rule. Probe 4 `reusable.rs` (`let mut it = 10..20_u32; let r = it.position(|x| x == 15); let n = it.next(); println!("{:?} {:?}", r, n);`) compiles silently and prints `Some(5) Some(16)` — *load-bearing* triple-witness probe: (a) `it` still bound after `.position(...)` (direct empirical witness for `&mut self` receiver, NOT `self`; same shape as lesson 153 Probe 2 / lesson 154 Probe 5 with method name rotated), (b) iterator's next yielded value is `Some(16)`, not `Some(10)` or `None` — `position` short-circuited on `15` and stopped advancing past the matching element, (c) **three distinct numbers visible at once**: `5` (returned index), `15` (matched element value, implicit), `16` (next element value) — load-bearing index/value disambiguation in a single output line. Probe 5 `empty.rs` (`(1..1_u32).position(|x| x == 5)`) compiles silently and prints `None` — half-open range yields nothing; closure never called; same shape as lesson 152 Probe 2's empty `reduce` returning `None` (once return type is `Option<_>`, the empty case has its own slot); contrast lessons 153/154 where empty returned plain `false`/`true`. Probe 6 `count_calls.rs` (`let mut count = 0_u32; let r = (10..20_u32).position(|x| { count += 1; x == 15 }); println!("{:?} {}", r, count);`) compiles silently and prints `Some(5) 6` — *direct numeric* witness for short-circuit on `true` AND zero-based-index identity: closure called exactly 6 times for `x = 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15` (5 falses then 1 true), not 10 times for the full range; the returned index `Some(5)` is *one less than the call count `6`* because indexing starts at 0 — same `usize` counter shape as lesson 138's `enumerate`. Composes lesson 144 capture + lesson 023 mutation + lesson 148's FnMut auto-impl; same shape as lessons 153 Probe 5 / 154 Probe 4. Probe 7 `type_pin.rs` (`let r: Option<usize> = (10..20_u32).position(|x| x == 15);`) compiles silently and prints `Some(5)` — empirical witness that the inferred return type is exactly `Option<usize>`; first time `Option<usize>` appears as the return type of a closure-driven consumer in the run; composes lesson 119's `Option<T>` with `T = usize` (lesson 077). Probe 8 `non_closure_arg.rs` (`(1..4_u32).position(7)`) fires E0277 with rustc spelling `expected an FnMut(u32) closure, found {integer}` — same shape as lessons 153 Probe 6 / 154 Probe 7 with method name rotated; rustc's `note: required by a bound in position` points at `library/core/src/iter/traits/iterator.rs:3112:4` (*after* lesson 153's `:2863:4` for `any` and lesson 154's `:2809:4` for `all`, matching the corpus order at `trait.Iterator.md:2557` `all`, `:2599` `any`, `:2763` `position`); confirms the trait spelling `FnMut(u32)` matches `any`/`all` exactly despite the type-parameter rename from `<F>` to `<P>` (purely cosmetic). Probe 9 `needs_mut.rs` (`let it = 10..20_u32; let _r = it.position(|x| x == 15);`) fires E0596 with rustc spelling `cannot borrow it as mutable, as it is not declared as mutable` — same shape as lessons 131/153/154 with method name rotated; direct empirical witness that `position`'s receiver is `&mut self`. Source choice: `Range<u32>` (lessons 091 + 080 + 081); closure parameter is `u32` by value; closure bodies are plain `u32 == u32 -> bool` per lesson 013; no `&u32`, no deref-read, no `as` cast, no `Vec`, no `.iter()` / `.into_iter()`. Choice of working probe `(10..20_u32).position(|x| x == 15)` is deliberate: the index `5` and the matched value `15` are *clearly different numbers* — sharper version of the corpus's `[1, 2, 3].position(|x| x == 2) = Some(1)` example where index and value are nearby.
main_concept: `Iterator::position` is the third closure-driven predicate consumer, the *third* method in the by-value-predicate-consumer triplet (`any` 153, `all` 154, `position` 155). The signature, verbatim from `output/docs/rust/std/iter/trait.Iterator.md:2763`, reads `fn position<P>(&mut self, predicate: P) -> Option<usize> where Self: Sized, P: FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool,` — *identical* signature shape to `any`'s and `all`'s except for two notes: (a) the type-parameter slot is named `<P>` instead of `<F>` (documentation convention "P for predicate"; functionally equivalent — Probe 8 empirical: rustc still spells the trait `FnMut(u32)`), and (b) the return slot is `Option<usize>` instead of `bool`. The latter is the only structurally-load-bearing rotation today. Five structural facts under one centered move, four of which carry from lessons 153/154 unchanged. (1) *Same signature shape as `any`/`all`*: one type parameter `<P>` (cosmetically renamed from `<F>`), receiver `&mut self`, exactly one non-receiver argument `predicate: P`, closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool` (single-parameter parens-bound). All these were installed by lessons 153/154 and are reused without re-derivation. (2) *Same iterator-still-usable rule*: `position` borrows the iterator mutably and leaves it usable; iterator's position lands just past the matching element. Probe 4 empirical: `Some(5) Some(16)` — same structure as lesson 153 Probe 2 / lesson 154 Probe 5 with method name rotated. Probe 9 empirical: omitting `mut` on the binding fires E0596. (3) *Same diagnostic shapes for misuse*: E0277 on non-closure arg (Probe 8, payload `FnMut(u32)`) and E0596 on non-mut binding (Probe 9) are identical to lessons 153/154 with method name rotated. (4) *Same short-circuit-on-`true` polarity as `any`*: closure called only until first `true` return; then `position` stops. Corpus `trait.Iterator.md:2772-2773`: "`position()` is short-circuiting; in other words, it will stop processing as soon as it finds a `true`." Probe 6 empirical: closure called exactly 6 times for `x == 15` match in `10..20_u32`, not 10 times for the full range. (5) **NEW: return type rotates from `bool` to `Option<usize>`.** Composes lesson 119's `Option<T>` with `T = usize` (lesson 077). Two coupled sub-facts: (5a) `Some(idx)` carries the **zero-based index** of the iterator element where the closure first returned `true`. The index is a position in the iterator's yielded sequence (counted from `0`, increments by `1` per element — same `usize` counter as lesson 138's `enumerate`), NOT the element value. This is the **load-bearing semantic** today: the matched value and the returned index are different numbers in general. Probe 1 empirical: `(10..20_u32).position(|x| x == 15) = Some(5)` — element value `15`, index `5`. Probe 3 empirical: first-element match returns `Some(0)` (zero-base). Probe 6 empirical: call count `6` − 1 = index `5`. Probe 4 empirical: three distinct numbers `5`, `15`, `16` in a single output line — index, matched value, next value. (5b) `None` covers two cases: closure never returns `true` (no match; iterator walked to exhaustion — Probe 2 empirical), or iterator was empty (closure never called — Probe 5 empirical). Once the return type is `Option<_>`, the empty case has its own slot — same dual as lesson 152's empty `reduce` returning `None`. Contrast with lessons 153/154 where the empty case had to be encoded into the bool return: empty-`any` was `false`, empty-`all` was `true`; today empty-`position` is `None`. The audience crosses from "`any`/`all` return plain `bool` from a single-parameter `FnMut(_) -> bool` closure; both short-circuit and leave the iterator usable" (lessons 153/154) to "`position` is the same shape with the answer rotated: same closure bound, same `&mut self`, same short-circuit. The return is `Option<usize>` instead of `bool`. `Some(idx)` carries the zero-based index of the iterator element where the closure first returned `true`; `None` means no element matched (or the iterator was empty). The index is a position in the yielded sequence, not the element value." No new error code today; the move is *signature-shape-only* relative to lessons 153/154 except for the return-type rotation. Today is *shallow operational*: one closure-driven consumer with single-parameter closure, one trait (`FnMut`), `Self::Item = u32`, Probe 6 captures + mutates (composes 144 + 023 + 148). Source choice — `Range<u32>` over `Vec::iter()` — carries lessons 149-154's discipline forward unchanged. Stabilized at 1.0.0 (`trait.Iterator.md:2761`); local toolchain 1.95.0. Decomposition note: this is the seventh move of the closure-driven Iterator methods sub-arc (audit §4.4.1) and the third move of the predicate-consumer arc — closing the by-value-predicate triplet (`any`, `all`, `position`). `position` is opened *after* `any`/`all` because (a) the empty-case behavior on `Option<_>`-returning consumers was already installed by lesson 152 (`reduce`) and the audience can map it onto `position` without re-derivation; (b) the existential-versus-universal duality of `any`/`all` reads more naturally with both bool-returning siblings adjacent before introducing the wrapper-rotation; (c) the index-vs-value distinction is the smallest possible new structural fact — one bit of new info beyond the bool-returning siblings. Likely next moves per audit §4.4.1: `find` (predicate consumer with `&Self::Item` parameter — gates on deref-read sub-arc); `find_map` (predicate consumer with `FnMut(Self::Item) -> Option<B>` bound); `rposition` (same shape as today, starts from the right; gates on `DoubleEndedIterator`); `filter` (first lazy adapter with predicate closure; `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool`; surfaces double-reference). Explicitly defer: `rposition` (gates on `DoubleEndedIterator`); `find` / `find_map` (sibling moves; gate on deref-read or `Option<B>` return shape); methods on `Option<usize>` (`.unwrap()`, `.map()`, `if let`); `usize::MAX` overflow corner case (named at `:2775-2785`, impractical to probe); `try_for_each` / `try_fold` (short-circuit-with-`?`, gated on Try sub-arc); `v.iter()` / `v.into_iter()` source shapes; `match` on `Option<usize>` *as a centered concept*.
depends_on: 154-iterator-all (load-bearing — direct sibling. Today reuses signature shape (`<F>(&mut self, f: F) -> bool` rotated to `<P>(&mut self, predicate: P) -> Option<usize>`), receiver `&mut self`, closure bound `FnMut(Self::Item) -> bool`, iterator-still-usable rule, the `let mut`-required rule for `&mut self` receivers, and the diagnostic shapes for E0277 and E0596 — all installed by lessons 153/154 and reused today on `position` without re-derivation. Today rotates only the return slot from `bool` to `Option<usize>`. Probe 4 empirical: same structure as lesson 154 Probe 5 with method name rotated and matched value shifted from `5` to `15`; Probe 8 same as lesson 154 Probe 7; Probe 9 same as lesson 154 Probe 8), 153-iterator-any (load-bearing — direct sibling. Today reuses the short-circuit-on-`true` polarity from `any` (lesson 154's `all` inverted to short-circuit on `false`; today's `position` returns to `any`'s polarity). Probe 6 empirical: closure called exactly 6 times for `x == 15` in `10..20_u32` — short-circuits on first `true` like `any`, not on first `false` like `all`. The structural anchor for today's lesson body is the chain 153 → 154 → 155: signature shape unchanged across all three; only the bool-vs-Option<usize> return slot rotates today), 152-iterator-reduce (load-bearing — sibling closure-driven `Option<_>`-returning consumer. Today's empty-case `None` (Probe 5) has the same shape as lesson 152 Probe 2's empty `reduce` returning `None` — once the return type is `Option<_>`, the empty case has its own slot. The lesson body cites 152 once for this dual), 138-iterator-enumerate (load-bearing — installs the `usize` iteration index starting at 0 and incrementing by 1 per element. Today's index has the same identity. Probe 6 empirical: closure call count `6` − 1 = index `5`; first call sat at index `0`, second at `1`, ..., sixth at `5`. The numeric witness is exactly the same `usize` counter as enumerate's iteration index), 148-fn-fnmut-fnonce-distinction (load-bearing — bound is `FnMut`. Probes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 use closures that capture nothing — under 148's auto-impl rule they implement `Fn` and so all three traits. Probe 6 uses a closure that captures `count` and mutates it — under 148's rule it implements `FnMut` and `FnOnce` but not `Fn`. The bound `FnMut` accepts both shapes; same direct empirical witness as lessons 153 Probe 5 / 154 Probe 4), 147-fn-trait-parenthesized-bound (load-bearing — installs the parens-bound grammar `<F: FnMut(T) -> R>`; today reuses single-parameter form, identical to `any`/`all`), 144-closure-captures-outer-let (load-bearing only for Probe 6 — closure captures `count` from enclosing scope), 142-closure-literal-bound-and-called (load-bearing — installs `|param| body`; today's `|x|` reuses the form), 132-iterator-trait-declaration (load-bearing — installs the `Self::Item` associated-type slot. For `Range<u32>`, `Item = u32`. Probe 8 empirical: rustc spells `FnMut(u32)`), 131-iterator-next-call (load-bearing — installs `&mut self` receiver shape on `next()` and the `let mut`-required rule via E0596 contrast. Today reuses both via the rotation chain established by lessons 153/154: Probe 4 calls `it.next()` after `.position(...)` returns; Probe 9 fires E0596 with the same payload shape as lessons 131/153/154 with method name rotated to `position`), 119-option-some-none (load-bearing — installs `Option<T>` with `Some(T)` / `None`. Today's return type `Option<usize>` is `Option<T>` with `T = usize`. Probes 1, 4, 6, 7 yield `Some(_)` outputs; Probes 2, 5 yield `None` outputs. Probe 7 type-pins `Option<usize>` empirically. The annotation context (a method whose return type is fixed to `Option<usize>`) pins `T = usize` without needing the explicit annotation that lesson 119 Probe 2 required for a bare `None`), 091-range-reversal-rev (load-bearing — installs `Range<A>: Iterator` for `A: Step` and the parens-rule for method calls. The half-open semantics drives Probe 5's empty case `1..1_u32`), 081-integer-literal-forms (load-bearing — `_u32` suffix on `10..20_u32`, `1..10_u32`, `5..10_u32`, `1..1_u32`, `1..4_u32`, `0_u32`), 080-integer-type-family (load-bearing — `u32`), 077-array-indexing-and-usize (load-bearing — installs `usize` integer type. Today's `Option<usize>` payload type is exactly `usize`; Probe 7 type-pins this explicitly), 023-compound-add-assign (load-bearing only for Probe 6 — `count += 1`), 013-comparison-operators (load-bearing — installs `==` on `u32` produces `bool`. Today's closure bodies use `==` (Probes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7); produces `bool` per lesson 013 — return type matches the bound's return slot `-> bool`), 011-println-positional-args (load-bearing — `println!("{:?}", r)` (Debug format for Option) and `println!("{:?} {:?}", ..., ...)` and `println!("{:?} {}", ..., ...)`), 003-read-rustc-diagnostic (load-bearing — installs the four-part diagnostic map. Today: E0277 (Probe 8, rotation-confirmation only); E0596 (Probe 9, rotation-confirmation only). No new error code today), 145-generic-function-type-parameter (cited — `<F>` / `<P>` slot; today's only generic), 005-let-binding (cited — `let r = ...`), 002-fn-main-entry-point (cited), 001-rustc-compile-and-run (cited), ordinary computer-use (terminal, plain-text editor, `rustc` on `PATH`, Linux/macOS shell)
unlocks: future "the `Iterator::find` consumer — predicate consumer whose closure parameter is `&Self::Item`, not `Self::Item`; first place the audience meets the *reference closure parameter* shape; pulls in the deref-read mechanic; surfaces the double-reference situation when the source iterator already yields references; returns `Option<Self::Item>`" moves, future "the `Iterator::find_map` consumer — predicate consumer with `FnMut(Self::Item) -> Option<B>` bound; composes today's short-circuit with `Option`-returning closure bodies; returns `Option<B>`" moves, future "the `Iterator::rposition` consumer — same shape as today's `position` but starts from the right; gates on `DoubleEndedIterator` trait sub-arc; the bound is more restrictive (`Self: Sized + ExactSizeIterator + DoubleEndedIterator`)" moves, future "the `Iterator::filter` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool` bound — surfaces the double-reference situation; returns `Filter<Self, P>`; with this method the end-to-end pipeline `.filter(...).map(...).fold(0, ...)` becomes teachable" moves, future "the `Iterator::inspect` lazy adapter with `FnMut(&Self::Item)` bound (no return); first place the reference closure parameter shape `&Self::Item` appears outside a predicate context" moves, future "methods on `Option<usize>` — `.unwrap()`, `.map()`, `.unwrap_or()`, `if let Some(i) = r { ... }`; lesson 119 named these as deferred and they remain so today" moves, future "the `Iterator::try_for_each` and `Iterator::try_fold` consumers — short-circuit-with-`?` variants; gate on the `Try` trait sub-arc (audit §4.4.4)" moves, future "the `usize::MAX` overflow corner case — corpus-named at `trait.Iterator.md:2775-2785`; impractical to probe; deferred" moves, future "the index/position naming distinction — both names refer to the same `usize` counter from lesson 138; detailed treatment deferred" moves, future "the remaining 21 closure-driven Iterator methods (audit §4.4.1) — `filter_map`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `map_while`, `scan`, `partition`, `flat_map`, `try_for_each`, `max_by`, `max_by_key`, `min_by`, `min_by_key`, `is_sorted_by`, `is_sorted_by_key`; each composes today's wrapper-or-consumer + closure-bound mechanic with method-specific shape" moves
```
